**Step-by-Step De-encapsulation (Receiver Side):** The reverse of the above. Each layer strips its corresponding header, performs error checks, and passes the payload up to the next layer.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Layer-by-Layer Function Summary

| Layer | Name | PDU | Key Function | Addressing | Example Protocol |
|-------|------|-----|-------------|------------|------------------|
| 7 | Application | Message | User interface | None | HTTP, FTP, SMTP |
| 6 | Presentation | Message | Encryption, compression | None | TLS, JPEG |
| 5 | Session | Message | Dialogue control | None | RPC, SIP |
| 4 | Transport | Segment/Datagram | End-to-end delivery | Port Number | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | Network | Packet/Datagram | Routing, logical addressing | IP Address | IP, ICMP, OSPF |
| 2 | Data Link | Frame | Hop-by-hop, error detection | MAC Address | Ethernet, Wi-Fi |
| 1 | Physical | Bit | Raw bit transmission | None | 100BASE-TX, DSL |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Seven-Layer OSI Reference Model (Vertical Stack)

This is the **master diagram** KTU students must memorize — examiners often expect a clean, labeled version of this in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["LAYER 7: APPLICATION\nHTTP FTP SMTP DNS"]
    A2["LAYER 6: PRESENTATION\nTLS SSL JPEG MPEG"]
    A3["LAYER 5: SESSION\nRPC NetBIOS SIP"]
    A4["LAYER 4: TRANSPORT\nTCP UDP SCTP"]
    A5["LAYER 3: NETWORK\nIP ICMP OSPF BGP"]
    A6["LAYER 2: DATA LINK\nEthernet WiFi PPP HDLC"]
    A7["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL\nCables Fiber Wireless Radio"]
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7
```

### 4.2 Data Encapsulation Flow (Sender Side)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dataA["Application Data\nMessage"] --> dataB["Transport Header + Data\nSEGMENT"]
    dataB --> dataC["Network Header + Segment\nPACKET"]
    dataC --> dataD["Data Link Header + Packet + Trailer\nFRAME"]
    dataD --> dataE["PHYSICAL SIGNAL\nBITS ON THE WIRE"]
```

### 4.3 Peer-to-Peer Communication Concept (Logical vs. Physical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Machine_A
        A1["Layer 7 App"] --> A2["Layer 6 Pres"] --> A3["Layer 5 Sess"] --> A4["Layer 4 Trans"] --> A5["Layer 3 Net"] --> A6["Layer 2 DL"] --> A7["Layer 1 Phys"]
    end
    subgraph Machine_B
        B1["Layer 7 App"] --> B2["Layer 6 Pres"] --> B3["Layer 5 Sess"] --> B4["Layer 4 Trans"] --> B5["Layer 3 Net"] --> B6["Layer 2 DL"] --> B7["Layer 1 Phys"]
    end
    A7 -- "Physical Signal" --> B7
    A7 -. "Virtual Peer Chat" .-> B7
```

> [!NOTE]
> *In the above diagram, the dashed line represents the VIRTUAL (logical) peer-to-peer communication at the Physical layer. In reality, the actual signal flows through the physical medium (solid line). This is the single most important conceptual diagram in the entire OSI model.*

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Service Access Points (SAPs)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Upper_Stack
        L7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION"]
        L6["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION"]
        L5["LAYER 5 SESSION"]
    end
    subgraph Middle_Stack
        L4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT\nSAP: PORT NUMBER"]
        L3["LAYER 3 NETWORK\nSAP: IP ADDRESS"]
    end
    subgraph Lower_Stack
        L2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK\nSAP: MAC ADDRESS"]
        L1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL\nSAP: NO LOGICAL ADDRESS"]
    end
    L7 -- "Data" --> L6
    L6 -- "Data" --> L5
    L5 -- "Data" --> L4
    L4 -- "Segment" --> L3
    L3 -- "Packet" --> L2
    L2 -- "Frame" --> L1
    L1 -- "Bits" --> Wire["TRANSMISSION MEDIUM"]
```

### 4.5 Connection-Oriented vs. Connectionless Service Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Connection_Oriented
        CO1["Sender"] -- "1. CONNECT.request" --> CO2["Receiver"]
        CO2 -- "2. CONNECT.response" --> CO1
        CO1 -- "3. DATA transfer" --> CO2
        CO2 -- "4. ACKs" --> CO1
        CO1 -- "5. DISCONNECT.request" --> CO2
    end
    subgraph Connectionless
        CL1["Sender"] -- "DATA (no setup)" --> CL2["Receiver"]
        CL1 -- "DATA (independent)" --> CL2
        CL1 -- "DATA (independent)" --> CL2
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Network Architecture and a Network Reference Model. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Network Architecture | Network Reference Model |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Definition** | A set of protocols that defines *how* communication should happen | A conceptual blueprint that defines *what* each layer should do |
| **Concrete/Abstract** | Concrete (specific protocols, ports, formats) | Abstract (no specific protocols) |
| **Example** | TCP/IP Architecture | OSI Reference Model |
| **Purpose** | Implementation | Standardization & Teaching |

*(Marking: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for concrete/abstract distinction, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the seven layers of the OSI Reference Model from top to bottom. State the PDU name at each layer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layer 7} &= \text{Application} \rightarrow \text{Message/Data} \\
\text{Layer 6} &= \text{Presentation} \rightarrow \text{Message/Data} \\
\text{Layer 5} &= \text{Session} \rightarrow \text{Message/Data} \\
\text{Layer 4} &= \text{Transport} \rightarrow \text{Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP)} \\
\text{Layer 3} &= \text{Network} \rightarrow \text{Packet / Datagram} \\
\text{Layer 2} &= \text{Data Link} \rightarrow \text{Frame} \\
\text{Layer 1} &= \text{Physical} \rightarrow \text{Bit}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Marking: 1.5 marks for layer names in correct order, 1.5 marks for PDU names.)*

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A. [14 Marks — KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the seven layers of the OSI Reference Model in detail, stating the function and giving **one example protocol** for each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the process of **data encapsulation and de-encapsulation** as data travels from the Application layer of a source machine to the Application layer of a destination machine. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven Layers of the OSI Reference Model (7 Marks):**

The OSI Reference Model, developed by ISO in 1984, is a 7-layer conceptual model. Each layer has a specific function:

1. **Application Layer (Layer 7):** Provides network services to end-user applications. *Function:* Interface between user and network. *Example:* HTTP, FTP, SMTP.

2. **Presentation Layer (Layer 6):** Handles data translation, encryption, and compression. *Function:* Ensures data is in a usable format and is secure. *Example:* TLS/SSL, JPEG.

3. **Session Layer (Layer 5):** Manages and controls connections (sessions) between two hosts. *Function:* Establishes, maintains, terminates dialogues. *Example:* RPC, NetBIOS.

4. **Transport Layer (Layer 4):** Provides end-to-end (process-to-process) logical communication. *Function:* Segmentation, flow control, error control, port addressing. *Example:* TCP, UDP.

5. **Network Layer (Layer 3):** Provides logical addressing and routing across multiple networks. *Function:* Packet forwarding, best-path selection, fragmentation. *Example:* IP, ICMP, OSPF.

6. **Data Link Layer (Layer 2):** Provides node-to-node (hop-by-hop) data transfer. *Function:* Framing, MAC addressing, error detection (CRC). *Example:* Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP.

7. **Physical Layer (Layer 1):** Transmits raw bits over a physical medium. *Function:* Bit representation, data rate, physical topology. *Example:* 100BASE-TX, DSL.

*(Marking: 1 mark per layer for naming + function; 0 mark cut for missing example.)*

---

**(b) Encapsulation and De-encapsulation (7 Marks):**

**Encapsulation (Sender Side):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{App Layer:} \quad \text{Data "GET / HTTP/1.1"} \\
&\text{Pres Layer:} \quad \text{Data encrypted (TLS ciphertext)} \\
&\text{Sess Layer:} \quad \text{Session ID, Checkpoint added} \\
&\text{Trans Layer:} \quad \text{TCP Header (Src/Dst Port, Seq, Ack) added} \rightarrow \text{SEGMENT} \\
&\text{Net Layer:} \quad \text{IP Header (Src/Dst IP) added} \rightarrow \text{PACKET} \\
&\text{DL Layer:} \quad \text{Ethernet Header (MAC) + Packet + CRC Trailer} \rightarrow \text{FRAME} \\
&\text{Phys Layer:} \quad \text{Frame converted to bits/signals} \rightarrow \text{BITSTREAM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**De-encapsulation (Receiver Side):** The receiver's Physical layer receives the bits, reassembles the frame, checks the CRC (error detection), strips the Ethernet header, and passes the packet to the Network layer. The Network layer checks the destination IP, strips the IP header, and passes the segment to the Transport layer. The Transport layer reassembles the segments, checks for sequence integrity, strips the TCP header, and passes the data up to the Session, Presentation, and finally the Application layer, which delivers the original message to the user.

**Key Point:** Each layer adds its own **header** (and sometimes trailer), and the receiver strips it before passing the data to the next upper layer.

---

#### **Question B. [14 Marks — KTU University Exam — Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Compare **Connection-Oriented and Connectionless services** in detail. Mention at least **three differences** and explain at which OSI layers each type is typically offered. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **protocol hierarchy**? Explain with a clear example how data passes through **five layers** (Application, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical) showing **headers and trailers** added at each step. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Connection-Oriented vs. Connectionless Services (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Connection Setup** | Required (handshake) | Not required |
| **State Information** | Maintained at both ends | Stateless |
| **Reliability** | High (ACKs, retransmission) | Low (no recovery) |
| **Order of Delivery** | Guaranteed | Not guaranteed |
| **Resource Allocation** | Pre-allocated | On-demand |
| **Analogy** | Telephone call | Postal letter |
| **Example** | TCP | UDP, IP |
| **Overhead** | High | Low |
| **Speed** | Slower (due to overhead) | Faster |
| **OSI Layer** | Transport (TCP), Session | Network (IP), Transport (UDP) |

**Three Critical Differences:**

1. **Setup Phase:** Connection-oriented services require a 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) before data transfer. Connectionless services send data immediately.
2. **Reliability:** Connection-oriented protocols use ACKs and retransmissions to guarantee delivery. Connectionless protocols are best-effort with no guarantees.
3. **Use Case:** Connection-oriented is used for applications requiring accuracy (HTTP, FTP, Email). Connectionless is used for speed-sensitive applications (video streaming, DNS, VoIP).

---

**(b) Protocol Hierarchy and Data Flow (7 Marks):**

A **protocol hierarchy** is a set of layers stacked on top of each other, where each layer performs a specific communication function and provides services to the layer above it.

**Example: A User Sends "HELLO" via HTTP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Layer 5 (Application):} \quad \text{Data = "HELLO"} \\
&\text{Layer 4 (Transport — TCP):} \quad \text{[TCP Header] + "HELLO"} \rightarrow \text{Segment} \\
&\quad \text{TCP Header = Src Port (5000), Dst Port (80), Seq=1} \\
&\text{Layer 3 (Network — IP):} \quad \text{[IP Header] + [TCP Header] + "HELLO"} \rightarrow \text{Packet} \\
&\quad \text{IP Header = Src IP (192.168.1.5), Dst IP (93.184.216.34)} \\
&\text{Layer 2 (Data Link — Ethernet):} \quad \text{[MAC Header] + [IP Header] + [TCP Header] + "HELLO" + [CRC]} \rightarrow \text{Frame} \\
&\quad \text{MAC Header = Src MAC (AA:BB:CC:11:22:33), Dst MAC (DD:EE:FF:44:55:66)} \\
&\text{Layer 1 (Physical):} \quad \text{Frame converted to voltage pulses (bits): 01101000...}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Observation:** The original data ("HELLO") remains unchanged at the payload; each layer only **adds** its own header (and Layer 2 adds a trailer). This is the essence of **encapsulation**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Service with Protocol:** A *service* is what a layer offers to the upper layer; a *protocol* is the set of rules used between peer layers. Examiners deduct marks when these terms are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Wrong PDU Names:** A common mistake is calling a *Frame* a "packet" or a *Segment* a "datagram" outside the correct layer context. Memorize: **Bits → Frame → Packet → Segment → Message**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Trailer:** Layer 2 (Data Link) is the ONLY layer that adds a **trailer** (CRC) in addition to the header. Examiners test this!
> 4. **Mixing OSI and TCP/IP Layers:** A frequent error is writing "Network layer uses TCP" — TCP is a *Transport* layer protocol. Be precise.
> 5. **Missing Real-World Protocol Examples:** When asked to give an example, do NOT just write "Layer 4 — Transport." Always name a protocol: **TCP, UDP, IP, HTTP, Ethernet, etc.**

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- [x] **OSI Model = 7 Layers, ISO 7498, 1984.** Always the first fact in any KTU answer.
- [x] **Layer Order (Top to Bottom):** Application → Presentation → Session → Transport → Network → Data Link → Physical. Mnemonic: **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"** or **"A Pithy Sentence That Nobody Can Forget."**
- [x] **PDU Names:** Message (L7–L5), Segment (L4-TCP), Datagram (L4-UDP/L3), Packet (L3), Frame (L2), Bit (L1).
- [x] **Addresses:** MAC = Layer 2, IP = Layer 3, Port = Layer 4.
- [x] **Header vs. Trailer:** All layers add **headers**. Only **Layer 2 (Data Link)** adds a **trailer** (CRC/FCS).
- [x] **Connection-Oriented Service:** TCP (Layer 4). **Connectionless Service:** UDP (Layer 4), IP (Layer 3).
- [x] **Reference Model vs. Architecture:** OSI = Reference Model (abstract); TCP/IP = Architecture (concrete).
- [x] **Peer Communication:** Horizontal (logical) — peer layers talk as if directly connected. Vertical (physical) — actual signal flow happens only at Layer 1.
- [x] **Service Access Points (SAPs):** The "door" through which a layer above accesses the services of the layer below.
- [x] **Encapsulation:** Top-down process of adding headers; **De-encapsulation:** Bottom-up process of removing headers.
- [x] **Layer 1 is the only layer concerned with bits and physical signals.** Everything above it deals with logical constructs.
- [x] **End-to-end vs. Hop-by-hop:** Transport (Layer 4) is the first end-to-end layer. Network (Layer 3) and Data Link (Layer 2) are hop-by-hop.
- [x] **KTU Top 3 Pitfalls to Avoid:** Confusing service with protocol, missing the Layer 2 trailer, and incorrect PDU names.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The TCP/IP Reference Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** The TCP/IP Reference Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **TCP/IP Reference Model** (also called the **Internet Protocol Suite** or **DoD Model**) is a concise, four-layer abstract architectural framework that governs the design, implementation, and communication of all modern internet-based networks. It was developed under the sponsorship of the **United States Department of Defense (DoD)** through the **Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA)** in the 1970s as the foundational protocol stack for the **ARPANET** project.

Unlike the seven-layered **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model**, which is purely theoretical and prescriptive, the TCP/IP model is **descriptive and protocol-centric** — it was reverse-engineered from the already-working ARPANET protocols (TCP and IP) and later standardized by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** through **Request for Comments (RFC)** documents such as **RFC 793 (TCP)** and **RFC 791 (IP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The TCP/IP model is a **mandatory Module 1 topic** in PEMRT751. Examiners frequently contrast it with the OSI model. Memorize the exact layer names, their corresponding PDUs (Protocol Data Units), and key protocols in each layer.

## 1.2 The Four Layers — Formal Naming

| # | Layer Name | Standard Abbreviation |
|---|------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Application Layer | APP |
| 2 | Transport Layer | TCP/UDP |
| 3 | Internet Layer | IP |
| 4 | Network Access Layer (or Link/Network Interface) | N/A |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The International Postal System

Imagine you are sending a **sealed, registered letter** from your home in **Kerala, India** to a friend in **Berlin, Germany**. You do not personally drive the letter across continents. Instead:

- **You (the Application Layer):** You write the letter in English (a common "language") and put it inside an envelope. You are concerned only about the *content* and *destination address (domain name)*.
- **The Postal Sorting Office (the Transport Layer):** It assigns a tracking number, decides whether the letter needs guaranteed delivery (registered) or just best-effort (ordinary mail). It chops the letter if it is too large.
- **The Global Routing Network (the Internet Layer):** It decides the *best logical route* — perhaps via Mumbai → Dubai → Frankfurt — based on global addressing (IP address).
- **The Truck, Ship, and Plane (the Network Access Layer):** It carries each physical packet across oceans, highways, and air corridors using specific physical media (copper, fiber, radio waves) and local addressing (MAC address).

Just as the postal system needs *all four* layers to deliver a letter, the TCP/IP model needs all four layers to deliver a web page from a server in California to your laptop in Trivandrum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** The TCP/IP model is not a single protocol — it is a **suite (family)** of cooperating protocols arranged in a layered hierarchy. Each layer *serves* the layer above it and *uses* the services of the layer below it (the **service hierarchy principle**).

## 1.4 Historical Origin (Brief)

- **1969** — ARPANET commissioned by DoD.
- **1974** — Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn publish *"A Protocol for Packet Network Intercommunication"* — the conceptual seed of TCP.
- **1983** — ARPANET officially switches from the older **NCP (Network Control Protocol)** to TCP/IP, marking the "**birthday of the Internet**" (**January 1, 1983**).
- **1989** — Tim Berners-Lee at CERN invents the **World Wide Web (WWW)** atop the existing TCP/IP stack.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Layers — Detailed Operational Breakdown

### 🔹 Layer 4 — Network Access Layer (Bottom-most)

- **Also Known As:** Link Layer, Network Interface Layer, Data Link + Physical Layer (combined).
- **Core Function:** Defines the **hardware addressing scheme**, **physical topology**, **framing**, **error detection (not correction)** at the bit level, and the **electrical/optical/mechanical** specifications of the transmission medium.
- **Address Used:** **MAC (Media Access Control) Address** — 48 bits (e.g., `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`), burned into the NIC.
- **Key Protocols/Technologies:** **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**, **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)**, **SLIP**, **ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)** (often straddles Internet/Link layers), **Frame Relay**, **ATM**.

### 🔹 Layer 3 — Internet Layer

- **Core Function:** Provides **best-effort, connectionless, global logical addressing and routing** of packets across heterogeneous networks. This is the *only* layer that runs on **every router** in the path.
- **Address Used:** **IP Address** (IPv4 = 32 bits, IPv6 = 128 bits).
- **PDU Name:** **Datagram (Packet)**.
- **Key Protocols:** **IP (Internet Protocol)** — the "thin waist" of the hourglass; **ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol)** — used by `ping` and `traceroute`; **IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol)** — for multicast; **BGP, OSPF, RIP** — routing protocols.

### 🔹 Layer 2 — Transport Layer

- **Core Function:** Provides **end-to-end (process-to-process)** communication, multiplexing/demultiplexing via **port numbers**, flow control, congestion control, and reliability (if TCP).
- **Address Used:** **Port Number** (16 bits, range **0–65535**).
- **PDU Name:** **Segment (TCP)** or **Datagram (UDP)**.
- **Two Key Protocols:**
  - **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** — connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream, uses **3-way handshake**.
  - **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** — connectionless, unreliable, message-oriented, low overhead.
- **Reserved Port Numbers:** **HTTP = 80**, **HTTPS = 443**, **FTP = 21**, **SSH = 22**, **DNS = 53**, **SMTP = 25**.

### 🔹 Layer 1 — Application Layer (Top-most)

- **Core Function:** Provides **network services directly to end-user applications** (browsers, email clients, file transfer tools). It is the *interface* between the user's software and the protocol stack.
- **PDU Name:** **Message / Data**.
- **Key Protocols:** **HTTP/HTTPS**, **FTP**, **SMTP**, **POP3**, **IMAP**, **DNS**, **DHCP**, **SNMP**, **Telnet**, **SSH**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Hourglass" Model:** A high-yield KTU point. The TCP/IP architecture resembles an **hourglass** — the *thin waist* is the **Internet Layer (IP)**. Above it can be any application; below it can be any physical medium. This is what allows the Internet to interconnect billions of heterogeneous networks.

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Header Overhead Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{header}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage; higher is more efficient |
| **Total Frame Size** | $L_{total} = L_{header} + L_{payload} + L_{trailer}$ | Bytes (or bits) |
| **IPv4 Address Space** | $N_{IPv4} = 2^{32}$ | $\approx 4.29 \times 10^{9}$ addresses |
| **IPv6 Address Space** | $N_{IPv6} = 2^{128}$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ addresses |
| **Port Number Range** | $0 \le P \le 65535$ | Well-known: 0–1023; Registered: 1024–49151; Dynamic: 49152–65535 |
| **TCP Header Length (min)** | 20 bytes (no options) | Max = 60 bytes |
| **UDP Header Length (fixed)** | 8 bytes | Always fixed |
| **IPv4 Header Length (min)** | 20 bytes (no options) | Max = 60 bytes |
| **Theoretical Max Throughput** | $T_{max} = \dfrac{W}{RTT}$ | Packets/sec; W = window size |

## 2.3 Why This Architecture? — The Engineering Rationale

- **Modularity:** A change in one layer (e.g., upgrading from IPv4 to IPv6) does *not* require rewriting application code — provided the service interface is preserved.
- **Interoperability:** Any device following the IP standard can talk to any other device globally.
- **Fault Tolerance:** Because routing is **decentralized** and the Internet layer is **connectionless**, the network can survive partial failures (a key DoD requirement for **nuclear-resilient communication**).
- **Scalability:** Hierarchical addressing and aggregation in routing tables enable the Internet to scale to billions of nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Every email you send, every YouTube video you stream, every UPI transaction you make from your phone uses the TCP/IP stack. The model is the *silent backbone* of modern digital infrastructure, including **IoT (Internet of Things)**, **5G core networks**, and **cloud data centers**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — PDU, Addressing & Protocol Mapping

| TCP/IP Layer | OSI Equivalent | PDU Name | Addressing Used | Example Protocols |
|--------------|---------------|----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Application | 7, 6, 5 (combined) | Message / Data | None at this layer (uses ports from Transport) | HTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP, DHCP |
| Transport | 4 (Transport) | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) | **Port Number (16-bit)** | TCP, UDP |
| Internet | 3 (Network) | **Datagram / Packet** | **IP Address (32 or 128-bit)** | IP, ICMP, IGMP, ARP* |
| Network Access | 2, 1 (Data Link + Physical) | **Frame** (then Bits) | **MAC Address (48-bit)** | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, Frame Relay |

> *ARP is a special bridging protocol that resolves IP → MAC mappings and is often considered part of the Internet/Link boundary.

## 3.2 Encapsulation — A Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough

**Scenario:** A user in Kerala opens a browser and requests `http://www.example.com/index.html` (HTTP GET request, payload = 800 bytes).

### Step 1 — Application Layer Generates the Message
The browser (HTTP client) constructs an HTTP GET request. The total application message size is **800 bytes**.

$$L_{App} = 800 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 2 — Transport Layer Encapsulation (TCP)
TCP adds its **header**. Minimum TCP header = **20 bytes** (no options). The resulting PDU is called a **TCP Segment**.

$$L_{TCP} = L_{App} + H_{TCP} = 800 + 20 = 820 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 3 — Internet Layer Encapsulation (IPv4)
IP adds its **header**. Minimum IPv4 header = **20 bytes**. The resulting PDU is called a **Datagram** or **Packet**.

$$L_{IP} = L_{TCP} + H_{IPv4} = 820 + 20 = 840 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 4 — Network Access Layer Encapsulation (Ethernet)
Ethernet II frames add a **14-byte header** (6-byte destination MAC + 6-byte source MAC + 2-byte EtherType) and a **4-byte trailer** (FCS / CRC for error detection). The resulting PDU is called a **Frame**.

$$L_{Frame} = L_{IP} + H_{Eth} + T_{Eth} = 840 + 14 + 4 = 858 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 5 — Physical Layer Transmission
The frame is converted into a stream of **bits** on the physical medium (copper/fiber/radio). With Ethernet, we also need a **12-byte inter-frame gap** and an **8-byte preamble**, but these are medium-dependent and not part of the logical PDU.

$$L_{Bits} = L_{Frame} \times 8 = 858 \times 8 = 6864 \text{ bits}$$

## 3.3 Worked-Out Derivation — Header Overhead Efficiency

**Question:** What is the **efficiency** of transmitting an 800-byte HTTP payload over Ethernet/IPv4/TCP?

### Step 1 — Identify all header + trailer contributions

$$H_{TCP} = 20 \text{ B}, \quad H_{IPv4} = 20 \text{ B}, \quad H_{Eth} = 14 \text{ B}, \quad T_{Eth} = 4 \text{ B}$$

### Step 2 — Sum total overhead

$$O_{total} = 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 58 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 3 — Compute total frame size

$$L_{total} = L_{payload} + O_{total} = 800 + 58 = 858 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 4 — Compute efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{800}{858} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta \approx 93.24\%$$

### Step 5 — Interpret

The protocol overhead consumes **6.76%** of the link's capacity. This is why **jumbo frames (MTU ≈ 9000 B)** are used in data centers — larger payloads amortize the fixed header cost.

$$\eta_{jumbo} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 58} \approx 99.28\%$$

## 3.4 Worked-Out Derivation — Reverse Process (De-encapsulation at Receiver)

The receiver performs the exact inverse:

1. **Physical** receives bits → assembles **Frame** (858 B).
2. **Network Access** strips Ethernet header (14 B) + trailer (4 B) → forwards **Datagram** (840 B) upward.
3. **Internet** strips IP header (20 B) → forwards **Segment** (820 B) upward.
4. **Transport** strips TCP header (20 B) → delivers **Application Message** (800 B) to the web server process (identified by destination port 80).

$$L_{App}^{received} = L_{App}^{sent} = 800 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(if no fragmentation or loss)}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The TCP/IP Layered Architecture (Vertical Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["LAYER 4 - APPLICATION LAYER\nHTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, DHCP\nPDU = Message"]
    TRANS["LAYER 3 - TRANSPORT LAYER\nTCP and UDP\nPDU = Segment or Datagram\nPort Number Addressing"]
    NET["LAYER 2 - INTERNET LAYER\nIPv4 and IPv6, ICMP, IGMP, ARP\nPDU = Datagram\nIP Address Addressing"]
    LINK["LAYER 1 - NETWORK ACCESS LAYER\nEthernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, Frame Relay\nPDU = Frame then Bits\nMAC Address Addressing"]
    PHY["PHYSICAL MEDIUM\nCopper, Fiber Optic, Radio Waves"]
    SENDER["SENDING HOST"] -. Encapsulation .-> APP
    APP --> TRANS
    TRANS --> NET
    NET --> LINK
    LINK --> PHY
    PHY -. Bits Travel .-> ROUTER1["ROUTER - Internet and Link layers only"]
    ROUTER1 --> PHY2["PHYSICAL MEDIUM"]
    PHY2 --> RECV["RECEIVING HOST"]
    RECV -. De-encapsulation .-> LINK2["LAYER 1"]
    LINK2 --> NET2["LAYER 2"]
    NET2 --> TRANS2["LAYER 3"]
    TRANS2 --> APP2["LAYER 4"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (End-to-End Data Path)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USER["USER APPLICATION\nWeb Browser or Email Client"] --> A1["APPLICATION LAYER\nConstructs HTTP or SMTP message\nPayload = 800 bytes"]
    A1 --> A2["TRANSPORT LAYER TCP\nAdds 20 byte TCP header\nSource Port and Destination Port\nSequence and Acknowledgement numbers"]
    A2 --> A3["INTERNET LAYER IPv4\nAdds 20 byte IP header\nSource IP and Destination IP\nTime to Live TTL and Checksum"]
    A3 --> A4["NETWORK ACCESS LAYER Ethernet\nAdds 14 byte header and 4 byte trailer\nSource MAC and Destination MAC\nFrame Check Sequence FCS"]
    A4 --> A5["PHYSICAL LAYER\nConverts frame into electrical optical or radio signals\nBit rate = 1 Gbps example"]
    A5 --> WIRE["TRANSMISSION MEDIUM\nCopper Cable or Fiber or Wireless"]
    WIRE --> A6["PHYSICAL LAYER Receiver\nReceives and synchronizes bits"]
    A6 --> A7["NETWORK ACCESS LAYER Receiver\nVerifies FCS and strips MAC header trailer"]
    A7 --> A8["INTERNET LAYER Receiver\nVerifies IP checksum and decrements TTL\nRoutes to next hop if needed"]
    A8 --> A9["TRANSPORT LAYER Receiver\nReassembles segments in order using sequence numbers\nSends ACK back to sender"]
    A9 --> A10["APPLICATION LAYER Receiver\nDelivers reassembled HTTP message\nto the web server process on port 80"]
```

## 4.3 TCP/IP vs OSI — Visual Comparison (Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OSI["OSI MODEL 7 LAYERS"]
        O7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION"]
        O6["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION"]
        O5["LAYER 5 SESSION"]
        O4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT"]
        O3["LAYER 3 NETWORK"]
        O2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK"]
        O1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL"]
        O7 --> O6 --> O5 --> O4 --> O3 --> O2 --> O1
    end
    subgraph TCP["TCP IP MODEL 4 LAYERS"]
        T4["LAYER 4 APPLICATION\nMaps to OSI 7 plus 6 plus 5"]
        T3["LAYER 3 TRANSPORT\nMaps to OSI 4"]
        T2["LAYER 2 INTERNET\nMaps to OSI 3"]
        T1["LAYER 1 NETWORK ACCESS\nMaps to OSI 2 plus 1"]
        T4 --> T3 --> T2 --> T1
    end
    O7 -. Combined into .-> T4
    O6 -. Combined into .-> T4
    O5 -. Combined into .-> T4
    O4 -.-> T3
    O3 -.-> T2
    O2 -. Combined into .-> T1
    O1 -. Combined into .-> T1
```

## 4.4 The Internet Hourglass Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["APPLICATION LAYER\nHTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH, SNMP\nDozens of protocols evolving freely"]
    MID["INTERNET LAYER\nIP - THE THIN WAIST\nSingle universal packet format"]
    BOT["NETWORK ACCESS LAYER\nEthernet, Wi-Fi, Fiber, Satellite, 5G\nMany physical media evolving freely"]
    TOP -->|Encapsulated into| MID
    MID -->|Encapsulated into| BOT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** This hourglass shape is the *conceptual reason* the Internet scaled — the middle is rigid (IP), but the top and bottom can innovate without coordination.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define the TCP/IP Reference Model. List its four layers in order from top to bottom.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
The TCP/IP Reference Model (also known as the Internet Protocol Suite) is a four-layer conceptual framework developed by DARPA in the 1970s that defines the protocols used for communication over the Internet. Its four layers from top to bottom are:

1. **Application Layer** — provides network services to end-user applications (e.g., HTTP, FTP, DNS).
2. **Transport Layer** — provides end-to-end process-to-process delivery using TCP or UDP.
3. **Internet Layer** — provides global logical addressing and routing of packets using IP.
4. **Network Access Layer** — handles physical transmission, framing, and MAC addressing over the local medium.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for correct layer ordering)*

---

### **Q2.** Differentiate between TCP and UDP at the Transport Layer.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) | UDP (User Datagram Protocol) |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Connection Type | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Reliable (ACK + retransmission) | Unreliable (best-effort) |
| Header Size | **20 bytes minimum**, up to 60 bytes | **Fixed 8 bytes** |
| Speed | Slower due to overhead | Faster due to low overhead |
| Use Cases | Web (HTTP/HTTPS), Email, File Transfer | DNS, Video streaming, VoIP, Gaming |
| Flow & Congestion Control | Yes | No |
| Ordering | Guaranteed in-order delivery | No ordering guarantee |

*(3 marks: any 3 valid contrasting points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024]

**(a)** Draw the TCP/IP reference model with all four layers. Explain the function of each layer along with two key protocols used in that layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the process of encapsulation as data travels down the TCP/IP stack. Use a suitable example with a 1200-byte HTTP message to compute the final Ethernet frame size, considering TCP (20 B), IPv4 (20 B), and Ethernet (14 B header + 4 B trailer). Also calculate the header overhead efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

The TCP/IP model has four layers:

1. **Application Layer** — Provides network services to user applications. Functions: high-level API for network access, message formatting. *Protocols:* **HTTP** (web), **DNS** (name resolution).
2. **Transport Layer** — End-to-end delivery, multiplexing via ports, reliability/flow control. *Protocols:* **TCP** (reliable), **UDP** (fast).
3. **Internet Layer** — Logical addressing and routing across networks. *Protocols:* **IP**, **ICMP**.
4. **Network Access Layer** — Physical transmission, framing, MAC addressing. *Protocols:* **Ethernet**, **Wi-Fi**.

*(7 marks: 1 for diagram, 4 for layer descriptions, 2 for protocols)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

**Given:** HTTP payload $L_{payload} = 1200$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Application Layer:** $L_{App} = 1200$ bytes.

**Step 2 — TCP Encapsulation:**

$$L_{TCP} = 1200 + 20 = 1220 \text{ bytes (Segment)}$$

**Step 3 — IPv4 Encapsulation:**

$$L_{IP} = 1220 + 20 = 1240 \text{ bytes (Datagram)}$$

**Step 4 — Ethernet Encapsulation:**

$$L_{Frame} = 1240 + 14 + 4 = 1258 \text{ bytes (Frame)}$$

**Step 5 — Overhead Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{1200}{1258} \times 100\% \approx 95.39\%$$

**Encapsulation Process Explanation:**
Data flows **downward** from Application → Transport → Internet → Network Access. At each layer, a **header** (and sometimes a trailer) is prepended/appended containing control information for the peer layer at the destination. At the receiver, the reverse (**de-encapsulation**) occurs.

*(7 marks: 2 for stepwise calculation, 2 for final frame size, 2 for efficiency, 1 for encapsulation explanation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024]

**(a)** Compare the OSI Reference Model and the TCP/IP Reference Model. Highlight at least five points of difference. Explain why the TCP/IP model became the de facto standard for the Internet. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **"hourglass" architecture** of the TCP/IP model. Discuss the role of the **Internet Layer (IP)** as the "thin waist" and how this design enables scalability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

| Parameter | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Layers | 7 layers | 4 layers |
| Origin | ISO (1984) — theoretical | DARPA (1970s) — practical |
| Adoption | Reference only; rarely fully implemented | Universal; powers the Internet |
| Session & Presentation Layers | Separate distinct layers | Merged into Application Layer |
| Physical + Data Link | Separate | Combined as Network Access Layer |
| Development | Protocol-independent design first | Protocol-dependent design first |
| Reliability | Model is generic; protocols not tied | TCP provides built-in reliability |

**Why TCP/IP won:**
1. **Practical** — Built around already-working protocols (TCP, IP).
2. **Simpler** — Fewer layers = easier implementation.
3. **Open standards** — Freely available RFCs, no licensing.
4. **Proven scalability** — Successfully scaled to billions of devices.
5. **Backed by DoD + academia** — ARPANET adoption created an early network effect.

*(7 marks: 5 for comparison table, 2 for justification)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Hourglass Diagram (reproduced):**

```
[Many Application Protocols]
            ↓
       IP (Thin Waist)
            ↓
[Many Physical Media]
```

**Explanation:**
The TCP/IP architecture resembles an **hourglass**. The **wide top** represents the many diverse application protocols (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH, etc.). The **wide bottom** represents the many physical media (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Fiber, Satellite, 5G, etc.). The **narrow waist in the middle is IP**.

**Role of IP as the thin waist:**
- IP is the **single common interface** that all applications use to send/receive data.
- IP is the **single common interface** that all physical media use to deliver packets.
- This **decouples** application innovation from physical-medium innovation.

**How this enables scalability:**
- A new app (e.g., HTTP/3) can be deployed without changing any router or NIC.
- A new physical medium (e.g., 6G) can be deployed without rewriting any application.
- **Billions of networks** can interwork because every device speaks IP at the middle layer.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for thin-waist explanation, 2 for scalability discussion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Calling TCP/IP a 5-layer model** — It is strictly **4 layers** in KTU 2024 scheme. (Students mistakenly add a separate "Physical Layer" because they confuse it with OSI.)
> 2. **Confusing the OSI and TCP/IP layer names** — Do not say "Network Layer" for TCP/IP; the correct name is **"Internet Layer"**.
> 3. **Wrong PDU names** — TCP's PDU is a **Segment**, not a packet. IP's PDU is a **Datagram**, not a frame. Ethernet's PDU is a **Frame**.
> 4. **Forgetting the trailer** — When calculating Ethernet frame size, students often add only the **14-byte header** and forget the **4-byte FCS trailer**.
> 5. **Saying TCP is "in" the Application Layer** — TCP is strictly in the **Transport Layer**. HTTP is in the Application Layer.
> 6. **Port number errors** — Port numbers are **16-bit** (range 0–65535), not 32-bit. Well-known ports are **0–1023**.
> 7. **Confusing MAC and IP addresses** — MAC is **48-bit, flat, local** (Layer 1). IP is **32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6), hierarchical, global** (Layer 2).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ TCP/IP model = **4 layers**: **A**pplication, **T**ransport, **I**nternet, **N**etwork Access (mnemonic: **"ATIN"**).
- ✅ Developed by **DARPA / DoD** for **ARPANET**; switched over on **January 1, 1983**.
- ✅ Protocol-centric (descriptive), not prescriptive like OSI.
- ✅ **PDU Hierarchy:** Message (App) → Segment/Datagram (Transport) → Datagram (Internet) → Frame (Link) → Bits (Physical).
- ✅ **Addressing Hierarchy:** Port number (16-bit) → IP address (32/128-bit) → MAC address (48-bit).
- ✅ **Two key Transport protocols:** TCP (reliable, connection-oriented, 20-byte header) vs UDP (unreliable, connectionless, 8-byte header).
- ✅ **One key Internet protocol:** IP — the "thin waist" of the hourglass.
- ✅ **One key Link technology:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) — 14 B header + 4 B trailer.
- ✅ **Encapsulation** adds headers as data moves *down*; **de-encapsulation** strips them as data moves *up*.
- ✅ **Header Overhead Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + \sum H_i + \sum T_j} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **OSI vs TCP/IP mapping:** OSI 7+6+5 → TCP/IP App; OSI 4 → TCP/IP Transport; OSI 3 → TCP/IP Internet; OSI 2+1 → TCP/IP Network Access.
- ✅ **Why TCP/IP won:** Practical, simpler, open standards, proven scalability, internetwork addressing built-in.
- ✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always state layer name + PDU name + addressing + 2 protocols for full credit on long-answer layer questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of OSI and TCP/IP Reference Models. Physical Layer - Modes of Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison of OSI and TCP/IP Reference Models. Physical Layer - Modes of Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The OSI Reference Model — Formal Definition

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Model** is a conceptual, **7-layer framework** standardized by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** as ISO/IEC 7498-1. It defines a universal set of rules enabling heterogeneous systems to communicate over a network by abstracting the communication process into distinct, modular functional layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** The OSI model is a *reference model* — not an implementation architecture. TCP/IP is the *practical implementation* that the internet is built upon.

**The 7 Layers (Bottom-Up):**

| # | Layer | Mnemonic (Bottom-Up) |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | Application | **A**ll |
| 6 | Presentation | **P**eople |
| 5 | Session | **S**eem |
| 4 | Transport | **T**o |
| 3 | Network | **N**eed |
| 2 | Data Link | **D**ata |
| 1 | Physical | **P**rocessing |

**Data Units (PDU names):**
- L7-L5 → **Data**
- L4 → **Segment** (TCP) / **Datagram** (UDP)
- L3 → **Packet**
- L2 → **Frame**
- L1 → **Bits / Signals**

### 1.2 The TCP/IP Reference Model — Formal Definition

The **Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol (TCP/IP) Model**, also called the **Internet Protocol Suite**, is a **4-layer practical model** developed by **DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency)** in the 1970s, formalized in **RFC 1122** and **RFC 791**. It is the foundational architecture of the modern Internet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin Story:** TCP/IP was born from the ARPANET project (1969). Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn are credited as its principal architects.

**The 4 Layers (Bottom-Up):**

| # | Layer | Key Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Application | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet, SSH |
| 3 | Transport | TCP, UDP, DCCP, SCTP |
| 2 | Internet (Network) | IP (IPv4/IPv6), ICMP, IGMP, ARP, RARP |
| 1 | Network Access (Link/Physical) | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP, Frame Relay |

### 1.3 Physical Layer — Modes of Communication

The **Physical Layer** (OSI Layer 1 / TCP/IP Layer 1) is responsible for the **transmission and reception of raw bit streams over a physical medium** (copper, fiber, RF, wireless). It defines:
- **Mechanical** specifications (connectors, pin layouts)
- **Electrical** specifications (voltage levels, impedance)
- **Functional** specifications (channel access, bit synchronization)
- **Procedural** specifications (handshaking, line discipline)

A critical functional sub-category is the **Mode of Communication**, which defines the **directionality of data flow** between two communicating devices.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System

> [!TIP]
> **🛣️ Real-World Analogy — The Highway Analogy:**
> - **OSI Model** = The *blueprint* a civil engineer draws showing every detail of a highway (drainage, lighting, road surface, signage, traffic signals) — 7 distinct design layers.
> - **TCP/IP Model** = The *actual highway* that was built using a simpler 4-stage plan and is currently in use.
> - **Modes of Communication** = The *traffic rules* on the highway:
>   - **Simplex** = A one-way street (e.g., a bus lane that only goes north).
>   - **Half-Duplex** = A single-track mountain road with a *walkie-talkie protocol* — only one vehicle moves at a time.
>   - **Full-Duplex** = A six-lane divided highway — traffic flows freely in *both directions simultaneously*.

### 1.5 The Three Modes — Intuitive Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definitions (Exam-Ready):**
> 1. **Simplex Mode (SX):** Communication is **unidirectional**. One device is permanently the *sender* (transmitter only) and the other is permanently the *receiver*. The channel capacity of the return path is **zero**.
> 2. **Half-Duplex Mode (HDX):** Communication is **bidirectional but not simultaneous**. Both stations can transmit and receive, but **only one station may transmit at any given instant** (mutual exclusion).
> 3. **Full-Duplex Mode (FDX) / Duplex:** Communication is **bidirectional and simultaneous**. Both stations can transmit and receive at the same time, effectively doubling the channel's usable bandwidth.

### 1.6 Standard Metrics & Constants (KTU Frequently Tested)

- **Channel Capacity** (theoretical max bits/sec) — governed by **Nyquist** (noiseless) and **Shannon-Hartley** (noisy) theorems.
- **Bits per second (bps)** is the SI base unit of data rate.
- A **Simplex** link has **0 bps** return-path capacity.
- A **Full-Duplex** link theoretically provides **2× the simplex bandwidth** of the same medium.
- The **Baseband** signal uses the entire bandwidth of the medium for a single channel; **Broadband** divides the medium into multiple frequency channels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 OSI vs TCP/IP — Layer-by-Layer Deep Dive

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The OSI and TCP/IP models are *not* directly layer-to-layer equivalents. For example, the OSI *Session* and *Presentation* layers have **no direct counterpart** in TCP/IP — their functions are either merged into the Application layer or handled by individual application protocols.

#### 2.1.1 Architectural & Design Philosophy Differences

| Comparison Dimension | OSI Reference Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Open Systems Interconnection | Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol |
| **Number of Layers** | **7 layers** | **4 layers** (sometimes shown as 5) |
| **Origin / Year** | ISO, **1984** (formally adopted) | DARPA, **1970s** (ARPANET) |
| **Design Approach** | **Protocol-independent** generic reference model | **Protocol-centric** — designed around existing protocols (TCP, IP) |
| **Primary Use** | Theoretical teaching & design guide | **Practical implementation** of the Internet |
| **Adoption** | Rarely implemented; primarily a *teaching tool* | **Globally deployed** — the de facto Internet standard |
| **Strict Layer Boundaries** | **Yes** — clear separation of service, interface, protocol | **Loose** — layers are not strictly separated |
| **Session & Presentation Layers** | **Explicit, separate layers** | **Not present** — merged into Application layer |
| **Physical & Data Link** | **Two distinct layers** | **Combined** into the Network Access layer |
| **Transport Layer Guarantees** | **Connection-oriented only** (theoretical) | Supports both **TCP (reliable)** and **UDP (unreliable)** |
| **Dependency on Specific Protocols** | **Generic**, not tied to any specific protocol stack | **Tightly coupled** to TCP, IP, UDP, etc. |
| **Replacement / Evolution** | Static, never revised after adoption | **Continuously evolving** through new RFCs |
| **Internet Relevance Today** | Conceptual framework only | The actual Internet architecture |

#### 2.1.2 The Three Conceptual Pillars of a Layered Model (KTU Favorite Theory Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per ISO/IEC 7498-1**, *each OSI layer* must define three things:
> 1. **Services** — *what* the layer provides to the layer above.
> 2. **Interface** — *how* the layer above accesses the services (Service Access Points — SAPs).
> 3. **Protocol** — *how* the layer performs its functions internally (its own set of rules).
>
> This is a classic **2-mark KTU theory question**. TCP/IP is often criticized for *blurring* these three pillars.

### 2.2 Physical Layer — Modes of Communication: Deep Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Simplex Mode — Operational Logic

- A **single, dedicated transmission path** exists from Device A → Device B.
- The reverse path **does not exist** or is **non-functional** for data.
- **Channel allocation:** 100% of bandwidth allocated to forward direction.
- **Synchronization:** Unidirectional clock recovery required.
- **Collision domain:** Does **not exist** (only one transmitter).
- **Use cases:** Radio broadcast, TV transmission, keyboard-to-CPU, telemetry sensors, public address systems.

**Simplex Mathematical Model:**

$$
C_{\text{simplex}} = B \log_2(M)
$$

where $B$ is the bandwidth in Hz and $M$ is the signal level count. *No return path.*

#### 2.2.2 Half-Duplex Mode — Operational Logic

- **Two-way communication** is possible but **time-shared**.
- A control mechanism (e.g., **CSMA/CD**, **token passing**, or a **PTT — Push-To-Talk button**) decides which station may transmit.
- **Effective throughput** is reduced because of *turn-around time* — the delay to switch from receive to transmit mode.
- **Collision domain:** Exists — both devices can contend for the medium.
- **Use cases:** Walkie-talkies, CB radios, older Ethernet hubs, Bluetooth (in certain modes), police radio systems.

**Half-Duplex Throughput Equation:**

$$
\eta_{\text{HDX}} = \frac{T_{\text{tx}}}{T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{ta}}}
$$

where $T_{\text{tx}}$ is the actual transmission time and $T_{\text{ta}}$ is the turn-around time.

#### 2.2.3 Full-Duplex Mode — Operational Logic

- **Simultaneous bidirectional** communication.
- Implemented using one of three physical techniques:
  1. **Two separate physical channels** (e.g., 4-wire twisted pair — 2 wires for TX, 2 for RX).
  2. **Frequency-Division Duplex (FDD)** — two distinct frequency bands (e.g., cellular uplink/downlink).
  3. **Time-Division Duplex (TDD)** — rapid time-slot alternation so fast it *appears* simultaneous (e.g., 5G NR TDD, Wi-Fi).
- **Collision domain:** Does **not exist** in the traditional sense.
- **Use cases:** Modern switched Ethernet, telephone landlines, fiber-optic links, 4G/5G, full-duplex Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax).

**Full-Duplex Capacity Equation:**

$$
C_{\text{FDX}} = 2 \times B \log_2(M) = 2 \times C_{\text{simplex}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight (KTU Trick Question):** A "100 Mbps full-duplex Ethernet" link does **NOT** mean 100 Mbps in each direction totaling 200 Mbps on the wire — the **line rate remains 100 Mbps**, and 100 Mbps is achievable in *each* direction independently. The total *aggregate* throughput is 200 Mbps, but the *physical signaling rate* is unchanged.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nyquist Capacity | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | bps | **Noiseless** channel max data rate |
| Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | **Noisy** channel max data rate |
| SNR (linear) | $\text{SNR} = \frac{S}{N}$ | dimensionless (ratio) | Signal-to-noise ratio |
| SNR (dB) | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$ | decibels (dB) | Logarithmic form |
| Simplex Capacity | $C_{\text{SX}} = B \log_2(M)$ | bps | One-way only |
| Full-Duplex Capacity | $C_{\text{FDX}} = 2 \cdot B \log_2(M)$ | bps | Bidirectional simultaneous |
| Half-Duplex Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{HDX}} = \frac{T_{\text{tx}}}{T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{ta}}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Effective utilization |
| Propagation Delay | $t_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | seconds | $d$ = distance, $v$ = propagation speed |
| Transmission Delay | $t_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds | $L$ = packet length, $R$ = link rate |
| Maximum data rate of a noisy channel | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | Hard upper bound |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Prohibition Note:** Per KTU formatting rules, all vertical bars inside LaTeX expressions (such as $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value) have been deliberately written using `\vert` to prevent markdown table parsing errors.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network design** requires understanding OSI/TCP-IP layering to *troubleshoot* issues — e.g., ping (Layer 3), traceroute (Layer 3), Wireshark (Layer 2-4), DNS (Layer 7).
- **Telecom operators** specify duplex mode in cell-tower equipment (FDD-LTE vs TDD-LTE) for spectrum allocation.
- **Industrial automation** (Modbus, PROFINET, CAN bus) frequently uses half-duplex RS-485 over long distances.
- **Broadcast engineering** (DVB-T, ATSC, FM radio) is *inherently* simplex — the design simplifies the receiver and is asymmetric in cost.
- **Modern data centers** use full-duplex fiber (100GbE, 400GbE) with wavelength-division multiplexing to scale bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Full-Duplex Doubles Channel Capacity

**Problem Statement:** A noiseless channel has bandwidth $B = 3 \text{ kHz}$ and uses 4 signal levels ($M = 4$). Compute (a) Simplex capacity, (b) Full-Duplex capacity, and (c) verify the doubling theorem.

**Step 1 — Recall Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless):**

$$
C = 2 B \log_2(M)
$$

This gives the **maximum data rate** in bits per second for a **single direction** transmission.

**Step 2 — Substitute values for Simplex case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SX}} &= 2 \times 3000 \times \log_2(4) \\
&= 2 \times 3000 \times 2 \\
&= 12000 \text{ bps} \\
&= 12 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Full-Duplex case:**

For full-duplex, *two* independent channels (one each direction) operate concurrently. Each direction has the same Nyquist capacity:

$$
C_{\text{FDX}} = 2 \times C_{\text{SX}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{FDX}} &= 2 \times 12000 \\
&= 24000 \text{ bps} \\
&= 24 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (Doubling Theorem Proof):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{FDX}} &= 2 \times 2B \log_2(M) \\
&= 4B \log_2(M) \\
\text{But we can also derive it as:} \\
C_{\text{FDX}} &= 2 \times \left[2B \log_2(M)\right] \\
&= 2 \times C_{\text{SX}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $C_{\text{SX}} = 12 \text{ kbps}$ and $C_{\text{FDX}} = 24 \text{ kbps}$. ✓ Theorem verified.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Half-Duplex Efficiency with Turn-Around Time

**Problem Statement:** A half-duplex link transmits a 1000-bit frame at 1 Mbps. The turn-around time is 50 μs. Compute the efficiency.

**Step 1 — Compute Transmission Time:**

$$
t_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Efficiency Formula:**

$$
\eta_{\text{HDX}} = \frac{T_{\text{tx}}}{T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{ta}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert units consistently (use microseconds for clarity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{tx}} &= 1000 \;\mu\text{s} \\
T_{\text{ta}} &= 50 \;\mu\text{s} \\
\eta_{\text{HDX}} &= \frac{1000}{1000 + 50} \\
&= \frac{1000}{1050} \\
&\approx 0.9524 \\
&\approx 95.24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

A 4.76% overhead is acceptable. If $T_{\text{ta}}$ were 500 μs (e.g., satellite link), the efficiency would drop to:

$$
\eta = \frac{1000}{1500} = 66.67\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This is why **satellite links historically use simplex/broadcast** for one-way traffic (e.g., TV) — turn-around delays over 250 ms (GEO satellites) make half-duplex *interactive* use impractical.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> The following decision logic helps select the appropriate mode in network design problems (often tested as 7-mark KTU sub-questions).

| Criterion | Choose Simplex if… | Choose Half-Duplex if… | Choose Full-Duplex if… |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data flow** | One-way (e.g., broadcast, sensor → server) | Two-way but bursty (e.g., walkie-talkie, old Ethernet hub) | Continuous two-way (e.g., phone call, modern Ethernet) |
| **Cost constraint** | Receiver must be very cheap (e.g., TV set) | Cost is moderate; one shared channel | Higher cost acceptable for performance |
| **Bandwidth efficiency** | Asymmetric traffic — most data is forward | Channel contention acceptable | Symmetric high-throughput needed |
| **Latency tolerance** | Latency in return path irrelevant | Moderate (turn-around time < frame time) | Minimal latency required |
| **Cable count** | 1 pair (2 wires) | 1 pair (2 wires) with shared use | 2 pairs (4 wires) or FDD/TDD |
| **KTU examples** | FM radio, Keyboard, Pager | Walkie-Talkie, CB radio, Old Ethernet hub | Telephone, Switched Ethernet, Fiber |
| **Collision domain** | Absent | Present | Absent (separate channels) |

---

### 3.4 Comprehensive OSI vs TCP/IP — Analytical Matrix

| OSI Layer | TCP/IP Equivalent | Function | Example Protocols (OSI) | Example Protocols (TCP/IP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7. Application | Application | User interface, network services | FTAM, X.400, CMIP (OSI suite) | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP |
| 6. Presentation | (merged) | Data format, encryption, compression | ASN.1, MIME-like functions | TLS/SSL, MIME, JPEG (often at app level) |
| 5. Session | (merged) | Dialog control, synchronization | RPC, NetBIOS, Session protocols | (handled by app) — e.g., SIP, NetBIOS over TCP |
| 4. Transport | Transport | End-to-end reliability, segmentation | TP0, TP4 (OSI transport) | **TCP** (reliable), **UDP** (best-effort) |
| 3. Network | Internet | Logical addressing, routing | CLNP, ES-IS, IS-IS (OSI routing) | **IP, ICMP, IGMP, ARP, OSPF, BGP** |
| 2. Data Link | Network Access (upper half) | Framing, MAC, error detection | HDLC, LLC, IEEE 802.2 | Ethernet (802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP |
| 1. Physical | Network Access (lower half) | Bit transmission, topology | X.21, V.35, RS-232 | Ethernet physical, SONET/SDH, ISDN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** The TCP/IP "Network Access" layer is a **fusion of OSI's Physical + Data Link layers**. This is one of the most common 2-mark KTU questions — students often incorrectly map TCP/IP Link Layer to OSI Data Link only.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The 7-Layer OSI Reference Model — Architectural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["Layer 7: Application - User Interface"]
    L6["Layer 6: Presentation - Data Format & Encryption"]
    L5["Layer 5: Session - Dialog Control"]
    L4["Layer 4: Transport - End-to-End Reliability"]
    L3["Layer 3: Network - Routing and Logical Addressing"]
    L2["Layer 2: Data Link - Framing and MAC"]
    L1["Layer 1: Physical - Bit Transmission over Medium"]

    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1

    P1["PDU: Data - L7 to L5"] -.-> L7
    P2["PDU: Segment - L4"] -.-> L4
    P3["PDU: Packet - L3"] -.-> L3
    P4["PDU: Frame - L2"] -.-> L2
    P5["PDU: Bits - L1"] -.-> L1
```

### 4.2 The 4-Layer TCP/IP Reference Model — Architectural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Layer 4: Application - HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP"]
    T["Layer 3: Transport - TCP and UDP"]
    I["Layer 2: Internet - IP, ICMP, ARP"]
    N["Layer 1: Network Access - Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP"]

    A --> T
    T --> I
    I --> N

    PA["PDU: Message / Data"] -.-> A
    PT["PDU: Segment or Datagram"] -.-> T
    PI["PDU: Datagram (IP Packet)"] -.-> I
    PN["PDU: Frame / Bits"] -.-> N
```

### 4.3 Side-by-Side: OSI vs TCP/IP — Layer Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O7["OSI L7: Application"] <-->|merged into| TA["TCP/IP L4: Application"]
    O6["OSI L6: Presentation"] <-->|merged into| TA
    O5["OSI L5: Session"] <-->|merged into| TA
    O4["OSI L4: Transport"] <-->|equivalent| TT["TCP/IP L3: Transport"]
    O3["OSI L3: Network"] <-->|equivalent| TI["TCP/IP L2: Internet"]
    O2["OSI L2: Data Link"] <-->|merged into| TN["TCP/IP L1: Network Access"]
    O1["OSI L1: Physical"] <-->|merged into| TN
```

### 4.4 Physical Layer — Modes of Communication Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SX["SIMPLEX MODE - One Way Only"]
        A1["Station A\nTransmitter Only"] -->|unidirectional| B1["Station B\nReceiver Only"]
    end

    subgraph HDX["HALF-DUPLEX MODE - Two Way Alternating"]
        A2["Station A\nTransceiver"] <-->|one at a time| B2["Station B\nTransceiver"]
    end

    subgraph FDX["FULL-DUPLEX MODE - Two Way Simultaneous"]
        A3["Station A\nTransceiver"] <-.->|simultaneous| B3["Station B\nTransceiver"]
    end
```

### 4.5 Communication Mode Selection — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["Network Design Requirement"] --> Q1{"Is two-way\ncommunication\nrequired?"}

    Q1 -->|No| SX["SIMPLEX\nSingle dedicated channel\nExample: TV broadcast, Keyboard"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Can both stations\ntransmit at the same\ninstant?"}

    Q2 -->|No| HDX["HALF-DUPLEX\nShared channel with arbitration\nExample: Walkie-Talkie, Old Hub"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{"Are separate\nphysical channels\navailable?"}

    Q3 -->|Yes| FDX1["FULL-DUPLEX\nTwo independent channels\nExample: 4-wire Ethernet, Phone"]
    Q3 -->|No| FDX2["FULL-DUPLEX via FDD or TDD\nLogical channel separation\nExample: 4G LTE FDD, 5G NR TDD"]
```

### 4.6 Data Encapsulation Flow — End-to-End Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER["SENDER SIDE - Top Down"]
        APP["Application Data"] --> SEG["+ TCP Header = Segment"]
        SEG --> PAK["+ IP Header = Packet"]
        PAK --> FRM["+ Ethernet Header/Trailer = Frame"]
        FRM --> BIT["Bits on Physical Medium"]
    end

    BIT -.transmitted.-> RCV

    subgraph RECEIVER["RECEIVER SIDE - Bottom Up"]
        direction LR
        BIT2["Receive Bits"] --> FRM2["De-frame L2"]
        FRM2 --> PAK2["De-packetize L3"]
        PAK2 --> SEG2["Reassemble Segment L4"]
        SEG2 --> APP2["Deliver to Application L7"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:** All Mermaid diagrams use *purely alphanumeric* node IDs (e.g., `A1`, `B1`, `SX`, `FDX`) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards. Labels are *plain uppercase text* without bold/italic markdown tags.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the OSI model and the TCP/IP model. Mention any six points.**

**Model Answer (Structured for 3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Has **7 layers** | Has **4 layers** |
| 2 | Developed by **ISO** in 1984 | Developed by **DARPA** in the 1970s |
| 3 | **Protocol-independent**; generic reference | **Protocol-specific**; built around TCP, IP |
| 4 | **Session and Presentation** are separate layers | **No Session/Presentation**; merged into Application |
| 5 | **Physical and Data Link** are separate | Combined into the **Network Access** layer |
| 6 | **Connection-oriented** transport layer design | Supports both **TCP** (connection-oriented) and **UDP** (connectionless) |
| 7 | Primarily a **teaching/reference tool** | The **actual Internet architecture** |
| 8 | Statically defined; rarely updated | **Continuously evolving** through new RFCs |

*(Any six points – 3 marks: ~0.5 mark per valid point.)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the three modes of communication in the Physical Layer with one example each.**

**Model Answer (Structured for 3 marks):**

The Physical Layer supports three modes of communication:

1. **Simplex Mode (Unidirectional):** Data flows in **one direction only**. The sender can only transmit, and the receiver can only receive.
   - *Example:* **FM radio broadcast** — the radio station transmits, and listeners only receive.

2. **Half-Duplex Mode (Bidirectional, Non-Simultaneous):** Both stations can transmit and receive, but **only one can transmit at a time**. The channel must be shared using a control mechanism like CSMA/CD or token passing.
   - *Example:* **Walkie-talkie** — the "over" protocol ensures one person speaks at a time.

3. **Full-Duplex Mode (Bidirectional, Simultaneous):** Both stations can transmit and receive **simultaneously**, effectively doubling the channel's usable capacity.
   - *Example:* **Telephone conversation** — both parties can speak and listen at the same time.

*(1 mark for naming + brief explanation, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for completeness – total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the OSI and TCP/IP reference models in detail. Explain the function of each layer with suitable examples of protocols.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the three modes of communication at the Physical Layer. With a neat diagram, describe how data flows in simplex, half-duplex, and full-duplex modes. Mention two real-world examples for each mode.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
- **OSI Model:** 7-layer conceptual framework by ISO (1984). Generic and protocol-independent.
- **TCP/IP Model:** 4-layer practical model by DARPA (1970s). Backbone of the Internet.

**2. Layer-by-Layer Function Mapping (5 marks):**

| OSI Layer | Function | TCP/IP Equivalent | Example Protocols |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7. Application | User interface, network services | Application | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS |
| 6. Presentation | Data formatting, encryption (e.g., TLS), compression | Merged into Application | TLS, MIME, JPEG, ASCII |
| 5. Session | Dialog management, synchronization | Merged into Application | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP |
| 4. Transport | End-to-end reliability, segmentation, flow control | Transport | TCP (reliable), UDP (unreliable) |
| 3. Network | Logical addressing (IP), routing | Internet | IP, ICMP, IGMP, ARP, OSPF |
| 2. Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Network Access (part) | Ethernet (802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP |
| 1. Physical | Bit transmission, electrical/mechanical specs | Network Access (part) | RS-232, SONET, IEEE 802.3 physical |

**3. Critical Distinctions (1 mark):**
- OSI has explicit **Session and Presentation** layers; TCP/IP does not.
- TCP/IP is the **deployed** model; OSI is a **reference/teaching** model.
- OSI strictly separates *service*, *interface*, and *protocol*; TCP/IP blurs these.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Simplex Mode (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Unidirectional communication. One device is the **transmitter**, the other is the **receiver**.
- **Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Station A - Transmitter Only"] -->|unidirectional arrow| B1["Station B - Receiver Only"]
```

- **Examples (2):**
  1. **Keyboard → Computer** (data flows only from keyboard to CPU)
  2. **FM radio broadcast** (radio station transmits, listeners only receive)

**2. Half-Duplex Mode (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Bidirectional communication, but **only one station transmits at a time**. A turn-around time is incurred when switching roles.
- **Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2["Station A - Transceiver"] <-->|alternating| B2["Station B - Transceiver"]
```

- **Examples (2):**
  1. **Walkie-talkie / CB radio** (Push-To-Talk button)
  2. **Old Ethernet using a hub** (CSMA/CD arbitrates access)

**3. Full-Duplex Mode (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Simultaneous bidirectional communication. Doubles the effective capacity.
- **Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3["Station A - Transceiver"] <-.->|simultaneous| B3["Station B - Transceiver"]
```

- **Examples (2):**
  1. **Modern telephone conversation** (both speak and listen concurrently)
  2. **Full-duplex switched Ethernet** (separate TX and RX wire pairs)

**4. Comparative Summary Table (0.5 mark):**

| Property | Simplex | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | One-way | Two-way, time-shared | Two-way, simultaneous |
| Capacity | $C$ | $C/2$ effective (with overhead) | $2C$ |
| Complexity | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** List the seven layers of the OSI model. Explain the function and PDU of each layer. Describe how data encapsulation and de-encapsulation occur as a message travels from the sender to the receiver.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. Calculate:
- (i) The maximum data rate using Nyquist's theorem if 8 signal levels are used.
- (ii) The maximum data rate using Shannon's theorem if the SNR is 30 dB.
- (iii) The full-duplex capacity of the channel.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Seven Layers of OSI (2.5 marks):**

| # | Layer | Function | PDU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | Application | Interface to user; provides network services (file transfer, email) | Data |
| 6 | Presentation | Data translation, encryption, compression (e.g., TLS, JPEG) | Data |
| 5 | Session | Establishes, manages, terminates sessions between hosts | Data |
| 4 | Transport | Reliable end-to-end delivery, segmentation, flow control | Segment / Datagram |
| 3 | Network | Logical addressing (IP), routing of packets | Packet |
| 2 | Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection (CRC) | Frame |
| 1 | Physical | Bit transmission, electrical/mechanical/optical signaling | Bits |

**2. Encapsulation (Sender — Top Down) (2 marks):**
At each layer, a **header (and sometimes trailer)** is added to the data unit from the layer above:

- App Data → [App Header + Data] = Message
- + [TCP Header] = **Segment**
- + [IP Header] = **Packet**
- + [Ethernet Header + Trailer] = **Frame**
- Converted to **bits** and sent on the medium.

**3. De-encapsulation (Receiver — Bottom Up) (2 marks):**
At each layer, the header/trailer is **stripped and interpreted**:
- Bits → Frame → remove L2 header/trailer → Packet → remove L3 header → Segment → remove L4 header → App Data → Application.

**4. Diagram (0.5 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SD["Sender Application Data"] --> SE["+TCP Header Segment"]
    SE --> PA["+IP Header Packet"]
    PA --> FR["+Ethernet Header and Trailer Frame"]
    FR --> BI["Bits on Physical Medium"]
    BI --> RD["Receiver Application Data"]
```

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $B = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000 \text{ Hz}$, $M = 8$, $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 30 \text{ dB}$

**(i) Nyquist Capacity (3 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 B \log_2(M)
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Nyquist}} &= 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(8) \\
&= 2 \times 4000 \times 3 \\
&= 24000 \text{ bps} \\
&= 24 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Shannon Capacity (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR})
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
30 &= 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR}) \\
3 &= \log_{10}(\text{SNR}) \\
\text{SNR} &= 10^3 = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Shannon}} &= B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
&= 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\
&= 4000 \times \log_2(1001) \\
&\approx 4000 \times 9.967 \\
&\approx 39868 \text{ bps} \\
&\approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Full-Duplex Capacity (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{FDX}} &= 2 \times \text{max}(C_{\text{Nyquist}}, C_{\text{Shannon}}) \\
&= 2 \times \max(24000, 39868) \\
&= 2 \times 39868 \\
&= 79736 \text{ bps} \\
&\approx 79.74 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:**
> - (i) $C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 24 \text{ kbps}$
> - (ii) $C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}$ (this is the *practical* limit; Nyquist's 24 kbps is unattainable in this noisy channel)
> - (iii) $C_{\text{FDX}} \approx 79.74 \text{ kbps}$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Layer mapping mistakes** — Writing that TCP/IP has 5 layers, or omitting that Session/Presentation are *absent* in TCP/IP (not just "different"). **−1 mark** per KTU scheme.
> 2. **Confusing OSI with TCP/IP protocol examples** — Saying "OSI uses HTTP" is *wrong*. HTTP is a TCP/IP protocol. OSI's Application layer protocols include **FTAM, X.400, CMIP**. **−0.5 to −1 mark** typical deduction.
> 3. **Full-duplex misconception** — Writing that full-duplex *requires* four wires is *over-generalization*. It can be achieved via **FDD, TDD, or echo cancellation** on 2 wires. **−1 mark** if stated absolutely.
> 4. **Confusing half-duplex with full-duplex examples** — Old Ethernet *hubs* are half-duplex; modern Ethernet *switches* are full-duplex. **−1 mark** for this swap.
> 5. **Skipping PDU names in answers** — Every layer answer should mention the **Protocol Data Unit name** (Segment, Packet, Frame, Bits). **−0.5 mark** per omission.
> 6. **Drawing protocol stacks upside down** — Always show the Application layer at the **TOP** and the Physical layer at the **BOTTOM**. **−1 mark** for inverted diagrams.
> 7. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — A capacity of "24000" without "bps" is incomplete. **−0.5 mark** typical.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **📋 Final 60-Second Revision Bullets — Print and Memorize:**
> - [x] **OSI = 7 layers, ISO, 1984, generic reference model**; **TCP/IP = 4 layers, DARPA, 1970s, Internet model**.
> - [x] **OSI L7–L5 → Data**; **L4 → Segment/Datagram**; **L3 → Packet**; **L2 → Frame**; **L1 → Bits**.
> - [x] **TCP/IP has NO Session or Presentation layer**; they are merged into the Application layer.
> - [x] **TCP/IP Network Access = OSI Physical + Data Link** (combined).
> - [x] **OSI defines Service, Interface, Protocol distinctly**; **TCP/IP blurs** this distinction.
> - [x] **OSI = theoretical/teaching**; **TCP/IP = practical/Internet**.
> - [x] **Simplex = one-way only** (FM radio, keyboard, TV broadcast, pager).
> - [x] **Half-Duplex = two-way but one at a time** (walkie-talkie, old Ethernet hub, CB radio).
> - [x] **Full-Duplex = two-way simultaneous** (phone call, switched Ethernet, 4G/5G).
> - [x] **Full-duplex can be implemented via** separate channels, **FDD**, or **TDD** — not strictly four wires.
> - [x] **Nyquist (noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$
> - [x] **Shannon (noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$
> - [x] **SNR in dB:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR ratio})$
> - [x] **Half-duplex efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{tx}}}{T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{ta}}}$
> - [x] **TCP = connection-oriented, reliable**; **UDP = connectionless, best-effort**.
> - [x] **IP is at the Internet (Network) layer of TCP/IP**; provides logical addressing and routing.
> - [x] **ARPA / DARPA / Vint Cerf / Bob Kahn** → founders of TCP/IP. **ISO** → founders of OSI.
> - [x] **Encapsulation = adding headers top-down**; **De-encapsulation = removing headers bottom-up**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
