<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Topology (KTU 2024 Definition):** The physical topology of a network refers to the actual geometric arrangement of nodes (computers, switches, routers) and the physical media (cables, wireless links) that interconnect them. It describes **how the network is physically laid out**, as opposed to *logical topology*, which describes how data flows regardless of physical design.

In the KTU 2024 scheme for **PEMRT751 – Computer Networks**, Module 1 lays the groundwork for understanding network classification, and physical topology is the foundational concept upon which medium access control, performance, and reliability metrics are built. The six standard physical topologies recognized by KTU are:

| S.No | Topology | Type |
|------|----------|------|
| 1 | Bus (Linear Bus) | Wired |
| 2 | Star | Wired |
| 3 | Ring | Wired |
| 4 | Mesh (Full / Partial) | Wired |
| 5 | Tree (Hierarchical) | Wired |
| 6 | Hybrid | Wired / Mixed |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Roads in a City"**
> Imagine a city map. **Bus topology** is a single main road with houses on either side. **Star topology** is a roundabout (central hub) with branching roads going outwards. **Ring topology** is a circular one-way lane. **Mesh topology** is a city grid where you can reach any house via multiple alternate roads. **Tree topology** is like a river system — main river → tributaries → streams. **Hybrid topology** is simply a mix of two or more of these in different regions of the city.

When a KTU student studies "physical topology," they should think of the **cables and boxes they could touch**, not the data signals travelling inside.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Vocabulary for Module 1:**
> - **Node / Station:** Any device connected to the network (PC, switch, router).
> - **Link / Channel:** The physical medium connecting two nodes.
> - **Tap:** A connector where a node attaches to a shared medium.
> - **Terminator:** A resistor placed at the ends of a bus to absorb signals and prevent reflection.
> - **Hub (Physical Star Center):** A Layer-1 repeater device used in star topology.
> - **MAU (Multistation Access Unit):** The physical box used in Token Ring networks.
> - **Backbone Cable:** The central high-capacity cable in bus/tree topology.

**Performance Metrics Highlighted in KTU Syllabus:**
- **Cost** of cabling and devices
- **Scalability** — ease of adding new nodes
- **Fault Tolerance** — ability to survive a single link/node failure
- **Cable Length** and **Maximum Nodes** supported
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Top-by-Top Analysis

### 2.1.1 BUS Topology (Linear Bus)

**Operational Logic:**
1. All nodes share a single common backbone cable (the "bus").
2. Data is broadcast in **both directions** along the cable.
3. A terminator (typically **50 Ω** resistor) is required at both physical ends to prevent signal reflection.
4. A node transmits only when the medium is idle (CSMA/CD principle, covered later in the module).

**Why and How It Works:**
- It is the **simplest and cheapest** topology to install for small networks.
- The medium is a **passive** medium — the bus itself does not amplify signals.
- Signal **degrades with distance and number of taps**, limiting practical length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In bus topology, a single break in the backbone cable **brings down the entire network segment**. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### 2.1.2 STAR Topology

**Operational Logic:**
1. Every node connects via a **dedicated point-to-point link** to a central device (Hub or Switch).
2. The central device is a **repeater** (Hub — Layer 1) or **MAC bridge** (Switch — Layer 2).
3. Communication between any two nodes must pass through the central device.
4. If the central device fails, the entire network halts.

**Why and How It Works:**
- **Easy to install and modify** — adding/removing a node does not disturb the rest.
- Centralized management and monitoring become simple.
- **Higher cost** due to extra cabling and the central device.

### 2.1.3 RING Topology

**Operational Logic:**
1. Each node connects to **exactly two neighbours**, forming a closed loop.
2. Data travels in **one direction** (unidirectional) in a single ring, or in **two directions** in a dual ring (used in SONET/FDDI).
3. Each node acts as a **repeater**, regenerating the signal before forwarding it.
4. A *Token* (a special 3-byte control frame) circulates; a node can transmit only when it holds the token (Token Ring, IEEE 802.5).

**Why and How It Works:**
- Performs predictably under **heavy load** (no collisions as in CSMA/CD).
- A single link failure in a single ring **halts the entire network** unless it is a *dual ring* with wrap-around recovery.

### 2.1.4 MESH Topology

**Operational Logic:**
1. Every node is connected to **every other node** (Full Mesh) or to a **subset** of nodes (Partial Mesh).
2. Each link is a dedicated point-to-point connection.
3. Routing decisions are made using algorithms such as **shortest path** or **link-state protocols** (OSPF — covered in higher semesters).
4. The topology is inherently **redundant**.

**Why and How It Works:**
- **Maximum fault tolerance** — multiple paths between any two nodes.
- **Expensive** in cabling and switch ports; reserved for **backbone networks** (e.g., ISP cores, military, data centers).

### 2.1.5 TREE (Hierarchical) Topology

**Operational Logic:**
1. It is a **combination of star and bus** topologies, arranged in a hierarchy.
2. A central root node branches out into secondary nodes (using star groups), which in turn branch into tertiary nodes.
3. Data flows from leaves towards the root or vice-versa, following the hierarchy.

**Why and How It Works:**
- Excellent for **structured cabling in large buildings/campuses**.
- Easily **scalable** by adding new branches.
- Failure of a root segment isolates entire sub-branches.

### 2.1.6 HYBRID Topology

**Operational Logic:**
1. It is a **deliberate integration of two or more different topologies**, chosen to leverage the strengths of each.
2. Example: A star-bus hybrid — multiple star clusters connected to a central bus backbone (common in modern Ethernet offices).
3. Example: A star-ring hybrid — multiple Token Ring MAUs interconnected by a central hub device.

**Why and How It Works:**
- Used in **enterprise and campus networks** where uniform topology is impractical.
- Maximum **design flexibility**, but increased **design and management complexity**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following mathematical relations are essential for solving topology problems in KTU ESE. The number of physical links required is a high-frequency question in Part A and Part B.

**Table 1 — KTU Cheat Sheet: Physical Topology Metrics**

| Topology | Number of Physical Links (for $n$ nodes) | Number of Ports per Node (Avg.) | Redundancy | Failure Impact |
|----------|------------------------------------------|---------------------------------|------------|----------------|
| Bus | $1$ backbone + $n$ drop cables = $n+1$ (treating backbone as 1 logical link) | $1$ tap per node | None | Cable break ⇒ total failure |
| Star | $n$ | $1$ (plus 1 hub) | None (but central hub can be redundant) | Hub failure ⇒ total failure |
| Ring | $n$ | $2$ | Single ring — none; Dual ring — high | Single break ⇒ total failure (single ring) |
| Full Mesh | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n-1$ | Maximum | Tolerates up to $(n-2)$ link failures |
| Partial Mesh | $1 \le L \le \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Variable | Tunable | Tunable |
| Tree | $n-1$ (for a strict tree) | Variable | Low to medium | Branch root failure isolates subtree |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Do not confuse the **number of cables** with the **number of ports**. In a star, $n$ cables are needed, but the hub has $n$ ports. In a full mesh, the *total* number of point-to-point links is $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$, and this is the answer students must write.

**Table 2 — Comparative Trade-off Matrix**

| Parameter | Bus | Star | Ring | Mesh | Tree | Hybrid |
|-----------|-----|------|------|------|------|--------|
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium | Highest | Medium-High | High |
| Installation | Easy | Easy | Complex | Very Complex | Moderate | Complex |
| Scalability | Limited | Good | Limited | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| Fault Tolerance | Poor | Poor (hub) | Poor (single ring) | Excellent | Moderate | Good |
| Performance under Load | Degrades | Good (with switch) | Predictable | Excellent | Good | Depends |
| Example Use | Legacy Ethernet | Modern LAN | Token Ring LAN | ISP Backbone | Campus LAN | Enterprise WAN |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these topologies appear in the industry:**
> - **Bus:** Legacy 10BASE2 / 10BASE5 Ethernet (now obsolete, but KTU syllabus still tests).
> - **Star:** Every modern office LAN (Cat 6 cabling to a gigabit switch).
> - **Ring:** SONET/SDH telecom rings, FDDI backbones, industrial fieldbuses (Token Ring is now retired in most offices).
> - **Mesh:** Wireless Mesh Networks (WMN), ISP backbones, military tactical networks.
> - **Tree:** Structured cabling in multi-floor buildings (TIA-568 standard), cable TV distribution.
> - **Hybrid:** Almost every real enterprise network (e.g., a campus with star-clustered floors connected by a fiber ring backbone).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through the **canonical KTU 14-mark derivation problems**: counting links, ports, and total cable length for each topology, along with worked examples.

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Links in Full Mesh

**Problem Formulation:**
Given $n$ nodes, derive the total number of dedicated point-to-point links required in a *full mesh* topology where every node is connected to every other node.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Let us denote the $n$ nodes as $N_1, N_2, N_3, \dots, N_n$.

**Step 2:** For each node $N_i$, the number of *direct* connections required is $(n-1)$, because a node does not connect to itself.

**Step 3:** If we naively sum the connections for all $n$ nodes, we obtain:

$$
L_{\text{naive}} = n \times (n-1)
$$

**Step 4:** However, each physical link is shared between **exactly two** nodes. For example, the link between $N_1$ and $N_2$ is counted once in $N_1$'s connections and once in $N_2$'s connections. Therefore, we are double-counting every link.

**Step 5:** To correct for this double count, we divide by 2:

$$
L_{\text{full mesh}} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 6:** Final closed-form expression:

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{full mesh}} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $n = 6$ nodes,

$$
L_{\text{full mesh}} = \frac{6 \times 5}{2} = \frac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ links}
$$

**Verification by Hand (for $n=4$):**
Pairs to connect: $\{1,2\}, \{1,3\}, \{1,4\}, \{2,3\}, \{2,4\}, \{3,4\}$ → 6 links.
Using formula: $\frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$. ✔ Matches.

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Ports on the Central Hub in Star

**Problem Formulation:** For $n$ nodes in a star, what is the minimum number of ports required on the central hub?

**Step 1:** Each of the $n$ nodes requires **one dedicated link** to the hub.
**Step 2:** Each link consumes **one port** on the hub.
**Step 3:** Total ports required:

$$
P_{\text{hub}} = n
$$

**Step 4:** If we want $k$-port redundancy (i.e., 1 spare port per cluster), the practical specification is:

$$
P_{\text{hub, practical}} = n + 1
$$

**Step 5:** Final formula:

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{hub}} = n \quad \text{(or } n + k \text{ with } k \text{ spares)}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Cable Length in Bus Topology

**Problem Formulation:** $n$ nodes are equally spaced on a linear bus of total length $L_{\text{total}}$. Each drop cable has length $d$. Compute the total cable length.

**Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** The backbone spans the entire bus:

$$
L_{\text{backbone}} = L_{\text{total}}
$$

**Step 2:** Each of the $n$ nodes contributes a drop cable of length $d$:

$$
L_{\text{drops}} = n \times d
$$

**Step 3:** Total cable length:

$$
L_{\text{bus}} = L_{\text{backbone}} + L_{\text{drops}} = L_{\text{total}} + n \cdot d
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $d$ in terms of $L_{\text{total}}$ if nodes are equally spaced at distance $\Delta x$ from the cable. Then $d$ is typically small (e.g., $d = 0.5\,\text{m}$ to $3\,\text{m}$).

**Numerical Example:** $n=10$ nodes, $L_{\text{total}}=500$ m, $d=2$ m:

$$
L_{\text{bus}} = 500 + 10 \times 2 = 520 \text{ m}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Topologies

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a synthesis of the derivations above and is the most-asked KTU Module 1 comparison question. Memorize it for the 14-mark answer.

| Comparison Aspect | Bus | Star | Ring | Full Mesh |
|------------------|-----|------|------|-----------|
| Topology structure | Single shared backbone | Node-hub-node | Closed loop | Every node connected to all |
| Transmission mode | Half-duplex (with collision possible) | Full-duplex (with switch) | Unidirectional token | Full-duplex on each link |
| Cable cost | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | Highest |
| Reliability | Low (single cable) | Moderate (hub SPOF) | Low (single ring) | Highest |
| Best fit | Small, low-budget LAN | Office LANs | Industrial LANs | Backbone / critical networks |
| Add/remove node | Disruptive | Non-disruptive | Disruptive | Non-disruptive |

## 3.5 Bonus: Failure Analysis of Single Ring vs Dual Ring

**Derivation of Failure Tolerance:**

For a single ring with $n$ nodes and $n$ links, a single break in **any one link** causes a total network failure. The number of tolerated link failures is:

$$
F_{\text{single ring}} = 0
$$

For a dual ring (counter-rotating) with $2n$ links, a single break can be recovered by **wrapping** (folding the two rings into a single logical ring). The number of tolerated link failures is:

$$
F_{\text{dual ring}} = 1
$$

For a full mesh with $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ links, the number of tolerated link failures (for full connectivity) is:

$$
F_{\text{full mesh}} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} - (n-1) = \frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}
$$

**Closed-form:**

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{full mesh}} = \frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** $n=5$ nodes in full mesh.

$$
F_{\text{full mesh}} = \frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6 \text{ link failures tolerated}
$$

```mermaid

    busCheck --> ringCheck{DUAL RING?}
    ringCheck --> singleRing[RING SINGLE: TOTAL OUTAGE]
    ringCheck --> dualRing[RING DUAL: WRAP RECOVERY, NETWORK SURVIVES]
    busCheck --> meshCheck{ALTERNATE PATH EXISTS?}
    meshCheck --> meshFull[MESH FULL: TRAFFIC REROUTED, NO OUTAGE]
    meshCheck --> meshPartial[MESH PARTIAL: MAY OR MAY NOT SURVIVE]
    busCheck --> treeCheck{SUBTREE AFFECTED?}
    treeCheck --> treeOut[TREE: SUBTREE ISOLATED, OTHERS SURVIVE]
    busCheck --> hybridOut[HYBRID: SURVIVAL DEPENDS ON FAILED SEGMENT TOPOLOGY]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define physical topology. List any four physical topologies used in computer networks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Physical topology refers to the physical arrangement of nodes and the connecting media in a network. It describes how devices and cables are spatially organized, independent of how data flows.
- **Four physical topologies (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
  1. Bus topology
  2. Star topology
  3. Ring topology
  4. Mesh topology (or Tree / Hybrid)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**What is the role of a terminator in a bus topology? What value of resistance is typically used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role (2 marks):** A terminator is a resistor connected at **both physical ends** of the bus backbone. Its role is to **absorb the electromagnetic signal** when it reaches the end of the cable, thereby preventing **signal reflection** that would otherwise cause data corruption and standing waves on the medium.
- **Resistance value (1 mark):** Typically a **50 Ω resistor** is used (matching the characteristic impedance of standard coaxial cables like 10BASE2 / 10BASE5).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `Option A: Quantitative Mesh Analysis`

> `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice Question]`

**a)** Derive the number of point-to-point links required for a **full mesh** topology of $n$ nodes. (7 marks)

**b)** A company wants to connect 8 branch offices in a full mesh. Compute (i) the number of links, (ii) the number of ports on each switch if one switch is placed at each office, and (iii) the number of link failures the network can tolerate while remaining fully connected. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — Full Mesh Link Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the requirement (1 mark):**
In a full mesh, every node must have a direct physical link to every other node. The goal is to find the total number of such links, given $n$ nodes.

**Step 2 — Connections per node (1 mark):**
A single node $N_i$ must connect to the remaining $(n-1)$ nodes. So each node contributes $(n-1)$ connections.

**Step 3 — Naive summation (1 mark):**
If we sum over all $n$ nodes:

$$
S_{\text{naive}} = n \times (n-1) = n^2 - n
$$

**Step 4 — Double-counting correction (2 marks):**
Each physical link connects **two** nodes, so when we sum node-wise, every link is counted twice (once from each endpoint). Therefore, we divide by 2:

$$
L_{\text{full mesh}} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{full mesh}} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Justification / Verification (1 mark):**
For $n=2$: $L = \frac{2 \times 1}{2} = 1$ link. ✔
For $n=3$: $L = \frac{3 \times 2}{2} = 3$ links (a triangle). ✔

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $n = 8$ branch offices in full mesh.

**(i) Number of links (2 marks):**

$$
L = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{8 \times 7}{2} = \frac{56}{2} = 28 \text{ links}
$$

**(ii) Number of ports per switch (3 marks):**
Since each office has one switch that must connect to all the **other 7** offices:

$$
P_{\text{per switch}} = n - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7 \text{ ports}
$$

(Total ports across all 8 switches = $8 \times 7 = 56$ ports — note this matches the naive summation step in the derivation.)

**(iii) Tolerated link failures (2 marks):**
For a full mesh, the network remains connected as long as there is at least one path between every pair. The minimum number of links required to keep $n$ nodes connected is $(n-1)$ (a spanning tree). So tolerated failures:

$$
F = L - (n-1) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} - (n-1) = (n-1)\left(\frac{n}{2} - 1\right) = \frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}
$$

For $n=8$:

$$
F = \frac{7 \times 6}{2} = \frac{42}{2} = 21 \text{ link failures}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `Option B: Comparative Topology Analysis`

> `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice Question]`

**a)** Compare **bus, star, ring, and mesh** topologies with respect to **(i) cabling cost, (ii) fault tolerance, (iii) ease of adding a new node, and (iv) performance under heavy load.** (7 marks)

**b)** A small office has 12 computers. The IT manager is confused between **bus, star, and ring** topologies. Recommend the most suitable topology with proper justification, mentioning at least **three** practical reasons. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)

| Aspect (0.25 each cell) | Bus (0.5 mark) | Star (0.5 mark) | Ring (0.5 mark) | Mesh (0.5 mark) |
|------------------------|----------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **(i) Cabling cost** | Lowest — single backbone | Moderate — $n$ dedicated links | Moderate — $n$ links in loop | Highest — $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ links |
| **(ii) Fault tolerance** | Very poor — cable break kills network | Poor — hub is SPOF | Poor in single ring, good in dual ring | Excellent — multiple paths |
| **(iii) Ease of adding new node** | Difficult — must break bus | Easy — plug new cable to hub | Difficult — must break ring | Easy — add new links |
| **(iv) Performance under heavy load** | Degrades due to collisions | Excellent (with switch) | Predictable (token-based) | Excellent (no contention) |

**Conclusion sentence (0.5 mark):** Each topology has a sweet spot — bus for tiny legacy LANs, star for modern offices, ring for predictable industrial use, and mesh for high-availability backbones.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — Recommendation (7 marks)

**Recommendation: STAR topology** (1 mark for direct answer)

**Justification — three practical reasons (2 marks each):**

**Reason 1 — Cost-effectiveness for $n=12$:**
A star with 12 PCs requires only **12 dedicated drop cables** of moderate length (typically Cat 6, ~3 m each). The total cable length is much smaller than a full mesh (which would need $\frac{12 \times 11}{2} = 66$ links). The only extra cost is the central switch, which is now inexpensive (a 16-port gigabit switch costs less than ₹3,000).

**Reason 2 — Fault isolation:**
In bus or ring topology, a single cable break takes down the entire segment. In a star, a single cable failure affects only that one PC. The rest of the office remains online — this is critical for a working office environment.

**Reason 3 — Scalability and management:**
Adding the 13th employee later requires only plugging in one more cable to the switch. No disruption to the existing 12 PCs. A modern managed switch also provides per-port monitoring, VLANs, and traffic prioritization.

**Bonus reason (optional, 1 mark):**
Modern star-topology Ethernet supports **full-duplex gigabit speeds** with no collisions, giving far better performance than legacy bus (10BASE2) or ring (Token Ring 4/16 Mbps).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Confusing physical and logical topology:** Students often write "ring topology uses a central hub" — this is wrong. A logical ring can run over a physical star (e.g., Token Ring over a MAU). Examiners will deduct 1 mark for mixing these.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\div 2$ in mesh:** Writing $n(n-1)$ instead of $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing the terminator:** In bus topology questions, failing to mention **50 Ω terminators** at both ends costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Hub vs Switch confusion:** Saying "star uses a switch" without qualification is acceptable, but stating "star uses only a hub" is incomplete because the question may be about modern Ethernet.
> 5. **Ignoring real-world examples:** Adding a one-line example (e.g., "Star: used in modern offices with Cat 6 cabling") earns a goodwill mark.
> 6. **Cable length units:** Always quote cable length in **metres** and link counts as pure numbers; mixing units loses marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Physical Topologies (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Physical topology = physical layout of nodes and cables (NOT data flow).
- **Six topologies:** Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid.
- **Bus:** Single backbone, 50 Ω terminators at both ends, cheapest, lowest reliability.
- **Star:** Central hub/switch, dedicated point-to-point links, modern Ethernet standard.
- **Ring:** Closed loop, unidirectional token passing, predictable under load, dual ring supports wrap-recovery.
- **Mesh:** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ links in full mesh; maximum fault tolerance; used in backbones and wireless mesh.
- **Tree:** Hierarchical; combines star and bus; structured cabling standard.
- **Hybrid:** Mix of two or more topologies; real-world enterprise networks.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - Full mesh links: $L = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$
  - Star hub ports: $P = n$
  - Tolerated failures in full mesh: $F = \frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}$
  - Bus cable length: $L_{\text{bus}} = L_{\text{backbone}} + n \cdot d_{\text{drop}}$
- **Failure behavior summary:**
  - Bus → one break = total failure
  - Star → hub failure = total failure
  - Single Ring → one break = total failure
  - Dual Ring → one break = recoverable
  - Full Mesh → survives up to $\frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}$ failures
- **Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare," use a **table** with at least 4 parameters — this earns full marks faster than a paragraph answer.
- **Real-world mapping:** Bus ≈ 10BASE2 legacy; Star ≈ office LAN; Ring ≈ SONET/FDDI; Mesh ≈ ISP core / Wi-Fi mesh; Tree ≈ campus LAN; Hybrid ≈ enterprise WAN.
- **Common 3-markers:** Definition of physical topology, role of terminator, list of topologies, formula for full mesh links.
- **Common 14-markers:** Derivation of mesh links with numerical example, comparison of 4+ topologies, recommendation with justification for a given scenario.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Encoding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signal Encoding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Signal Encoding** is the systematic technique of converting digital data (binary 0s and 1s) or analog information into a form of electrical, optical, or electromagnetic **signal patterns** that can be efficiently transmitted across a physical communication medium while preserving data integrity, synchronization, and bandwidth efficiency.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, signal encoding is categorized under the **Physical Layer** of the OSI Reference Model and is fundamentally responsible for converting source information into transmittable energy waveforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Signal Encoding bridges the gap between the *Digital (Bit) Domain* and the *Analog (Signal) Domain*. Mastering this topic is critical because **every other layer above the Physical Layer assumes that the bits have been correctly encoded at this stage.**

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Conversion Families

Signal encoding is broadly classified into **four families** based on the source and destination information types:

| # | Conversion Type | Source → Destination | Common Schemes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Digital-to-Digital** | Digital bits → Digital signal | NRZ-L, NRZI, Manchester, Differential Manchester, AMI, B8ZS, HDB3 |
| **2** | **Analog-to-Digital** | Analog info → Digital signal | PCM (Pulse Code Modulation), Delta Modulation |
| **3** | **Digital-to-Analog** | Digital bits → Analog signal | ASK, FSK, PSK, QPSK, QAM |
| **4** | **Analog-to-Analog** | Analog info → Analog signal | AM, FM, PM |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you want to send a message in **English (Digital Data)** to a friend living far away through a **telephone wire (Analog Transmission Channel)**. 
>
> - The **English sentence** is your digital binary data (`01001000 01100101 01101100 01101100 01101111`). 
> - The **telephone wire** cannot carry English letters directly — it only carries *sound waves* (analog signals). 
> - **Signal Encoding** is like the process of **speaking the letters aloud (converting bits into audio tones)** so your friend can hear, decode, and understand them on the other end. 
> - If you shout the message (high amplitude = 1) or whisper (low amplitude = 0), that choice of representation is the **encoding scheme**.

## 1.4 Why Is Signal Encoding Necessary?

- **Synchronization:** Ensures the receiver's clock stays aligned with the sender's clock to identify bit boundaries.
- **DC Component Suppression:** Eliminates low-frequency components that cannot pass through certain channels (e.g., transformers).
- **Bandwidth Efficiency:** Reduces the bandwidth required for transmission.
- **Error Detection Capability:** Some schemes inherently detect baseline wander and single-bit errors.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) Optimization:** Maximizes noise immunity.

## 1.5 Core Performance Metrics (Standard Engineering Metrics in **Bold**)

- **Bit Rate (N):** Number of bits transmitted per second (**bps**).
- **Baud Rate / Signal Rate (S):** Number of signal elements (symbols) per second (**baud**).
- **Bandwidth (B):** Range of frequencies required for transmission (**Hz**).
- **DC Component:** Average voltage level; should ideally be **zero**.
- **Synchronization:** Inherent clock recovery capability of the encoding scheme.
- **Complexity & Cost:** Hardware effort required to implement the encoder/decoder.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Digital-to-Digital Conversion (Line Coding)

Line coding converts binary digits into digital signals (voltage pulses). It is the **most heavily tested sub-topic** in KTU Module 1.

### 2.1.1 Unipolar vs. Polar vs. Bipolar Schemes

**A. Unipolar Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ)**

- Binary `1` → Positive voltage (+V)
- Binary `0` → Zero voltage (0V)
- *Disadvantage:* **DC component** is non-zero (average is +V/2), and synchronization is poor for long strings of `0`s or `1`s.

**B. Polar NRZ-L (Level)**

- Binary `1` → +V
- Binary `0` → −V
- *Disadvantage:* Baseline wander and DC component still exist.

**C. Polar NRZ-I (Invert on One)**

- Binary `1` → Transition (invert signal)
- Binary `0` → No transition (hold previous level)
- *Advantage:* Better synchronization than NRZ-L because of guaranteed transitions on `1`s.

**D. Return-to-Zero (RZ)**

- Binary `1` → +V for the **first half** of the bit period, then returns to 0V
- Binary `0` → 0V for entire bit period
- *Advantage:* Excellent synchronization.
- *Disadvantage:* **Doubles the bandwidth** (signal change occurs twice per bit).

**E. Manchester (Biphase-L)**

- Binary `1` → Transition from **High to Low** (mid-bit falling edge)
- Binary `0` → Transition from **Low to High** (mid-bit rising edge)
- *Standard used in:* **10Base-T Ethernet**.
- *Advantage:* Self-clocking with no DC component.

**F. Differential Manchester**

- Binary `0` → Transition at the **start** of the bit period
- Binary `1` → **No** transition at the start of the bit period
- Mid-bit transition is always present (clock reference).
- *Standard used in:* **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)**.

**G. Bipolar AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion)**

- Binary `0` → No signal (0V)
- Binary `1` → Alternating +V and −V (successive 1s flip polarity)
- *Advantage:* No DC component; good synchronization.

**H. B8ZS (Bipolar with 8-Zero Substitution) — North American T1 Standard**

- Replaces any string of **8 consecutive zeros** with a special violation pattern: `000VB0VB`.
- *V* = bipolar violation (same polarity as previous 1).

**I. HDB3 (High Density Bipolar 3) — European E1 Standard**

- Replaces any string of **4 consecutive zeros** with `000V` or `B00V`.
- Ensures even number of bipolar violations.

## 2.2 Digital-to-Analog Conversion (Bandpass Transmission)

This is required when digital data must travel through a **bandpass channel** (e.g., wireless, telephone lines).

| Scheme | Full Form | Modulated Parameter | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ASK** | Amplitude Shift Keying | Amplitude | $s(t) = A \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ |
| **FSK** | Frequency Shift Keying | Frequency | $s(t) = A \cdot \cos(2\pi f_i t)$ |
| **PSK** | Phase Shift Keying | Phase | $s(t) = A \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$ |
| **QAM** | Quadrature Amplitude Modulation | Both Amplitude + Phase | $s(t) = d_1 \cos(2\pi f_c t) + d_2 \sin(2\pi f_c t)$ |

## 2.3 Analog-to-Digital Conversion (PCM)

**Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is the canonical analog-to-digital conversion technique:

1. **Sampling:** Use Nyquist Theorem: $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$
2. **Quantization:** Map continuous amplitudes to discrete levels (L levels).
3. **Encoding:** Each sample is encoded using $\log_2(L)$ bits.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiners expect direct application of these formulas — memorize them as the primary scoring lever.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bit Rate (Capacity)** | $N = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | $T_b$ = bit duration (seconds) |
| 2 | **Signal Rate (Baud)** | $S = N \times \dfrac{1}{r}$ | $r$ = number of data bits per signal element |
| 3 | **Data Rate from Levels** | $N = S \times \log_2 L$ | $L$ = number of signal levels |
| 4 | **Bandwidth (NRZ)** | $B = \dfrac{N}{2}$ | Minimum theoretical bandwidth |
| 5 | **Bandwidth (Manchester / RZ)** | $B = N$ | Two transitions per bit |
| 6 | **Bandwidth (Multilevel MLT-3)** | $B = \dfrac{N}{4}$ | For MLT-3 encoding |
| 7 | **Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless)** | $C = 2B \log_2 L$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $L$ = signal levels |
| 8 | **Shannon Capacity (Noisy)** | $C = B \log_2 \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio |
| 9 | **PCM Bits per Sample** | $n = \log_2 L$ | $L$ = number of quantization levels |
| 10 | **Baud Rate of FSK** | $S_{FSK} = N$ | Same as bit rate for binary FSK |
| 11 | **Baud Rate of BPSK** | $S_{BPSK} = N$ | Same as bit rate for binary PSK |
| 12 | **Baud Rate of QPSK** | $S_{QPSK} = \dfrac{N}{2}$ | 2 bits per symbol |
| 13 | **QAM Signal Elements** | $L = \text{combinations of amplitude-phase pairs}$ | e.g., 16-QAM: 4 amp × 4 phase |
| 14 | **Carrier Frequency for FSK** | $f_c = \dfrac{f_1 + f_2}{2}$ | Mid-point frequency |
| 15 | **Differential Encoding Rule** | $\text{NRZ-I: new level} = \text{old level} \oplus \text{bit}$ | XOR operation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manchester Encoding** powers **10 Mbps Ethernet (10Base-T)** — every KTU lab uses it.
- **NRZ-I** is the base for **USB (Universal Serial Bus)** signaling.
- **B8ZS / HDB3** enable long-distance **T1/E1 telecom trunk lines**.
- **QAM-256** carries **cable broadband (DOCSIS 3.1)**.
- **BPSK / QPSK** are core to **satellite communication (DVB-S2)** and **Wi-Fi (802.11)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Bit Rate to Bandwidth for NRZ Schemes

### Step 1 — Define the bit duration

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{N}
$$

**Explanation:** If we transmit $N$ bits per second, then each bit occupies a time slot of $1/N$ seconds. This is the *fundamental period* of one bit.

### Step 2 — Express the signal element duration

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{S}
$$

**Explanation:** A signal element is one pulse of voltage or one symbol. Its duration is the reciprocal of the baud rate.

### Step 3 — Relate bit rate and signal rate

$$
S = N \times \frac{1}{r}
$$

**Explanation:** Each signal element carries $r$ bits of information. Therefore, the number of signal elements needed per second is the bit rate divided by the bits-per-element.

### Step 4 — Compute the bandwidth for NRZ schemes

$$
B_{NRZ} = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{N}{2 \cdot r}
$$

**Explanation:** The minimum theoretical bandwidth of a line code equals **half the signal rate** because a complete cycle of a fundamental frequency requires one positive and one negative alternation.

### Step 5 — Solve a worked example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Problem:** A system uses **NRZ-L encoding** to transmit **1 Mbps (N = 1,000,000 bps)** with **r = 1 bit/signal element**. Compute the bandwidth.

**Solution:**

$$
S = N \times \frac{1}{r} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{1}{1} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ baud}
$$

$$
B_{NRZ} = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{2} = 500{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 500 \text{ kHz}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Shannon Capacity vs. Nyquist Capacity

### Case A — Noiseless Channel (Nyquist Formula)

$$
C = 2 B \log_2 L \quad \text{(bits per second)}
$$

**Step-by-step meaning:**

- $2B$ = maximum number of signal changes per second across bandwidth $B$ (Nyquist rate).
- $\log_2 L$ = number of bits encoded per signal level.
- Multiplying gives the maximum bit rate.

### Case B — Noisy Channel (Shannon Formula)

$$
C = B \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Step-by-step meaning:**

- $S/N$ is the signal-to-noise ratio (a unitless power ratio, often expressed in dB).
- $\log_2(1 + S/N)$ = theoretical maximum bits per Hz (spectral efficiency).
- Multiplying by $B$ yields the channel capacity.

### Step C — Worked Comparison Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem:** A channel has $B = 4$ kHz and $S/N = 1023$ (i.e., 30 dB). Compare Nyquist vs. Shannon capacity.

**Nyquist (assuming 2 signal levels, $L = 2$):**

$$
C_{Nyquist} = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 2 = 8000 \text{ bps}
$$

**Shannon:**

$$
C_{Shannon} = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1023) = 4000 \times \log_2(1024)
$$

$$
= 4000 \times 10 = 40{,}000 \text{ bps}
$$

**Conclusion:** Shannon gives the **absolute theoretical upper bound**, while Nyquist assumes an idealized noiseless scenario. Real systems fall between the two.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Manchester Encoding Walkthrough

**Bit stream:** `0 1 0 0 1 1`

| Bit | Manchester Transition Rule | Resulting Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| `0` | Low → High (mid-bit rising edge) | ┌─┐ ┌─┐ |
| `1` | High → Low (mid-bit falling edge) | ┌─┘ └─┐ |
| `0` | Low → High | ┌─┐ ┌─┐ |
| `0` | Low → High | ┌─┐ ┌─┐ |
| `1` | High → Low | ┌─┘ └─┐ |
| `1` | High → Low | ┌─┘ └─┐ |

**Key observation:** Every bit contains a mid-bit transition. This guarantees a clock transition every $T_b/2$ seconds, eliminating synchronization loss.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Line Codes

| Scheme | Bandwidth (Hz) | DC Component | Clock Sync | Error Detection | Bit Rate vs. Baud |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NRZ-L** | $N/2$ | High | Poor | None | Same |
| **NRZ-I** | $N/2$ | High | Better | None | Same |
| **Bipolar AMI** | $N/2$ | None | Good | Limited | Same |
| **Manchester** | $N$ | None | Excellent | None | Same |
| **Differential Manchester** | $N$ | None | Excellent | Better | Same |
| **2B1Q (MLT-3)** | $N/4$ | None | Good | None | $S = N/2$ |
| **B8ZS / HDB3** | $N/2$ | None | Good | Strong | Same |

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example: QAM Spectral Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem:** A modem uses **16-QAM** with a baud rate of $S = 2400$ symbols/sec. Compute (a) the bit rate and (b) the minimum bandwidth.

**Solution:**

### Part (a) — Bit Rate

$$
N = S \times \log_2 L = 2400 \times \log_2 16
$$

$$
= 2400 \times 4 = 9600 \text{ bps}
$$

**Explanation:** 16-QAM has 16 distinct signal elements ($L = 16$), so each element encodes $\log_2 16 = 4$ bits.

### Part (b) — Minimum Bandwidth

$$
B = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{2400}{2} = 1200 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is the same configuration used by the **V.32 modem standard (9600 bps)** historically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Signal Encoding Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Information: Digital Bits or Analog Waveform] --> B{Encoding Family Selector}
    B -->|Digital to Digital| C1[Line Coding Module]
    B -->|Digital to Analog| C2[Bandpass Modulation Module]
    B -->|Analog to Digital| C3[Pulse Code Modulation Module]
    B -->|Analog to Analog| C4[Carrier Modulation Module]
    C1 --> D1[NRZ NRZI Manchester AMI B8ZS HDB3]
    C2 --> D2[ASK FSK PSK QAM QPSK]
    C3 --> D3[Sampling Quantization Binary Encoding]
    C4 --> D4[AM FM PM]
    D1 --> E[Multiplexer and Line Driver]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Transmission Medium: Copper Fiber Wireless]
    F --> G[Receiver Decoder Block]
    G --> H[Recovered Original Information]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart for Choosing a Line Code

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Line Coding Scheme] --> Q1{Channel is Synchronous?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Need Zero DC Component?}
    Q1 -->|No| NRZ[Use NRZ or NRZ-I]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Cost Sensitive?}
    Q2 -->|No| RZ[Use RZ or Manchester]
    Q3 -->|Yes| AMI[Use Bipolar AMI]
    Q3 -->|No| MCH[Use Manchester Encoding]
    AMI --> Q4{Long Distance T1 E1 Trunk?}
    Q4 -->|Yes T1 North America| B8ZS[Use B8ZS Scrambling]
    Q4 -->|Yes E1 Europe| HDB3[Use HDB3 Scrambling]
    Q4 -->|No| AMIFinal[Plain Bipolar AMI]
    MCH --> Ethernet[Use Manchester IEEE 802.3]
    RZ --> TokenRing[Use Differential Manchester IEEE 802.5]
```

## 4.3 Bit-to-Signal Mapping Diagram (Manchester)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Bit0[Input Bit 0] --> M0[Mid bit Rising Edge]
    Bit1[Input Bit 1] --> M1[Mid bit Falling Edge]
    M0 --> Out0[Output: Low to High Pulse]
    M1 --> Out1[Output: High to Low Pulse]
    Out0 --> CLK[Clock Recovery from Mid Bit Transition]
    Out1 --> CLK
    CLK --> SYNC[Self Clocking Synchronization Achieved]
```

## 4.4 PSK Constellation Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BPSK[BPSK Constellation 2 Points]
        P1[Point at 0 degrees: Binary 0]
        P2[Point at 180 degrees: Binary 1]
    end
    subgraph QPSK[QPSK Constellation 4 Points]
        Q1[Point at 45 degrees: 00]
        Q2[Point at 135 degrees: 01]
        Q3[Point at 225 degrees: 11]
        Q4[Point at 315 degrees: 10]
    end
    subgraph QAM16[16 QAM Constellation 16 Points]
        A1[A1] --> A2[A2]
        A2 --> A3[A3]
        A3 --> A4[A4]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **Signal Encoding**. List the four types of signal encoding with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

Signal encoding is the process of converting source information (digital bits or analog waveforms) into a transmittable signal format compatible with the physical medium.

The four types are:
1. **Digital-to-Digital Conversion** — Example: Manchester Encoding (used in 10Base-T Ethernet).
2. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion** — Example: Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) used in telephony.
3. **Digital-to-Analog Conversion** — Example: Phase Shift Keying (PSK) used in Wi-Fi.
4. **Analog-to-Analog Conversion** — Example: Frequency Modulation (FM) used in radio broadcasting.

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Why is **Manchester Encoding** preferred over **NRZ-L** for Ethernet LANs? State two reasons.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Synchronization:** Manchester encoding guarantees a mid-bit transition for every bit, ensuring self-clocking. NRZ-L has long runs of identical voltage levels that cause **clock drift** at the receiver.
2. **No DC Component:** Manchester has a zero average DC level (equal positive and negative excursions), allowing it to pass through AC-coupled transformers and channels that block DC. NRZ-L has a non-zero DC component, causing **baseline wander**.

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q (a)** Explain the various **Digital-to-Digital encoding schemes** with suitable diagrams. Compare them based on bandwidth, DC component, and synchronization. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Digital-to-digital (line) coding converts binary bits into voltage pulses. The major schemes are:

1. **Unipolar NRZ:** Bit 1 → +V, Bit 0 → 0V. Simple but has DC component and poor synchronization.

2. **Polar NRZ-L:** Bit 1 → +V, Bit 0 → −V. Reduced DC component compared to unipolar but still suffers baseline wander.

3. **Polar NRZ-I:** Bit 1 → Invert signal, Bit 0 → No change. Better synchronization than NRZ-L.

4. **RZ (Return-to-Zero):** Bit 1 → +V for first half, then 0V. Provides excellent synchronization but doubles bandwidth.

5. **Manchester:** Bit 1 → High-to-Low mid-bit transition; Bit 0 → Low-to-High mid-bit transition. Self-clocking with zero DC component. Used in **10Base-T Ethernet**.

6. **Differential Manchester:** Mid-bit transition always present; Bit 0 → additional transition at start; Bit 1 → no transition at start. Better noise immunity than Manchester. Used in **Token Ring**.

7. **Bipolar AMI:** Bit 0 → 0V; Bit 1 → alternating +V and −V. No DC component, good synchronization, but long runs of zeros cause loss of clock.

8. **B8ZS:** Substitutes 8-zero string with `000VB0VB` to maintain synchronization in T1 lines.

9. **HDB3:** Substitutes 4-zero string with `000V` or `B00V` for E1 European telecom.

**Comparison Table:**

| Scheme | Bandwidth | DC Component | Synchronization |
|---|---|---|---|
| NRZ-L | $N/2$ | High | Poor |
| NRZ-I | $N/2$ | High | Better |
| RZ | $N$ | Low | Excellent |
| Manchester | $N$ | Zero | Excellent |
| Differential Manchester | $N$ | Zero | Excellent |
| Bipolar AMI | $N/2$ | Zero | Good |
| B8ZS / HDB3 | $N/2$ | Zero | Excellent |

**Q (b)** A channel has a bandwidth of **4 kHz** and a **signal-to-noise ratio of 30 dB**. Calculate: **(i)** Maximum data rate using **Nyquist** (assuming 4 signal levels), **(ii)** Maximum data rate using **Shannon's** theorem. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### (i) Nyquist Bit Rate

Given: $B = 4000$ Hz, $L = 4$ levels.

$$
C = 2B \log_2 L
$$

$$
C = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 4 = 2 \times 4000 \times 2 = 16{,}000 \text{ bps}
$$

**Explanation:** Nyquist's noiseless formula directly yields the bit rate given the bandwidth and signal levels.

### (ii) Shannon Capacity

Given: $S/N \text{ in dB} = 30$. Convert to linear scale:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

$$
C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

$$
C = 4000 \times \log_2(1001) \approx 4000 \times 9.97 \approx 39{,}882 \text{ bps}
$$

**Conclusion:** Shannon's bound (~40 kbps) is the **theoretical maximum**, while Nyquist's bound (16 kbps) is the achievable rate under the given signal levels.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (a)** With neat signal diagrams, explain **ASK, FSK, and PSK** techniques. Compare their **baud rates** and **noise immunity**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)

- Binary 1 → Carrier present with amplitude $A$
- Binary 0 → Carrier absent (amplitude 0)
- Mathematically: $s(t) = A \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ when bit = 1; $s(t) = 0$ when bit = 0.
- *Highly susceptible to noise* because noise directly affects amplitude.

### Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)

- Binary 1 → Carrier frequency $f_1$
- Binary 0 → Carrier frequency $f_2$
- Mathematically: $s(t) = A \cdot \cos(2\pi f_i t)$ for $i \in \{1, 2\}$.
- *Better noise immunity* than ASK; used in low-speed modems.

### Phase Shift Keying (PSK)

- Binary 1 → Carrier with phase $0°$
- Binary 0 → Carrier with phase $180°$
- Mathematically: $s(t) = A \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$ where $\phi \in \{0, \pi\}$.
- *Best noise immunity* among the three; used in Wi-Fi, satellite, and Bluetooth.

**Baud Rate Comparison:**

For all three (binary modulation with 1 bit per symbol):

$$
S_{ASK} = S_{FSK} = S_{BPSK} = N \text{ baud}
$$

**Noise Immunity Ranking (Best → Worst):** PSK > FSK > ASK.

**Q (b)** Explain **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** with a block diagram. A PCM system uses **256 quantization levels** and samples a signal at **8 kHz**. Compute the **bit rate** of the system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### PCM Block Diagram Explanation

PCM consists of three stages:

1. **Sampler:** Samples the analog input at frequency $f_s$ (must satisfy Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$).
2. **Quantizer:** Maps each continuous sample to the nearest of $L$ discrete levels.
3. **Encoder:** Converts each quantized level into a binary codeword of $n = \log_2 L$ bits.

The output is a stream of binary digits representing the original analog waveform.

### Numerical Computation

Given:
- $L = 256$ quantization levels
- $f_s = 8000$ samples/sec

Number of bits per sample:

$$
n = \log_2 L = \log_2 256 = 8 \text{ bits/sample}
$$

Bit rate:

$$
N = f_s \times n = 8000 \times 8 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Real-world context:** This is exactly the **G.711 PCM standard** used in digital telephony — a 4 kHz voice channel sampled at 8 kHz with 8-bit linear quantization yielding **64 kbps**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — KTU 2024 Board Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the sketch:** In line coding questions, examiners allocate **2 to 3 marks** purely for the waveform diagram. Drawing the **mid-bit transition** of Manchester correctly is the single most-failed sub-requirement.
> 2. **Forgetting the dB-to-linear conversion:** When given $S/N$ in dB, students often plug $30$ directly into Shannon's formula instead of $10^{30/10} = 1000$. This yields a wrong answer and full mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing Baud and Bit Rate:** Many students use $N$ and $S$ interchangeably. For multilevel schemes like **16-QAM**, the baud rate is **1/4** of the bit rate.
> 4. **Misstating the bandwidth formula:** Manchester has bandwidth $N$, not $N/2$. Mixing this up is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Missing the carrier frequency in FSK:** When asked to design FSK, always mention the **mid-frequency $f_c = (f_1 + f_2)/2$** and the **frequency deviation $\Delta f$**.
> 6. **Ignoring the "synchronization" criterion:** Comparison questions always require you to comment on **clock recovery**. NRZ schemes score poorly here.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Signal Encoding (KTU Module 1)**
>
> - **Signal Encoding** = converting source information into transmittable signals.
> - **Four families:** Digital-Digital, Analog-Digital, Digital-Analog, Analog-Analog.
> - **NRZ schemes** have bandwidth **$N/2$** but suffer from synchronization issues.
> - **Manchester** has bandwidth **$N$** and zero DC component — the **10Base-T Ethernet standard**.
> - **Differential Manchester** is used in **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)**.
> - **B8ZS** is for North American T1 lines; **HDB3** is for European E1 lines.
> - **Bipolar AMI** eliminates DC component but cannot handle long zero strings.
> - **ASK** modulates amplitude — most noise-prone.
> - **FSK** modulates frequency — better noise immunity.
> - **PSK** modulates phase — best noise immunity; foundation of BPSK, QPSK.
> - **QAM** combines amplitude and phase — highest spectral efficiency.
> - **Nyquist (Noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2 L$.
> - **Shannon (Noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
> - **Convert dB to linear** using $S/N = 10^{(dB/10)}$.
> - **PCM formula:** Bit rate $= f_s \times \log_2 L$.
> - **Spectral efficiency:** QPSK has $S = N/2$; 16-QAM has $S = N/4$.
> - **Self-clocking property** is the **defining advantage** of Manchester encoding.
> - **Baud Rate ≠ Bit Rate** in multilevel/modulated schemes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeaters and Hub
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Repeaters and Hub

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The OSI Physical Layer Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** *"Data Communication; Transmission media; Network devices — Repeaters, Hubs, Bridges, Switches, Routers, Gateways."*

Before we study the devices, we must locate them precisely in the networking stack. The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model partitions networking into **7 layers**. **Repeaters** and **Hubs** are the *only* two devices in the entire KTU syllabus that operate exclusively at **Layer 1 (Physical Layer)**. They do not understand frames, packets, IP addresses, or MAC addresses — they only understand **electrical pulses, light pulses, or radio waves** as raw bits.

---

## 1.2 Repeater — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repeater:** A **2-port Physical Layer device** that receives a weakened or distorted analog/digital signal from one network segment, **regenerates** (retimes and re-amplifies) the signal bit-by-bit, and forwards the reconstructed signal onto another segment. Its sole purpose is to **extend the physical reach** of a LAN beyond the natural attenuation limits of the transmission medium.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Megaphone in a Valley"

Imagine two people standing at opposite ends of a long, noisy valley trying to talk. By the time the second person hears the first voice, it is faint and garbled. A repeater acts like a person standing in the middle of the valley with a **megaphone**:
- It **listens** to the weakened voice (input segment).
- It **regenerates** the voice at full volume (re-amplifies).
- It **re-broadcasts** it forward (output segment).

The repeater does **not** translate languages, route messages, or decide who hears — it only makes the signal strong enough to travel the next leg of the journey.

### Standard Engineering Metrics for a Repeater

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Number of Ports | **2** (Input + Output) |
| Operating Layer | **OSI Layer 1 (Physical)** |
| Processing Granularity | **Bit-by-bit regeneration** |
| Latency Introduced | **≈ 1 to 2 bit-times** (per repeater) |
| Logical Domains Affected | **Single Collision Domain, Single Broadcast Domain** |

---

## 1.3 Hub — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hub (Ethernet Hub):** A **multi-port Physical Layer device** that functions as a *common connection point* for nodes in a star-topology network. When it receives a bit on any one port, it **retransmits that bit out of every other active port**, simultaneously and without any intelligent decision-making. An Ethernet hub is functionally equivalent to a **repeater with N ports**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Town Crier in a Village Square"

Picture an old village square with a single **Town Crier** standing on a podium. Whenever any villager makes an announcement:
1. The crier **listens** to that one villager.
2. The crier then **shouts the same message to every other villager** standing around the square.

The crier does **not** know which villagers actually need the message, does **not** keep secrets, and does **not** remember who said what. Every villager hears *everything* and must decide for themselves whether to act on the message. This is exactly how a hub operates.

### Hub Taxonomy — Three Sub-Classes

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **three categories of hubs** as a 3-mark short-answer question.

- **Passive Hub:** Acts purely as a physical connector (a "cable junction"). It does **not** amplify or regenerate signals. Think of it as a *multi-way splitter*. Typical use: small wiring closets.
- **Active Hub:** Contains electronic circuitry to **regenerate and re-amplify** incoming signals before broadcasting. Effectively a multi-port repeater. Requires external power.
- **Intelligent Hub (Smart Hub):** An active hub with added diagnostic LEDs, port-monitoring capabilities, and sometimes basic management (SNMP support). Considered a transitional device between hubs and switches.

### Key Engineering Properties of a Hub

- **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** device — no MAC awareness.
- All ports share **one common Collision Domain**.
- All ports share **one common Broadcast Domain**.
- Operates using **CSMA/CD** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection).
- **Half-duplex** communication only (a node cannot send and receive simultaneously on the same port).
- Cannot filter traffic; every frame is forwarded to every port except the source port.

---

## 1.4 Layer-1 Device Placement Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both Repeaters and Hubs are **Layer-1 devices**, meaning they are **transparent to data-link layer protocols**. A 10BASE-T hub will forward *any* Layer-2 frame regardless of whether it is Ethernet, Token Ring, or any other framed protocol — provided the bit rate matches.

**Analogy Recap:**

| Device | Real-World Analogy | Smart Decisions? |
|---|---|---|
| Repeater | Megaphone in a valley | No |
| Passive Hub | Junction box (electrical wiring) | No |
| Active Hub | Town crier in a village square | No |

In the next section, we will examine the *mathematical* and *timing* foundations that govern why these devices must exist and what their hard limits are.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Repeaters? — The Physics of Signal Degradation

Every transmission medium (copper cable, optical fiber, wireless channel) introduces **signal impairments** as the bit travels. The four primary impairments that a repeater is designed to combat are:

- **Attenuation:** Signal power loss, typically measured in **dB/km**. Cables like Cat5e lose roughly **22 dB per 100 meters** at 100 MHz.
- **Distortion:** Different frequency components of the signal travel at slightly different speeds, smearing the waveform.
- **Noise:** Thermal noise (Johnson-Nyquist noise) and crosstalk accumulate along the cable.
- **Jitter:** Small timing variations between successive bits.

If a bit travels too far, the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the receiver drops below the threshold needed for reliable bit detection. The repeater **solves this** by:
1. **Re-amplifying** the analog voltage swing back to standard levels (e.g., +2.5 V for 1-bit, −2.5 V for 0-bit on 10BASE-T).
2. **Re-timing** the bits using a local clock to remove jitter.

> [!NOTE]
> A repeater performs **analog-to-digital → decision → digital-to-analog** on every bit. This is why it is sometimes called a "**bit regenerator**" rather than a simple amplifier.

---

## 2.2 Hub Architecture — The Common Bus Emulation

A hub internally contains a **single shared electrical bus** that connects all ports. When a bit enters any port:

1. The hub's repeater core **detects the bit** on the input port.
2. The hub **re-amplifies and re-times** the bit.
3. The bit is **broadcast simultaneously** out of every other active port.

This is why a hub is described as a "**logical bus**" inside a "**physical star**" — the wiring topology is star (each node has its own dedicated cable to the hub), but the electrical behavior is identical to a shared bus where every node sees every other node's traffic.

### Hub Operation Rules (Critical for KTU Exams)

- **Rule 1:** All ports share a **single collision domain**.
- **Rule 2:** If two ports transmit simultaneously, a **collision** occurs at the hub, and CSMA/CD requires both nodes to back off.
- **Rule 3:** The hub does **not buffer** frames — it forwards bits *as they arrive*.
- **Rule 4:** The total throughput of the hub is **shared** by all ports. For a 10 Mbps hub with 24 ports, the **aggregate bandwidth remains 10 Mbps**, not 240 Mbps.

> [!WARNING]
> This is the single most important conceptual contrast between a **Hub** and a **Switch** (a switch gives each port **dedicated bandwidth**).

---

## 2.3 The 5-4-3 Rule — Classic KTU High-Yield Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **5-4-3 Rule** was a strict design rule for **10BASE5 (Thick Ethernet)** and **10BASE2 (Thin Ethernet)** networks. Although largely historical today, KTU examiners still pose it as a conceptual and numerical question.

**Statement of the Rule:**
Between any two nodes on an Ethernet network, the path may consist of at most:
- **5** network segments (cable runs).
- **4** repeaters (or hubs).
- **3** segments that contain **active end nodes** (called "populated segments").
- The remaining **2** segments serve only as **link segments** between repeaters (no nodes attached).

### Origin of the 5-4-3 Rule

The rule exists because of **propagation delay** constraints. For CSMA/CD to function, when a sender transmits the **minimum frame size**, it must still be transmitting when the first bit returns from the farthest node as a collision signal — otherwise, the sender will think the transmission was successful when in fact a collision occurred at the far end.

For **10 Mbps Ethernet**:
- Minimum frame size = **64 bytes = 512 bits**.
- Time to transmit 512 bits = **51.2 µs**.
- This sets a **maximum round-trip delay budget** for the entire network.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for any numerical problem on Repeaters and Hubs in the KTU 2024 scheme. **Memorize the unit columns** — they are where students lose marks.

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v_{prop}$ | Signal propagation speed (in cable) | $v_{prop} = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}} \approx 2 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| $t_{prop}$ | Propagation delay (one-way) | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{L}{v_{prop}}$ | seconds |
| $t_{RT}$ | Round-trip delay | $t_{RT} = 2 \cdot t_{prop} + N_{rep} \cdot t_{rep}$ | seconds |
| $t_{slot}$ | Slot time (10BASE-T) | $t_{slot} = 512 \cdot t_{bit}$ | seconds |
| $t_{bit}$ | Time for 1 bit at R bps | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | seconds |
| $D_{max}$ | Maximum collision domain diameter | $D_{max} = v_{prop} \cdot \dfrac{t_{slot}}{2}$ | meters |
| $N_{seg}$ | Maximum number of segments (5-4-3) | $N_{seg} \le 5$ | dimensionless |
| $N_{rep}$ | Maximum number of repeaters | $N_{rep} \le 4$ | dimensionless |
| $SNR_{dB}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB |
| $P_{recv}$ | Received power after attenuation | $P_{recv} = P_{tx} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L/10}$ | Watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical constants for 10BASE-T:**
> - $R = 10 \text{ Mbps} \Rightarrow t_{bit} = 100 \text{ ns}$
> - $t_{slot} = 51.2 \text{ µs}$
> - $D_{max} \approx 2500 \text{ meters}$ (theoretical)

**Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin the design of **Industrial Ethernet**, **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)**, and **Substation Communication Networks (IEC 61850)**. Every time an engineer specifies a maximum cable length between two switches, they are implicitly applying variants of these constraints.

---

## 2.5 Limitations of Repeaters and Hubs

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why are hubs and repeaters unsuitable for modern large networks?"* Memorize these 4 reasons.

1. **Single Collision Domain** → as the number of nodes grows, **collision probability rises sharply**, destroying throughput.
2. **No Filtering or Segmentation** → every frame reaches every node, wasting bandwidth and CPU cycles.
3. **No Security Boundary** → any node can sniff all traffic on the hub (passive eavesdropping is trivial).
4. **Limited Scalability** → the 5-4-3 rule and physical layer timing constraints cap the network size.

**Real-world consequence:** Hubs were **completely phased out of enterprise networks by the late 1990s**, replaced by switches. They survive today only in niche applications: small home labs, network packet-capture setups, and certain **industrial deterministic Ethernet** repeaters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Network Diameter for 10BASE-T Ethernet

### Goal
Derive the maximum theoretical end-to-end length of a 10BASE-T collision domain.

### Step 1 — Identify the Timing Constraint

CSMA/CD requires that a transmitting station must still be on the air when the **last bit of a collision** propagates back from the farthest point in the network. Therefore:

$$ t_{transmission} \ge t_{round-trip-propagation} $$

### Step 2 — Compute the Transmission Time of the Minimum Frame

For 10BASE-T, the minimum legal Ethernet frame is 64 bytes. The IEEE 802.3 standard adds an 8-byte preamble + SFD before the frame, but for the slot-time calculation, the constraint is the **512-bit slot time**:

$$ t_{slot} = 512 \cdot t_{bit} = 512 \cdot \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{6}} $$

$$ t_{slot} = 512 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 51.2 \text{ µs} $$

### Step 3 — Equate Slot Time to Maximum Round-Trip Delay

The full round-trip delay $t_{RT}$ includes the one-way propagation through the cable AND the internal latency through repeaters:

$$ t_{slot} \ge t_{RT,max} = 2 \cdot t_{prop,max} + N_{rep} \cdot t_{rep} $$

For a network with **no repeaters** ($N_{rep} = 0$), the constraint simplifies to:

$$ 51.2 \text{ µs} \ge 2 \cdot t_{prop,max} $$

### Step 4 — Solve for Maximum Propagation Time

$$ t_{prop,max} \le \frac{51.2 \text{ µs}}{2} = 25.6 \text{ µs} $$

### Step 5 — Convert to Maximum Cable Length

Using the standard propagation speed in twisted-pair copper cable $v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (≈ 200,000 km/s, which is roughly 67% the speed of light):

$$ D_{max} = v_{prop} \cdot t_{prop,max} $$

$$ D_{max} = (2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}) \cdot (25.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}) $$

$$ D_{max} = 5120 \text{ meters} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The **official IEEE 802.3 figure** is **2500 meters** for the collision domain diameter when accounting for repeater internals, velocity factor tolerances, and safety margins. The 5120 m number is the *theoretical upper bound ignoring repeater latency*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 5-4-3 Rule Numerical Verification

### Given
A 10BASE2 Ethernet network consists of **5 coaxial segments** connected by **4 repeaters**, with **3 populated segments** containing end nodes and **2 link segments** connecting only repeaters.

Assume each segment is **185 m** long (max allowed for 10BASE2 thin coax) and each repeater introduces a **delay of 25 bit-times**.

### Step 1 — Total Physical Cable Length

$$ L_{total} = 5 \times 185 \text{ m} = 925 \text{ m} $$

### Step 2 — Compute One-Way Propagation Delay

$$ t_{prop,1way} = \frac{L_{total}}{v_{prop}} = \frac{925}{2 \times 10^{8}} $$

$$ t_{prop,1way} = 4.625 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 4.625 \text{ µs} $$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Repeater Latency

At 10 Mbps, one bit-time is $100$ ns. So:

$$ t_{rep,total} = 4 \times 25 \times 100 \text{ ns} = 10{,}000 \text{ ns} = 10 \text{ µs} $$

### Step 4 — Compute the Round-Trip Delay

$$ t_{RT} = 2 \cdot t_{prop,1way} + t_{rep,total} $$

$$ t_{RT} = 2 \cdot 4.625 + 10 = 19.25 \text{ µs} $$

### Step 5 — Compare to the Slot Time Budget

The slot time is $51.2$ µs. Since:

$$ t_{RT} = 19.25 \text{ µs} \ll 51.2 \text{ µs} = t_{slot} $$

**Conclusion:** The 5-4-3 configuration **safely satisfies** the CSMA/CD timing constraint, with a safety margin of:

$$ \text{Margin} = 51.2 - 19.25 = 31.95 \text{ µs} $$

This is exactly why the IEEE committee selected **5 / 4 / 3** as the universal design rule — it guarantees collision detection on every minimum-sized frame.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal Attenuation and Power Budget

### Given
- Transmit power $P_{tx} = +10$ dBm.
- Cable attenuation $\alpha = 22$ dB per 100 m (Cat5e at 100 MHz).
- Receiver sensitivity threshold $P_{rx,min} = -36$ dBm.

### Step 1 — Attenuation as a Function of Length

$$ A(L) = \alpha \cdot \frac{L}{100} = 22 \cdot \frac{L}{100} = 0.22 \cdot L \quad [\text{dB}] $$

### Step 2 — Compute Maximum Length

The maximum length is reached when received power equals the receiver sensitivity:

$$ P_{rx} = P_{tx} - A(L) = P_{rx,min} $$

$$ 10 - 0.22 \cdot L_{max} = -36 $$

$$ 0.22 \cdot L_{max} = 46 $$

$$ L_{max} = \frac{46}{0.22} \approx 209 \text{ m} $$

### Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation

This 209 m figure is why Cat5e is officially rated at **100 m**. The remaining **~109 m of headroom** is needed to account for **connector losses** (≈ 0.5 dB each), **patch-panel losses**, and **safety margin** against temperature-induced attenuation variation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the cable run exceeds 100 m, the **Bit Error Rate (BER)** rises exponentially. A repeater at the 100 m mark **restores the signal** to its original +10 dBm level, effectively resetting the attenuation budget for the next segment.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Layer-1 Devices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is a **favorite 14-mark KTU question** because it forces the student to demonstrate breadth across multiple devices.

| Property | Repeater | Passive Hub | Active Hub | Switch (Layer 2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 1 | Layer 1 | Layer 1 | Layer 2 |
| Number of Ports | 2 | 4 to 12 | 4 to 48 | 4 to 96 |
| Signal Regeneration | Yes | No | Yes | Yes (per port) |
| Collision Domains | 1 (shared) | 1 (shared) | 1 (shared) | **N (one per port)** |
| Broadcast Domains | 1 (shared) | 1 (shared) | 1 (shared) | 1 (by default) |
| Filtering Capability | None | None | None | **MAC-based** |
| Dedicated Bandwidth per Port | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Frame Buffering | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Full-Duplex Support | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Address Awareness | None | None | None | **48-bit MAC** |

This matrix is the foundation on which the KTU Module 1 exam questions are typically constructed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Repeater Operation

The diagram below illustrates the **internal signal processing** pipeline of a repeater. Each block represents a *hardware functional unit* inside the device.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Segment A Input Port] --> RX1[Analog Front End]
    RX1 --> SAMPLE[Bit Sampler at Local Clock]
    SAMPLE --> DECIDE[Threshold Comparator]
    DECIDE --> REGEN[Waveform Regenerator]
    REGEN --> AMP[Line Driver Amplifier]
    AMP --> TX1[Segment B Output Port]
    REGEN --> JDG[Jitter Detector]
    JDG --> CLK[Clock Recovery PLL]
    CLK --> SAMPLE
    DECIDE --> COL[Collision Detector]
    AMP --> COL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Analog Front End** receives the degraded analog waveform from Segment A.
- The **Clock Recovery PLL** extracts a clean local clock from the incoming bit stream.
- The **Threshold Comparator** decides whether each bit is a 0 or 1.
- The **Waveform Regenerator** builds a clean, fresh electrical pulse representing that bit.
- The **Line Driver Amplifier** boosts the pulse to full Ethernet voltage levels for Segment B.
- The **Collision Detector** watches for simultaneous energy on both ports (used to forward the CSMA/CD collision-presence signal, called **SQE / Heartbeat**).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Active Hub (Multi-Port Repeater)

The hub is logically a *star* electrically but a *bus* in behavior. The central functional block is a **common shared repeater core**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HUB[Active Ethernet Hub Layer 1 Device]
        direction TB
        BUS[Common Internal Electrical Bus]
    end
    BUS --- P1[Port 1 NIC Node 1]
    BUS --- P2[Port 2 NIC Node 2]
    BUS --- P3[Port 3 NIC Node 3]
    BUS --- P4[Port 4 NIC Node 4]
    BUS --- P5[Port 5 NIC Node 5]
    BUS --- P6[Port 6 NIC Node 6]
    HUB -- Any bit in -> Any bit broadcast to all other ports out
```

**Operational note:** When Node 1 transmits, the hub's internal bus carries the bit to Ports 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 — but **not** back to Port 1 (the source). This prevents the sender from receiving an immediate echo of its own transmission.

---

## 4.3 Network Topology — Multi-Segment Ethernet with Repeaters and Hubs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node 1] --> H1[Hub A Repeater 1]
    N2[Node 2] --> H1
    N3[Node 3] --> H1
    H1 --> RP1[Repeater R1]
    H2[Hub B Repeater 2] --> RP1
    N4[Node 4] --> H2
    N5[Node 5] --> H2
    H2 --> RP2[Repeater R2]
    H3[Hub C Repeater 3] --> RP2
    N6[Node 6] --> H3
    N7[Node 7] --> H3
    N8[Node 8] --> H3
    style H1 fill:#FFE5B4
    style H2 fill:#FFE5B4
    style H3 fill:#FFE5B4
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each **Hub** (Hub A, Hub B, Hub C) acts as a repeater for its locally attached nodes.
- The **Repeater-to-Repeater** links (Hub A ↔ R1 ↔ Hub B) form the **link segments** of the 5-4-3 rule.
- The entire left-to-right chain spans **3 populated segments** (the three hubs) and **2 link segments** (the two inter-repeater cables).
- All nodes share **one common collision domain** — a node in Hub A will collide with a node in Hub C if both transmit simultaneously.

---

## 4.4 Collision Domain Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CD1[Single Shared Collision Domain for Hub A]
        HA[Hub A]
        HA --- NA1[Node 1]
        HA --- NA2[Node 2]
        HA --- NA3[Node 3]
    end
    subgraph CD2[Single Shared Collision Domain for Hub B]
        HB[Hub B]
        HB --- NB1[Node 4]
        HB --- NB2[Node 5]
    end
    CD1 -- Connected by Repeater Link Segment --> CD2
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that even though there are two physical "clusters" (Hub A and Hub B), they belong to the **same logical collision domain** because they are connected by repeaters that forward collisions. Only a **bridge, switch, or router** can split a collision domain — never a repeater or hub.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A in KTU typically contains 5 questions of 3 marks each, and a student answers any 2 or 3 depending on the pattern. These two questions are the **highest-yield** for the Repeaters and Hubs topic.

---

### Question A1 — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> *"Differentiate between a passive hub, an active hub, and a repeater. Mention the OSI layer at which each operates."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive Hub | Active Hub | Repeater |
|---|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 1 | Layer 1 | Layer 1 |
| Signal Regeneration | **No** (purely a connector) | **Yes** (amplifies & re-times) | **Yes** (amplifies & re-times) |
| Number of Ports | Multi | Multi | Exactly 2 |
| Function | Provides a physical junction point only | Multi-port repeater; broadcasts bits to all other ports | Connects 2 segments and regenerates signals to extend range |

All three are **Physical Layer (OSI Layer 1)** devices. Passive hubs cannot extend network reach; active hubs and repeaters both regenerate signals, but an active hub broadcasts to multiple ports while a repeater forwards to a single counterpart segment.

---

### Question A2 — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> *"Why is the throughput of a 10 Mbps, 24-port Ethernet hub only 10 Mbps and not 240 Mbps? Explain with reference to the collision domain."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hub is essentially a **multi-port repeater** with a **single shared internal electrical bus**. Because all 24 ports share this single physical medium, they all share **one common collision domain** and must use **CSMA/CD** to arbitrate access.

Only **one node** can successfully transmit at any instant — if two transmit simultaneously, a collision occurs and both must back off. The aggregate bandwidth of **10 Mbps is shared** across all active nodes, not summed per port. In contrast, a **switch** creates a dedicated collision domain per port and would offer up to 240 Mbps of aggregate capacity under full load.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's 2024 scheme requires **internal choice** in Part B. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> *"Explain the 5-4-3 rule for Ethernet networks. State the assumptions under which the rule is valid."*

**Model Answer:**

The **5-4-3 rule** is a design guideline for the **maximum topology** of a 10 Mbps Ethernet network using repeaters. The rule states that between any two nodes on the network:

1. There may be at most **5** cable segments in the signal path.
2. These segments may be connected by at most **4** repeaters.
3. At most **3** of the 5 segments may be **populated segments** (containing end nodes).
4. The remaining **2** segments are **link segments** used solely to interconnect repeaters (no end nodes attached).

**Why the rule exists:**
CSMA/CD requires that the **first bit of a collision** must propagate back to the sender **before the sender finishes transmitting the minimum-size frame (64 bytes = 512 bits)**. The slot time for 10BASE-T is **51.2 µs**, and this sets an upper bound on the round-trip propagation delay. With repeater latencies, cable velocities, and safety margins, the 5-4-3 configuration is the maximum topology that satisfies this constraint on a 10BASE5/10BASE2 coax network.

**Valid assumptions:**
- 10 Mbps bit rate.
- Propagation velocity $v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in coaxial cable.
- Maximum repeater internal latency ≤ 25 bit-times per repeater.
- All segments conform to their standard maximum lengths (10BASE5: 500 m; 10BASE2: 185 m).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> *"Consider a 10BASE5 Ethernet LAN with 4 repeaters and 5 segments, each segment being 500 m long. Verify whether the 5-4-3 rule is satisfied with respect to the CSMA/CD slot time budget. Assume repeater latency = 25 bit-times per repeater."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute one-way propagation delay through all 5 segments.

$$ t_{prop} = \frac{5 \times 500}{2 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 12.5 \text{ µs} $$

**Step 2:** Compute the cumulative repeater latency (4 repeaters × 25 bit-times).

$$ t_{rep} = 4 \times 25 \times \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{100}{10^{7}} = 10 \text{ µs} $$

**Step 3:** Compute the round-trip delay.

$$ t_{RT} = 2 \cdot t_{prop} + t_{rep} = 2 \cdot 12.5 + 10 = 35 \text{ µs} $$

**Step 4:** Compare against slot time.

$$ t_{slot} = 51.2 \text{ µs} $$

**Step 5:** Conclusion.

Since $t_{RT} = 35 \text{ µs} < 51.2 \text{ µs} = t_{slot}$, the 5-4-3 rule is **satisfied** with a safety margin of $51.2 - 35 = 16.2$ µs. The network will correctly detect all collisions before the sender finishes transmitting the minimum 64-byte frame.

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> *"With a neat block diagram, explain the internal functioning of an Ethernet repeater. Why is it called a 'bit regenerator' rather than an 'amplifier'?"*

**Model Answer:**

A repeater is a **two-port Layer-1 device** with the following internal functional blocks:

1. **Receiver (Analog Front End):** Detects the incoming electrical signal from Segment A and amplifies it to a level suitable for sampling.
2. **Clock Recovery (PLL):** Extracts a clean local clock signal from the transitions in the incoming bit stream, eliminating jitter.
3. **Decision Circuit (Comparator):** Uses the recovered clock to sample the incoming waveform at the optimal instant and decides whether each bit is a 0 or 1.
4. **Waveform Regenerator:** Builds a **fresh, clean electrical pulse** representing the decided bit.
5. **Line Driver:** Boosts the regenerated pulse to the standard Ethernet voltage levels and transmits it onto Segment B.
6. **Collision Detector:** Detects simultaneous energy on both segments and forwards the collision-presence (SQE) signal to both sides.

It is called a **bit regenerator** rather than a **simple amplifier** because:
- A pure analog amplifier would simply scale up the noisy, jittered waveform — including all its noise and distortion.
- A repeater actually makes a **binary decision** on each bit, then creates a **brand-new waveform** from that decision. The output is therefore **noise-free and jitter-free**, regardless of how degraded the input was.

This regeneration is the key reason a chain of repeaters can extend an Ethernet network far beyond the natural attenuation limit of a single cable run.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
