> *"A Cat5e cable has an attenuation of 22 dB per 100 m at 100 MHz. If the transmitter sends a signal at +5 dBm and the receiver sensitivity is −30 dBm, compute the maximum cable length before a repeater is required. Assume connector losses total 4 dB."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total allowable attenuation budget.

$$ A_{max} = P_{tx} - P_{rx,min} - A_{connector} $$

$$ A_{max} = 5 - (-30) - 4 = 31 \text{ dB} $$

**Step 2:** Express attenuation as a function of length.

$$ A(L) = 22 \cdot \frac{L}{100} = 0.22 \cdot L $$

**Step 3:** Set attenuation equal to the budget and solve.

$$ 0.22 \cdot L_{max} = 31 $$

$$ L_{max} = \frac{31}{0.22} \approx 140.9 \text{ m} $$

**Step 4:** Engineering interpretation.

Any cable longer than **≈ 140 m** (after accounting for the 4 dB connector losses) would deliver a signal weaker than the receiver sensitivity, leading to excessive Bit Error Rate. A **repeater** must be placed at or before the 140 m mark to **regenerate** the signal back to its original +5 dBm level and reset the attenuation budget for the next segment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — Examiner's Pitfall Report:**
>
> 1. **Confusing hub with switch throughput** — Writing that "a 24-port 10 Mbps hub gives 240 Mbps" is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. Always clarify that the 10 Mbps is *shared*, not summed.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** in the 5-4-3 derivation — examiners insist on showing the unit conversions (ms, µs, ns). A correct number without units loses 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. **Calling a repeater a Layer-2 device** — it is **strictly Layer 1**. Any reference to MAC addresses or frames inside a repeater description is **factually wrong** and will be penalized.
> 4. **Stating that hubs "filter" or "isolate" traffic** — hubs cannot filter. This is the defining feature of a switch.
> 5. **Omitting the collision-domain explanation** when comparing hub vs. switch — examiners specifically look for the phrase "**single shared collision domain**".
> 6. **Misremembering the 5-4-3 numbers** — the correct order is **5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments**. Reversing these in a 14-mark answer is a serious error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — memorize before entering the exam hall:**

- Repeaters and Hubs are **OSI Layer 1 (Physical Layer)** devices.
- A **repeater** has **exactly 2 ports** and extends network reach by regenerating degraded signals bit-by-bit.
- A **hub** is a **multi-port repeater**; bits received on any one port are broadcast to all other ports.
- Hubs come in three flavors: **Passive** (no regeneration), **Active** (regenerates), and **Intelligent** (regenerates + diagnostics).
- Both devices share **one collision domain** and **one broadcast domain** across all ports.
- Aggregate hub bandwidth is **shared**, not summed — a 24-port 10 Mbps hub = 10 Mbps total.
- **5-4-3 Rule:** 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments (for 10BASE5/10BASE2 Ethernet).
- The **slot time** for 10BASE-T Ethernet is **51.2 µs** (512 bit-times at 10 Mbps).
- **Maximum one-way propagation delay** for slot time = **25.6 µs**.
- **Maximum theoretical diameter** of a 10BASE-T collision domain ≈ **5120 m** (≈ 2500 m in practice).
- Repeaters **regenerate** signals; they do not merely amplify — they make a binary decision and recreate a clean waveform.
- Repeaters and hubs are **transparent** to all upper-layer protocols — they do not interpret frames, MAC addresses, or IP packets.
- Hubs are **half-duplex only**; full-duplex requires switches.
- Modern networks have **phased out hubs** in favor of switches because switches provide dedicated bandwidth per port and isolate collision domains.
- A **bridge, switch, or router** is needed to segment a collision domain — **never** a repeater or hub.
- The **Cat5e attenuation figure** of 22 dB/100 m at 100 MHz is a standard benchmark for cable-run calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Media Overview. Performance Indicators - Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Media Overview. Performance Indicators - Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Transmission Media — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Medium** is the physical pathway (or the free-space channel) that physically carries the information-carrying electromagnetic signal from the **sender (source)** to the **receiver (destination)** in a data communication system.

In the OSI Reference Model, the Transmission Medium forms the **lowest sub-layer of the Physical Layer (Layer 1)**. It is responsible only for **bit propagation** — it does not interpret, route, or correct the signal. The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies transmission media into two principal domains:

| Domain | Alternate Name | Medium Type | Signal Nature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Guided Media** | Wired / Bounded | Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Optical Fiber | Confined to a physical cable |
| **Unguided Media** | Wireless / Unbounded | Air, Vacuum, Water | Propagated as free-space EM waves |

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Perspective (Forouzan):** A transmission medium can be thought of as a **"highway"** that connects two cities (the devices). Just as a highway's width, surface quality, and speed limit determine how many vehicles (bits) can travel per hour, a medium's physical and electromagnetic properties determine how fast and reliably data can be carried.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Pipeline" Model

Imagine you need to deliver water from a reservoir to a city:

- **Bandwidth** = the *diameter (width)* of the pipe → how much water can flow at once.
- **Throughput** = the *actual water quantity* that reaches the city per second (depends on pressure, friction, and blockages).
- **Latency / Delay** = the *travel time* of a single water droplet from source to destination.
- **Attenuation** = *friction loss* — the longer the pipe, the weaker the water pressure at the other end.
- **Noise** = *leakage and contamination* from neighbouring pipes or external sources.

In data communication, **electrons or photons replace water**, and the **pipe is replaced by copper, glass, or air**. This analogy will repeatedly help you visualize every performance indicator we discuss.

### 1.3 Why Performance Indicators Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an exam-friendly framing: the **Performance Indicators** (also called *Quality of Service (QoS) parameters* or *network metrics*) are the measurable quantities that allow an engineer to *evaluate, compare, and certify* a transmission medium before it is deployed in a LAN, MAN, WAN, or wireless link.

KTU 2024 expects you to define and differentiate the **five primary performance indicators**:
1. **Bandwidth** (B)
2. **Throughput** (T)
3. **Latency / Delay** (D)
4. **Jitter**
5. **Error Rate / Bit Error Rate (BER)**
6. **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**

All of these are ultimately constrained by the **physics of the medium**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Core Performance Indicators — Operational Logic

#### A) Bandwidth (B)
- **Definition (Digital):** The maximum number of bits that can be transmitted per second over a channel. Unit: **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Definition (Analog):** The range of frequencies that a medium can pass without significant attenuation. Unit: **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Why it matters:** Higher bandwidth → more data per unit time → faster communication.

#### B) Throughput (T)
- **Definition:** The *actual* number of bits successfully delivered per second.
- **Why it differs from Bandwidth:** Throughput $\le$ Bandwidth due to **protocol overhead, collisions, queuing delays, and retransmissions**.
- **Real-world example:** A 100 Mbps Ethernet link typically delivers a throughput of **60–80 Mbps** under practical load.

#### C) Latency / Delay (D)
- **Definition:** The total time taken for a single bit (or packet) to travel from source to destination.
- **Four constituents of delay:**

$$
D_{total} = D_{prop} + D_{trans} + D_{queue} + D_{proc}
$$

Where each term is explained in the formula sheet below.

#### D) Jitter
- **Definition:** The **variation in inter-packet arrival times**. If packets arrive at irregular intervals, jitter is high.
- **Critical in:** VoIP, live video streaming, real-time gaming. High jitter → choppy audio/video.

#### E) Bit Error Rate (BER)
- **Definition:** Ratio of bits received in error to the total bits transmitted.

$$
BER = \frac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{\text{Total bits received}}
$$

### 2.2 Physical Factors That Limit Bandwidth

A medium's bandwidth is fundamentally limited by three physical phenomena:

1. **Attenuation (α):** The progressive loss of signal power with distance. Measured in **dB/km**.
2. **Distortion:** Different frequency components of the signal travel at slightly different velocities → the signal "spreads out" and overlaps.
3. **Noise (Thermal, Induced, Crosstalk, Impulse):** Random unwanted energy added to the signal, reducing the effective signal-to-noise ratio.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **core cheat-sheet** for solving any numerical problem from this topic in the KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Special Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Propagation Delay** $D_{prop}$ | $D_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = link length (m), $v$ = propagation speed (m/s) | For copper: $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s; Fiber: $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s; Air: $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | **Transmission Delay** $D_{trans}$ | $D_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) | Time taken to push all bits of a packet onto the wire |
| 3 | **Total Nodal Delay** $D_{total}$ | $D_{prop} + D_{trans} + D_{queue} + D_{proc}$ | All terms in **seconds (s)** | Sum of all four delay components |
| 4 | **Bandwidth–Delay Product** $BDP$ | $BDP = R \times D_{prop}$ | $R$ in bps, $D_{prop}$ in s → units: **bits** | Represents the number of bits that "fill" the pipe |
| 5 | **Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless)** | $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $M$ = signal levels, $C$ in bps | Maximum data rate of a **noiseless** channel |
| 6 | **Shannon Capacity (Noisy)** | $C = B \log_2 (1 + S/N)$ | $B$ in Hz, $S/N$ = Signal-to-Noise ratio (unitless) | Absolute upper bound for a **noisy** channel |
| 7 | **SNR in Decibels** | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | $S/N$ is the linear ratio | Always positive for usable channels |
| 8 | **Attenuation** | $\alpha = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | $P_{in}, P_{out}$ in Watts or mW | Result in dB |
| 9 | **BER** | $BER = \dfrac{\text{Bits in Error}}{\text{Total Bits Received}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Often expressed as $10^{-k}$ |
| 10 | **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = 2 \times D_{prop} + 2 \times D_{trans}$ | All in seconds | Ignoring queueing & processing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction KTU Examiners Test:** *Bandwidth* (theoretical capacity) **$\neq$** *Throughput* (real delivery). A 1 Gbps link can never deliver 1 Gbps in practice.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Used to dimension *backbone fiber links* between cities.
- **Data Centers:** Bandwidth–Delay Product determines **TCP window sizing** for high-speed inter-rack links.
- **Satellite Communication:** Very high propagation delay (~270 ms GEO) → dictates special protocols.
- **Real-time Systems:** Jitter control is more critical than raw bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bandwidth vs Throughput Relationship

We can mathematically relate **theoretical bandwidth** ($R$) to **actual throughput** ($T$) by defining an *efficiency factor* $\eta$:

$$
\eta = \frac{T}{R}, \quad 0 < \eta \le 1
$$

**Step 1:** Consider a raw link bandwidth of $R = 100 \text{ Mbps}$.

**Step 2:** Protocol overhead (headers, ACKs, retransmissions) consumes $R_{oh} = 20 \text{ Mbps}$.

**Step 3:** Medium access contention consumes $R_{c} = 10 \text{ Mbps}$.

**Step 4:** Therefore, the effective payload throughput is:

$$
T = R - R_{oh} - R_{c}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting values:

$$
T = 100 - 20 - 10 = 70 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 6:** Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \frac{70}{100} = 0.70 = 70\%
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Four-Component Total Delay (Kurose–Ross Model)

The total nodal delay experienced by a packet at a single router is:

$$
D_{nodal} = D_{proc} + D_{queue} + D_{trans} + D_{prop}
$$

**Step 1 — Processing Delay ($D_{proc}$):**
Time taken to examine the packet header, check for bit-level errors, and decide the outgoing interface. Typically microseconds.

$$
D_{proc} \approx 1 \text{ to } 10 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Queuing Delay ($D_{queue}$):**
Time spent waiting in the buffer for the outgoing link. Depends on **traffic intensity** $I = \frac{La}{R}$:

$$
D_{queue} = \frac{I}{1 - I} \cdot \frac{L}{R}, \quad 0 \le I < 1
$$

If $I \to 1$, the queue grows unbounded.

**Step 3 — Transmission Delay ($D_{trans}$):**
Time to push all $L$ bits of the packet onto the link at rate $R$:

$$
D_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Propagation Delay ($D_{prop}$):**
Time for one bit to physically travel across the link of length $d$ at speed $v$:

$$
D_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}
$$

**Step 5 — Combined Result:**

$$
D_{nodal} = D_{proc} + D_{queue} + \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{v}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless Channel)

**Premise:** A noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz can be encoded with $M$ discrete signal levels.

**Step 1:** A noiseless channel can support at most $2B$ signal changes per second (Nyquist's sampling bound).

**Step 2:** If each signal change carries $\log_2 M$ bits, the bit rate $C$ becomes:

$$
C = 2B \log_2 M
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**
$B = 4000 \text{ Hz}$ (telephone line), $M = 4$ levels (2 bits per level):

$$
C = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 4 = 2 \times 4000 \times 2 = 16000 \text{ bps}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Shannon's Capacity Theorem (Noisy Channel)

**Premise:** A real channel has thermal and induced noise, characterized by the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (S/N)**.

**Step 1:** Shannon showed the maximum theoretical bit rate is:

$$
C = B \log_2 (1 + S/N)
$$

**Step 2:** To express $S/N$ in dB:

$$
(S/N)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**
$B = 4000 \text{ Hz}$, $SNR_{dB} = 30 \text{ dB}$.

Find linear $S/N$:

$$
10 \log_{10}(S/N) = 30 \Rightarrow \log_{10}(S/N) = 3 \Rightarrow S/N = 10^3 = 1000
$$

Compute capacity:

$$
C = 4000 \times \log_2 (1 + 1000) = 4000 \times \log_2 (1001)
$$

$$
C = 4000 \times 9.967 \approx 39870 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard answer for the KTU classic problem**: *“A channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz and SNR of 30 dB. Find the Shannon capacity.”*

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Bandwidth–Delay Product (BDP)

**Step 1:** Consider a link of bandwidth $R$ and one-way propagation delay $D_{prop}$.

**Step 2:** In time $D_{prop}$, the sender pushes exactly $R \times D_{prop}$ bits onto the wire **before the first bit arrives at the receiver**.

**Step 3:** This volume of bits is the "pipe capacity":

$$
BDP = R \times D_{prop} \quad \text{(bits)}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**
$R = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bps}$, $d = 200{,}000 \text{ km}$ (transatlantic cable), $v = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$.

$$
D_{prop} = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^8} = 1 \text{ s}
$$

$$
BDP = 10^9 \times 1 = 10^9 \text{ bits} = 1 \text{ Gbit}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This huge BDP is precisely why the **TCP window scale option** was introduced — to keep the pipe full!

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Formula?

| Problem Clue in Question | Applicable Theorem / Formula | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| *“noiseless channel”* or *“no noise”* | **Nyquist** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | No noise → no entropy limit |
| *“noisy channel”* with $S/N$ given | **Shannon** $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ | Real-world bound |
| *“how many bits can fit in the pipe”* | **BDP** $= R \times D_{prop}$ | Channel-fill volume |
| *“time for a packet to leave the sender”* | $D_{trans} = L/R$ | Push-onto-wire time |
| *“time for a bit to cross the wire”* | $D_{prop} = d/v$ | Travel time |
| *“actual data delivered per second”* | **Throughput** $T$ | Real measurement |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Transmission Media

```mermaid
graph TD
    TM[Transmission Media] --> GUIDED[Guided Media Wired]
    TM --> UNGUIDED[Unguided Media Wireless]

    GUIDED --> TP[Twisted Pair]
    GUIDED --> COAX[Coaxial Cable]
    GUIDED --> FO[Fiber Optic]

    TP --> UTP[Unshielded TP - UTP]
    TP --> STP[Shielded TP - STP]
    UTP --> CAT5[Cat 5e - 100 Mbps]
    UTP --> CAT6[Cat 6 - 1 Gbps]
    UTP --> CAT7[Cat 7 - 10 Gbps]

    COAX --> BASEC[Baseband Coax]
    COAX --> BROADC[Broadband Coax]

    FO --> SMF[Single Mode Fiber]
    FO --> MMF[Multi Mode Fiber]
    SMF --> LONG[Long Haul - 100 km]
    MMF --> SHORT[Short Haul - 2 km]

    UNGUIDED --> RF[Radio Frequency 3 kHz to 300 GHz]
    UNGUIDED --> IR[Infrared Optical]
    UNGUIDED --> SAT[Satellite Microwave]

    RF --> VHF[VHF UHF Terrestrial]
    RF --> MICROW[Line of Sight Microwave]
    SAT --> LEO[LEO Orbit 500 to 2000 km]
    SAT --> GEO[GEO Orbit 35786 km]
```

### 4.2 The Five Performance Indicators — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[Source] -->|Bits| A[Encoding Modulation]
    A -->|Signal| M[Transmission Medium]
    M -->|Attenuated Noisy Signal| D[Decoding]
    D -->|Recovered Bits| R[Receiver]

    subgraph PERF[Performance Indicators Measured at Receiver]
        B[Bandwidth Hz or bps]
        T[Throughput bps]
        L[Latency seconds]
        J[Jitter ms]
        E[BER bit error rate]
    end

    M -.-> B
    M -.-> T
    M -.-> L
    M -.-> J
    M -.-> E
```

### 4.3 Anatomy of a Data Packet Travelling Through a Link (Total Delay Decomposition)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Sender
    participant WIRE as Physical Medium
    participant RTR as Router
    participant RCV as Receiver

    SRC->>SRC: Processing Delay D_proc
    SRC->>SRC: Queuing Delay D_queue
    SRC->>WIRE: Transmission Delay D_trans = L / R
    WIRE->>RTR: Propagation Delay D_prop = d / v
    RTR->>RCV: Repeat nodal delays at every hop
    RCV-->>SRC: ACK returns RTT = 2 x D_prop + 2 x D_trans
```

### 4.4 Bandwidth vs Throughput — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    BW[Channel Bandwidth Theoretical Maximum R] -->|minus| OH[Protocol Overhead Headers ACKs]
    BW -->|minus| COL[Collisions Contention]
    BW -->|minus| ERR[Retransmissions and Errors]
    OH --> TH[Actual Throughput T]
    COL --> TH
    ERR --> TH
    TH -->|Always less than or equal to| BW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** *Bandwidth* is the *theoretical ceiling*; *Throughput* is the *practical floor-to-ceiling reality*. In the KTU valuation, you will lose 1 mark if you use these two terms interchangeably.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**  
**Define transmission media. Differentiate between guided and unguided media with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A *transmission medium* is the physical path or free-space channel over which electromagnetic signals carrying data propagate from a source to a destination in a communication system.

| Feature | Guided Media | Unguided Media |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Path | Confined to a physical cable | Free-space propagation |
| Examples | Twisted Pair, Coaxial Cable, Optical Fiber | Radio waves, Microwave, Infrared, Satellite |
| Security | Higher (physically enclosed) | Lower (interceptable) |
| Attenuation | Lower and predictable | Higher and weather-sensitive |
| Cost | Moderate to High (cabling) | Lower initial cost |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for table with examples and key differences)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**  
**Briefly explain the four components of total delay in a packet-switched network.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The total end-to-end delay is:

$$
D_{total} = D_{proc} + D_{queue} + D_{trans} + D_{prop}
$$

1. **Processing Delay ($D_{proc}$):** Time for a router to examine the packet header and determine the outgoing link (~µs).
2. **Queuing Delay ($D_{queue}$):** Time waiting in the router buffer for the link to become free (variable, depends on congestion).
3. **Transmission Delay ($D_{trans}$):** Time to push all $L$ bits of the packet onto the link of rate $R$, given by $D_{trans} = L / R$.
4. **Propagation Delay ($D_{prop}$):** Time for one bit to physically travel across the link of length $d$ at speed $v$, given by $D_{prop} = d / v$.

*(1 mark for naming + 1 mark for formulas + 1 mark for explanation)*

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Modified]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the classification of transmission media with neat block diagrams. Compare the three major guided media in terms of bandwidth, attenuation, EMI susceptibility, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A link has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. The signal-to-noise ratio at the receiver is 40 dB.  
(i) Find the Shannon capacity of this channel.  
(ii) If the channel is noiseless and uses 16 signal levels, find the Nyquist bit rate.  
(iii) Compare the two results and justify the practical choice. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Classification of Transmission Media (7 marks)**

Transmission media are classified into two broad categories:

1. **Guided (Wired / Bounded) Media** — Signal propagates within a solid physical conductor.

   i. **Twisted Pair Cable (TP):**  
      - *Construction:* Two insulated copper wires twisted helically.  
      - *Types:* UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair), STP (Shielded Twisted Pair).  
      - *Bandwidth:* Up to 10 Gbps (Cat 7 / Cat 8).  
      - *Attenuation:* High at high frequencies; max segment ~100 m.  
      - *EMI Susceptibility:* High (UTP), Reduced (STP).  
      - *Applications:* LANs, telephone lines, Ethernet (10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T).

   ii. **Coaxial Cable:**  
      - *Construction:* Central copper conductor, dielectric insulator, metallic shield, outer jacket.  
      - *Bandwidth:* Up to ~1 GHz; typical data rates 10–100 Mbps.  
      - *Attenuation:* Lower than TP; max segment ~500 m.  
      - *EMI Susceptibility:* Low (due to shielding).  
      - *Applications:* Cable TV, broadband internet, early Ethernet (10BASE-2, 10BASE-5).

   iii. **Optical Fiber Cable:**  
      - *Construction:* Glass/plastic core, cladding, buffer, jacket. Light propagates by **Total Internal Reflection**.  
      - *Bandwidth:* Up to **Tbps** (terabits/sec).  
      - *Attenuation:* Very low (~0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm).  
      - *EMI Susceptibility:* **Zero** (immune to EMI).  
      - *Applications:* Backbone networks, undersea cables, FTTH, data centers.

| Parameter | Twisted Pair | Coaxial | Fiber Optic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Max Bandwidth | ~1 GHz | ~1 GHz | >50 THz |
| Attenuation | High | Moderate | Very Low |
| EMI Susceptibility | High | Low | None |
| Typical Use | LAN, Telephony | CATV, Legacy LAN | Backbone, FTTH |

*(3 marks for classification + 2 marks for diagrams/descriptions + 2 marks for comparison table)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $B = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000 \text{ Hz}$, $SNR_{dB} = 40 \text{ dB}$

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear ratio:**

$$
SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N) = 40
$$

$$
\log_{10}(S/N) = 4 \Rightarrow S/N = 10^4 = 10000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's Theorem:**

$$
C = B \log_2 (1 + S/N) = 4000 \times \log_2 (1 + 10000) = 4000 \times \log_2 (10001)
$$

$$
C = 4000 \times 13.288 \approx 53152 \text{ bps} \approx 53.15 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Nyquist's Theorem (Noiseless, $M = 16$):**

$$
C_{Nyquist} = 2B \log_2 M = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 16 = 8000 \times 4 = 32000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and Justification:**

- The **Shannon capacity (53.15 kbps)** is the absolute maximum achievable *in the presence of noise*.
- The **Nyquist rate (32 kbps)** is achievable *only if the channel is truly noiseless* and the receiver can distinguish 16 voltage levels.
- In practice, **Shannon's bound governs** because all real channels have thermal and induced noise.
- Modems on 4 kHz telephone channels are designed to operate close to 56 kbps, demonstrating that real systems approach but never exceed Shannon's limit.

*(2 marks for Shannon + 2 marks for Nyquist + 2 marks for comparison + 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

**(a)** Define Bandwidth and Throughput. A network link has a theoretical bandwidth of 1 Gbps. Due to protocol overhead (15%) and collision-related retransmissions (5%), calculate the effective throughput. List two real-world scenarios where throughput is significantly less than bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define Bit Error Rate (BER) and Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). The received signal power is 200 µW and the noise power is 8 µW on a 6 kHz channel.  
(i) Calculate the SNR in dB.  
(ii) Calculate the Shannon capacity.  
(iii) If 100 million bits are transmitted and 50 bits are received in error, compute the BER. Comment on link quality. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Bandwidth vs Throughput (7 marks)**

- **Bandwidth:** Theoretical maximum rate at which data can be transmitted over a link, measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Throughput:** Actual rate of *successful* data delivery, accounting for overhead, errors, and contention.

**Calculation:**

- Theoretical Bandwidth: $R = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bps}$
- Overhead Loss: $15\% \Rightarrow 0.15 \times 10^9 = 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ bps}$
- Retransmission Loss: $5\% \Rightarrow 0.05 \times 10^9 = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ bps}$
- Net Throughput:

$$
T = 10^9 - 1.5 \times 10^8 - 5 \times 10^7 = 10^9 - 2 \times 10^8 = 8 \times 10^8 \text{ bps} = 800 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Real-world scenarios where throughput $\ll$ bandwidth:**
1. **Wi-Fi networks** — CSMA/CA contention and half-duplex operation reduce a 600 Mbps 802.11n link to ~200 Mbps throughput.
2. **Satellite internet (GEO)** — Long propagation delay (~540 ms RTT) causes TCP to keep its window small, limiting throughput even on a high-bandwidth link.

*(2 marks definitions + 2 marks calculation + 2 marks scenarios + 1 mark justification)*

---

**(b) BER and SNR Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$P_S = 200 \,\mu W$, $P_N = 8 \,\mu W$, $B = 6 \text{ kHz} = 6000 \text{ Hz}$
Total bits transmitted = $10^8$, bits in error = $50$.

**(i) SNR in dB:**

$$
S/N = \frac{200}{8} = 25
$$

$$
SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(25) = 10 \times 1.3979 \approx 13.98 \text{ dB}
$$

**(ii) Shannon Capacity:**

$$
C = B \log_2 (1 + S/N) = 6000 \times \log_2(1 + 25) = 6000 \times \log_2(26)
$$

$$
C = 6000 \times 4.7004 \approx 28202 \text{ bps} \approx 28.2 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(iii) BER and Comment:**

$$
BER = \frac{50}{10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Link Quality Comment:** A BER of $5 \times 10^{-7}$ is considered **excellent** for digital communication. For reference:
- Optical fiber typically achieves BER $\le 10^{-12}$.
- Copper Ethernet links operate at BER $\le 10^{-10}$.
- Wireless links typically have BER $\approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$.

Hence, the link in this problem performs comparably to a high-quality copper link and is well within acceptable limits for most data applications.

*(2 marks SNR + 2 marks Shannon + 2 marks BER + 1 mark quality justification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing units of bandwidth:** Analog bandwidth is in **Hz**; Digital bandwidth is in **bps**. Mixing them up costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear ratio** before applying Shannon's formula. Shannon uses the *linear* $S/N$, not $S/N_{dB}$.
> 3. **Using the wrong base in logarithms:** All information-theory formulas use $\log_2$ (base 2), not $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$.
> 4. **Calling Bandwidth = Throughput:** This is the most common 1-mark deduction. Always state *“Bandwidth is theoretical, throughput is actual.”*
> 5. **Ignoring the $2B$ factor in Nyquist:** Many students write $C = B \log_2 M$ — this is incomplete; the factor **2** is the Nyquist sampling rate.
> 6. **Using speed of light in copper:** Propagation speed in copper is $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (about 67% of $c$), not $3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Transmission Media** is the physical (or wireless) path that carries the signal — it sits at the **bottom of Layer 1 (OSI)**.
- **Guided media** = bounded (twisted pair, coaxial, fiber). **Unguided media** = unbounded (radio, microwave, IR, satellite).
- **Five primary performance indicators:** Bandwidth, Throughput, Latency, Jitter, BER (and SNR as a supporting parameter).
- **Bandwidth** is *theoretical* (max possible); **Throughput** is *actual* (what you get). Always $T \le R$.
- **Total delay** has **four** components: $D_{proc}$, $D_{queue}$, $D_{trans}$, $D_{prop}$.
- **Propagation delay** $D_{prop} = d / v$ depends on **distance and medium**.
- **Transmission delay** $D_{trans} = L / R$ depends on **packet size and link rate**.
- **Bandwidth–Delay Product (BDP)** = $R \times D_{prop}$ = the *number of bits that fill the pipe*.
- **Nyquist (Noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ — depends on **number of signal levels**.
- **Shannon (Noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — depends on **SNR**, not on $M$.
- **Shannon's bound is absolute** — no coding scheme can exceed it. Nyquist's bound assumes a *perfect, noiseless* channel.
- **SNR in dB:** $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ — must convert to linear ratio before using in Shannon.
- **BER** of $10^{-6}$ is acceptable; $10^{-9}$ or better is required for high-speed fiber.
- **Attenuation** grows with distance — optical amplifiers (EDFA) and repeaters are used to compensate.
- **Speed of signal:** $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper/fiber; $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum/air.
- **Jitter** matters most for real-time traffic (VoIP, video conferencing, online gaming).
- **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** is critical for TCP — it sets the minimum ACK waiting time.
- KTU 2024 favorite 14-mark combination: *(Classification + Nyquist/Shannon numerical)* or *(Performance Indicators + Delay calculation)*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Throughput
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Throughput

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Throughput (Kurose & Ross / Tanenbaum Definition):**
> *Throughput* is the **rate (in bits/second)** at which data bits are *successfully delivered* from a sender to a receiver across a communication path in an actual networking scenario. It is the **useful, effective data delivery rate** at the application layer, accounting for protocol overhead, collisions, retransmissions, and bottleneck limitations.

Mathematically, the instantaneous throughput over a time interval $[0, t]$ is expressed as:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Amount of data (bits) successfully delivered}}{\text{Time taken (seconds)}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{bits}}{\text{second}} \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT751 – Module 1):** Throughput is taught alongside *Bandwidth, Latency, and Delay*. The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish **Bandwidth (capacity)** from **Throughput (actual delivered rate)** — a classic two-mark differentiator.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Pipe Model

Imagine a **wide water pipeline** (this is your *bandwidth* — the maximum theoretical width of the pipe). Water (data) is being pumped through it.

- **Bandwidth** = The **maximum width** the pipe *could* theoretically allow.
- **Throughput** = The **actual volume of water** that successfully comes out the other end per second.

Why doesn't throughput equal bandwidth? Because of:
- **Friction** (analogous to *transmission errors and retransmissions*)
- **Leaks** (analogous to *packet header overhead*)
- **Partial blockages** (analogous to *congestion and bottlenecks*)
- **Sharing** with neighbors (analogous to *multiple users sharing a link*)

So even if the pipe is huge, you may only get a small stream of water out the other end — that stream is the **throughput**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU-Standard Units)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput | $\rho$ or $T$ | **bits per second (bps)** |
| Bandwidth | $B$ or $R$ | **bits per second (bps)** |
| File size | $F$ | **bits** |
| Transmission delay | $d_{trans}$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Number of users | $N$ | dimensionless |
| Transmission probability | $p$ | dimensionless ($0 \le p \le 1$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering examples of throughput measurement:**
> - **Internet Speed Test:** Reports *throughput* (actual rate), not theoretical *bandwidth* of the fiber link.
> - **5G Network:** Advertised *bandwidth* may be 1 Gbps, but real-world *throughput* in a crowded stadium may be only ~50 Mbps.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bandwidth vs Throughput — The Core Distinction

This is the **single most important conceptual comparison** for KTU exams. Examiners almost always ask this as a direct 2-mark or 3-mark question.

| Aspect | Bandwidth | Throughput |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Maximum *capacity* of a link | Actual *delivered* data rate |
| **Nature** | Theoretical upper bound | Practical, measured value |
| **Depends on** | Physical medium + technology | Congestion, overhead, errors, sharing |
| **Symbolic value** | $B$ (fixed for a given link) | $\rho$ or $T$ (varies dynamically) |
| **Relation** | $\rho \le B$ always holds | — |
| **Analogy** | Width of a pipe | Actual water flowing out per second |

### 2.2 Factors Affecting Throughput (The "Why" Behind Reduced Throughput)

The throughput of a network path is degraded by the following factors — these are *high-yield exam topics*:

1. **Protocol Overhead** — Every packet carries headers (e.g., 20-byte TCP + 20-byte IP headers). If payload is $L$ bits and header is $H$ bits, only $\frac{L}{L+H}$ fraction is *useful data*.

2. **Multiple Access / Contention** — When $N$ users share a link of bandwidth $B$, the available per-user throughput drops. With pure time-sharing: $\rho_{\text{per user}} = \frac{B}{N}$.

3. **Bottleneck Link** — End-to-end throughput is governed by the **slowest link in the path**:
$$
\rho_{\text{end-to-end}} = \min(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_k)
$$

4. **Retransmissions due to Errors** — Bit Error Rate (BER) forces retransmissions, effectively reducing delivered throughput.

5. **Propagation & Queueing Delays** — Do not reduce throughput directly, but increase the **time** to deliver each bit, thus lowering the *rate*.

6. **Network Congestion** — Routers drop packets, triggering TCP-style congestion control that throttles the sender's rate.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **core formula inventory** for every Throughput problem KTU can ask. Memorize these.

| # | Scenario | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic file transfer | $\rho = \dfrac{F}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | $F$ = file size, $T$ = total time |
| 2 | Single link, single user | $\rho = \dfrac{L}{L+H} \cdot B$ | Header overhead considered |
| 3 | End-to-end (multi-hop) | $\rho = \min(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_k)$ | **Bottleneck** determines it |
| 4 | $N$ users sharing link bandwidth $B$ equally | $\rho_{\text{each}} = \dfrac{B}{N}$ | Idealized time-division |
| 5 | $N$ users, each transmits with prob. $p$ | $\rho = N \cdot p \cdot r$ (expected offered load) | $r$ = rate when transmitting |
| 6 | Packet transmission delay | $d_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length, $R$ = link rate |
| 7 | Circuit switching throughput | $\rho_{\text{cs}} = \dfrac{\text{User data rate}}{\text{Link rate}}$ | Dedicated path, no contention |
| 8 | Packet switching throughput | $\rho_{\text{ps}} = \dfrac{\text{Delivered packets}}{\text{Time}}$ | Varies with load |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The throughput concept is **mission-critical** in:

- **Data Center Engineering** — Engineers measure *bisection bandwidth throughput* to verify switch fabric performance.
- **Telecommunications (5G/6G)** — Network operators guarantee a *minimum sustained throughput* per user in SLAs.
- **Storage Networks (SAN/NAS)** — Disk-to-disk backup throughput determines backup window duration.
- **Content Delivery Networks (CDN)** — Throughput optimization via edge caching directly impacts user experience (e.g., Netflix, YouTube streaming).
- **Industrial IoT** — Time-sensitive control loops require deterministic minimum throughput guarantees.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throughput with Header Overhead

**Problem Setup:** A link has bandwidth $B = 10$ Mbps. Each packet carries a payload of $L$ bits and a header of $H$ bits. The header does *not* contribute useful application data. Find the maximum application-layer throughput.

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

> **Step 1 — Identify total bits transmitted per packet.**
> A single packet occupies the link for a duration equal to its total transmission time. The total bits pushed onto the wire per packet:
$$
\text{Total bits per packet} = L + H
$$

> **Step 2 — Identify useful (application) bits per packet.**
> Only the payload $L$ is useful data delivered to the application layer. The header $H$ is overhead consumed by protocol layers.
$$
\text{Useful bits per packet} = L
$$

> **Step 3 — Compute the efficiency factor (fraction of useful data).**
$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Useful bits}}{\text{Total bits}} = \frac{L}{L+H}
$$

> **Step 4 — Multiply by raw link bandwidth to get effective throughput.**
$$
\boxed{\;\rho = \eta \cdot B = \frac{L}{L+H} \cdot B\;}
$$

**Example Numerical Evaluation:** If $B = 10$ Mbps, $L = 8000$ bits, $H = 2000$ bits:

$$
\eta = \frac{8000}{8000 + 2000} = \frac{8000}{10000} = 0.8
$$

$$
\rho = 0.8 \times 10 \text{ Mbps} = 8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> So out of the 10 Mbps raw bandwidth, only **8 Mbps** is actual application throughput.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End Throughput with Bottleneck Identification

**Problem Setup:** A client downloads a file from a server through 3 links:
- Link 1 (Client → ISP Router): $B_1 = 100$ Mbps
- Link 2 (ISP Router → Backbone): $B_2 = 20$ Mbps
- Link 3 (Backbone → Server): $B_3 = 50$ Mbps

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

> **Step 1 — Apply the bottleneck principle.**
> Data must traverse *all* three links sequentially. The data cannot flow out of any link faster than that link's capacity. The slowest link **strangles** the entire flow — like the narrowest section of a pipe determining total flow.
$$
\rho_{\text{end-to-end}} = \min(B_1, B_2, B_3)
$$

> **Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**
$$
\rho_{\text{end-to-end}} = \min(100, 20, 50) \text{ Mbps}
$$

> **Step 3 — Identify the minimum and the bottleneck link.**
$$
\boxed{\;\rho_{\text{end-to-end}} = 20 \text{ Mbps, with Link 2 as the bottleneck.}\;}
$$

> **Step 4 — Compute file transfer time for $F = 100$ Mbit file.**
$$
T = \frac{F}{\rho} = \frac{100 \text{ Mbit}}{20 \text{ Mbps}} = 5 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Throughput with $N$ Users Sharing a Link

**Problem Setup:** A shared link has bandwidth $B = 100$ Mbps. There are $N = 10$ users. Each user is active (transmitting) with probability $p = 0.2$, and when active, transmits at rate $r = 5$ Mbps. Find the expected offered load and the resulting throughput.

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

> **Step 1 — Expected fraction of users active at any instant.**
> Since each user is independent and active with probability $p$, the expected number of simultaneously active users is:
$$
N_{\text{active}} = N \cdot p
$$

> **Step 2 — Expected aggregate offered load.**
$$
L_{\text{offered}} = N_{\text{active}} \cdot r = N \cdot p \cdot r
$$

> **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$
L_{\text{offered}} = 10 \times 0.2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> **Step 4 — Compare with link capacity.**
> Since $L_{\text{offered}} = 10$ Mbps $< B = 100$ Mbps, the link is **not congested**. Therefore, the throughput equals the offered load:
$$
\boxed{\;\rho = 10 \text{ Mbps}\;}
$$

> **What if the offered load exceeds B?** If $N \cdot p \cdot r > B$, congestion occurs, packets queue, get dropped, and effective throughput saturates near $B$. This is a classic KTU counter-question.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Scenarios

| Scenario | Raw Bandwidth | Effective Throughput | Efficiency $\rho / B$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 Mbps link, 8000b payload, 2000b header | 10 Mbps | **8 Mbps** | 80% |
| 3-hop path, bottleneck at 20 Mbps | 100/20/50 Mbps | **20 Mbps** | 20% (of best link) |
| 10 users sharing 100 Mbps link, $p=0.2$ | 100 Mbps | **10 Mbps** (expected) | 10% |
| Pure error-free dedicated link, no overhead | 10 Mbps | **10 Mbps** | 100% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The Analytical Matrix above is *exactly* the kind of comparison a 7-mark sub-question in Part B will ask you to produce. Practice writing it cleanly in your answer sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End Throughput with Bottleneck

The diagram below maps the **client-to-server file transfer path** through three links of different capacities, with the **bottleneck link** visually identified.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CLIENT] -->|LINK 1: 100 Mbps| B[ROUTER A]
    B -->|LINK 2: 20 Mbps BOTTLENECK| C[ROUTER B]
    C -->|LINK 3: 50 Mbps| D[SERVER]

    E[BANDWIDTH INPUT B1 100 Mbps] --> A
    F[BANDWIDTH INPUT B2 20 Mbps] --> B
    G[BANDWIDTH INPUT B3 50 Mbps] --> C
    H[FILE SIZE F bits] --> D

    I[END TO END THROUGHPUT = MIN B1 B2 B3 = 20 Mbps] --> B

    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style I fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- The **yellow-highlighted router** is the bottleneck node.
- The **red callout** states the end-to-end throughput is governed by the minimum of all link rates.
- The throughput equals the **narrowest pipe's capacity**, not the average.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Throughput Computation Pipeline

The diagram below shows the **functional pipeline** of how raw bandwidth $B$ is reduced to actual throughput $\rho$ through various loss stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[RAW LINK BANDWIDTH B bps] --> Q[STEP 1: HEADER OVERHEAD]
    Q --> R[STEP 2: MULTIPLE ACCESS CONTENTION]
    R --> S[STEP 3: TRANSMISSION ERRORS AND RETX]
    S --> T[STEP 4: BOTTLENECK LIMITATION]
    T --> U[STEP 5: CONGESTION CONTROL THROTTLING]
    U --> V[FINAL EFFECTIVE THROUGHPUT RHO bps]

    P --> P1[Example: 100 Mbps]
    Q --> Q1[Multiply by L divided by L plus H]
    R --> R1[Divide by N users, factor p]
    S --> S1[Multiply by 1 minus BER raised to L]
    T --> T1[Take minimum of B1 B2 B3]
    U --> U1[Apply TCP AIMD backoff]
    V --> V1[Example: 8 Mbps]

    style P fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#FFF
    style V fill:#2ECC71,stroke:#196F3D,color:#FFF
    style P1 fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#000
    style V1 fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#196F3D,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide for the Pipeline:**
- Each step **degrades** the raw bandwidth.
- The final throughput $\rho$ is the *net result after all losses*.
- In an ideal link (no overhead, no errors, single user, no congestion), $\rho = B$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between **Bandwidth** and **Throughput** in a computer network. Why can throughput never exceed bandwidth?*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bandwidth | Throughput |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Maximum *capacity* of a link | Actual *delivered* data rate |
| Type | Theoretical property | Measured, real-world rate |
| Unit | bps | bps |

**Why $\rho \le B$:** Bandwidth is the physical upper limit imposed by the medium. Throughput is the rate at which *useful* application data actually arrives at the receiver after accounting for header overhead, collisions, retransmissions, and bottleneck constraints. Since these factors can only *reduce* the delivered data, throughput can never exceed bandwidth. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define **end-to-end throughput**. What is the **bottleneck link** in a multi-hop path?*

**Model Answer:**

**End-to-end throughput** is the rate at which data is successfully delivered from source to destination across the entire network path, including all intermediate links and routers.

**Bottleneck link** is the link with the *lowest* bandwidth in the multi-hop path. It limits the end-to-end throughput because data cannot flow through the path any faster than the slowest link allows. Mathematically:
$$
\rho_{\text{end-to-end}} = \min(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_k)
$$
**[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students commonly lose marks by **confusing bandwidth and throughput** in the same problem — clearly state which is which.
> - Do NOT forget to mention the **header overhead factor** $\frac{L}{L+H}$ in overhead-based questions. This is a silent killer where 2–3 marks are routinely lost.
> - In bottleneck questions, students often pick the *first* link or the *last* link as the bottleneck — always show the **min() computation explicitly**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between *bandwidth* and *throughput* in a computer network. Derive the expression for the effective throughput when a packet of size $L$ bits with a header of $H$ bits is transmitted over a link of bandwidth $B$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 5 Mbps link is shared by 20 users using pure time-division multiplexing. Each user is active 10% of the time. Calculate the throughput per user. If 5 users transmit simultaneously at full rate, what happens? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **Step 1 — Define bandwidth and throughput.**
> - *Bandwidth* $B$: theoretical maximum data rate a link can support.
> - *Throughput* $\rho$: actual delivered data rate observed at the receiver.

> **Step 2 — Set up the packet structure.**
> A packet of $L$ payload bits + $H$ header bits is sent over the link. Total bits on the wire per packet:
$$
\text{Total bits} = L + H
$$

> **Step 3 — Fraction that is useful application data.**
$$
\eta = \frac{L}{L + H}
$$

> **Step 4 — Multiply by raw bandwidth to get effective throughput.**
$$
\boxed{\;\rho = \frac{L}{L + H} \cdot B\;}
$$

> **Step 5 — Interpretation.** The fraction $\frac{H}{L+H}$ represents pure protocol overhead that *consumes* bandwidth but delivers no useful data. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

> **Step 1 — Per-user share under TDM (Time Division Multiplexing).**
> 20 users share 5 Mbps link equally:
$$
B_{\text{per slot}} = \frac{5 \text{ Mbps}}{20} = 0.25 \text{ Mbps} = 250 \text{ kbps}
$$

> **Step 2 — Each user is active only 10% of the time.**
> Effective throughput per user:
$$
\boxed{\;\rho_{\text{user}} = 0.10 \times 0.25 \text{ Mbps} = 0.025 \text{ Mbps} = 25 \text{ kbps}\;}
$$

> **Step 3 — What if 5 users transmit simultaneously at full rate?**
> 5 users × 0.25 Mbps = 1.25 Mbps < 5 Mbps link capacity.
> No congestion. Each gets its full 0.25 Mbps. **No packet loss.**

> **Step 4 — However**, if 25 or more users try to transmit simultaneously, aggregate demand exceeds 5 Mbps. **Congestion occurs**, queue builds up at the multiplexer, and packets are dropped, reducing effective throughput. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of *bottleneck link* in a multi-hop network path with a neat diagram. A file of size 4 MB is to be transferred across 4 links of bandwidths 8 Mbps, 4 Mbps, 6 Mbps, and 2 Mbps. Find the end-to-end throughput and the total transfer time. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Two users are sharing a 1 Mbps link using packet switching. Each user generates 500 kbps when active but is active only 30% of the time. Calculate the offered load, determine whether congestion occurs, and find the throughput. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **Step 1 — Define bottleneck link.**
> In a multi-hop path, the link with the smallest bandwidth acts as the bottleneck, limiting the entire end-to-end flow.

> **Step 2 — Identify link bandwidths.**
> $B_1 = 8$ Mbps, $B_2 = 4$ Mbps, $B_3 = 6$ Mbps, $B_4 = 2$ Mbps.

> **Step 3 — Compute minimum (bottleneck).**
$$
\rho_{\text{e2e}} = \min(8, 4, 6, 2) = 2 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> **Step 4 — Convert file size to bits.**
> $F = 4 \text{ MB} = 4 \times 8 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 32 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ Mbits}$.

> **Step 5 — Compute total transfer time.**
$$
\boxed{\;T = \frac{F}{\rho} = \frac{32 \text{ Mbits}}{2 \text{ Mbps}} = 16 \text{ seconds}\;}
$$

> **Step 6 — Note:** Even though Link 1 is 8 Mbps, the file takes 16 seconds because the slowest link (Link 4, 2 Mbps) governs. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

> **Step 1 — Compute expected aggregate offered load.**
> Each user is active 30% of the time at 500 kbps.
> Expected load per user = $0.30 \times 500 = 150$ kbps.
> For 2 users:
$$
L_{\text{offered}} = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ kbps}
$$

> **Step 2 — Compare with link capacity.**
> $L_{\text{offered}} = 300$ kbps $< B = 1000$ kbps = 1 Mbps.
> **No congestion** — the link can handle the offered load.

> **Step 3 — Compute throughput.**
> When offered load is below capacity, throughput = offered load:
$$
\boxed{\;\rho = 300 \text{ kbps}\;}
$$

> **Step 4 — Verification of bandwidth utilization.**
> Link utilization = $300/1000 = 30\%$. The link is underutilized. **[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Throughput (Module 1)**

- ⭐ **Throughput** = *actual* data delivery rate (bps), not theoretical capacity.
- ⭐ **Bandwidth** = *maximum* link capacity. Always $\rho \le B$.
- ⭐ **Bottleneck** = slowest link in a multi-hop path. End-to-end throughput = $\min(B_1, \ldots, B_k)$.
- ⭐ **Header overhead** reduces throughput by factor $\frac{L}{L+H}$.
- ⭐ For $N$ users sharing a link: expected offered load $= N \cdot p \cdot r$.
- ⭐ If offered load **exceeds** link capacity $\Rightarrow$ **congestion** $\Rightarrow$ throughput saturates near $B$.
- ⭐ **File transfer time** $T = F / \rho$ where $F$ is in bits and $\rho$ is in bps.
- ⭐ KTU exams frequently test: (1) bandwidth vs throughput distinction, (2) bottleneck computation, (3) header overhead, (4) multi-user sharing.
- ⭐ Units: throughput is always reported in **bits per second (bps)**, with kilo/mega/giga prefixes.
- ⭐ Remember: $1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps, $1$ MB $= 8 \times 10^6$ bits. This conversion is *the single most common source of silly mistakes* in KTU numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Latency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Latency** (also called **End-to-End Delay**) is the total time taken for an entire message or data packet to travel from the source node to the destination node across a network. Mathematically, it is the sum of all individual delays encountered at every node (router/switch) and every link along the transmission path.

**Units of Measurement:**
- The standard SI unit is **seconds (s)**, but in practical networking, latency is usually expressed in **milliseconds (ms)** or **microseconds (μs)**.
- The standard metric symbol used in KTU textbooks is **L** or **d_end-to-end**.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Highway Journey

Imagine you are sending a parcel via a delivery truck across multiple cities:

| Network Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Transmission Delay** | Time taken to **load the parcel onto the truck** at each city warehouse |
| **Propagation Delay** | Time taken for the **truck to physically drive** from one city to the next |
| **Processing Delay** | Time the **warehouse manager takes to check the address** and decide the next route |
| **Queuing Delay** | Time the **truck spends waiting in a traffic jam** at a toll plaza |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024)**
> Latency is a **fundamental performance metric** of the network layer. KTU Module 1 explicitly requires students to compute latency for both single-hop and multi-hop (end-to-end) scenarios using the four-delay decomposition model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Four Components of Nodal Delay

When a packet arrives at a router, it experiences **four sequential delays** before being forwarded:

### 1️⃣ Processing Delay (d_proc)
- **What:** Time the router's CPU takes to examine the packet header, check for bit-level errors, and determine the outgoing link via routing table lookup.
- **Range:** Typically **microseconds (μs)** in modern routers.
- **Depends on:** Router hardware speed, routing algorithm complexity.

### 2️⃣ Queuing Delay (d_queue)
- **What:** Time the packet waits in the **router's buffer (queue)** before it can be transmitted onto the outgoing link.
- **Range:** Highly variable — from **0 ms to several seconds** under congestion.
- **Depends on:** Traffic intensity (ρ = La/R), number of packets already in queue.

### 3️⃣ Transmission Delay (d_trans)
- **What:** Time required to **push all the bits of the packet onto the physical link**. It is the time between the first bit and the last bit leaving the router.
- **Depends on:** Packet size (L) and link bandwidth (R).

### 4️⃣ Propagation Delay (d_prop)
- **What:** Time taken for a single bit to **physically travel** from one end of the link to the other through the transmission medium.
- **Depends on:** Physical distance (d) and propagation speed (s) of the medium.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Delay Type | Formula | Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Processing Delay | $d_{proc}$ | Router-specific | **μs** to **ms** |
| 2 | Queuing Delay | $d_{queue}$ | Traffic dependent | **0** to **ms** |
| 3 | Transmission Delay | $d_{trans} = L / R$ | $L$ = packet length, $R$ = bandwidth | **ms** |
| 4 | Propagation Delay | $d_{prop} = d / s$ | $d$ = distance, $s$ = speed | **ms** |
| 5 | **Total Nodal Delay** | $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$ | All four components | **ms** |
| 6 | **End-to-End Latency** | $d_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_{nodal,i}$ | Sum across all routers | **ms** |
| 7 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** | $BDP = R \times d_{prop}$ | $R$ in bps, $d_{prop}$ in s | **bits** |
| 8 | Traffic Intensity | $\rho = La / R$ | $L$ = pkt length, $a$ = arrival rate | **dimensionless** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - In **5G networks**, latency targets are **< 1 ms** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication - URLLC).
> - In **satellite communication** (GEO), one-way latency is ~**240 ms** due to high propagation delay.
> - In **financial trading networks**, every **microsecond** of latency translates to millions of dollars in profit/loss.
> - The **BDP formula** is critical for designing TCP window sizes and network buffers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Total Nodal Delay Equation

The total time a packet spends at a single router is the **sum of the four sequential delays**, since the packet must pass through each stage before moving to the next link.

$$
d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}
$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- The packet **first** arrives → enters buffer → *wait* for queue (d_queue)
- Then **CPU processes** the header → *decides* route (d_proc)
- Then **bits are pushed** onto link bit-by-bit (d_trans)
- Finally, **signal propagates** through the medium (d_prop)

### Derivation 2: End-to-End Latency Across N Routers

For a packet traversing a path with **N routers** (i.e., N+1 links), the end-to-end latency is the sum of nodal delays at all intermediate routers **plus** the propagation delays across all links.

$$
d_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_{proc,i} + d_{queue,i} + d_{trans,i}) + \sum_{j=1}^{N+1} d_{prop,j}
$$

**Assumption:** The processing, queuing, and transmission delays are **consolidated per router**, while propagation delay occurs **per link**.

### Derivation 3: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

The BDP represents the **maximum number of bits that can be "in flight"** on a link at any instant — i.e., the number of bits the sender can transmit before receiving the first acknowledgment.

$$
BDP = R \times d_{prop}
$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- In time $d_{prop}$, the sender continuously transmits bits at rate $R$ bps.
- Total bits transmitted before the first bit reaches the receiver:

$$
\text{Bits} = R \, \text{(bits/s)} \times d_{prop} \, \text{(s)} = R \times d_{prop}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem:** A packet of **1500 bytes** is sent over a link of bandwidth **2 Mbps** and physical length **4000 km**. Propagation speed is **2 × 10⁸ m/s**. Processing + Queuing delay = **5 ms**. Calculate the total nodal delay.

**Step 1: Convert packet length to bits**

$$
L = 1500 \, \text{bytes} \times 8 \, \text{bits/byte} = 12000 \, \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Transmission Delay**

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000 \, \text{bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \, \text{bits/s}}
$$

$$
d_{trans} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 6 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Propagation Delay**

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{4000 \times 10^3 \, \text{m}}{2 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}}
$$

$$
d_{prop} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{s} = 20 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4: Sum All Four Delays**

$$
d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}
$$

$$
d_{nodal} = 5 \, \text{ms} + 6 \, \text{ms} + 20 \, \text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{nodal} = 31 \, \text{ms}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A: Block-Level Flow of Four Delays at a Single Router

This diagram maps the **sequential processing pipeline** that each packet undergoes at a router node.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PACKET ARRIVES AT ROUTER"]:::start --> B["BUFFER QUEUE WAITING ZONE"]
    B --> C["QUEUING DELAY D QUEUE"]
    C --> D["CPU HEADER INSPECTION UNIT"]
    D --> E["PROCESSING DELAY D PROC"]
    E --> F["TRANSMITTER PUSHES BITS ONTO LINK"]
    F --> G["TRANSMISSION DELAY D TRANS"]
    G --> H["SIGNAL TRAVELS THROUGH MEDIUM"]
    H --> I["PROPAGATION DELAY D PROP"]
    I --> J["PACKET REACHES NEXT HOP"]:::end

    classDef start fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef end fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The packet flows **left → right** through the four delay stages.
- Each block represents a **discrete time-consuming operation**.
- Total time spent at the router = **sum of all four delay blocks**.

### Diagram B: End-to-End Latency Across a Multi-Hop Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["SOURCE HOST"]:::src --> R1["ROUTER 1"]:::rtr
    R1 --> R2["ROUTER 2"]:::rtr
    R2 --> R3["ROUTER N"]:::rtr
    R3 --> D["DESTINATION HOST"]:::dst

    L1["LINK 1 D PROP 1"]:::link --- R1
    L2["LINK 2 D PROP 2"]:::link --- R2
    L3["LINK N D PROP N"]:::link --- R3

    classDef src fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef dst fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef rtr fill:#9467bd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef link fill:#8c564b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each **router block** internally contains all four delays (d_proc, d_queue, d_trans).
- Each **link block** contributes one propagation delay (d_prop).
- The **total end-to-end latency** is the sum of all nodal delays plus all link propagation delays.

### Diagram C: Transmission Timeline Visualization

```mermaid
gantt
    title Packet Transmission Timeline at Sender
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Bit Stream
    First Bit Leaves Sender       :a1, 0, 1ms
    Middle Bits Transmitting      :a2, 1ms, 11ms
    Last Bit Leaves Sender        :a3, 11ms, 12ms

    section Propagation
    First Bit Arrives at Receiver :b1, 20ms, 21ms
    Last Bit Arrives at Receiver  :b2, 31ms, 32ms
```

**Key Insight from Timeline:**
- Transmission delay (0–12 ms) and propagation delay (12–32 ms) occur **sequentially in time** but represent **physically distinct phenomena**.
- The first bit experiences only propagation delay; the last bit experiences both.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define latency. List and briefly explain its four components.*

**Model Answer:**
**Latency** is the total time taken for a packet to travel from source to destination across a network. The four components are:
1. **Processing Delay** — Time for router to examine packet header and determine route.
2. **Queuing Delay** — Time packet waits in buffer before transmission.
3. **Transmission Delay** — Time to push all packet bits onto the link, given by $d_{trans} = L/R$.
4. **Propagation Delay** — Time for a bit to physically travel through the medium, given by $d_{prop} = d/s$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for components)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Distinguish between transmission delay and propagation delay with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Transmission Delay | Propagation Delay |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $d_{trans} = L/R$ | $d_{prop} = d/s$ |
| **Depends on** | Packet length, bandwidth | Distance, medium speed |
| **Nature** | Pushes bits onto wire | Bit travels through wire |
| **Example** | Sending 1500 B file on 1 Mbps link = 12 ms | Signal travelling 2000 km at 2×10⁸ m/s = 10 ms |

*(3 marks: 1 for table + 1 each for examples)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the four components of latency at a router with neat diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

At each router, a packet experiences four sequential delays:

**1. Processing Delay (d_proc):** The router examines the packet's header fields (source/destination IP), checks for bit errors using checksum, and performs a routing table lookup to determine the outgoing interface. It depends on router CPU speed and table size.

**2. Queuing Delay (d_queue):** After processing, the packet is placed in the output buffer. If the outgoing link is busy transmitting other packets, the new packet must wait. This delay varies from **0 ms** (no queue) to **several ms** (heavy congestion). Traffic intensity $\rho = La/R$ determines the average queue length.

**3. Transmission Delay (d_trans):** Once at the front of the queue, the packet's bits are shifted onto the link one by one. For a packet of $L$ bits and link rate $R$ bps:

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

**4. Propagation Delay (d_prop):** Once a bit enters the physical medium, it travels at speed $s$ (≈ 2×10⁸ m/s for copper, 3×10⁸ m/s for vacuum) over distance $d$:

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s}
$$

**Total Nodal Delay:**

$$
d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}
$$

*(Refer to Section 4 Diagram A for visual representation.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A packet of size 2000 bytes traverses a link of bandwidth 4 Mbps and length 6000 km. If propagation speed = 2.5 × 10⁸ m/s and processing + queuing delay = 4 ms, calculate the total nodal delay.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert packet to bits**

$$
L = 2000 \times 8 = 16000 \, \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2: Transmission Delay**

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{16000}{4 \times 10^6} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 4 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Propagation Delay**

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{6000 \times 10^3}{2.5 \times 10^8} = 24 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 24 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4: Total Nodal Delay**

$$
d_{nodal} = d_{proc+queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop} = 4 + 4 + 24 = 32 \, \text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{nodal} = 32 \, \text{ms}}
```

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for bandwidth-delay product. Explain its significance in TCP window sizing.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**
Consider a sender transmitting bits at rate $R$ bps over a link of length $d$. The propagation delay for the first bit to reach the receiver is $d_{prop} = d/s$.

During this propagation time, the sender continues transmitting bits at rate $R$. Therefore, the total number of bits that can be transmitted before the first bit arrives at the receiver is:

$$
BDP = R \times d_{prop}
$$

**Significance:**
- The BDP defines the **maximum number of "in-flight" bits** on the link.
- For **TCP**, the sender's window size must be at least equal to the BDP to fully utilize the link's bandwidth.
- If the window is smaller than BDP, the sender will be **idle waiting for ACKs**, wasting bandwidth.
- For example: a 100 Mbps link with $d_{prop}$ = 25 ms has BDP = 2.5 × 10⁶ bits ≈ 312 KB, requiring a TCP window of at least 312 KB for full utilization.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A 500-byte message is sent through a network with 3 routers. Each link has bandwidth 1 Mbps and length 2000 km. Propagation speed = 2 × 10⁸ m/s. At each router: processing delay = 1 ms, queuing delay = 3 ms. Calculate end-to-end latency.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Transmission Delay (per router, same packet size)**

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{500 \times 8}{1 \times 10^6} = \frac{4000}{10^6} = 4 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2: Propagation Delay (per link)**

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{2000 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^8} = 10 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Nodal Delay at each router**

$$
d_{nodal} = 1 + 3 + 4 + 10 = 18 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4: End-to-End Latency (3 routers, 3 links)**

$$
d_{e2e} = 3 \times (d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans}) + 3 \times d_{prop}
$$

$$
d_{e2e} = 3 \times (1 + 3 + 4) + 3 \times 10 = 24 + 30 = 54 \, \text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{e2e} = 54 \, \text{ms}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse **transmission delay** with **propagation delay**. Remember: Transmission = pushing bits onto wire; Propagation = bit travelling through wire.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert **bytes → bits** (multiply by 8). This causes wrong answers in nearly 30% of KTU scripts.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In end-to-end problems, students use $N$ routers but forget there are $N+1$ links (source-to-router1, router1-router2, ..., lastRouter-destination). For the packet's first and last hops, processing/transmission delay occurs at source/destination hosts too.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not converting units properly — km → m (multiply by 10³), Mbps → bps (multiply by 10⁶), and final answer to ms.
> - **Marks Loss:** Typically **2–3 marks deducted** for unit errors alone in KTU board evaluation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ Latency = **Total time** for packet to reach destination (source → destination).
- ✅ Four components: **Processing, Queuing, Transmission, Propagation** — always recall in this order.
- ✅ Transmission delay formula: $d_{trans} = L / R$ (depends on **packet size** and **bandwidth**).
- ✅ Propagation delay formula: $d_{prop} = d / s$ (depends on **distance** and **medium speed**).
- ✅ Always convert **bytes → bits** by multiplying by 8.
- ✅ Always convert **km → m** by multiplying by $10^3$ when using SI units.
- ✅ Propagation speed in copper/fiber ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s**; in vacuum ≈ **3 × 10⁸ m/s**.
- ✅ Queuing delay is the **only variable** component — all others are deterministic for a given packet.
- ✅ Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) determines the **optimal TCP window size** to fully utilize the link.
- ✅ End-to-end latency = **sum of all nodal delays** + **sum of all link propagation delays**.
- ✅ Higher bandwidth ≠ lower latency; bandwidth affects transmission delay, not propagation delay.
- ✅ **GEO satellites** have ~240 ms latency purely due to propagation (~36,000 km altitude).
- ✅ **5G URLLC** target: end-to-end latency **< 1 ms** for autonomous vehicles and remote surgery.
- ✅ KTU exam favorites: numerical problems on $d_{nodal}$, $d_{e2e}$, and BDP appear in **every semester**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Queuing Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Queuing Time # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Queuing Delay (d_queue)** is defined as the time a packet spends waiting inside the buffer (queue) of a network node — typically a router or switch — before it can be selected for transmission onto the outgoing link. It is one of the four canonical components of **nodal (per-hop) processing delay** in packet-switched networks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Total Nodal Delay Equation:** > $$d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$$ > where $d_{proc}$ = processing delay, $d_{queue}$ = queuing delay, $d_{trans}$ = transmission delay, $d_{prop}$ = propagation delay. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, queuing delay is treated as a **stochastic (random) variable** because it depends on the burstiness and statistical arrival pattern of packets at the router — not a deterministic value. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine a **single-lane toll booth** on a highway: - Cars arrive randomly (just like packets arriving at a router). - The toll booth can service only **one car at a time** (just like a transmission link can transmit only one bit/packet at a time). - If cars keep arriving faster than the toll booth can serve them, a **queue** forms behind the booth. - The time a particular car waits **from joining the line** until **its turn at the booth** is the **Queuing Delay**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** If cars arrive slowly, the line is short, and waiting time is negligible. If cars arrive in bursts, even a fast toll booth cannot clear the queue instantly, and waiting time explodes. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Traffic Intensity (I)** — a dimensionless ratio: $I = \dfrac{La}{R}$ - **Transmission Rate (R)** — measured in **bits/second (bps)** - **Packet Length (L)** — measured in **bits** - **Packet Arrival Rate (a)** — measured in **packets/second** - **Mean Queuing Delay** — measured in **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)** > [!TIP] > For KTU exams, always quote units explicitly. A numerical answer without units will attract a **0.5 to 1 mark deduction** under strict valuation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Queuing Delay Arises When a packet arrives at a router, the following sequence unfolds: 1. **Packet Inspection:** The router inspects the packet header (processing delay $d_{proc}$). 2. **Buffer Lookup:** The router determines the outgoing interface from its routing table. 3. **Queue Insertion:** If the outgoing link is busy transmitting another packet, the new packet is **appended to the FIFO (First-In-First-Out) buffer** associated with that interface. 4. **Wait Phase:** The packet waits until all packets queued ahead of it have been transmitted. 5. **Transmission Phase:** Once the packet reaches the head of the queue, its bits are pushed onto the link (transmission delay $d_{trans}$). 6. **Propagation:** The bits travel physically across the medium (propagation delay $d_{prop}$). > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Queue Wait Phase (Step 4)** is precisely where **Queuing Delay** is incurred. ## 2.2 Traffic Intensity — The Decisive Parameter The **single most important parameter** governing queuing delay is the **Traffic Intensity**: $$I = \frac{La}{R}$$ - **Numerator ($La$):** Average rate at which **bits** are arriving at the queue (bits/second). - **Denominator ($R$):** Rate at which the link can **drain** bits (bits/second). ### Interpretation of the Three Regimes | Regime | Condition | Physical Meaning | Queuing Behavior | |---|---|---|---| | **Underloaded** | $La/R < 1$ | Arrivals slower than service | Queue is short, $d_{queue}$ is small and finite | | **Critical Load** | $La/R \approx 1$ | Arrivals nearly equal service | $d_{queue}$ grows large and is highly variable | | **Overloaded** | $La/R > 1$ | Arrivals faster than service | Queue grows **without bound** — system is unstable | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** When $La/R > 1$, the **average queuing delay becomes infinite** in theory. Students often compute a finite number — this is incorrect. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I = \dfrac{La}{R}$ | Traffic Intensity | dimensionless | | 2 | $d_{queue} = \dfrac{L \cdot (n-1)}{R}$ | Queuing delay for a packet finding $n-1$ packets ahead | seconds | | 3 | $d_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Transmission delay (constant for fixed $L$) | seconds | | 4 | $N = a \cdot d$ | **Little's Law**: avg. # in system = arrival rate × avg. time in system | packets | | 5 | $N = \lambda \cdot T$ | Little's Law (alternative notation) | packets | | 6 | $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ | Avg. queue length for **M/M/1** queue | packets | | 7 | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Avg. waiting time in queue (M/M/1) | seconds | | 8 | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Server utilization (M/M/1) | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > In KTU Computer Networks (PEMRT751), focus primarily on **Formula 1 (Traffic Intensity)**, **Formula 2 (Queuing Delay)**, and **Little's Law (Formula 4)**. The M/M/1 results (6–8) appear in advanced problems or numerical modules. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Router/Switch Design:** Network engineers dimension buffer sizes using traffic intensity analysis to prevent packet loss. - **QoS Engineering:** Voice-over-IP (VoIP) and video streaming demand **strict queuing delay bounds** — hence QoS queues with priority handling. - **5G/6G URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication):** Queuing delay budgets are part of the **1 ms end-to-end latency** target. - **Data Center Networks:** Tail-latency optimization (P99 queuing delay) is critical for hyperscaler cloud platforms. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Queuing Delay as a Function of Packets Ahead in Queue **Setup:** Suppose a packet $P$ arrives at a FIFO queue. It finds $(n-1)$ other packets already waiting. Each packet ahead has length $L$ bits, and the link transmits at rate $R$ bits/sec. **Step 1:** Time to transmit a single packet = Transmission delay. $$t_{single} = \frac{L}{R}$$ **Step 2:** Time to transmit all $(n-1)$ packets ahead of $P$ = $(n-1)$ times the per-packet transmission time. $$t_{ahead} = (n-1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}$$ **Step 3:** Since $P$ must wait for all packets ahead to be transmitted before its own transmission begins, the **queuing delay** for $P$ is: $$d_{queue} = \frac{L \cdot (n-1)}{R}$$ **Step 4:** Taking the **expected value** over the random variable $n$: $$\mathbb{E}[d_{queue}] = \frac{L \cdot (\mathbb{E}[n] - 1)}{R}$$ This shows that **average queuing delay is directly proportional to the average number of packets already in the queue** when the new arrival occurs. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Queuing Delay via Little's Law (KTU High-Yield) **Little's Law Statement:** Under steady-state conditions in any queueing system: $$N = a \cdot d$$ where $N$ = average number of packets in the system, $a$ = average arrival rate (packets/sec), $d$ = average time a packet spends in the system. **Step 1:** Decompose $d$ into queuing time + transmission time: $$d = d_{queue} + d_{trans}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Little's Law to the **entire system (queue + transmission)**: $$N = a \cdot (d_{queue} + d_{trans})$$ **Step 3:** The average number of bits in the system = (avg. # of packets) × (bits per packet): $$\text{Bits in system} = N \cdot L = a \cdot L \cdot (d_{queue} + d_{trans})$$ **Step 4:** Note that $a \cdot L$ is the **bit arrival rate** (bits/sec). For a stable system, this equals the transmission rate $R$ (on average, what comes in must go out): $$a \cdot L \approx R \quad \text{(traffic balance)}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute and rearrange: $$\begin{aligned} N \cdot L &= R \cdot (d_{queue} + d_{trans}) \\ N \cdot L &= R \cdot d_{queue} + R \cdot d_{trans} \\ R \cdot d_{queue} &= N \cdot L - R \cdot d_{trans} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6:** Since $d_{trans} = L/R$, the term $R \cdot d_{trans} = L$.
