> A repeater solves (1) and (2) by **regeneration**, but **cannot fully solve (3) when dispersion is severe** — which is why the segment distance limits (e.g., 100m for UTP) are chosen so that dispersion stays below the ISI tolerance window.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of a Digital (Regenerative) Repeater — Step-by-Step

A digital repeater executes the following **four-step process** on every incoming bit:

1. **Sense (Threshold Detection):** Sample the incoming voltage at the midpoint of the bit cell. Compare to $V_{th} = \pm V_{threshold}$.
2. **Decode (Hard Decision):** Output a clean `0` or `1` (no analog intermediate).
3. **Retime (Clock Recovery):** Re-derive the bit boundary from the recovered clock; place the new bit transition in the center of a fresh bit cell.
4. **Retransmit:** Drive the recovered bit onto the outgoing segment at full transmit power.

> **Engineering Insight:** This 4-step process is why a digital repeater **adds latency** — typically **2 to 3 bit times** (≈ 200–300 ns at 10 Mbps). This delay is critical to the **5-4-3 rule** derivation in §2.4.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical cheat sheet** the KTU board examiner expects you to know cold. Memorize every row.

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Unit | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Attenuation (dB) | $A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | dB | Cable loss budget |
| 2 | Voltage Attenuation (dB) | $A = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{out}}\right)$ | dB | Sensor / line-driver design |
| 3 | SNR | $SNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | BER estimator |
| 4 | BER (approx, for BPSK) | $BER \approx Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \cdot SNR}\right)$ | dimensionless | Link budget |
| 5 | Max segment count | **5 segments** | — | 10BASE5/10BASE2 |
| 6 | Max repeater count | **4 repeaters** | — | 10BASE5/10BASE2 |
| 7 | Max populated segments | **3 segments** | — | 5-4-3 Rule |
| 8 | Repeater latency | $t_{rep} \approx 2$ to $3$ bit times | $\mu$s | Round-trip budget |
| 9 | Slot time (10 Mbps Ethernet) | $t_{slot} = 512$ bit times = $51.2\,\mu$s | $\mu$s | Collision window |
| 10 | Max end-to-end distance (10BASE5) | $5 \times 500\,m = 2500\,m$ | m | 5-4-3 Rule |
| 11 | Max nodes per 10BASE5 segment | 100 | — | 10BASE5 spec |
| 12 | Max nodes per 10BASE2 segment | 30 | — | 10BASE2 spec |
| 13 | Max segment length 10BASE5 | 500 m | m | 10BASE5 spec |
| 14 | Max segment length 10BASE2 | 185 m | m | 10BASE2 spec |
| 15 | Max segment length 10BASE-T | 100 m | m | Twisted-pair spec |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar (absolute value) notation has been written as `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve the markdown table delimiter integrity. Do not write `$x \vert 2$` in a table cell — it will break KTU's auto-renderer.

### 2.4 The 5-4-3 Rule — Why It Exists

The **5-4-3 Rule** is a **cornerstone KTU exam topic** for any Module 2 question on classic Ethernet design. It states:

> *In a 10 Mbps CSMA/CD Ethernet network using repeaters, the maximum topology is **5 segments, connected by 4 repeaters, with at most 3 of those segments being "populated" (i.e., containing end stations).***

**Why these specific numbers?**
- The IEEE 802.3 specification defined a **maximum round-trip propagation delay of 51.2 μs** (the *slot time*) so that a transmitting station can detect a collision before it finishes sending the smallest legal frame (64 bytes × 8 bits × 100 ns = 51.2 μs at 10 Mbps).
- Each **500 m segment** contributes ≈ **25.6 μs** of one-way propagation delay (≈ 5.085 ns/m × 500 m, accounting for velocity of propagation $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in coax).
- The 4 repeaters add ≈ 4 × 2 μs = 8 μs of repeater latency.
- Sum: $5 \times 25.6\,\mu s + 4 \times 2\,\mu s = 128 + 8 = 136\,\mu s$ — well over slot time. But the *signal degradation* (not just delay) on a chain of 5 segments forces the limit to **5 segments** anyway.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coaxial cable TV distribution:** A cascade of RF amplifiers (analog repeaters) feeds thousands of homes.
- **Submarine fiber-optic links:** A trans-Pacific cable may have **50–100 optical repeaters** (EDFAs + regenerators) spanning 10,000 km.
- **Cellular backhaul:** Microwave radios use digital repeaters to extend point-to-point links.
- **Industrial CAN bus:** Repeaters isolate and extend CAN segments in automotive and factory networks.
- **Historical Ethernet:** Every thick-wire (10BASE5) and thin-wire (10BASE2) LAN relied on repeaters before switches rendered them obsolete.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 *does* ask questions on repeaters even though they are "legacy" — because the **principles of signal regeneration, collision domain segmentation, and the 5-4-3 rule** remain directly relevant to modern switch behavior (each switch port = a fresh collision domain, just like a repeater segment).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Network Diameter from the 5-4-3 Rule (10BASE5)

> **Problem Statement:** A classic Ethernet LAN is built from 10BASE5 segments. A student uses 4 repeaters to chain 5 segments together. Given the IEEE 802.3 timing parameters, derive the maximum end-to-end network diameter and verify the 5-4-3 rule's physical feasibility.

**Step 1 — Velocity of propagation in polyethylene-dielectric coax:**

$$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}}{\sqrt{2.25}} = 2.0 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$$

*Logic:* Polyethylene dielectric has relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.25$, giving a velocity factor of $0.66c$.

**Step 2 — One-way propagation delay per metre of coax:**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{2.0 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}} = 5.0\ \text{ns/m}$$

**Step 3 — One-way propagation delay over one 500 m 10BASE5 segment:**

$$t_{seg} = 500\ \text{m} \times 5.0\ \text{ns/m} = 2500\ \text{ns} = 2.5\ \mu s$$

**Step 4 — Repeater latency (assume 2 μs per repeater, IEEE 802.3 worst case):**

$$t_{rep} = 2.0\ \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Total one-way delay across 5 segments + 4 repeaters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{one\text{-}way} &= (5 \times t_{seg}) + (4 \times t_{rep}) \\
&= (5 \times 2.5\ \mu s) + (4 \times 2.0\ \mu s) \\
&= 12.5\ \mu s + 8.0\ \mu s \\
&= 20.5\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Round-trip delay (transmitter must hear collision before end-of-frame):**

$$t_{RTD} = 2 \times t_{one\text{-}way} = 2 \times 20.5\ \mu s = 41.0\ \mu s$$

**Step 7 — Compare with IEEE 802.3 slot time:**

$$t_{slot} = 51.2\ \mu s \quad\Rightarrow\quad t_{RTD} = 41.0\ \mu s < 51.2\ \mu s \;\; \checkmark$$

*Conclusion:* The 5-4-3 rule's 2500 m diameter is **timing-feasible** because the round-trip delay (41.0 μs) leaves **10.2 μs of headroom** within the 51.2 μs collision-detection window.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Attenuation Budget for a Single Coaxial Segment

**Problem:** A 10BASE2 segment uses RG-58 coax with attenuation $\alpha = 5\ \text{dB}/100\ \text{m}$ at 10 MHz. The NIC transmit power is $P_{tx} = 1.0\ \text{mW}$. The minimum receiver sensitivity is $P_{rx,min} = 0.5\ \mu\text{W}$. Find the maximum segment length.

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum allowable attenuation in dB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{max} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{tx}}{P_{rx,min}}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}(2000) \\
&= 10 \times 3.301 \\
&= 33.01\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for max cable length using $\alpha$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= \frac{A_{max}}{\alpha} = \frac{33.01\ \text{dB}}{5\ \text{dB}/100\ \text{m}} \\
&= 6.602 \times 100\ \text{m} \\
&= 660.2\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the 10BASE2 spec (185 m) and explain the gap:**

The calculated physical limit of **660 m** is far above the **185 m** spec. The 185 m limit is dictated by the **timing / collision-detection constraint**, **not** attenuation. A repeater would only be needed if $L > 185\ \text{m}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Trap:** A common exam question asks *"If attenuation allows 660 m, why is the 10BASE2 segment limited to 185 m?"* The correct answer is **propagation delay / CSMA-CD collision detection timing**, not signal power.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When a Repeater Is / Is Not Required

| Scenario | Distance | Attenuation Limit OK? | Timing Limit OK? | **Repeater Needed?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Two PCs on 10BASE-T | 80 m | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Two PCs on 10BASE2 | 150 m | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Two PCs on 10BASE2 | 200 m | Yes | No (exceeds 185 m) | **Yes** |
| 10BASE5 link | 500 m | Yes | Yes (single segment) | **No** |
| 10BASE5 link | 600 m | Marginal | No (exceeds 500 m) | **Yes** |
| Fiber link (single mode) | 80 km | Yes (with EDFA budget) | Yes | **Yes** (optical amplifier) |
| UTP Cat5e run | 150 m | Marginal (crosstalk + loss) | No (exceeds 100 m) | **Yes** |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: SNR and BER Coupling in a Cascaded Repeater Chain

**Problem:** A signal passes through 4 regenerative repeaters. Each repeater adds noise such that the output SNR of one link degrades by 1 dB. Input SNR at repeater 1 is 30 dB. Find the SNR at the output of repeater 4.

**Step 1 — Convert input SNR from dB to linear ratio:**

$$SNR_0 = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Add noise powers (incoherent):** Each repeater reduces SNR by 1 dB → linear factor of $10^{-0.1} = 0.7943$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SNR_1 &= 1000 \times 0.7943 = 794.3 \\
SNR_2 &= 794.3 \times 0.7943 = 630.9 \\
SNR_3 &= 630.9 \times 0.7943 = 501.2 \\
SNR_4 &= 501.2 \times 0.7943 = 398.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert final linear SNR back to dB:**

$$SNR_4\ (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(398.1) = 26.0\ \text{dB}$$

*Result:* Four repeaters degrade the SNR from **30 dB → 26 dB**, a total loss of 4 dB.

> **Key insight:** A *regenerative* digital repeater's SNR contribution is *bounded* (≈ 1 dB per hop), whereas an *analog* repeater's contribution is *unbounded* (each hop multiplies the noise power). This is why digital regeneration dominated LAN design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Regenerative Repeater

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Segment\nPhysical Medium] --> B[Front-End\nReceiver]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner\nEqualizer + Amplifier]
    C --> D[Threshold Detector\nDecision Circuit]
    D --> E[Clock Recovery\nPLL]
    E --> F[Re-timed Bit Stream\nFresh Bit Cell]
    F --> G[Line Driver\nRetransmit]
    G --> H[Outgoing Segment\nPhysical Medium]

    style A fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#fde68a,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#fde68a,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading Guide:** Yellow stages form the *analog front end*; amber stage is the *hard-decision regenerator* (the heart of a digital repeater); green stage is the *clean reconstructed bit stream* — note that all analog noise from the input has been **discarded** by the threshold detector before re-driving the line.

---

### 4.2 Network Topology Showing the 5-4-3 Rule

```mermaid
graph LR
    SEG1[Segment 1\nPOPULATED\n185 m / 30 nodes]:::pop
    SEG2[Segment 2\nPOPULATED\n185 m / 30 nodes]:::pop
    SEG3[Segment 3\nPOPULATED\n185 m / 30 nodes]:::pop
    SEG4[Segment 4\nLINK ONLY\n185 m / 0 nodes]:::link
    SEG5[Segment 5\nLINK ONLY\n185 m / 0 nodes]:::link
    R1[Repeater 1]:::rep
    R2[Repeater 2]:::rep
    R3[Repeater 3]:::rep
    R4[Repeater 4]:::rep

    SEG1 --- R1
    R1 --- SEG2
    SEG2 --- R2
    R2 --- SEG3
    SEG3 --- R3
    R3 --- SEG4
    SEG4 --- R4
    R4 --- SEG5

    classDef pop fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px
    classDef link fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:1px
    classDef rep fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading Guide:** Green segments host end stations; blue segments are *link-only* repeater-to-repeater interconnects (purely to extend distance); red boxes are repeaters. **5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated** — the canonical 5-4-3 configuration.

---

### 4.3 OSI Layer Placement and Device Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    L7[Layer 7 Application\nHTTP FTP SMTP]:::l7
    L6[Layer 6 Presentation\nSSL JPEG MPEG]:::l6
    L5[Layer 5 Session\nRPC NetBIOS]:::l5
    L4[Layer 4 Transport\nTCP UDP]:::l4
    L3[Layer 3 Network\nIP Router]:::l3
    L2[Layer 2 Data Link\nSwitch Bridge MAC]:::l2
    L1[Layer 1 Physical\nRepeater Hub]:::l1

    GATE[Gateway]:::gw
    ROUT[Router]:::rout
    BR[Bridge / Switch]:::br
    HUB[Hub / Repeater]:::hub

    L7 --- GATE
    L6 --- GATE
    L5 --- GATE
    L4 --- GATE
    L4 --- ROUT
    L3 --- ROUT
    L3 --- BR
    L2 --- BR
    L2 --- HUB
    L1 --- HUB

    classDef l1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333
    classDef l2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#333
    classDef l3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333
    classDef l4 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#333
    classDef l5 fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#333
    classDef l6 fill:#ffe4e6,stroke:#333
    classDef l7 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#333
    classDef hub fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:3px
    classDef br fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rout fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gw fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **Repeater / Hub** box (bold red border) spans *only* Layer 1. As you move up the device hierarchy, each device class spans more layers and gains more intelligence — and more cost.

---

### 4.4 Analog vs. Digital Repeater — Noise Handling Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Analog Repeater Chain]
        A1[Input: Signal + Noise] --> A2[Amp Stage 1\nAmplifies Both] --> A3[Amp Stage 2\nNoise Grows] --> A4[Output: Stronger + Noisier]
    end
    subgraph B[Digital Repeater Chain]
        B1[Input: Signal + Noise] --> B2[Threshold Detect\nDecide 0 or 1] --> B3[Clean Bit] --> B4[Re-clock] --> B5[Re-drive\nFresh Signal] --> B6[Output: Clean + Strong]
    end

    style A2 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b
    style A3 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b
    style A4 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d
    style B2 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46
    style B3 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46
    style B4 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46
    style B5 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46
    style B6 fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#047857
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **red cascade** shows noise *compounding* in the analog chain (unbounded growth). The **green chain** shows noise *reset* at every regenerative hop (bounded growth) — the fundamental reason modern digital repeaters replaced analog line amplifiers in LANs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a repeater. Why is it classified as a Layer 1 device?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **repeater** is a physical-layer (Layer 1) networking device that receives an incoming signal, regenerates it (amplifies, retimes, and reconstructs the bit), and retransmits the clean signal on to the next network segment. **(1 mark)**
>
> It is classified as a Layer 1 device because it operates **only on raw bits** traveling over the physical medium — it does **not** interpret frames, MAC addresses, IP packets, or any other protocol data unit. **(1 mark)**
>
> Its sole purpose is to overcome the **attenuation, noise, and distortion** that limit the maximum cable length of a single segment. **(1 mark)**

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between an analog and a digital repeater.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | Analog Repeater | Digital Repeater |
> |---|---|---|
> | Action | Amplifies the entire waveform | Decodes each bit, then reconstructs |
> | Noise | Amplified along with signal (compounds) | Discarded at threshold detector (bounded) |
> | BER | Increases with cascade length | Roughly constant per hop |
> | Use case | Long-haul analog telecom, cable TV | IEEE 802.3 Ethernet, fiber regenerators |
>
> **(2 marks for the contrast table, 1 mark for the key insight on noise compounding vs. noise rejection.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is **14 marks** with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. The first student answers Q1(a)+Q1(b) or Q2(a)+Q2(b); the second student answers the alternate.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q1(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **operational mechanism of a regenerative repeater** with a neat block diagram. Why is regeneration preferred over simple amplification in a digital LAN?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the role (2 marks):**
A regenerative repeater is placed between two LAN segments. When the signal on segment 1 attenuates below a usable level, the repeater **regenerates a clean replica** of the bit stream and forwards it to segment 2, effectively extending the network diameter.

**Step 2 — Block diagram (2 marks):**

> [Diagram: `Incoming → Receiver → Equalizer → Threshold Detector → Clock Recovery → Line Driver → Outgoing`]

**Step 3 — Four-stage process (2 marks):**
1. **Threshold detection:** Sample input voltage at the mid-bit cell. If $V > +V_{th}$ → output `1`; if $V < -V_{th}$ → output `0`.
2. **Decision (hard decode):** Output is a *clean* binary level — no analog residue.
3. **Clock recovery (PLL):** Re-derive the bit timing from the recovered transitions; place the new bit edge at the centre of a fresh bit cell.
4. **Re-drive:** Push the reconstructed bit onto the next segment at full transmit power.

**Step 4 — Why regeneration over amplification (1 mark):**
Simple amplification would multiply the input noise by the same gain as the signal, leading to **unbounded BER growth** along the cascade. Regeneration **discards the noise** at each hop, so the **BER is reset to ≈ 0** at every repeater. This is the *core reason* digital regeneration is mandatory in 10/100/1000 Mbps Ethernet.

---

**Q1(b) [7 marks]** Derive the maximum network diameter for a **10BASE5 Ethernet** network using the **5-4-3 rule**, assuming repeater latency of 2 μs each and slot time of 51.2 μs.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — 5-4-3 rule statement (1 mark):**
Maximum **5 segments** connected by **4 repeaters**, with only **3 segments** populated by end stations.

**Step 2 — Segment length spec (1 mark):**
10BASE5 maximum segment length $L_{seg} = 500\ \text{m}$.

**Step 3 — Velocity of propagation in PE coax (1 mark):**
$v = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r} = 3 \times 10^{8} / \sqrt{2.25} = 2.0 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$.

**Step 4 — Propagation delay per metre (1 mark):**
$t_{pd} = 1 / v = 5.0\ \text{ns/m}$.

**Step 5 — One-way segment delay (1 mark):**
$t_{seg} = 500\ \text{m} \times 5.0\ \text{ns/m} = 2.5\ \mu\text{s}$.

**Step 6 — Total one-way delay (1 mark):**
$t_{one\text{-}way} = (5 \times 2.5) + (4 \times 2.0) = 12.5 + 8.0 = 20.5\ \mu\text{s}$.

**Step 7 — Round-trip check + diameter (1 mark):**
$t_{RTD} = 2 \times 20.5 = 41.0\ \mu s < 51.2\ \mu s$ ✔.
**Maximum network diameter** = $5 \times 500\ \text{m} = \mathbf{2500\ \text{m}}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q2(a) [7 marks]** What is **attenuation**? Compute the **maximum allowable cable length** for a 10BASE2 segment given transmit power $P_{tx} = 1.0\ \text{mW}$, receiver sensitivity $P_{rx,min} = 0.5\ \mu\text{W}$, and cable attenuation $\alpha = 5\ \text{dB}/100\ \text{m}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define attenuation (1 mark):**
Attenuation is the **gradual loss of signal power** as it propagates through a physical medium, expressed in dB.

**Step 2 — Formula (1 mark):**
$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$.

**Step 3 — Compute max allowable attenuation (2 marks):**
$A_{max} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2000) = 33.01\ \text{dB}$.

**Step 4 — Solve for cable length (2 marks):**
$L_{max} = A_{max} / \alpha = 33.01 / 0.05 = 660.2\ \text{m}$.

**Step 5 — Compare with 10BASE2 spec (1 mark):**
The physical attenuation limit of **660 m** exceeds the IEEE 802.3 timing limit of **185 m** — so the segment is bounded by **CSMA/CD collision detection timing**, not by signal power.

---

**Q2(b) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast a **repeater, bridge, and router** with respect to OSI layer, intelligence, address awareness, and typical use case.

**Model Solution:**

| Attribute | Repeater | Bridge / Switch | Router |
|---|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) |
| Address awareness | None (raw bits) | MAC addresses | IP addresses |
| Function | Regenerate signal | Forward frames by MAC | Forward packets by IP |
| Collision domains | Single shared | One per port | One per port |
| Broadcast domain | Single shared | Single shared | One per interface |
| Intelligence | None | Spanning Tree, MAC learning | Routing protocols (OSPF, BGP) |
| Typical use | Extend cable length | Segment LANs | Interconnect different networks |
| KTU example | 10BASE2/5 repeater | Ethernet switch | Home Wi-Fi router |

**(2 marks for the table, 5 marks distributed as 1 mark per attribute row × 3 devices, plus 2 marks for a concluding sentence on the increasing-intelligence trend.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Repeater questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing repeater with hub (or hub with switch).** A repeater has **2 ports**; a hub has **multiple ports but is logically a multi-port repeater**. A switch is **not** a hub.
> 2. **Stating "repeater amplifies the signal."** Strictly, a **regenerative** digital repeater *reconstructs* the signal; amplification is the *analog* repeater's job. Mixing the two loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Memorizing "5-4-3" without the *why*.** Examiners want you to derive it from the **51.2 μs slot time** at 10 Mbps. A bare statement of the rule scores 1 mark; the derivation scores 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting repeater latency (2 μs per hop).** In a 4-repeater chain, the total is 8 μs, which is **non-negligible** vs. the 51.2 μs budget. Examiners actively look for this term.
> 5. **Writing `|x|` (vertical bar) inside a markdown table.** The auto-marker / renderer will treat it as a column delimiter and corrupt the row. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 6. **Stating that a repeater "filters traffic" or "reduces collisions."** It does **neither**. It merely extends the collision domain. Only bridges/switches break collision domains.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **repeater** is a **Layer 1** device that **regenerates, retimes, and retransmits** the raw bit stream.
- [x] Repeaters are **transparent** to Layer 2 and above — no MAC or IP awareness.
- [x] **Analog repeater** = amplifier (compounds noise); **Digital repeater** = regenerator (discards noise).
- [x] Repeater latency ≈ **2–3 bit times** (≈ 200–300 ns at 10 Mbps).
- [x] **5-4-3 Rule:** 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments — valid for 10 Mbps CSMA/CD Ethernet.
- [x] 10BASE5: 500 m/segment; 10BASE2: 185 m/segment; 10BASE-T: 100 m/segment.
- [x] **5 × 500 m = 2500 m** is the maximum 10BASE5 diameter; 5 × 185 m = 925 m for 10BASE2.
- [x] Slot time = **51.2 μs** at 10 Mbps; derived from minimum 64-byte frame × 8 bits × 100 ns.
- [x] Velocity of propagation in PE coax ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** (velocity factor 0.66c).
- [x] Attenuation formula: $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$.
- [x] SNR formula: $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$.
- [x] Repeater **does NOT** filter traffic, **does NOT** break collision domains, **does NOT** understand addressing.
- [x] Modern Ethernet **replaced** repeaters/hubs with switches; each switch port = a fresh collision domain.
- [x] Optical-fiber repeaters = **EDFAs** (amplify) + **regenerators** (re-time) for trans-oceanic links.
- [x] 10BASE5 allows **100 nodes per segment**; 10BASE2 allows **30 nodes per segment**.
- [x] Round-trip delay budget at 10BASE5 max diameter = **41.0 μs < 51.2 μs** ✔ (10.2 μs safety margin).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hubs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hubs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hub** is a **physical layer (Layer 1)** networking device that serves as a common connection point for multiple devices in a Local Area Network (LAN). It functions as a **multiport repeater**, taking an incoming electrical signal on one port and regenerating (repeating) and broadcasting it out to **all other active ports**, regardless of the intended destination.

In the **KTU 2024 syllabus context**, a hub is classified strictly as an **OSI Layer 1 (Physical Layer) device** that performs only:
- **Signal regeneration** (reconditioning attenuated signals)
- **Signal amplification** (restoring signal strength)
- **Signal broadcasting** (fan-out to all other ports)

It contains **no MAC address table, no packet buffer intelligence, and no collision detection mechanism** in passive variants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A hub is a Physical Layer device that acts as a central connection point for multiple network nodes, retransmitting incoming electrical signals to all other connected ports, thereby forming a single shared broadcast/collision domain."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **public address (PA) system** in a railway station:

- When the **station master speaks** into the microphone (one input port), the voice is **amplified and broadcast** to **every speaker in the entire station** (all other output ports).
- The PA system does **not know** which specific speaker should broadcast the message — it just **amplifies and distributes** the signal to **everyone**.
- If **two people speak into different microphones at the same time**, the resulting sound is **garbled** (this is exactly what happens in a hub when two devices transmit simultaneously — a **collision**).
- The PA system is **dumb** — it has no brains, no memory, no filtering, no destination awareness.

> **A hub is the network equivalent of this PA system.** It listens on one port, amplifies what it hears, and shouts it out on every other port — whether or not anyone was listening for that particular message.

## 1.3 Position in the OSI Model

| OSI Layer | Function | Hub Support |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 7 (Application) | User interface | ❌ No |
| Layer 4 (Transport) | End-to-end delivery | ❌ No |
| Layer 3 (Network) | Logical addressing/routing | ❌ No |
| Layer 2 (Data Link) | MAC addressing, framing | ❌ No |
| **Layer 1 (Physical)** | **Bit transmission, electrical signaling** | ✅ **YES** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Because a hub operates at **Layer 1**, it has no awareness of **MAC addresses**, **IP addresses**, or **frames**. It only deals with **raw electrical bits / optical pulses**.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics (BOLD Constants)

- **Maximum segments per hub (10BASE-T):** **4** repeater segments maximum
- **Maximum nodes per collision domain (10BASE-T):** **1024** nodes (theoretical)
- **Typical port count:** **4, 8, 12, 16, 24, or 48** ports
- **Operating speed (classical):** **10 Mbps** or **100 Mbps** (shared across all ports)
- **Maximum cable length per segment (UTP):** **100 meters**
- **5-4-3 Rule (legacy 10BASE-T):** **5** segments, **4** repeaters, **3** populated segments
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How a Hub Works

The internal operation of a hub can be broken down into a precise **four-stage pipeline**:

1. **Signal Reception (Input Stage)**
   - An electrical signal arrives on **one specific port** (e.g., Port 3).
   - The signal has likely traveled through copper cabling (UTP/STP) and suffered **attenuation** (signal weakening), **noise corruption**, and **timing jitter**.

2. **Signal Conditioning (Repeater Core)**
   - The hub's repeater circuitry **reconstructs the bit pattern**.
   - It **re-amplifies** the signal to its original voltage levels.
   - It **re-times** the signal edges to clean up jitter.
   - The repeater treats the data as an **opaque stream of bits** — it has no concept of "who sent this" or "what does this mean".

3. **Signal Broadcasting (Distribution Stage)**
   - The regenerated signal is **fanned out to ALL other ports** simultaneously.
   - The **source port is excluded** from the broadcast (a feature called **auto-partitioning** in some hubs prevents immediate echo).
   - All other connected devices **receive the signal**, but only the **intended destination** actually processes the frame (based on MAC address matching at Layer 2 of the receiving NIC).

4. **Collision Handling (Shared Medium)**
   - Since all ports share **one physical medium logically**, transmissions from any port interfere with all other ports.
   - A hub forms a **single collision domain** across all its ports.
   - Most modern active hubs support **CSMA/CD** at the connected NICs but **do not perform collision detection themselves** — they just propagate collision signals.

## 2.2 Classification of Hubs

| Hub Type | Signal Handling | Intelligence | Power Required | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive Hub** | No regeneration, just physical wiring connection | None | No (just cable junction) | Small wiring concentrators |
| **Active Hub** | Regenerates and amplifies signals (true repeater) | None (still L1) | Yes (external power) | Standard LAN hubs |
| **Intelligent Hub (Managed Hub)** | Active regeneration + basic monitoring | Minimal (L1/L2 diagnostics) | Yes | Network management |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** All three types are still considered **Layer 1 devices**. Even "intelligent hubs" do not break the L1 boundary for data forwarding — any intelligence is purely for **management/monitoring**, not for traffic forwarding decisions.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth per node (shared) | $BW_{node} = \dfrac{BW_{total}}{N}$ | Mbps | $N$ = number of active transmitting nodes |
| Total collision domain size | All hub ports share **one** collision domain | Logical | Critical for CSMA/CD |
| Maximum propagation delay (5-4-3) | $t_{prop} \le 25.6 \mu s$ (for 10 Mbps Ethernet) | Microseconds | $512 \text{ bit times} = 51.2 \mu s$ round-trip |
| Minimum frame size | $L_{min} = 2 \cdot t_{prop} \cdot BW$ | Bits | Required for collision detection |
| Number of hubs in series (10BASE-T) | At most **4** repeaters in path | Count | Governed by timing budget |
| Maximum nodes per segment | $\le 1024$ (10BASE-T standard) | Nodes | Practical limit is much lower |
| Maximum segment length | $\le 100$ m (UTP, 10/100BASE-TX) | Meters | Beyond this, attenuation too high |
| Signal attenuation budget | $\le 11.5$ dB (10BASE-T end-to-end) | Decibels | Including patch cables and connectors |
| Slot time (10 Mbps Ethernet) | $T_{slot} = 51.2 \mu s$ | Microseconds | $T_{slot} = 512 \text{ bit times}$ |
| Slot time (100 Mbps Ethernet) | $T_{slot} = 5.12 \mu s$ | Microseconds | $T_{slot} = 512 \text{ bit times}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule Applied:** All absolute values in equations above use **plain parenthesized expressions** instead of the `$\vert ... \vert$` syntax, to avoid breaking markdown table parsing. When writing in a free-text equation block (not inside a table), use $\vert$ or $\mid$ as appropriate.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although largely **obsolete in modern enterprise networks** (replaced by switches), hubs still find application in:

- **Network packet capture / sniffing** — When ethical hackers use Wireshark on a hubbed segment, they see ALL traffic (because the hub broadcasts to every port). Modern switches require **port mirroring (SPAN)** to achieve the same effect.
- **Industrial control loops** — Some legacy SCADA/ICS systems still use hubs for deterministic broadcast behavior.
- **Cable plant test fixtures** — Passive hubs are used as simple wiring concentrators in test labs.
- **Educational training kits** — Used in CCNA/Net+ labs to teach collision domain concepts before introducing switches.
- **Embedded / IoT debugging** — Sometimes used in microcontroller networks to broadcast debug packets to all nodes simultaneously.

## 2.5 The 5-4-3 Rule (Detailed)

In legacy **10BASE-T Ethernet** networks using hubs (repeaters), the **5-4-3 rule** was mandatory for proper CSMA/CD operation:

- **5** — Maximum of **5 segments** between any two nodes
- **4** — Maximum of **4 repeaters (hubs)** between any two nodes
- **3** — Maximum of **3 segments** that can have **end stations (nodes)** attached
- The remaining **2 segments** are **inter-repeater links** (only hubs at both ends, no end stations)

> [!WARNING]
> This rule is a **timing-budget rule**, not an electrical rule. It exists because signal propagation through **5 segments and 4 repeaters** must complete within the **51.2 $\mu$s slot time** required by 10 Mbps Ethernet CSMA/CD.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Frame Size for CSMA/CD on a Hubbed Network

The minimum frame size in Ethernet is **not arbitrary** — it is mathematically derived from the requirement that the **sender must still be transmitting** when the **first collision signal returns** to it. This is the **collision detection constraint**.

### Step 1: Define the fundamental constraint

The transmission time of the minimum frame must be at least equal to **twice the maximum one-way propagation delay** (round-trip time):

$$L_{min} \cdot t_{bit} \ge 2 \cdot t_{prop,max}$$

Where:
- $L_{min}$ = minimum frame length in bits
- $t_{bit}$ = time to transmit one bit (bit time)
- $t_{prop,max}$ = maximum one-way propagation delay between the two most distant nodes

### Step 2: Express bit time in terms of bandwidth

The bit time is the reciprocal of the bandwidth:

$$t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{BW}$$

Substituting:

$$L_{min} \cdot \dfrac{1}{BW} \ge 2 \cdot t_{prop,max}$$

### Step 3: Solve for minimum frame size

Rearranging for $L_{min}$:

$$L_{min} \ge 2 \cdot t_{prop,max} \cdot BW$$

### Step 4: Apply to standard 10 Mbps Ethernet (10BASE-T)

For 10BASE-T, the maximum allowed propagation delay through the **maximum legal 5-4-3 path** is **25.6 $\mu$s** one-way (51.2 $\mu$s round-trip). Substituting:

$$L_{min} \ge 2 \cdot (25.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{6})$$

$$L_{min} \ge 2 \cdot 25.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10 \times 10^{6}$$

$$L_{min} \ge 2 \cdot 25.6 \cdot 10$$

$$L_{min} \ge 512 \text{ bits}$$

$$L_{min} = 64 \text{ bytes (since } 512 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bytes)}$$

This is precisely why the **minimum Ethernet frame is 64 bytes** (excluding preamble and SFD).

### Step 5: Apply to 100 Mbps Ethernet (Fast Ethernet)

For 100BASE-TX, the slot time was reduced to 5.12 $\mu$s to keep the network diameter manageable:

$$T_{slot} = 5.12 \mu s$$

$$L_{min} = 5.12 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 100 \times 10^{6} = 512 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bytes}$$

The minimum frame size remains **64 bytes**, but the maximum network diameter **shrinks by a factor of 10** (from 2500 m to 250 m).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Hubs in Network Design

| Design Parameter | Hub Behavior | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Collision domain** | All ports = ONE collision domain | Total network load is shared; only one node can transmit successfully at a time |
| **Broadcast domain** | All ports = ONE broadcast domain | Broadcast storms affect every connected device |
| **Address learning** | None (Layer 1) | Every frame reaches every port — wasted bandwidth |
| **Forwarding decision** | None — pure fan-out | No security isolation between nodes |
| **Effective bandwidth per node** | $BW_{total} / N_{active}$ | Degrades linearly with active users |
| **Latency** | Bit propagation time only (no buffering) | Very low latency, but high collision probability |
| **Scalability** | Poor (typically $\le 4$ hubs in series) | Limited by 5-4-3 rule |
| **Security** | Inherently weak (all traffic visible to all) | Sniffing is trivial |
| **Cost (legacy)** | Very low | One of the few advantages |
| **Power consumption** | Low (passive) to moderate (active) | Less than switches in large deployments |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Bandwidth Calculation on a Hubbed Segment

**Problem:** A 100 Mbps Ethernet hub has **8 active nodes** connected. All 8 nodes are **simultaneously attempting to transmit**. After CSMA/CD arbitration, only **one node transmits at a time**, and the average number of **collision events per second** is observed to be **200**.

**Question:** Calculate the **effective bandwidth available per node** under steady state, assuming CSMA/CD has stabilized with no further collisions beyond those observed.

### Step 1: Determine the effective bandwidth

In a hubbed shared medium, the **total bandwidth of 100 Mbps** is shared among all nodes. With perfect CSMA/CD arbitration:

$$BW_{eff,node} = \dfrac{BW_{total}}{N_{active}} = \dfrac{100 \text{ Mbps}}{8}$$

$$BW_{eff,node} = 12.5 \text{ Mbps per node (theoretical maximum)}$$

### Step 2: Account for collision overhead

Each collision consumes **slot time** of idle waiting and retransmission. With 200 collisions per second and a slot time of 5.12 $\mu$s (for 100 Mbps):

$$T_{collision\ overhead} = 200 \times 5.12 \mu s = 1024 \mu s = 1.024 \text{ ms per second}$$

The fraction of time wasted in collision recovery:

$$f_{waste} = \dfrac{1.024 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ s}} = 0.001024 = 0.1024\%$$

### Step 3: Adjusted effective bandwidth

$$BW_{real,node} = BW_{eff,node} \times (1 - f_{waste})$$

$$BW_{real,node} = 12.5 \text{ Mbps} \times (1 - 0.001024)$$

$$BW_{real,node} \approx 12.487 \text{ Mbps per node}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The collision overhead is **negligible at low loads** but **catastrophic at high loads**, leading to the classic **Ethernet throughput collapse** as offered load approaches saturation. This is a primary reason hubs were replaced by switches.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hub Internal Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PORT 1: Input Signal"] --> B["SIGNAL CONDITIONER: Amplify Regenerate Retime"]
    C["PORT 2: Input Signal"] --> B
    D["PORT 3: Input Signal"] --> B
    E["PORT 4: Input Signal"] --> B
    F["PORT N: Input Signal"] --> B
    B --> G["FAN-OUT DISTRIBUTOR: Broadcast to all ports except source"]
    G --> H["PORT 1: Output"]
    G --> I["PORT 2: Output"]
    G --> J["PORT 3: Output"]
    G --> K["PORT 4: Output"]
    G --> L["PORT N: Output"]
```

**Interpretation:** Every input port feeds into a **shared signal conditioner**, which then fans the signal out to **all output ports simultaneously** (except the source port). The hub has **no memory of which port sent what** — it just amplifies and broadcasts.

## 4.2 Hubbed Network Topology with Single Collision Domain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HUB1["HUB CENTRAL DEVICE LAYER 1 MULTIPORT REPEATER"]
        P1["Port 1"]
        P2["Port 2"]
        P3["Port 3"]
        P4["Port 4"]
        P5["Port 5"]
        P6["Port 6"]
    end
    PC1["HOST A: PC Workstation"] --- P1
    PC2["HOST B: Server"] --- P2
    PC3["HOST C: Printer"] --- P3
    PC4["HOST D: Router"] --- P4
    PC5["HOST E: VoIP Phone"] --- P5
    PC6["HOST F: Workstation"] --- P6
    CD["SINGLE COLLISION DOMAIN: All 6 hosts share 1 domain"]
    HUB1 --- CD
```

**Interpretation:** All six end devices share **one common collision domain** because the hub provides no segmentation. If **Host A** and **Host B** transmit simultaneously, a **collision** occurs and is detected by both, triggering CSMA/CD backoff.

## 4.3 Hub Hierarchy Showing the 5-4-3 Rule

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["HUB 1: Segment 1 with end nodes"] --> H2["HUB 2: Inter repeater link"]
    H2 --> H3["HUB 3: Segment 3 with end nodes"]
    H3 --> H4["HUB 4: Inter repeater link"]
    H4 --> H5["HUB 5: Segment 5 with end nodes"]
    NT["Note: 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments"]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **maximum legal topology** under the 5-4-3 rule for 10BASE-T Ethernet. Beyond this, propagation delay would exceed the **25.6 $\mu$s budget** and CSMA/CD would fail.

## 4.4 Hub vs Switch — Logical Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HUB_DIAG["HUB TOPOLOGY"]
        H_CORE["Hub Core: Broadcast to ALL ports"]
        H_CORE --> HA["Node A: receives"]
        H_CORE --> HB["Node B: receives"]
        H_CORE --> HC["Node C: receives"]
        H_CORE --> HD["Node D: receives"]
    end
    subgraph SW_DIAG["SWITCH TOPOLOGY"]
        SW_CORE["Switch Core: MAC table lookup"]
        SW_CORE --> SA["Node A: receives only if A is destination"]
        SW_CORE --> SB["Node B: receives only if B is destination"]
        SW_CORE --> SC["Node C: receives only if C is destination"]
        SW_CORE --> SD["Node D: receives only if D is destination"]
    end
```

**Interpretation:** The hub **floods everywhere**; the switch **delivers selectively**. This single architectural difference is the **entire reason** hubs are obsolete in modern networks.

## 4.5 Signal Regeneration Process Inside a Hub

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WEAK INPUT SIGNAL: Attenuated Noisy Jittered"] --> B["AMPLIFIER STAGE: Restore voltage levels"]
    B --> C["CLOCK RECOVERY STAGE: Re time bit edges"]
    C --> D["EQUALIZATION STAGE: Compensate frequency distortion"]
    D --> E["STRONG OUTPUT SIGNAL: Clean Retimed Re amplified"]
    E --> F["FAN OUT TO ALL OTHER PORTS"]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **bit-level processing pipeline** of a hub. It treats the input as a **pure electrical waveform** and rebuilds it — without ever inspecting the **semantic content** of the bits.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a hub. Why is it classified as a Physical Layer device in the OSI model?**

**Model Answer:**

A **hub** is a physical layer (Layer 1) networking device that acts as a **central connection point** for multiple devices in a LAN. It functions as a **multiport repeater** — it receives an electrical signal on one port, regenerates and amplifies it, and **broadcasts it to all other active ports** simultaneously.

It is classified as a **Physical Layer device** because:
- It operates on **raw electrical signals (bits)**, not on frames or packets.
- It has **no awareness of MAC addresses** or any higher-layer addressing.
- It performs **no logical forwarding decisions** — only physical signal regeneration and fan-out.
- It does not understand the **structure of a frame** (no header parsing, no FCS checking).

The hub's only intelligence is in **reconditioning the electrical waveform** and **distributing it to all other ports**.

---

### Question 2: Collision Domain `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: All ports of a hub form a single collision domain. Justify this statement with a suitable diagram and explanation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **collision domain** is a network segment in which **simultaneous transmissions by two or more devices result in a signal collision**.

In a hub, all ports are **electrically interconnected** through a common shared bus internally. When a device connected to **Port 1** transmits, the signal is **broadcast to every other port** (2, 3, 4, …, N). If at the same moment a device on **Port 3** also transmits, the **two signals collide** on the shared internal medium, producing a garbled waveform at every other port.

This is why all ports of a hub are said to share **one single collision domain** — the electrical signal on any one port **directly interferes** with the signal on any other port. The hub provides **no segmentation, no buffering, and no collision isolation** between its ports.

This shared-collision-domain behavior is the **primary performance bottleneck** of hubs and the main reason they were replaced by **switches**, which create a **separate collision domain per port**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Hubs in Network Architecture `[KTU University Exam - May 2025]`

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **internal architecture** of a hub with a neat block diagram. Compare **passive, active, and intelligent hubs** in a tabular form.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] With a suitable example, explain the **5-4-3 rule** for 10BASE-T Ethernet networks using hubs. Why was this rule necessary, and what physical quantity does it constrain?

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a)

**Internal Architecture:**

The hub consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Input Ports (RJ-45 connectors)** — Receive attenuated electrical signals from connected devices via UTP cables.
2. **Signal Conditioner (Repeater Core)** — Performs three sub-functions:
   - **Amplification** — Boosts signal voltage back to standard levels.
   - **Regeneration** — Reconstructs the bit pattern from the noisy input.
   - **Retiming** — Re-clocks the bit edges to remove jitter.
3. **Fan-Out Distributor** — Routes the regenerated signal to **all output ports except the source port**.
4. **Output Ports** — Drive the conditioned signal out onto the attached cables.

**Tabular Comparison of Hub Types:**

| Feature | Passive Hub | Active Hub | Intelligent Hub |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Signal Regeneration** | None | Yes (full regeneration) | Yes (full regeneration) |
| **Power Supply** | Not required | Required (external) | Required (external) |
| **Layer Operation** | Pure wiring (L1) | L1 (signal regeneration) | L1 with management features |
| **Monitoring/Diagnostics** | None | Minimal (LED indicators) | Extensive (SNMP, port stats) |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | Moderate |
| **Typical Use** | Cable patch panels | Standard LANs | Managed enterprise networks |
| **Collision Domain** | Single (shared) | Single (shared) | Single (shared) |
| **Auto-partitioning** | No | Yes (on most models) | Yes (configurable) |

> All three types form a **single collision domain** and operate at **OSI Layer 1** for data forwarding.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b)

**The 5-4-3 Rule — Definition:**

The 5-4-3 rule is a **design guideline for legacy 10BASE-T Ethernet** networks using repeaters/hubs. It states:

- **5** = Maximum of **5 cable segments** in the end-to-end path between the two most distant nodes.
- **4** = Maximum of **4 repeaters (hubs)** in the path.
- **3** = Maximum of **3 segments** that can have **end stations (DTEs)** attached.
- The remaining **2 segments** must be **inter-repeater links** (no end stations — only hubs at both ends).

**Example:**

A 10BASE-T network with 5 hubs cascaded in series:
- Segment 1 → Hub 1 (with PCs) → Segment 2 → Hub 2 (no PCs) → Segment 3 → Hub 3 (with PCs) → Segment 4 → Hub 4 (no PCs) → Segment 5 → Hub 5 (with PCs)

This is the **maximum legal topology**. Adding a 6th segment or 5th repeater would violate the rule.

**Why the Rule Exists — The Physical Constraint:**

The rule constrains the **maximum propagation delay** of the network. For CSMA/CD to function correctly in 10 Mbps Ethernet, a collision must be detected by the sender before it finishes transmitting the minimum frame (64 bytes = 512 bit times = 51.2 $\mu$s).

Therefore, the **round-trip propagation delay** must be $\le 51.2 \mu s$, giving a **one-way maximum of 25.6 $\mu$s**.

Exceeding this delay means the sender finishes transmitting **before** the collision signal returns — making collision detection **impossible** and corrupting the network.

**Conclusion:** The 5-4-3 rule is fundamentally a **timing-budget rule** that ensures CSMA/CD reliability in hubbed Ethernet networks.

---

### Question B — Hubs vs Switches and Modern Relevance `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Differentiate between a **hub and a switch** in terms of OSI layer, collision domain, bandwidth allocation, and address learning. Why are hubs no longer used in modern enterprise networks?

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] A company has a hub-based 100 Mbps Ethernet network with **10 active nodes**. Calculate:
   (i) The theoretical maximum bandwidth per node under ideal CSMA/CD conditions.
   (ii) The minimum frame size required if the maximum one-way propagation delay is **20 $\mu$s**.
   (iii) Comment on whether the standard 64-byte minimum frame is sufficient.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a)

| Parameter | Hub | Switch |
|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
| **Collision Domain** | One single (all ports share) | One per port (microsegmentation) |
| **Broadcast Domain** | One single (all ports share) | One single (still shared) |
| **Bandwidth Allocation** | Shared ($BW/N$) | Dedicated ($BW$ per port) |
| **Address Learning** | None — no MAC table | Yes — builds MAC address table |
| **Forwarding Logic** | Broadcast to all ports | Selective (unicast/multicast/filter) |
| **Frame Processing** | None — bits only | Reads destination MAC, checks FCS |
| **Latency** | Very low (bit-level only) | Higher (buffering + table lookup) |
| **Cost** | Very low | Moderate |
| **Security** | Inherently weak (broadcast) | Better (port isolation, VLANs) |

**Why Hubs Are Obsolete:**

1. **Scalability** — Hubbed networks collapse under high load due to collisions.
2. **Security** — Every node sees every frame, making sniffing trivial.
3. **Performance** — Shared bandwidth per node degrades linearly with users.
4. **No QoS** — No traffic prioritization possible at Layer 1.
5. **Modern switches are cheap** — The cost advantage of hubs has disappeared.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b)

**(i) Bandwidth per node:**

$$BW_{node} = \dfrac{BW_{total}}{N_{active}} = \dfrac{100 \text{ Mbps}}{10} = 10 \text{ Mbps per node}$$

**(ii) Minimum frame size required:**

Using the CSMA/CD constraint:

$$L_{min} = 2 \cdot t_{prop,max} \cdot BW$$

$$L_{min} = 2 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (100 \times 10^{6})$$

$$L_{min} = 2 \cdot 20 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{6}$$

$$L_{min} = 2 \cdot 20 \cdot 100$$

$$L_{min} = 4000 \text{ bits} = 500 \text{ bytes}$$

**(iii) Is the standard 64-byte frame sufficient?**

The standard 10/100 Mbps Ethernet minimum frame size is **64 bytes = 512 bits**. However, our calculation shows the network requires a **minimum of 500 bytes** to detect collisions safely.

**Conclusion:** ❌ The standard 64-byte frame is **NOT sufficient** for this network. A frame could be transmitted entirely (in 51.2 $\mu$s) before a collision signal returns (which takes 40 $\mu$s round-trip in this case — wait, 40 $\mu$s < 51.2 $\mu$s, so technically the standard 64-byte minimum IS sufficient here because $2 \cdot 20 \mu s = 40 \mu s < 51.2 \mu s$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Note (Examiner's Perspective):** Re-examine the propagation delay: $2 \cdot 20 \mu s = 40 \mu s$, which is **less than** the 51.2 $\mu$s slot time. Therefore, the **standard 64-byte minimum IS sufficient** for collision detection in this network. The student must show this comparison step explicitly.

The "500-byte" figure represents the **theoretical minimum** for exactly this propagation delay; in practice, Ethernet hardware uses 64 bytes as a design constant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Most common mark-loss area #1:** Forgetting to convert $\mu$s to seconds and Mbps to bps in the formula $L_{min} = 2 \cdot t_{prop} \cdot BW$. Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> - **Most common mark-loss area #2:** Concluding that a non-standard minimum is "required" without comparing it to the actual slot time. Always compare your computed minimum to **51.2 $\mu$s (10 Mbps)** or **5.12 $\mu$s (100 Mbps)** to check sufficiency.
> - **Most common mark-loss area #3:** Confusing **bandwidth per node** (shared medium) with **port speed** (link rate). The link to each node still runs at 100 Mbps, but the **effective throughput per node** is 10 Mbps.
> - **Most common mark-loss area #4:** Saying "hubs are switches" or "hubs have MAC tables" — this is a **layering error** and costs full marks on the classification sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ A **hub** is a **Layer 1 (Physical)** device, also called a **multiport repeater**.
- ✅ It performs **signal regeneration, amplification, and broadcasting** to all ports except the source.
- ✅ It has **NO MAC address table, NO frame awareness, NO intelligent forwarding**.
- ✅ All ports of a hub share **ONE collision domain** and **ONE broadcast domain**.
- ✅ Bandwidth is **shared**: $BW_{node} = BW_{total} / N_{active}$.
- ✅ **Three types**: Passive (no regeneration), Active (regeneration), Intelligent (management features).
- ✅ **5-4-3 rule** applies to 10BASE-T hub networks: 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 with end nodes.
- ✅ The 5-4-3 rule constrains **propagation delay** to $\le 25.6 \mu s$ one-way (51.2 $\mu$s round-trip).
- ✅ **Minimum Ethernet frame** = 64 bytes (512 bits) = derived from CSMA/CD timing.
- ✅ Slot time: **51.2 $\mu$s** for 10 Mbps, **5.12 $\mu$s** for 100 Mbps.
- ✅ Hubs are **obsolete** in modern networks — replaced by **switches** (Layer 2) which provide **microsegmentation** and **dedicated bandwidth per port**.
- ✅ Hubs are still useful for **packet sniffing** (because of broadcast behavior) and **legacy industrial networks**.
- ✅ Hubs are sometimes called **concentrators** in older textbooks.
- ✅ **Auto-partitioning** in active hubs: a port with excessive collisions is automatically disconnected.
- ✅ Hubbed networks suffer from **CSMA/CD throughput collapse** under high load.
- ✅ Maximum **100 m** UTP segment length applies to hub connections (10/100BASE-TX).
- ✅ The hub does **NOT** perform **collision detection** — it relies on the connected NICs to do so via CSMA/CD.
- ✅ A hub does **NOT** extend the **collision domain** boundaries in the sense of timing — it merely **repeats** the electrical signal, preserving the timing budget constraint of the 5-4-3 rule.
- ✅ **Logical topology** of a hubbed network is a **star**, but **physical/electrical topology** is a **shared bus** internally.
- ✅ Hubs support only **half-duplex** communication (one direction at a time per port) because of the shared collision domain.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bridges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bridges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bridge (Data Link Layer Switch):** A **bridge** is a Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) networking device that interconnects two or more network segments at the **MAC sublayer**, operating with **48-bit MAC (Medium Access Control) addresses** to intelligently filter, flood, or forward frames between segments based on a dynamically learned **forwarding/filtering table**.

Bridges are functionally classified under the **IEEE 802.1D** standard and serve to:
- **Segment collision domains** (reducing CSMA/CD contention).
- **Extend LAN distance** by interconnecting physically distant segments.
- **Filter traffic** locally using destination MAC addresses.
- Provide **store-and-forward** or **cut-through** switching mechanisms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Post Office Sorting Office**
> Imagine a building (your LAN) with thousands of employees (hosts) sending letters (frames) addressed to one another. Instead of shouting across the building, all letters go to a **Sorting Office (Bridge)**. The office worker opens the letter (reads the MAC address), checks a big register (the filtering table) to identify the correct floor/department, and routes the letter accordingly. If the recipient's address is **unknown**, the letter is **duplicated and sent to all floors (flooding)** — but only once, until the address is memorized.

In this analogy:
- **Letter** = Ethernet Frame
- **Recipient's address** = Destination MAC Address
- **Sorting Office Register** = Forwarding/Filtering Table
- **Unknown recipient** = Flooding
- **Different buildings (segments)** = Different Collision Domains

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Bridges?

Bridges solve three classic Ethernet problems:
1. **Collision Domain Limitation:** As hosts increase, CSMA/CD degrades performance. Bridges split LANs into separate collision domains.
2. **Distance Extension:** Original 10BASE5 (Thick Ethernet) was limited to **500 meters** per segment. Bridges allow cascading segments to extend reach.
3. **Traffic Isolation:** Local traffic on one segment is not propagated to another, conserving bandwidth.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction:** A **Hub** repeats bits at Layer 1 (one big collision domain). A **Bridge** filters frames at Layer 2 (separate collision domains, same broadcast domain).

## 1.4 Key Physical/Standard Constants

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Layer** | **Layer 2 (Data Link)** | Uses MAC addresses |
| **Standard** | **IEEE 802.1D** | Defines transparent bridging |
| **MAC Address Size** | **48 bits (6 octets)** | Globally unique hardware address |
| **Address Format** | **XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX (Hex)** | Burned into NIC |
| **Spanning Tree Standard** | **IEEE 802.1D / 802.1w (RSTP)** | Loop prevention |
| **Filtering Table Size** | Thousands of entries | Dynamically learned |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Bridge

A bridge operates as a **store-and-forward** device by default, executing the following logic for every received frame:

1. **Frame Reception:** The bridge accepts the frame on an incoming port.
2. **Address Table Lookup:** It consults the forwarding table using the destination MAC address.
3. **Decision Phase (The Tri-State Logic):**
   - **Case A — Forward:** If destination MAC is known AND resides on a *different* port → forward out that specific port.
   - **Case B — Filter:** If destination MAC is on the *same* port as source → discard (filter) the frame.
   - **Case C — Flood:** If destination MAC is *unknown* (not in table) → broadcast to all ports except the incoming one.
4. **Learning Phase:** The bridge records the **source MAC address** + **incoming port** + **timestamp** in the filtering table.

## 2.2 Bridge Filtering Table (Forwarding Database)

| Field | Description | Size |
|---|---|---|
| **MAC Address** | Destination/Source hardware address | **48 bits** |
| **Port Number** | Bridge interface where the host is reachable | 1 byte+ |
| **Age / Timestamp** | Last seen time for entry aging (typically **300 seconds**) | Integer |

> [!NOTE]
> Aging is critical — when an entry's age timer expires, the entry is purged. This handles topology changes gracefully.

## 2.3 Types of Bridges (KTU High-Yield)

### A. Transparent Bridge
- **Used in Ethernet LANs (IEEE 802.1D standard)**
- Hosts are *unaware* of the bridge's existence.
- Three core functions: **Learning, Forwarding, Flooding**.
- Requires **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)** to prevent loops.

### B. Source Routing Bridge
- **Used in Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) networks.**
- The **source host** embeds the entire route (a list of ring/bridge IDs) inside the frame header.
- The bridge merely reads the route and forwards; no MAC table required.
- Uses **explorer frames (all-routes broadcast)** to discover paths.

### C. Translational Bridge
- Connects networks of **different types** (e.g., Ethernet ↔ Token Ring).
- Performs **frame format translation** (changing headers, MTU, addressing format).
- Limited support; largely obsolete today.

## 2.4 Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) — Loop Prevention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When multiple bridges/paths exist between segments, **switching loops** occur, causing:
> - **Broadcast storms** (infinite frame circulation)
> - **MAC table instability** (flickering entries)
> - **Duplicate frames**

**STP Algorithm (Radialia Perlman's algorithm):**
1. Elect a **Root Bridge** (lowest Bridge ID = Bridge Priority + MAC address).
2. Compute the **lowest cost path** to the root for every switch (Root Port).
3. For each non-root segment, select the **Designated Bridge & Designated Port** (lowest cost to root).
4. **Block** all redundant ports (Alternate/Backup ports) → creates a *loop-free tree*.

**Path Cost Formula (Modern IEEE 802.1D-2004):**

$$\text{Path Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{LinkCost}_i$$

where link cost is inversely proportional to bandwidth:

$$\text{LinkCost} = \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Link Bandwidth}}$$

Reference Bandwidth = **20,000,000** (for 802.1D-1998) or **2,000,000,000** (for 802.1t revised).

| Link Speed | STP Cost (802.1D-1998) | STP Cost (802.1t Revised) |
|---|---|---|
| **10 Mbps** | **100** | 2,000,000 |
| **100 Mbps** | **19** | 200,000 |
| **1 Gbps** | **4** | 20,000 |
| **10 Gbps** | **2** | 2,000 |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Forwarding Decision** | $\text{Decision} = f(\text{DestMAC}, \text{SourcePort}, \text{Table})$ | Tri-state: Forward / Filter / Flood |
| **Aging Timer** | $T_{\text{age}} = 300 \text{ seconds}$ (default) | IEEE 802.1D default |
| **Bridge ID** | $\text{ID} = (\text{Priority} \times 2^{16}) \oplus \text{MAC}$ | 8-byte identifier |
| **Root Bridge Election** | Lowest Bridge ID wins | Priority is **0–61440** in steps of 4096 |
| **STP Path Cost** | $C = \sum \text{LinkCost}_i$ | Lower cost = preferred |
| **Total End-to-End Delay** | $D_{\text{total}} = D_{\text{prop}} + D_{\text{trans}} + D_{\text{queue}}$ | Through bridge store-and-forward |
| **Effective LAN Bandwidth** | $B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N_{\text{segments}} \times B}{1}$ | Each segment independent collision domain |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise LANs:** Bridges are the conceptual ancestors of modern **switches**, which are essentially multi-port bridges.
- **Campus Networks:** STP prevents loops in redundant fiber rings (common in metro Ethernet).
- **Industrial Control:** Translational bridges historically connected fieldbus and Ethernet in SCADA systems.
- **Data Center Fabrics:** Modern equivalents use **TRILL (RFC 6325)** or **SPB (IEEE 802.1aq)** to overcome STP's blocking inefficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Example: Bridge Learning & Forwarding

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** Three LAN segments (LAN1, LAN2, LAN3) connected by two bridges (B1, B2). Host A on LAN1 sends a frame to Host D on LAN2. Trace the bridge's learning and decision process.

### Step 1: Initial State — Empty Bridge Tables

| Bridge | MAC Table |
|---|---|
| B1 | Empty |
| B2 | Empty |

### Step 2: Host A (MAC: AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA) on LAN1 sends frame to Host D (MAC: DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD) on LAN2.

**B1 receives frame on Port 1 (connected to LAN1).**

**Step 2a — Learning Step:**
B1 records the source MAC in its table:

| MAC | Port | Timer |
|---|---|---|
| AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | 1 | 0 sec |

**Step 2b — Lookup Step:**
B1 searches for destination MAC **DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD** → **NOT FOUND**.

**Step 2c — Flooding Step:**
B1 floods the frame to all ports *except* Port 1 → sends out Port 2 (to LAN2) and Port 3 (to LAN3, if present).

### Step 3: B2 receives flooded frame on its Port connected to LAN2.

B2 also performs the same learning: records **AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA** mapped to its incoming port.

### Step 4: Host D replies.

Host D sends a frame with:
- Source MAC = DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD
- Destination MAC = AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA

B2 receives the reply on its LAN2 port.

**B2's Table After Learning:**

| MAC | Port |
|---|---|
| AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | Port-to-LAN1 (learned earlier) |
| DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD | Port-to-LAN2 (current source) |

**Lookup:** Destination AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA → **FOUND on LAN1 port** → forward selectively to LAN1.

**B1 receives reply:**

| MAC | Port |
|---|---|
| AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | Port-1 (LAN1) |
| DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD | Port-2 (LAN2) — newly learned |

**B1 forwards to Port 1** (LAN1), where Host A resides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Outcome:** After the first exchange, **both bridges have learned the location of all participating MACs**. Subsequent frames between A and D are forwarded selectively, **not flooded** — conserving bandwidth on LAN3.

---

## 3.2 STP Path Cost Calculation — Full Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Three switches (S1, S2, S3) in a triangle. S1 is root (Bridge ID = 32768 + MAC1). Determine which port gets blocked.

### Topology

```
        S2
       /  \
   100Mbps 1Gbps
     /      \
    S1------S3
       10Mbps
```

### Step 1: Identify Root Bridge
- **S1 is root** (lowest Bridge ID assumed).

### Step 2: Compute Cost from Each Non-Root Switch to Root

**For S2:**
- Path to S1 directly: **100 Mbps link**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{S2→S1}} = 19 \quad (\text{802.1D-1998 table})$$

- Path via S3: S2→S3 (1 Gbps = cost 4) + S3→S1 (10 Mbps = cost 100)

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{S2→S3→S1}} = 4 + 100 = 104$$

**S2 selects Root Port:** Direct link to S1 (cost 19 < 104).

**For S3:**
- Path to S1 directly: **10 Mbps**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{S3→S1}} = 100$$

- Path via S2: S3→S2 (1 Gbps = 4) + S2→S1 (100 Mbps = 19)

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{S3→S2→S1}} = 4 + 19 = 23$$

**S3 selects Root Port:** Path via S2 (cost 23 < 100).

### Step 3: Elect Designated Bridge per Segment

**Segment S1–S2:** Direct link. Both are candidates.
- S1's cost to root = 0
- S2's cost to root = 19
- **S1 wins (lower cost)** → S1's port is **Designated Port**. S2's port is also Designated (it's the root port for S2, but the designated port is on the segment — S1 is designated bridge, so S1's port is designated, and S2's port is root port, NOT designated).

**Segment S2–S3:** Both S2 and S3 attach to it.
- S2's cost to root = 19
- S3's cost to root = 23
- **S2 wins** → S2's port toward S3 is **Designated Port**.

**Segment S1–S3:** Both S1 and S3 attach to it.
- S1's cost = 0
- S3's cost = 23
- **S1 wins** → S1's port is **Designated Port**. S3's port becomes **Non-Designated (BLOCKED)**.

### Step 4: Final STP State

| Port | Role | Status |
|---|---|---|
| S1 → S2 | Designated | **Forwarding** |
| S1 → S3 | Designated | **Forwarding** |
| S2 → S1 | Root | **Forwarding** |
| S2 → S3 | Designated | **Forwarding** |
| S3 → S1 | — | **BLOCKED** |
| S3 → S2 | Root | **Forwarding** |

> [!TIP]
> The blocked port sits on S3's link to S1, breaking the triangle. No loops → no broadcast storm.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Hub (Layer 1) | Bridge (Layer 2) | Switch (Multi-port Bridge) | Router (Layer 3) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Physical | Data Link | Data Link | Network |
| **Uses** | Bits | MAC addresses | MAC addresses | IP addresses |
| **Collision Domains** | **1 (shared)** | **2 per bridge** | **N per switch** | **N per port** |
| **Broadcast Domains** | 1 | 1 | 1 (by default) | Many |
| **Loops Prevented By** | N/A | **STP** | **STP** | Routing protocols (OSPF) |
| **Forwarding Method** | Repeat bits | Store-and-forward | Cut-through / Store | Route lookup |
| **Intelligent?** | No | **Yes** (MAC learning) | **Yes** (CAM table) | **Yes** (routing table) |
| **Speed** | Slowest | Moderate | **Fastest (L2)** | Moderate (L3 lookup) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bridge Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inbound Ethernet Frame] --> B[Frame Reception Buffer]
    B --> C[Parse MAC Header]
    C --> D{SRC MAC in Table?}
    D -- No --> E[Add Entry: SRC MAC to Incoming Port]
    D -- Yes --> F[Refresh Aging Timer]
    E --> G{DEST MAC in Table?}
    F --> G
    G -- Yes, same port --> H[FILTER: Discard Frame]
    G -- Yes, different port --> I[FORWARD: Send to Specified Port]
    G -- No --> J[FLOOD: Send to All Ports Except Incoming]
    H --> K[End of Process]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Three-Segment Bridge Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAN1
        HostA[Host A MAC AA]
        HostB[Host B MAC BB]
    end
    subgraph LAN2
        HostC[Host C MAC CC]
        HostD[Host D MAC DD]
    end
    subgraph LAN3
        HostE[Host E MAC EE]
        HostF[Host F MAC FF]
    end
    Bridge1[Bridge 1]
    Bridge2[Bridge 2]
    HostA --- Bridge1
    HostB --- Bridge1
    Bridge1 --- HostC
    Bridge1 --- HostE
    Bridge1 --- Bridge2
    HostD --- Bridge2
    HostF --- Bridge2
```

## 4.3 Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) Loop Prevention Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Topology with Redundant Links] --> B1[All Switches Send BPDUs]
    B1 --> B2[Elect Root Bridge: Lowest Bridge ID]
    B2 --> B3[Each Switch Computes Lowest Cost Path to Root]
    B3 --> B4[Select Root Port per Non-Root Switch]
    B4 --> B5[For Each LAN Segment, Elect Designated Bridge]
    B5 --> B6[Designated Port = Forwarding]
    B5 --> B7[Non-Designated Port = BLOCKED]
    B7 --> B8[Loop-Free Logical Tree Established]
    B6 --> B8
```

## 4.4 Bridge Tri-State Decision Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FrameReceived
    FrameReceived --> Learning: Update Table with SRC MAC
    Learning --> Lookup: Search DEST MAC
    Lookup --> Filter: DEST on Same Port
    Lookup --> Forward: DEST on Different Port
    Lookup --> Flood: DEST Unknown
    Filter --> [*]
    Forward --> [*]
    Flood --> [*]
```

## 4.5 Layered Position of a Bridge in the OSI/TCP Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Application Layer]
    L6[Presentation Layer]
    L5[Session Layer]
    L4[Transport Layer]
    L3[Network Layer]
    L2[Data Link Layer - BRIDGE OPERATES HERE]
    L1[Physical Layer]
    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L2 -.Uses 48-bit MAC addresses.-> L2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define a Bridge. List the three main functions performed by a transparent bridge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** A bridge is a Layer 2 device that connects two or more LAN segments and uses MAC addresses to filter or forward frames.

**Three main functions of a transparent bridge (2 marks):**

1. **Learning** — Examining the source MAC address of incoming frames and recording them in the bridge's filtering table along with the port number on which they were received.
2. **Forwarding** — When a frame's destination MAC is in the table and located on a different port, the bridge forwards the frame out that specific port only.
3. **Flooding** — When the destination MAC is not in the table, the bridge forwards the frame out of all ports except the one it arrived on.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between a Hub, a Bridge, and a Router in terms of OSI layer, addressing, and broadcast domain handling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Device | OSI Layer | Addressing Used | Broadcast Domain | Collision Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hub** | Layer 1 (Physical) | None (repeats bits) | One | One (shared) |
| **Bridge** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | **MAC address (48-bit)** | One | **Splits into 2+** |
| **Router** | Layer 3 (Network) | **IP address (32-bit / 128-bit)** | **Separates** | Splits into N |

A hub is a dumb repeater, a bridge intelligently filters by MAC, and a router separates broadcast domains and routes packets between networks. (1 mark for layer + addressing clarity, 1 mark for broadcast domain, 1 mark for distinction explanation)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a) Explain in detail the operation of a Transparent Bridge with reference to its learning, filtering, and forwarding processes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **transparent bridge** operates in a manner that is "transparent" to the end hosts — the hosts are unaware of the bridge's presence. It uses a **filtering/forwarding table** (also called the **forwarding database, FDB**) that is dynamically built.

**1. Learning Process (2 marks):**
When a frame arrives at any port of the bridge, the bridge reads the **source MAC address** from the frame header. If this MAC is not already in the table, a new entry is created:

$$\text{Table Entry} = \{\text{SRC\_MAC}, \text{Incoming\_Port}, \text{Age\_Timer} = 0\}$$

If the MAC already exists, the **age timer is reset** to zero. By default, entries age out after **300 seconds** of inactivity.

**2. Filtering Process (2 marks):**
If the destination MAC address of an incoming frame is found in the table, and the corresponding port is the **same** as the incoming port, the frame is **filtered (discarded)**. This conserves bandwidth on other segments because the frame does not need to cross the bridge to reach a host on the same segment.

**3. Forwarding Process (2 marks):**
If the destination MAC is in the table and is on a **different** port, the bridge forwards the frame **only out that specific port** (selective forwarding). This isolates traffic and reduces collisions.

**4. Flooding Process (1 mark):**
If the destination MAC is **not** in the table, the bridge **floods** the frame to all ports except the incoming one. This is a one-time event — once the destination replies, the bridge learns its location and stops flooding.

> [!TIP]
> The bridge thus achieves **adaptive learning** with no manual configuration (plug-and-play).

---

#### **(b) With a suitable diagram, explain the Spanning Tree Protocol. How does it prevent switching loops? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Problem: Switching Loops (2 marks)**
When redundant paths exist between switches/bridges (for fault tolerance), broadcast or unknown-unicast frames get **forwarded indefinitely**, causing:
- **Broadcast storms** (consuming all bandwidth)
- **MAC table instability** (entries constantly refresh)
- **Multiple frame copies** delivered to hosts

**Solution: Spanning Tree Protocol — Radia Perlman's Algorithm (3 marks)**

1. **Root Bridge Election:** All bridges exchange **BPDUs (Bridge Protocol Data Units)**. The bridge with the **lowest Bridge ID** (priority + MAC) is elected as the **root bridge** of the topology.
2. **Root Port Selection:** Every non-root bridge selects one **Root Port** — the port offering the lowest cumulative path cost to the root.
3. **Designated Bridge/Port:** For each LAN segment, the bridge with the lowest cost to the root becomes the **Designated Bridge**, and its port on that segment is the **Designated Port** (forwarding state).
4. **Blocking Redundant Ports:** All remaining ports are placed in a **Blocking (Discarding) state**, breaking the loop.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
      [S2]
     /     \
  S1 ------- S3
   \       /
    \_____/  <-- Link between S1 and S3 blocked
```

The blocked link is logically "removed" by STP, creating a **tree structure** with no cycles. If a forwarding link fails, STP re-converges and unblocks an alternate path automatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> STP convergence typically takes **30–50 seconds** (Forward Delay = 15s × 2). The faster **RSTP (802.1w)** reduces this to under 6 seconds.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(a) Describe the Source Routing Bridge used in Token Ring networks. Compare it with Transparent Bridging. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Source Routing Bridge — Concept (3 marks):**
Source routing is a bridging mechanism standardized in **IEEE 802.5** for **Token Ring** networks. Unlike transparent bridging, the **routing decision is made by the sending host**, not the bridge.

- The source host discovers routes by sending **explorer frames** (also called **all-routes explorer** or **spanning-tree explorer**).
- These explorer frames traverse *all possible paths* through the network, and each bridge stamps its identifier onto the frame.
- When explorer frames reach the destination, the destination returns them to the source. The source now knows the **complete route** to the destination.
- The source host then embeds the **full route (ring number, bridge number sequence)** in the **Routing Information Field (RIF)** of every subsequent frame.
- Bridges do **not maintain MAC tables**; they simply read the RIF and forward the frame out the next segment.

**Comparison with Transparent Bridging (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Transparent Bridge | Source Routing Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| **Used In** | Ethernet (IEEE 802.1D) | Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) |
| **Routing Decision** | **Bridge** decides | **Source Host** decides |
| **MAC Table** | Maintained dynamically | **Not required** |
| **Frame Format** | Standard Ethernet header | Header has **RIF** field |
| **Route Discovery** | Passive learning | **Active explorer frames** |
| **Loop Handling** | **STP** | Single-route broadcasts (SRT) |
| **Host Complexity** | Simple | Higher (host must compute route) |
| **Bridge Complexity** | Higher (table, STP) | Lower (just forward by RIF) |
| **Bandwidth Overhead** | Low | High (explorer flood) |

---

#### **(b) Consider three bridges B1, B2, B3 interconnecting three LANs. Each bridge has a filtering table. Host H1 (MAC: 11) on LAN1 sends a frame to Host H2 (MAC: 22) on LAN2. Show the step-by-step MAC learning at each bridge. What is the role of flooding in this process? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Initial State:** All three bridges B1, B2, B3 have **empty filtering tables**.

**Step 1: H1 sends frame (SRC=11, DEST=22) onto LAN1. (1 mark)**

**Step 2: B1 receives the frame on its LAN1 port. (1 mark)**
- B1's **learning action:** It records SRC MAC 11 → Port 1 (LAN1).
- B1's **lookup action:** It searches for DEST MAC 22 → **NOT FOUND**.
- B1's **flooding action:** It floods the frame out all other ports (LAN2, LAN3) except Port 1.

| B1 Table | |
|---|---|
| MAC | Port |
| 11 | 1 (LAN1) |

**Step 3: B2 (assuming it's connected to LAN2 and LAN3) receives the flooded frame on its LAN2 port. (1 mark)**
- B2 records SRC MAC 11 → Port (LAN2 side).
- B2 looks up DEST 22 → not found → floods to its other ports (LAN3, etc.).

| B2 Table | |
|---|---|
| MAC | Port |
| 11 | LAN2 port |

**Step 4: H2 receives the frame (delivered via LAN2). H2 replies with SRC=22, DEST=11. (1 mark)**

**Step 5: B2 receives the reply on its LAN2 port. (1 mark)**
- B2 records SRC 22 → LAN2 port.
- B2 looks up DEST 11 → **FOUND** (learned earlier on LAN2 port).
