- Since DEST and SRC are on the same port → **FILTER (discard)** or forward depending on exact port numbering; if H2 is on a different LAN2 host port, it forwards locally on LAN2.

| B2 Table | |
|---|---|
| MAC | Port |
| 11 | LAN2 port |
| 22 | LAN2 port |

**Step 6: Reply reaches B1. (1 mark)**
- B1 records SRC 22 → LAN2 port.
- B1 looks up DEST 11 → **FOUND on Port 1 (LAN1)**.
- B1 forwards selectively to LAN1 → H1 receives the reply.

| B1 Table | |
|---|---|
| MAC | Port |
| 11 | 1 (LAN1) |
| 22 | 2 (LAN2) |

**Role of Flooding (1 mark):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Flooding** is the bridge's mechanism for handling **unknown destinations**. It guarantees the first-time delivery of a frame when the destination's location is not yet learned. Although inefficient, flooding is a *one-time event* — once any host replies, all bridges on the path learn the MAC, and subsequent traffic is forwarded selectively. Flooding is therefore a self-correcting learning aid.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Bridge Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing bridge with switch:** Marks deducted if students claim a switch is a "Layer 3 device" or if a bridge is said to operate at the network layer. **Bridges are STRICTLY Layer 2.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the aging mechanism:** Many students describe the filtering table as static. Examiners award a mark specifically for mentioning the **300-second aging timer** and the fact that entries are *dynamically* refreshed.
> 
> 3. **STP path cost wrong values:** Using the wrong STP cost table. For 100 Mbps, cost = **19** (802.1D-1998) or **200,000** (802.1t). Mixing these in one answer loses marks.
> 
> 4. **Confusing "Flooding" with "Broadcasting":** Flooding is a *unicast frame with unknown destination* being sent to all ports *except the source port*. Broadcasting is a *broadcast frame* (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) sent to **all ports including source**.
> 
> 5. **Source Routing misattribution:** Some students wrongly say source routing is used in Ethernet. It is **Token Ring / IEEE 802.5**.
> 
> 6. **Bridge ID calculation:** The Bridge ID is **8 bytes = 2 bytes (Priority) + 6 bytes (MAC)**. The priority is in increments of **4096** (default 32768). The lowest ID is the root.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the diagram:** In 7-mark questions on STP or Transparent Bridging, a diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Bridges"**

- ✅ A **bridge** is a **Layer 2 (Data Link Layer)** device that uses **48-bit MAC addresses** to filter and forward frames between LAN segments.
- ✅ Bridges are governed by the **IEEE 802.1D** standard.
- ✅ Bridges **split collision domains** but keep a **single broadcast domain**.
- ✅ A bridge is the conceptual predecessor of a modern **multi-port switch**.
- ✅ The three core functions of a transparent bridge are: **Learning, Filtering, Forwarding (and Flooding when needed)**.
- ✅ **Learning =** record source MAC + incoming port in the filtering table.
- ✅ **Filtering =** discard the frame if destination MAC is on the *same* port.
- ✅ **Forwarding =** send the frame out a *specific* port if the destination MAC is on a different port.
- ✅ **Flooding =** send the frame out *all ports except the incoming* one if the destination MAC is unknown.
- ✅ Aging timer is **300 seconds (5 minutes)** by default; entries are purged on expiry.
- ✅ Three types of bridges: **Transparent (Ethernet), Source Routing (Token Ring), Translational (interconnecting different LANs).**
- ✅ **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)** prevents switching loops caused by redundant links.
- ✅ STP follows Radia Perlman's algorithm: elect **Root Bridge → Root Ports → Designated Ports → Block the rest**.
- ✅ Root Bridge = lowest **Bridge ID (Priority + MAC)**.
- ✅ STP path cost is inversely proportional to link bandwidth. For 100 Mbps, cost = **19** (802.1D-1998).
- ✅ BPDUs (Bridge Protocol Data Units) are exchanged between bridges to run STP.
- ✅ STP convergence takes **30–50 seconds**; **RSTP (802.1w)** reduces this to **< 6 seconds**.
- ✅ **Source Routing Bridge** is used in **Token Ring (802.5)**: source host embeds the route in the **RIF** of the frame.
- ✅ **Translational Bridge** converts frame formats between heterogeneous LANs (e.g., Ethernet ↔ Token Ring).
- ✅ **Hub = Layer 1** (repeats bits, one collision domain). **Bridge = Layer 2** (filters by MAC, multiple collision domains). **Router = Layer 3** (routes by IP, separate broadcast domains).
- ✅ Bridges use **store-and-forward** by default; modern switches may use **cut-through** for lower latency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Switches

## 1.1 What is a Network Switch?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of Computer Networks, a **Switch** is a Layer-2 (Data Link Layer) or Layer-3 (Network Layer) device that connects multiple devices within a network and uses **packet switching** techniques to forward data frames or packets from a source to a destination based on **MAC addresses** (Layer-2 switch) or **IP addresses** (Layer-3 switch).

Formally, a switch is a multi-port networking device that:
- Receives incoming frames/packets on one of its input ports.
- Examines the destination address.
- Forwards the frame/packet to the correct output port using a forwarding table.
- Operates with minimal processing delay compared to traditional routers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A network switch is a device that connects devices together on a computer network by using **packet switching** to receive, process, and forward data to the destination device. Unlike a hub which broadcasts to all ports, a switch intelligently forwards only to the intended recipient.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Switching Techniques (KTU Module 2 Focus)

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, **Switches** primarily refers to the **switching mechanisms** used in telecommunications and computer networks. There are **three fundamental switching techniques**:

### A) Circuit Switching
A dedicated communication path (circuit) is established between sender and receiver **before** any data transmission. The path remains open for the entire duration of the communication.

### B) Message Switching
The entire message is treated as a single unit and is **stored at each intermediate switch** before being forwarded to the next hop. No dedicated path is reserved.

### C) Packet Switching
The message is **broken into smaller packets**, each packet may take a different route, and packets are reassembled at the destination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Telephone Call vs. The Post Office:**
> - **Circuit Switching** = A dedicated telephone line is reserved for your call. Even when you are silent, the line stays open and billed.
> - **Message Switching** = Sending a complete letter through multiple post offices. Each post office holds the entire letter before forwarding.
> - **Packet Switching** = Sending a book by tearing out pages and mailing each page separately through different post offices. The receiver reassembles the book.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Switches? (The Problem They Solve)

Imagine **8 devices** that need to communicate with each other. Without a switch:
- A **mesh topology** requires $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{8 \times 7}{2} = 28$ links. This is impractical.

With a switch:
- Only **8 links** are needed (one per device).
- The switch intelligently directs traffic.

**Key Performance Metrics for Switches (KTU Board Emphasis):**
- **Throughput:** Amount of data successfully transmitted per unit time.
- **Latency (Delay):** Time taken for a packet to travel from source to destination.
- **Packet Loss Probability:** Likelihood that a packet will be dropped due to congestion.
- **Jitter:** Variation in packet arrival times (critical for real-time applications).

---

## 1.4 Switch vs. Hub vs. Router (Quick Differentiation Table)

| Feature | Hub | Switch | Router |
|---|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) |
| Address Used | None (MAC broadcast) | **MAC Address** | **IP Address** |
| Forwarding Method | Broadcast to all ports | Selective forwarding | Path determination (routing) |
| Collision Domain | Single (shared) | Separate per port | Separate per port |
| Intelligence | None | **Forwarding table** | Routing table |
| Typical Use | Legacy networks | LANs | Inter-network (WANs) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A switch creates **separate collision domains** for each port, while a hub creates a **single shared collision domain**. This is a frequently tested concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Switching Paradigms — In-Depth Analysis

### 2.1.1 Circuit Switching (The "Reserved Highway" Model)

**Operational Principle:** A dedicated physical path is established between source and destination **before** data transfer begins. This path is maintained throughout the session.

**Three Phases of Circuit Switching:**
1. **Circuit Establishment:** Source sends a request to the network. Switches reserve a dedicated path.
2. **Data Transfer:** Data flows through the reserved path with no queuing delay.
3. **Circuit Teardown:** After transmission, the path is released.

**Implementation Methods within Circuit Switching:**
- **Space Division Switching:** Uses physically separate paths (crossbar switches).
- **Time Division Switching:** Uses time slots on a shared medium (TDM — Time Division Multiplexing).

**Pros:**
- Guaranteed bandwidth once circuit is established.
- No congestion during data transfer (since path is reserved).
- Suitable for real-time voice traffic (traditional PSTN telephone networks).

**Cons:**
- **Inefficient utilization:** Even during silence, the circuit remains occupied.
- **Setup delay:** Significant time spent establishing the circuit.
- **Single point of failure:** If any link in the circuit fails, the connection breaks.

---

### 2.1.2 Message Switching (The "Store-and-Forward Postal" Model)

**Operational Principle:** The entire message is sent as a single unit. Each intermediate switch **stores the entire message**, then forwards it when the next link is free.

**Why "Store-and-Forward"?**
Every switch along the path must receive the **complete message** before it can begin transmitting it onward. This is the hallmark of message switching.

**Pros:**
- **No dedicated path required** — improves network utilization.
- Switches can perform error checking and correction on the entire message.
- Traffic can be balanced across multiple paths.

**Cons:**
- **High latency** — entire message must arrive at each hop before forwarding.
- **Large buffer requirement** at each switch.
- Not suitable for real-time or interactive applications.

---

### 2.1.3 Packet Switching (The Modern Internet Backbone)

**Operational Principle:** The message is divided into smaller units called **packets**. Each packet has a **header** (containing source/destination addresses, sequence number) and a **payload** (the actual data).

**Two Sub-Models of Packet Switching:**

**(a) Datagram Packet Switching (Connectionless):**
- Each packet is treated **independently**.
- Packets may take **different routes** to the destination.
- Packets may arrive **out of order**.
- The destination reassembles them using sequence numbers.
- Example: **IP (Internet Protocol)**.

**(b) Virtual Circuit Packet Switching (Connection-Oriented):**
- A **logical path (virtual circuit)** is established before data transfer.
- All packets follow the **same path**.
- Packets arrive **in order**.
- Example: **X.25, Frame Relay, ATM, MPLS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Distinction:**
> In **Virtual Circuit switching**, all packets follow the **same route**, but resources are **not dedicated** (unlike circuit switching). This is called a "virtual" circuit because the path appears dedicated but actually shares physical resources.

---

## 2.2 Four Sources of Packet Delay (Forouzaser Model — KTU Favorite)

Every packet experiences **four types of delay** in a packet-switched network. This is one of the most frequently tested concepts in KTU exams.

### 2.2.1 Nodal Processing Delay ($d_{proc}$)
- Time taken by a switch/router to examine the packet header and determine the output port.
- Typically: **microseconds to milliseconds**.
- Symbol: $d_{proc}$

### 2.2.2 Queuing Delay ($d_{queue}$)
- Time a packet waits in the buffer/queue before being transmitted.
- **Variable** — depends on congestion level at the router.
- Symbol: $d_{queue}$

### 2.2.3 Transmission Delay ($d_{trans}$)
- Time taken to push all bits of a packet onto the link.
- **Deterministic** — can be calculated exactly.
- Formula: $d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}$
- Where: $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bits/second).
- Symbol: $d_{trans}$

### 2.2.4 Propagation Delay ($d_{prop}$)
- Time taken for a signal to travel from one end of the link to the other.
- **Physical property** — depends on distance and medium.
- Formula: $d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s}$
- Where: $d$ = length of physical link (meters), $s$ = propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper, $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum).

---

## 2.3 Total Nodal Delay Formula

The total delay experienced by a packet at a single node (switch/router) is:

$$d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$$

The total **end-to-end delay** across $N$ identical nodes is:

$$d_{end-to-end} = N \times (d_{proc} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}) + d_{queue\_total}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **store-and-forward** network, the transmission delay at each node is **NOT optional** — the entire packet must be received before forwarding. This is a KTU exam favorite.

---

## 2.4 End-to-End Delay Through Multiple Switches (Generalized)

For a packet of length $L$ traversing $N$ switches, each with transmission rate $R$, and $N+1$ links each of length $d$ with propagation speed $s$:

$$d_{end-to-end} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + (N+1) \cdot \frac{d}{s}$$

This formula assumes:
- Processing and queuing delays are **negligible**.
- No congestion.

---

## 2.5 Throughput Calculation

**Throughput** is the rate at which data is successfully delivered.

### 2.5.1 Instantaneous Throughput
Rate at a specific instant of time (bits/second).

### 2.5.2 Average Throughput
Total data delivered / Total time taken.

**For a file of size $F$ bits transmitted over $N$ links with bottleneck bandwidth $R$:**

$$\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_N) = \text{Bottleneck Rate}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission Delay | $d_{trans} = L / R$ | $L$ in bits, $R$ in bps |
| 2 | Propagation Delay | $d_{prop} = d / s$ | $d$ in meters, $s$ in m/s |
| 3 | Total Nodal Delay | $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$ | Seconds |
| 4 | End-to-End Delay ($N$ hops) | $d_{e2e} = N \cdot (L/R) + (N+1) \cdot (d/s)$ | Seconds |
| 5 | Bottleneck Throughput | $\min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_N)$ | bits/second |
| 6 | File Transfer Time | $T = F / R_{bottleneck}$ | Seconds |
| 7 | RTT (Round Trip Time) | $RTT = 2 \cdot d_{prop}$ | Seconds |
| 8 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = R \cdot d_{prop}$ | bits |
| 9 | Utilization (Stop-and-Wait) | $U = \frac{L/R}{L/R + 2 \cdot d_{prop}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 10 | Pipelined Utilization | $U = \frac{L/R}{L/R + RTT}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ in formulas like $\vert x \vert$ is rendered using `\vert` in LaTeX. Inside markdown tables, this prevents table breaking. KTU examiners love numerical problems involving these formulas.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Switching Technique | Real-World Deployment |
|---|---|
| Circuit Switching | PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network), ISDN |
| Message Switching | Early telegraph networks, some email store-and-forward systems |
| Datagram Packet Switching | **Internet (IP networks)**, UDP-based applications |
| Virtual Circuit Switching | **ATM** (Asynchronous Transfer Mode), **MPLS** (Multiprotocol Label Switching), Frame Relay, X.25 |

**Why is packet switching dominant in modern networks?**
- **Statistical multiplexing** allows dynamic sharing of bandwidth.
- **Robustness** — if one link fails, packets can reroute.
- **Efficiency** — bandwidth is not wasted during silent periods.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Delay with N Switches (Store-and-Forward)

### Problem Statement
Consider a packet of length $L$ bits traveling from a source to a destination through $N$ identical switches. Each link has bandwidth $R$ bps, length $d$ meters, and propagation speed $s$ m/s. Processing and queuing delays are negligible.

**Goal:** Derive the total end-to-end delay $d_{end-to-end}$.

---

### Step 1 — Visualize the Network Path

The packet must traverse:
- $N$ switches (each acting as a store-and-forward node).
- $N+1$ links (one before the first switch, one between each pair of switches, and one after the last switch).

---

### Step 2 — Count the Transmission Delays

In a **store-and-forward** switch, the entire packet must be received before it begins transmitting on the outgoing link.

So, at each of the $N$ switches, there is **one transmission delay** of duration $L/R$.

$$
\text{Total Transmission Delay} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R}
$$

This includes transmission at all $N$ switches. (The source's initial transmission is included in the first link's transmission, which we handle separately or absorbed in the formula depending on interpretation. In the classic KTU formulation, we assume the source's transmission is counted as one of the $N$ events.)

---

### Step 3 — Count the Propagation Delays

The signal must propagate across **each of the $N+1$ links**. Each link contributes a propagation delay of $d/s$.

$$
\text{Total Propagation Delay} = (N+1) \cdot \frac{d}{s}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Combine All Delays

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{end-to-end} &= \text{Total Transmission Delay} + \text{Total Propagation Delay} \\
&= N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + (N+1) \cdot \frac{d}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Add Processing and Queuing Delays (Full Form)

If we include nodal processing ($d_{proc}$) and queuing ($d_{queue}$) at each switch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{end-to-end} &= N \cdot \left( d_{proc} + d_{queue} + \frac{L}{R} \right) + (N+1) \cdot \frac{d}{s} \\
&= N \cdot d_{proc} + N \cdot d_{queue} + N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + (N+1) \cdot \frac{d}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 1 — KTU Board Style (Standard 7-Mark)

### Problem
A packet of length **1500 bytes** is sent from a source to a destination through **3 switches**. Each link has bandwidth **1 Mbps** and length **2000 km**. The propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Find the total end-to-end delay (ignore processing and queuing delays).

### Given
- $L = 1500$ bytes $= 1500 \times 8 = 12000$ bits
- $N = 3$ switches
- $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps
- $d = 2000$ km $= 2000 \times 10^3 = 2 \times 10^6$ m
- $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s

### Step 1 — Compute Transmission Delay per Switch

$$
\frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{10^6} = 0.012 \text{ seconds} = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Propagation Delay per Link

$$
\frac{d}{s} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ seconds} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the End-to-End Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{end-to-end} &= N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + (N+1) \cdot \frac{d}{s} \\
&= 3 \cdot (12 \text{ ms}) + (3+1) \cdot (10 \text{ ms}) \\
&= 36 \text{ ms} + 40 \text{ ms} \\
&= 76 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Answer
**Total End-to-End Delay = 76 milliseconds**

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 2 — Throughput Bottleneck

### Problem
A file of size **5 MB** is transmitted over a path with 4 links having bandwidths: **10 Mbps, 5 Mbps, 20 Mbps, 8 Mbps**.

### Step 1 — Identify Bottleneck

$$
R_{bottleneck} = \min(10, 5, 20, 8) = 5 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute File Transfer Time

$$
F = 5 \text{ MB} = 5 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 4 \times 10^7 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{F}{R_{bottleneck}} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 10^7}{5 \times 10^6} \\
&= 8 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Answer
**Transfer Time = 8 seconds**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Switching Techniques

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Message Switching | Datagram Packet Switching | Virtual Circuit Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Path Establishment | Required | Not Required | Not Required | Required (virtual) |
| Dedicated Path | **Yes (physical)** | No | No | **No (logical only)** |
| Store-and-Forward | Not applicable | **Yes (whole msg)** | **Yes (per packet)** | **Yes (per packet)** |
| Packet Ordering | N/A (stream) | Preserved | **Not guaranteed** | **Preserved** |
| Delay Type | Setup + Transmit | Variable, high | **Variable, lower** | Setup + Variable |
| Bandwidth Utilization | **Poor** (idle if silent) | Good | **Excellent** | Good |
| Congestion Handling | None | Possible | **TCP congestion control** | Limited |
| Suitability for Voice | **Excellent** | Poor | Fair (with QoS) | **Good (ATM)** |
| Routing Decision | At setup | Per message | **Per packet** | At setup only |
| Header Overhead | None | Minimal | **Per packet (large)** | Per packet (medium) |
| Example Network | PSTN, ISDN | Old telegraph | **Internet (IP)** | **ATM, MPLS** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "Which switching is best?", the answer depends on the application:
> - **Real-time voice** → Circuit Switching or Virtual Circuit (ATM).
> - **Bursty data traffic** → Datagram Packet Switching (Internet).
> - **Reliable message delivery** → Message Switching (legacy systems).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) Significance

The Bandwidth-Delay Product represents the **maximum number of bits that can be "in flight"** (unacknowledged) on a link at any time.

$$
BDP = R \cdot d_{prop}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** If you could "freeze" the link, the number of bits you would see on it (between sender and receiver) equals $BDP$.

**Engineering Utility:**
- Determines the **window size** needed for pipelined protocols.
- Determines the **buffer size** required at the receiver.
- Critical for **TCP window scaling**.

### Example Calculation

For $R = 100$ Mbps and $d_{prop} = 25$ ms (transcontinental link):

$$
BDP = 100 \times 10^6 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} \approx 312.5 \text{ KB}
$$

This means up to **312.5 KB of data** can be in transit simultaneously.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Network Switch Connecting Multiple Devices

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Host A] -->|Frame| Switch1[Layer 2 Switch]
    B[Host B] -->|Frame| Switch1
    C[Host C] -->|Frame| Switch1
    D[Host D] -->|Frame| Switch1
    Switch1 -->|Forwarded Frame| A
    Switch1 -->|Forwarded Frame| B
    Switch1 -->|Forwarded Frame| C
    Switch1 -->|Forwarded Frame| D
    Switch1 --> Switch2[Layer 3 Switch Router]
    Switch2 --> Net1[External Network Internet]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Layer 2 Switch receives frames from all hosts, consults its MAC address table, and forwards frames only to the appropriate destination port — avoiding unnecessary broadcast.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Datagram Packet Switching (Connectionless Routing)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Src[Source Host] -->|Packet 1| R1[Router A]
    Src -->|Packet 2| R1
    Src -->|Packet 3| R1
    R1 -->|Path 1| R2[Router B]
    R1 -->|Path 2| R3[Router C]
    R2 --> Dst[Destination Host]
    R3 --> Dst
    Dst -->|Reassemble| Final[Reassembled Message in Correct Order]
```

**Explanation:**
- Each packet (Packet 1, 2, 3) from Source is treated **independently**.
- Router A may forward Packet 1 via Router B and Packet 2 via Router C based on current congestion.
- Destination Host reassembles packets in the correct order using sequence numbers.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Virtual Circuit Switching (Connection-Oriented Routing)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SrcV[Source Host] -->|VC Setup Request| R1V[Router A]
    R1V --> R2V[Router B]
    R2V --> R3V[Router C]
    R3V -->|VC Setup Acknowledged| SrcV
    SrcV -->|Packet 1 via VC| R1V
    R1V -->|Packet 1| R2V
    R2V -->|Packet 1| R3V
    R3V --> DstV[Destination Host]
    SrcV -->|Packet 2 via VC| R1V
    R1V -->|Packet 2| R2V
    R2V -->|Packet 2| R3V
    R3V --> DstV
```

**Explanation:**
- A **virtual circuit** is established first (three-way handshake via VC Setup).
- All subsequent packets follow the **same logical path**: Source → Router A → Router B → Router C → Destination.
- Packets arrive **in order** at the destination.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Four Sources of Packet Delay at a Single Node

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Packet Arrives at Node] --> P2[Processing Delay d_proc]
    P2 --> P3[Queuing Delay d_queue]
    P3 --> P4[Transmission Delay d_trans L over R]
    P4 --> P5[Propagation Delay d_prop d over s]
    P5 --> P6[Packet Leaves Node on Next Link]
```

**Explanation:** Every packet experiences these four delays in sequence at every node (router/switch) along its path.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Store-and-Forward Mechanism at a Switch

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Packet Begins Arriving at Switch Input Port] --> B[Entire Packet Received L bits]
    B --> C[Switch Examines Destination Address]
    C --> D[Packet Stored in Output Buffer]
    D --> E[Packet Transmitted on Output Link]
    E --> F[Packet Leaves Switch]
```

**Explanation:** In store-and-forward switching, the switch must receive the **entire packet** before any bit is forwarded. This is the basis of transmission delay calculations.

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram: Circuit Switching Three Phases

```mermaid
graph TD
    Phase1[Phase 1: Circuit Establishment] --> Phase2[Phase 2: Data Transfer]
    Phase2 --> Phase3[Phase 3: Circuit Teardown]
    Phase1 -.Reserve Dedicated Path.-> Reserve[Physical Path Locked]
    Reserve --> Phase2
    Phase2 -.Continuous Stream.-> Data[Data Flows on Reserved Path]
    Data --> Phase3
    Phase3 -.Release Path.-> Release[Resources Returned to Pool]
```

**Explanation:** Circuit switching has three distinct phases. The dedicated path is held throughout, even during silent periods in the conversation.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q:** Differentiate between **Datagram Packet Switching** and **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching**.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Datagram Packet Switching | Virtual Circuit Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|
| Connection Setup | Not required | Required (virtual circuit establishment) |
| Routing Decision | Made **per packet** | Made **once at setup** |
| Path Followed | May differ for each packet | All packets follow the same path |
| Packet Ordering | **Not guaranteed** | **Guaranteed** |
| Header Overhead | Higher (full address per packet) | Lower (only VC identifier) |
| Example | IP (Internet) | ATM, X.25, MPLS |
| Failure Recovery | Easier (reroute per packet) | Harder (re-establish VC) |

**Memory Aid for KTU Exam:**
> "**D**atagram = **D**ifferent paths, **D**ifferent order"
> "**V**irtual Circuit = **V**ery **O**rdered (same path, in sequence)"

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q:** Explain the **four sources of delay** in a packet-switched network.

**Model Answer:**

The four sources of delay experienced by a packet at each node (switch/router) are:

1. **Processing Delay ($d_{proc}$):** Time to examine packet header, check errors, and determine output port. Typically microseconds.

2. **Queuing Delay ($d_{queue}$):** Time spent waiting in the output buffer. Depends on congestion. Highly variable.

3. **Transmission Delay ($d_{trans} = L/R$):** Time to push all bits of the packet onto the link. Determined by packet length and link bandwidth.

4. **Propagation Delay ($d_{prop} = d/s$):** Time for the signal to physically travel from sender to receiver. Depends on distance and medium.

**Total nodal delay formula:**

$$d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** In the End Semester Examination, every module carries an internal choice. For Module 2, you must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** (both 14 marks). Each question is split into **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Style]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Compare the three switching techniques (Circuit, Message, and Packet Switching) in detail. Explain why **packet switching is preferred for modern data networks**.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Circuit Switching:**
- A dedicated physical path is established between source and destination before communication.
- Three phases: Setup, Data Transfer, Teardown.
- **Advantages:** Guaranteed bandwidth, low delay during transfer, suitable for voice (PSTN).
- **Disadvantages:** Inefficient bandwidth utilization (idle during silence), setup delay, no flexibility.

**2. Message Switching:**
- Entire message is stored at each intermediate switch and forwarded later.
- **Advantages:** No dedicated path, better link utilization than circuit switching.
- **Disadvantages:** High latency (must receive whole message), large buffer requirement, not real-time.

**3. Packet Switching:**
- Message divided into smaller packets, each routed independently.
- Two variants: Datagram and Virtual Circuit.
- **Advantages:** Excellent bandwidth utilization via statistical multiplexing, robust (rerouting on failure), low delay for small packets.
- **Disadvantages:** Variable delay (jitter), possible out-of-order delivery, packet loss possible.

**Why Packet Switching is Preferred for Modern Networks:**
- **Statistical Multiplexing:** Bandwidth is shared dynamically among many users.
- **Bursty Traffic Handling:** Internet traffic is inherently bursty; packet switching efficiently handles bursts.
- **Robustness:** Multiple paths allow rerouting on link failure.
- **Scalability:** Easily scales to millions of users (Internet).
- **Cost-effective:** No need to reserve dedicated infrastructure.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** A packet of length **2000 bytes** travels through **4 switches**. Each link is **1000 km** long with bandwidth **2 Mbps**. Propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Calculate the **total end-to-end delay**.

**Given:**
- $L = 2000 \times 8 = 16000$ bits
- $N = 4$ switches
- $R = 2 \times 10^6$ bps
- $d = 1000 \text{ km} = 10^6$ m
- $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Transmission delay per switch**

$$
\frac{L}{R} = \frac{16000}{2 \times 10^6} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Propagation delay per link**

$$
\frac{d}{s} = \frac{10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Total end-to-end delay**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{end-to-end} &= N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + (N+1) \cdot \frac{d}{s} \\
&= 4 \cdot (8 \text{ ms}) + 5 \cdot (5 \text{ ms}) \\
&= 32 \text{ ms} + 25 \text{ ms} \\
&= 57 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: Total End-to-End Delay = 57 milliseconds**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Explain the **architecture and operation of a Layer-2 Switch**. How does it differ from a hub and a router? Describe the **learning process** of a switch.

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture of a Layer-2 Switch:**

A Layer-2 switch consists of:
- **Input Ports:** Receive incoming frames from connected devices.
- **Output Ports:** Transmit frames to destination devices.
- **Switching Fabric:** The internal mechanism (crossbar, shared memory, or bus) that routes frames from input to output.
- **MAC Address Table (Forwarding Table):** A CAM (Content Addressable Memory) that maps MAC addresses to switch ports.
- **Processor / Control Plane:** Manages the learning process and table updates.

**Operation Steps:**
1. Frame arrives at input port.
2. Switch reads **destination MAC address** from frame header.
3. Switch consults the **MAC address table**.
4. If MAC found → forward frame to specific output port (unicast).
5. If MAC not found → flood frame to all ports except incoming (unknown unicast flooding).
6. Switch updates the table with the **source MAC address** and input port (learning).

**Learning Process (Self-Learning Switch):**
- Initially, the MAC table is empty.
- When a frame arrives from Host A on Port 1, the switch records: `Host A → Port 1`.
- When Host A sends to Host B, switch doesn't know Host B's location, so it floods all ports.
- When Host B replies, switch learns: `Host B → Port 2`.
- Now switch knows both locations; subsequent frames are forwarded directly.

**Difference from Hub:**
- Hub is a Layer-1 device that broadcasts to all ports (no MAC table).
- Hub creates a **single collision domain**.
- Switch creates **separate collision domains** per port.
- Switch is **intelligent**; hub is **dumb**.

**Difference from Router:**
- Router operates at Layer-3 (uses IP addresses).
- Router connects **different networks**; switch connects devices **within the same network**.
- Router uses **routing tables and protocols (OSPF, BGP)**; switch uses MAC tables.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** A file of **4 MB** is transmitted over a network path with 3 links having bandwidths **10 Mbps, 4 Mbps, 6 Mbps**. Compute the **end-to-end throughput** and **total transfer time**.

**Given:**
- $F = 4 \text{ MB} = 4 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 3.2 \times 10^7$ bits
- Link bandwidths: $R_1 = 10$ Mbps, $R_2 = 4$ Mbps, $R_3 = 6$ Mbps

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Bottleneck**

$$
R_{bottleneck} = \min(10, 4, 6) = 4 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2: End-to-End Throughput**

$$
\text{Throughput} = R_{bottleneck} = 4 \text{ Mbps} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 3: Total Transfer Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{F}{\text{Throughput}} \\
&= \frac{3.2 \times 10^7}{4 \times 10^6} \\
&= 8 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:**
- **End-to-End Throughput = 4 Mbps**
- **Total Transfer Time = 8 seconds**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Where Students Lose Marks in Switch Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Circuit and Virtual Circuit Switching:** Students often say "Virtual Circuit = dedicated path." WRONG! Virtual Circuit has NO dedicated physical path — resources are shared. Only the **route is logical**, not physical.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the (N+1) in Propagation Delay:** A common mistake is using $N \cdot d/s$ instead of $(N+1) \cdot d/s$ for propagation. Remember: with $N$ switches, there are $N+1$ links!
>
> 3. **Not Converting Units Properly:** KTU boards specifically check unit conversions:
>    - **kbps ≠ KBps** (1 KBps = 8 kbps)
>    - **Mbps = $10^6$ bps** (NOT $2^{20}$ unless specified)
>    - **Distance in meters, speed in m/s** for consistency.
>
> 4. **Ignoring "Store-and-Forward" Assumption:** If the problem says store-and-forward, you MUST add $L/R$ at each switch. If it says "cut-through," only the destination processes the full packet.
>
> 5. **Mixing Datagram and Virtual Circuit Examples:** IP is datagram. ATM and MPLS are virtual circuit. Don't get these swapped.
>
> 6. **Missing the Bottleneck Concept:** In throughput problems, the answer is always the **MINIMUM** link bandwidth, not the average. KTU examiners award **2 marks** specifically for identifying the bottleneck correctly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Switches (KTU Module 2)**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- ☐ A **switch** is a multi-port device that forwards frames based on **MAC addresses** (L2) or **IP addresses** (L3).
- ☐ The three switching techniques are: **Circuit, Message, Packet Switching**.
- ☐ **Packet Switching** has two variants: **Datagram (connectionless)** and **Virtual Circuit (connection-oriented)**.
- ☐ The **Internet uses Datagram Packet Switching** (IP protocol).

### The Four Delays (High-Yield for Numerical Problems)
- ☐ **Processing Delay:** Examining the header.
- ☐ **Queuing Delay:** Waiting in buffer (only variable one).
- ☐ **Transmission Delay:** $d_{trans} = L / R$ (formula must be memorized).
- ☐ **Propagation Delay:** $d_{prop} = d / s$ (formula must be memorized).

### Critical Formulas (Memorize Verbatim)
- ☐ $d_{end-to-end} = N \cdot (L/R) + (N+1) \cdot (d/s)$
- ☐ Bottleneck Throughput $= \min(R_1, R_2, ..., R_N)$
- ☐ File Transfer Time $= F / R_{bottleneck}$
- ☐ Bandwidth-Delay Product $= R \times d_{prop}$

### Key Differentiations (Common Exam Questions)
- ☐ **Switch vs. Hub:** Switch = intelligent + separate collision domains; Hub = dumb + single collision domain.
- ☐ **Datagram vs. Virtual Circuit:** Datagram = per-packet routing, may arrive out of order; VC = per-session routing, in-order delivery.
- ☐ **Circuit vs. Virtual Circuit:** Circuit = physical dedication; VC = logical dedication only.

### Real-World Examples (Frequently Asked)
- ☐ **PSTN Telephone** → Circuit Switching.
- ☐ **IP Internet** → Datagram Packet Switching.
- ☐ **ATM, MPLS, Frame Relay** → Virtual Circuit Packet Switching.
- ☐ **Old Telegraph** → Message Switching.

### Numerical Problem Solving Tips
- ☐ Always convert packet length to **bits** (multiply bytes by 8).
- ☐ Always convert distance to **meters** for $d/s$ calculations.
- ☐ Always convert bandwidth to **bits per second** (bps).
- ☐ Use **scientific notation** to avoid arithmetic errors.
- ☐ Show **all intermediate steps** — KTU examiners award step-marks even if the final answer is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routers and Gateways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Routers and Gateways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Router — Formal Academic Definition

A **Router** is a Layer 3 (Network Layer) internetworking device that operates on packets, examines the destination IP address in the packet header, consults its routing table, and forwards the packet from one logical network to another logical network along the most optimal path. Routers form the fundamental building blocks of the modern Internet and operate primarily on the Internet Protocol (IP).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMRT751 — Module 2):**
> "A router is a device that connects two or more packet-switched networks or subnetworks. Its primary function is to forward IP packets between networks based on the destination IP address and the routing information stored in its routing table."

**Key Functional Properties of a Router:**
- Operates at the **OSI Network Layer (Layer 3)**.
- Maintains a **Routing Table** (also called a Forwarding Information Base — FIB).
- Performs **Path Determination** using routing algorithms.
- Supports **Logical Addressing (IP addressing)**.
- Can connect **dissimilar Layer 2 networks** (e.g., Ethernet to Wi-Fi to Serial).
- Provides **Broadcast Domain segmentation** (each port = separate broadcast domain).
- Implements **Packet Filtering / Access Control Lists (ACLs)** for basic security.

---

### 1.2 Gateway — Formal Academic Definition

A **Gateway** is a network device or software that operates at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** of the OSI model and is used to interconnect two networks that use **dissimilar network protocols or architectures**. It acts as a protocol translator, allowing communication between networks running entirely different protocol stacks (e.g., TCP/IP to IBM SNA, or IPv4 to IPv6).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> A **Router** operates at Layer 3 and connects **homogeneous networks** (same protocol like IP-to-IP).
> A **Gateway** operates at Layer 7 and connects **heterogeneous networks** (different protocol stacks entirely).
> In modern parlance, the term *default gateway* refers to the router that handles traffic leaving the local subnet — this is a **misnomer** in strict OSI terminology.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🛣️ Think of a Router as a Highway Interchange:**
> Imagine a national highway system where every city has a unique city code (the **IP address**). A **router** is like a highway interchange — it reads the city code on every car (packet) arriving at the junction, checks a giant city road-map (**routing table**), and directs the car onto the correct exit ramp toward its destination city. Cars on the same local road (same subnet) never need the interchange — they exit directly from their driveway.

> [!TIP]
> **🌐 Think of a Gateway as a Custom-Border Translator:**
> Imagine a country where the local population speaks **Malayalam** and the neighbouring country speaks **Japanese**. A **gateway** is the embassy interpreter sitting at the border — he doesn't just hand documents across (that's a router's job), he actually **translates the entire conversation** from one language (protocol) to another so that the two sides can understand each other. The translation overhead is high, but the two nations can finally communicate.

---

### 1.4 Physical & Operational Constants (Bolded Standards)

- Default Administrative Distance (Cisco) for static route = **1**
- Default Administrative Distance for OSPF = **110**
- Default Administrative Distance for RIP = **120**
- Default Hop Count limit in RIP = **15 hops** (16 = unreachable / infinity)
- Default IPv4 TTL (Time To Live) = **64** (Linux), **128** (Windows), **255** (Network Devices)
- Default MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) for Ethernet = **1500 bytes**

---

### 1.5 Router vs. Gateway — Quick Comparison Callout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line KTU Board Answer:**
> *Routers work on IP packets using routing tables to forward data between similar networks, while gateways translate between entirely different network protocols to enable communication across heterogeneous architectures.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Router

A modern router is internally composed of **four critical hardware subsystems**, working in tandem:

1. **Input Ports** — Receive incoming packets, perform physical layer termination, look up the destination address in the forwarding table, and queue the packet for the switching fabric.
2. **Switching Fabric** — The internal "highway" connecting input ports to output ports. Three primary switching techniques exist:
   - **Memory Switching** (oldest; packet copied to processor memory)
   - **Bus Switching** (shared bus; contention creates bottleneck)
   - **Crossbar Switching** (modern; N×N matrix provides parallel paths)
3. **Output Ports** — Dequeue packets from the switching fabric, buffer them, and transmit onto the outgoing link.
4. **Routing Processor** — Executes routing protocols (OSPF, BGP, RIP), builds the routing table (RIB), and installs forwarding entries in line-card ASICs (FIB).

### 2.2 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Packet Forwarding Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step:**

- **Step 1 — Reception:** A packet arrives on an input port; the physical layer converts bits into frames.
- **Step 2 — Layer 2 De-encapsulation:** The router strips the Ethernet header to expose the IP packet.
- **Step 3 — Header Inspection:** The Routing Processor (or line-card ASIC) inspects the **Destination IP Address** (32-bit IPv4 or 128-bit IPv6).
- **Step 4 — Longest Prefix Match (LPM):** The router searches the Forwarding Information Base (FIB) for the **most specific** matching route. This is hardware-accelerated via Ternary Content Addressable Memory (TCAM).
- **Step 5 — Decrement TTL:** The Time-To-Live field is decremented by 1. **Why?** To prevent routing loops and infinite circulation. If TTL reaches 0, the packet is **dropped** and an **ICMP Time Exceeded** message is sent back to the source.
- **Step 6 — Recalculate Checksum:** The IP header checksum is recomputed (because TTL changed). The payload (TCP/UDP) checksum is **NOT** recomputed because the payload is unchanged.
- **Step 7 — ARP Resolution:** If the next-hop IP is on a directly connected link, the router uses **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** to map the next-hop IP to its MAC address.
- **Step 8 — Re-encapsulation & Transmission:** A new Layer 2 header is prepended (with destination MAC = next-hop), and the packet is placed on the output port queue.

### 2.3 Types of Routing

| Routing Type | Description | Convergence Speed | KTU Exam Weight |
|--------------|-------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Static Routing** | Manually configured by network administrator | Instant (no protocol) | High (3-mark Q) |
| **Default Routing** | Special static route (0.0.0.0/0) used when no specific match exists | Instant | High (3-mark Q) |
| **Dynamic Routing** | Routers exchange information using protocols (RIP, OSPF, BGP) | Slow (seconds to minutes) | Very High (14-mark Q) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the essential mathematical relationships a KTU student must memorize for Module 2 numerical problems.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Unit | Purpose |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|------|---------|
| 1 | Number of usable hosts per subnet | $H = 2^{n} - 2$ | hosts | Where $n$ = number of host bits. Subtract 2 for network address and broadcast address. |
| 2 | Number of subnets from a borrowed prefix | $S = 2^{m}$ | subnets | Where $m$ = number of bits borrowed from host portion. |
| 3 | Total addresses per subnet | $T = 2^{n}$ | addresses | Where $n$ = number of host bits. |
| 4 | New subnet mask (CIDR after borrowing) | $M_{new} = /24 + m$ | bits | For a default Class C network. |
| 5 | Subnet Network ID | $NID = IP_{addr} \ \textbf{AND} \ Mask$ | decimal | Bitwise AND operation. |
| 6 | Maximum hop count (RIP) | $H_{max} = 15$ | hops | 16 indicates infinity / unreachable. |
| 7 | TTL decrement per router hop | $TTL_{new} = TTL_{old} - 1$ | units | Prevents routing loops. |
| 8 | Address block size | $B = 2^{(32 - p)}$ | addresses | Where $p$ = CIDR prefix length. |
| 9 | Subnet increment value | $I = 256 - \text{octet}$ | decimal | Used to find successive subnets. |
| 10 | Longest Prefix Match range bound | $\vert p_{LPM} \vert \geq \vert p_{any} \vert$ | bits | LPM picks the route with the **largest** prefix length. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Routers and Gateways are used in Industry:**
> - **Internet Service Providers (ISPs):** Core routers (Cisco CRS, Juniper MX series) aggregate millions of subscriber sessions.
> - **Enterprise WANs:** Branch offices connect to headquarters via routers using MPLS or SD-WAN.
> - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Gateways translate between Modbus/Profibus (OT protocols) and MQTT/HTTP (IT protocols).
> - **Cloud Data Centers:** Top-of-Rack (ToR) switches and spine routers (e.g., using BGP) form Clos topologies.
> - **Smart Homes:** The home router (192.168.1.1) is the default gateway for all smart appliances.
> - **VoIP Systems:** Gateways translate between traditional PSTN telephony (SS7) and IP-based VoIP (SIP).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Subnet Network ID Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Model Question Style):**
> A host has the IP address **192.168.45.130** with a subnet mask of **255.255.255.192**. Determine the **(a) Subnet Network ID, (b) Broadcast Address, (c) Usable Host Range, and (d) Total Usable Hosts.**

**Step 1 — Convert to Binary (Octet-by-Octet):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP_{decimal} &= 192.168.45.130 \\
IP_{binary} &= 11000000.10101000.00101101.10000010 \\
Mask_{binary} &= 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* Each decimal octet (0–255) is converted to its 8-bit binary equivalent. The mask's 1s represent the network portion, 0s represent the host portion.

**Step 2 — Apply the Bitwise AND Operation (per octet):**

For the network portion (first three octets), AND of any bit with 1 yields the bit itself. The critical octet is the **fourth octet**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Fourth\ octet\ AND &= 10000010 \ (\text{IP}) \\
&\ \ \textbf{AND}\ 11000000 \ (\text{Mask}) \\
&= 10000000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* In binary AND, the result is 1 only if **both** bits are 1. So bits at positions 7 and 6 retain their value, while bits at positions 5 through 0 become 0.

**Step 3 — Convert Result Back to Decimal:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Binary\ 10000000 &= 128 \text{ in decimal} \\
NID &= 192.168.45.128
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* Only the leftmost bit (value = 128) is 1; all others are 0; sum = 128. The prefix is /26 because there are 26 ones in the mask.

**Step 4 — Identify the Subnet Increment (Block Size):**

The interesting octet (last non-255 octet) is **256 − 192 = 64**. So subnets jump in steps of 64: 0, 64, 128, **192**, 256.

**Step 5 — Compute the Broadcast Address:**

The broadcast address is the **last address of the subnet block**, i.e., the next subnet's ID minus 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Next\ Subnet\ ID &= 192.168.45.128 + 64 = 192.168.45.192 \\
Broadcast &= 192.168.45.192 - 1 = 192.168.45.191
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Usable Host Range:**

The usable range excludes the network ID and broadcast.

$$
\begin{aligned}
First\ Usable\ Host &= NID + 1 = 192.168.45.129 \\
Last\ Usable\ Host &= Broadcast - 1 = 192.168.45.190
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Total Usable Hosts (Formula Application):**

Number of host bits $n = 32 - 26 = 6$ bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 2^{n} - 2 \\
H &= 2^{6} - 2 \\
H &= 64 - 2 \\
H &= 62 \text{ usable hosts}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Block (Board Exam Format):**
> - **(a) Subnet Network ID:** 192.168.45.128/26
> - **(b) Broadcast Address:** 192.168.45.191
> - **(c) Usable Host Range:** 192.168.45.129 — 192.168.45.190
> - **(d) Total Usable Hosts:** 62

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — TTL Decrement Trace Across a Multi-Hop Path

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A packet is sent from Host A with initial TTL = 64. The path is:
> A → R1 → R2 → R3 → R4 → Host B.
> Determine the TTL value at each stage and identify what happens if the path were extended to 65 routers.

**Step 1 — Define Initial Conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TTL_{initial} &= 64 \\
Number\ of\ router\ hops\ to\ reach\ B &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply TTL Decrement at Each Router (Each Router −1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TTL\ \text{at}\ R1 &= 64 - 1 = 63 \\
TTL\ \text{at}\ R2 &= 63 - 1 = 62 \\
TTL\ \text{at}\ R3 &= 62 - 1 = 61 \\
TTL\ \text{at}\ R4 &= 61 - 1 = 60 \\
TTL\ \text{at}\ Host\ B &= 60 \ (\text{host does NOT decrement TTL})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* Routers decrement TTL during forwarding. End hosts do **not** decrement TTL — they only set it at origination.

**Step 3 — Trace Extended Path (Pathological Case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TTL\ \text{at}\ R_{64} &= 64 - 64 = 0 \Rightarrow \textbf{Packet Dropped} \\
ICMP\ Time\ Exceeded\ sent\ back\ to\ A
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum\ Routers\ Traversable} &= TTL_{initial} \\
&= 64
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> This is exactly why tools like `traceroute` (Linux) and `tracert` (Windows) work — they send probes with TTL = 1, 2, 3, … and each router along the path responds with an ICMP Time Exceeded, revealing the path one hop at a time.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Default Gateway Selection Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A host with IP **10.0.0.25/24** wants to send a packet to destination **172.16.50.100**. The host's default gateway is **10.0.0.1**. Show the routing decision logic step by step.

**Step 1 — Determine if Destination is on the Local Subnet:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Host\ IP\ \textbf{AND}\ Mask &= 10.0.0.0\ \ (\text{local network}) \\
Destination\ \textbf{AND}\ Mask &= 172.16.0.0\ \ (\text{remote network})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decision: Routes Do Not Match → Use Default Gateway:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision} &= \text{“Local subnet match failed, forward to default gateway”} \\
Next\ Hop &= 10.0.0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — ARP Resolution for Default Gateway's MAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ARP\ Cache\ Lookup &\rightarrow \text{MAC of 10.0.0.1} \\
\text{If absent} &\rightarrow \text{ARP Request broadcast on 10.0.0.0/24} \\
\text{If present} &\rightarrow \text{Use cached MAC directly}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Construct Frame and Transmit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Destination\ MAC &= MAC_{10.0.0.1} \\
Source\ MAC &= MAC_{host} \\
Destination\ IP\ (\text{unchanged}) &= 172.16.50.100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board-Exam Point:** Layer 2 addresses (MAC) change at every hop. Layer 3 addresses (IP) remain **end-to-end unchanged** (unless NAT is in use).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Quick-Reference Table — for last-minute revision before the ESE.**

| Parameter | Router | Gateway |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 7 (Application) |
| **Primary Function** | Packet forwarding via IP | Protocol translation |
| **Network Type Connected** | Homogeneous (same IP family) | Heterogeneous (different protocols) |
| **Addressing** | IP-based (logical) | Depends on protocols (multi-layer) |
| **Routing Table** | Yes (RIB / FIB) | Not necessarily; uses mapping tables |
| **Speed** | Fast (hardware-accelerated) | Slower (high processing overhead) |
| **Typical Use Case** | ISP core routers, home Wi-Fi routers | Email gateway, VoIP-PSTN gateway, IoT edge |
| **Example Device** | Cisco ISR 4000, Juniper MX | Cisco AS5400 Universal Gateway, AWS API Gateway |
| **Broadcast Domain Handling** | Segments (one per port) | Passes through after translation |
| **Security** | ACLs, basic stateful inspection | Deep Packet Inspection (DPI), anti-virus |
| **Modern Alias** | "Default Gateway" in end-host context | "Application Gateway" or "Cloud Gateway" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams illustrate the internal architecture and operational flow of a router, plus the placement of a gateway in a heterogeneous network — as required for full marks on KTU 14-mark essay questions.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Modern Router

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Port 1] --> SF[Switching Fabric]
    P2[Input Port 2] --> SF
    P3[Input Port 3] --> SF
    SF --> OP1[Output Port 1]
    SF --> OP2[Output Port 2]
    SF --> OP3[Output Port 3]
    RP[Routing Processor] --> SF
    RP -. RIB.-> P1
    RP -. RIB.-> P2
    RP -. RIB.-> P3
    RP -. FIB tables.-> OP1
    RP -. FIB tables.-> OP2
    RP -. FIB tables.-> OP3
    RP --> Routing[Routing Protocols OSPF BGP RIP]
```

*Explanation:* Packets enter via input ports, are switched to output ports via the crossbar switching fabric, while the routing processor (running OSPF/BGP/RIP) builds and distributes the routing tables.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Gateway Operation in a Heterogeneous Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NetA[Network A TCP IP Stack]
        A1[Host A1 IPv4 Ethernet]
        A2[Host A2 IPv4 Ethernet]
    end
    subgraph NetB[Network B SNA Token Ring]
        B1[Host B1 SNA TR]
        B2[Host B2 SNA TR]
    end
    GW[Gateway Protocol Translator Layer 7] --- NetA
    GW --- NetB
    A1 -->|TCP IP Packet| GW
    GW -->|SNA Frame Translated| B1
    B2 -->|SNA Frame| GW
    GW -->|TCP IP Translated| A2
```

*Explanation:* Hosts in Network A speak TCP/IP; hosts in Network B speak SNA on Token Ring. The gateway performs bidirectional protocol translation at the Application Layer.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Sequential Packet Forwarding Flow Inside a Router

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Packet Arrives at Input Port] --> B[Step 2: Strip Layer 2 Header]
    B --> C[Step 3: Inspect Destination IP]
    C --> D[Step 4: Longest Prefix Match in FIB]
    D --> E{Step 5: TTL is 0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Step 6a: Drop Packet and Send ICMP Time Exceeded]
    E -- No --> G[Step 6b: Decrement TTL and Recompute IP Checksum]
    G --> H[Step 7: ARP Lookup for Next Hop MAC]
    H --> I[Step 8: Prepend New Layer 2 Header]
    I --> J[Step 9: Enqueue in Output Port Buffer]
    J --> K[Step 10: Transmit on Outgoing Link]
```

*Explanation:* This is the exact 10-step decision tree a router follows for every forwarded packet. KTU examiners often ask students to draw and label this flow for 7–10 marks.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Network Showing Router vs. Gateway Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Internet[Internet Cloud] --> ER[Edge Router ISP Boundary]
    ER --> FW1[Firewall]
    FW1 --> DR[Distribution Router Layer 3 Core]
    DR --> AR1[Access Router 1]
    DR --> AR2[Access Router 2]
    AR1 --> SW1[Switch Subnet 192.168.1.0]
    SW1 --> PC1[Host PC1]
    SW1 --> PC2[Host PC2]
    AR2 --> SW2[Switch Subnet 192.168.2.0]
    SW2 --> PC3[Host PC3]
    SW2 --> GW[Application Gateway Email Proxy]
    GW --> MailSrv[Internal Mail Server SNA Legacy]
```

*Explanation:* Routers (ER, DR, AR1, AR2) interconnect IP-based subnets. The Gateway (GW) sits between the modern IP network and the legacy SNA mail server, translating protocols at Layer 7.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern, mapped to **Course Outcome CO2** and **Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyze / Evaluate**. Each model answer is calibrated to the **mark-splitting pattern** used by KTU board examiners.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Router and a Gateway. Mention the OSI layer at which each device primarily operates. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Board-Expected Format):**

| Aspect | Router | Gateway |
|--------|--------|---------|
| OSI Layer | Layer 3 (Network Layer) | Layer 7 (Application Layer) |
| Function | Forwards IP packets between networks using routing tables | Translates between entirely different protocols |
| Network Type | Connects homogeneous networks | Connects heterogeneous networks |

> A Router works on the **Network Layer** and uses logical IP addresses to forward packets, whereas a Gateway operates at the **Application Layer** and performs full protocol translation, enabling communication between networks using completely different protocol stacks.

*(Board Tip: Mentioning the OSI layer explicitly fetches the third mark.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a Routing Table? List any four fields typically present in an IPv4 routing table. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Routing Table** is a data structure stored in a router's memory (or line-card TCAM) that contains entries used to determine the best path for forwarding an IP packet. Each entry maps a destination network prefix to a next-hop address and an outgoing interface.

**Four typical fields of an IPv4 routing table entry:**

1. **Destination Network / Prefix** — e.g., 192.168.10.0/24
2. **Subnet Mask / Prefix Length** — e.g., 255.255.255.0 (/24)
3. **Next Hop** — IP address of the next router, e.g., 10.0.0.2
4. **Outgoing Interface** — e.g., Gig0/0, Eth0/1
5. *(Bonus field)* **Metric / Administrative Distance** — e.g., 110 for OSPF
6. *(Bonus field)* **Route Source** — e.g., 'S' static, 'O' OSPF, 'R' RIP

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (Choose ONE out of TWO — 1 × 14 Marks = 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **internal architecture of a router** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of the **switching fabric** and the three switching techniques used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A host is configured with IP address **172.20.15.200** and subnet mask **255.255.240.0**.
Calculate the:
  (i) Subnet Network ID
  (ii) Broadcast Address
  (iii) Usable Host Range
  (iv) Total Number of Usable Hosts. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Internal Architecture of a Router (7 Marks):**

A modern router consists of **four major components**:

1. **Input Ports** (2 marks) — Terminate physical links, perform data-link decapsulation, and perform destination address lookup using the FIB stored in line-card TCAM. Each input port has its own forwarding engine.

2. **Switching Fabric** (3 marks) — The internal backbone that transfers packets from input ports to output ports. Three switching techniques are:

   - **Memory Switching (Store-and-Forward):** The packet is copied into router processor memory. Bottlenecked by memory bandwidth. Used in earliest generation routers. Switching speed = $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{Memory Bus Speed}$.

   - **Bus Switching:** A shared bus connects all input and output ports. Simple but contention-prone — only one packet can traverse the bus at a time. Bottleneck = bus speed.

   - **Crossbar Switching:** An N×N matrix of switches provides parallel, non-blocking paths. Modern high-performance routers (Cisco CRS, Juniper PTX) use crossbar fabrics. Switching speed = line-card speed (no shared bottleneck).

3. **Output Ports** (1 mark) — Buffer and dequeue packets from the switching fabric, then transmit onto the outgoing link. Implement queuing disciplines such as FIFO, WFQ, or strict priority.

4. **Routing Processor** (1 mark) — Executes routing protocols (OSPF, BGP, RIP), builds the Routing Information Base (RIB), computes the Forwarding Information Base (FIB), and downloads it to line cards.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Marking Hint:** Drawing the block diagram with FOUR labeled blocks (Input Ports, Switching Fabric, Output Ports, Routing Processor) fetches a minimum of 3 marks. Naming the three switching techniques and their relative performance fetches the remaining 4 marks.

---

**(b) Subnetting Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert to Binary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= 172.20.15.200 \\
Mask &= 255.255.240.0 \\
Mask\ \text{in}\ CIDR &= /20
\end{aligned}
$$

The interesting octet is the **third octet (240)**.

**Step 2 — Subnet Increment (Block Size):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Increment &= 256 - 240 = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

Subnet boundaries in the third octet: **0, 16, 32, 48, …, 240, 256**.

**Step 3 — Find the Network ID (for 200 → 3rd octet = 15):**

Since the third octet is 15, and 15 lies between 0 and 16:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NID\ (3rd\ octet) &= 0 \\
NID &= 172.20.0.0/20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Broadcast Address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Broadcast\ (3rd\ octet) &= 0 + 16 - 1 = 15 \\
Broadcast &= 172.20.15.255
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Usable Host Range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
First\ usable &= 172.20.0.1 \\
Last\ usable &= 172.20.15.254
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Usable Hosts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Host\ bits\ n &= 32 - 20 = 12 \\
H &= 2^{12} - 2 = 4096 - 2 = 4094
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Box (ESE Format):**
> - **(i) Subnet Network ID:** 172.20.0.0/20
> - **(ii) Broadcast Address:** 172.20.15.255
> - **(iii) Usable Host Range:** 172.20.0.1 → 172.20.15.254
> - **(iv) Total Usable Hosts:** 4094

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of a Gateway** with suitable examples. Discuss any **three situations** where gateways are essential. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step process that occurs inside a router when an IP packet is received** for forwarding. Include the role of TTL, ARP, and the FIB. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Gateway Concept and Use Cases (7 Marks):**

A **Gateway** is an Application Layer (Layer 7) device that translates between entirely different network protocols, allowing communication between two networks that use incompatible architectures.

Unlike a router, which simply forwards packets based on IP, a gateway actually **terminates the original protocol stack, interprets the data at the application level, and re-transmits it using the destination network's protocol stack**.

**Three Essential Situations Where Gateways Are Required:**

1. **Email Protocol Translation (2 marks):** When an organization with internal **X.400** mail system needs to communicate with external **SMTP/Internet** mail users, an **Email Gateway** (e.g., Microsoft Exchange Gateway) translates X.400 messages into SMTP format and vice versa.

2. **VoIP–PSTN Interconnect (2 marks):** A **VoIP Gateway** (e.g., Cisco VG224) converts voice calls between **IP-based SIP/H.323** networks and the **legacy PSTN (SS7)** telephony infrastructure, allowing modern IP phones to call traditional landline numbers.

3. **IoT / SCADA Integration (2 marks):** In industrial automation, a **protocol gateway** translates between **Modbus / Profibus (OT-side)** and **MQTT / HTTP (IT-side)**, enabling modern cloud dashboards to read data from legacy PLCs and sensors.

4. *(Bonus — 1 mark for any one more example)* — IPv4-to-IPv6 transition gateways, ERP-to-database gateways, payment gateway servers.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Marking Hint:** Defining gateway (2 marks) + 3 distinct examples with explanation (3 marks) + neat labeled diagram (2 marks) = full 7.

---

**(b) Step-by-Step Router Forwarding Process (7 Marks):**

When an IP packet arrives at a router, the following sequence is executed:

1. **Frame Reception (1 mark):** The packet arrives as a Layer 2 frame on an input port. The Layer 2 trailer (CRC) is verified, and the Layer 2 header is stripped to expose the IP packet.

2. **Destination IP Inspection (1 mark):** The router examines the **Destination IP Address** in the IP header. This address is **32 bits** for IPv4 or **128 bits** for IPv6.

3. **Longest Prefix Match (LPM) in FIB (1 mark):** The router performs a **Longest Prefix Match** lookup against the Forwarding Information Base (FIB), which is typically stored in **TCAM (Ternary Content Addressable Memory)** on the line card. The longest matching prefix determines the next-hop IP and outgoing interface.

4. **TTL Decrement and ICMP Handling (1 mark):** The router decrements the **Time-To-Live (TTL)** field by 1. If TTL becomes 0, the packet is **dropped** and an **ICMP Type 11 (Time Exceeded)** message is sent to the source IP. The IP header checksum is recomputed.

5. **ARP Resolution for Next Hop (1 mark):** If the next-hop is on a directly connected link, the router consults its **ARP cache** to map the next-hop IP to a **MAC address**. If not present, the router broadcasts an **ARP Request** and waits for the ARP Reply.

6. **Re-encapsulation and Queueing (1 mark):** A new Layer 2 header is prepended with **Destination MAC = next-hop MAC** and **Source MAC = outgoing interface MAC**. The IP addresses inside the packet remain unchanged.

7. **Transmission (1 mark):** The packet is placed in the output port's transmit buffer and sent onto the outgoing link. Quality of Service (QoS) and queuing policies (FIFO, WFQ, Strict Priority) are applied here.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Misconception to Avoid:**
> Students often write that the router changes the **IP address** of the packet — **WRONG**. Only the **MAC address** changes at every hop. The IP addresses remain end-to-end (unless NAT is involved, which is a separate concept taught in Module 4).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Router / Gateway Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "−2" in usable host formula:** Many students write $2^{n}$ instead of $2^{n} - 2$ and lose 1 mark per instance.
> 2. **Confusing Router Layer (3) with Gateway Layer (7):** The #1 most common conceptual error. If you write "Gateway operates at Layer 3," the examiner will mark it **WRONG** in red and award 0.
> 3. **Not showing AND operation explicitly in subnet questions:** KTU board expects to see the binary AND written out, not just the final answer. **Always show the bitwise AND step.**
> 4. **Wrong block size calculation:** Using $256 - \text{first non-255 octet}$ for the increment is correct, but many students misapply it to the wrong octet.
> 5. **Saying "router changes IP" — FALSE.** Only MAC addresses change hop-by-hop; IP addresses remain end-to-end.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark questions:** A 14-mark question without a neat labeled block diagram typically loses **2–3 marks** automatically. Always draw a diagram.
> 7. **Mixing up ICMP types:** Time Exceeded = Type 11, Destination Unreachable = Type 3. Examiners check this strictly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute checklist before entering the ESE Hall — Module 2 — Routers & Gateways:**

- ✅ A **Router** operates at **OSI Layer 3 (Network Layer)** and forwards packets between IP networks using a **routing table** (RIB / FIB).
- ✅ A **Gateway** operates at **OSI Layer 7 (Application Layer)** and translates between **different protocol stacks** (e.g., TCP/IP ↔ SNA, IPv4 ↔ IPv6, SIP ↔ SS7).
- ✅ The **"Default Gateway"** in a host is technically a **router** (Layer 3), not an Application Layer gateway. This is a terminology overload — clarify in your answer.
- ✅ A router performs **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** using **TCAM** for high-speed lookups.
- ✅ **TTL** is decremented by 1 at every router hop. If TTL = 0, packet is dropped and **ICMP Type 11 (Time Exceeded)** is sent.
- ✅ **MAC addresses change** at every hop. **IP addresses remain end-to-end** (without NAT).
- ✅ **Usable Hosts** = $2^{n} - 2$ (subtract 2 for Network ID and Broadcast Address).
- ✅ **Subnet Increment** = $256 - \text{last non-255 octet of mask}$.
- ✅ Router internal components: **Input Ports, Switching Fabric, Output Ports, Routing Processor**.
- ✅ **Switching Fabric types:** Memory (slowest) → Bus → Crossbar (fastest, non-blocking).
- ✅ **ARP** maps a **known IP address** to a **MAC address** within the local subnet.
- ✅ **Static Route Admin Distance = 1**; **OSPF = 110**; **RIP = 120**.
- ✅ **RIP maximum hop count = 15**; 16 = infinity (unreachable).
- ✅ Default MTU of Ethernet = **1500 bytes**; default IPv4 TTL = **64 / 128 / 255** depending on OS.
- ✅ Common gateway examples: **Email Gateway, VoIP Gateway, IoT/Modbus Gateway, Payment Gateway**.
- ✅ `traceroute` works by sending probes with **incrementing TTL** (1, 2, 3, …) and listening for **ICMP Time Exceeded** replies.
- ✅ Always draw a **neat, labeled block diagram** for any 14-mark question — it fetches 2–3 free marks.
- ✅ ICMP Type 11 = **Time Exceeded**; ICMP Type 3 = **Destination Unreachable**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Routers and Gateways | PEMRT751 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Layer Design Issues. Routing Algorithms - The Optimality Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Layer Design Issues. Routing Algorithms - The Optimality Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Network Layer — Design Issues

The **Network Layer** is the third layer of the **OSI Reference Model** and is responsible for delivering packets from a source host to a destination host across one or more networks. Before any routing algorithm can be executed, the network layer must resolve five canonical *design issues* identified in the KTU 2024 syllabus (Tanenbaum & Kurose-Ross framework).

### The Five Design Issues

1. **Store-and-Forward Packet Switching** — Every router must receive the **entire packet** before it can begin transmitting the first bit onto the outgoing link.
2. **Services Provided to the Transport Layer** — Whether the network offers a **connectionless** (datagram) or **connection-oriented** (virtual-circuit) service.
3. **Implementation of Connectionless Service** — Each packet is routed independently using a routing table lookup.
4. **Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service** — A virtual circuit (VC) is set up *before* data flows; all packets follow the same path.
5. **Comparison of Virtual-Circuit and Datagram Subnetworks** — Trade-offs in setup latency, routing overhead, and congestion handling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** Questions on *“Compare datagram and virtual-circuit networks”* (typically 7–14 marks) appear in nearly every End Semester paper. Memorize the comparison table.

### Conceptual Analogy — Why These Issues Matter

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Indian Postal System**
> - **Store-and-Forward** = A post office sorts the *complete letter* before putting it on the next truck; it does **not** rip the letter in half and forward half first.
> - **Connection-Oriented (VC)** = A *Registered Post with Acknowledgment* — you obtain a tracking number (VC ID) at the first post office, and the letter follows the *same physical route* through every sorting hub.
> - **Connectionless (Datagram)** = An *ordinary postcard* — each sorting hub independently decides the next flight, so two postcards from the same sender may arrive in different orders.

The five design issues answer: **how is the letter accepted, addressed, tracked, and routed?**

---

## 1.2 Routing Algorithms — The Optimality Principle

### Formal Definition

> **Routing Algorithm:** A distributed procedure executed by every router that decides the *outgoing line* on which to forward an incoming packet so that it eventually reaches its destination subnet.

### The Optimality Principle (Bellman, 1957)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Optimality Principle (formal statement):**
> *If router J lies on the optimal (least-cost) path from router I to router K, then the optimal path from J to K also lies along the same route.*

In other words, the *suffix* of an optimal path is itself optimal with respect to its own endpoints.

### Sink Tree

If the optimality principle holds, the set of optimal routes from **all sources to a given destination** forms a **sink tree** rooted at the destination. A sink tree has three properties:

- **Root** = destination subnet.
- **Branches** = unique outgoing links from each router.
- **No loops** — every packet is delivered in a *finite*, *bounded* number of hops.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Highway Network

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Driving from any city to Kochi**
> Every city in Kerala wants to find the *shortest path to Kochi*. KTU says: *“If the optimal road from Thrissur to Kochi passes through Aluva, then the optimal road from Aluva to Kochi is also part of that same route.”*
> Plotting all such optimal roads on one map produces a **tree rooted at Kochi** — the *sink tree*. There are no loops because detours would only add distance.

### Why the Optimality Principle Matters

Without it, every router must know the global topology. With it, **each router only needs to know its parent in the sink tree** — the routing table shrinks from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Store-and-Forward Switching — Mechanics

In a Store-and-Forward network, a router with $n$ input/output links operates as follows on receiving a packet of $L$ bits over a link of rate $R$ bps:

**End-to-End Delay Model:**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{L}{R_i} + d_{\text{prop},i} + d_{\text{proc},i} + d_{\text{queue},i} \right)
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $L$ | Packet length | **bits** |
| $R_i$ | Transmission rate of link $i$ | **bps** |
| $d_{\text{prop},i}$ | Propagation delay on link $i$ | **seconds** |
| $d_{\text{proc},i}$ | Processing delay at router $i$ | **seconds** |
| $d_{\text{queue},i}$ | Queuing delay at router $i$ | **seconds** |
| $N$ | Total number of hops (links) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** *Store-and-Forward* guarantees **bit-error isolation** — a corrupt packet is detected *only after* the full packet is buffered. **Cut-Through Switching** (used in some Ethernet switches) violates this and forwards bytes as they arrive.

## 2.2 Connectionless (Datagram) vs Connection-Oriented (Virtual Circuit)

### Connectionless Service (Internet / IP)

| Step | Action at Each Router |
|------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Read packet header → extract destination IP |
| 2 | Lookup routing table → choose next hop |
| 3 | Forward packet on the chosen outgoing link |

- No call setup.
- Routers maintain **no per-flow state**.
- Packets of the same flow may follow **different paths** → *out-of-order delivery* is possible.

### Connection-Oriented Service (ATM, Frame Relay, X.25)

| Phase | Action |
|-------|--------|
| **Setup** | Source sends `CALL REQUEST`; each router allocates a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** and reserves resources |
| **Data Transfer** | Every packet contains only the **VCI** (short, e.g., 12–24 bits); routers swap the VCI at each hop |
| **Teardown** | Source sends `CALL CLEAR`; routers deallocate resources |

### KTU Comparison Table (High-Yield — Reproduce in ESE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 12-row table appears verbatim (or paraphrased) in almost every ESE paper.

| Feature | Datagram Subnet | Virtual-Circuit Subnet |
|---------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Circuit setup | **Not needed** | **Required** |
| Addressing | Each packet carries full source + destination | Each packet carries a short **VC number** |
| State information | Routers keep **no connection state** | Each VC requires **router table space per VC** |
| Routing | Each packet routed **independently** | **Route chosen at setup**; same for all packets |
| Effect of router failure | Minimal — new routes computed | **All VCs through that router are terminated** |
| Congestion control | Difficult | Easy if VCs can be dynamically allocated |
| Quality of Service | Hard to guarantee | Easy with per-VC resource reservation |
| Internet protocol used | **IP** | **ATM, X.25, Frame Relay, MPLS** |
| Bandwidth wastage | Headers are long (e.g., 20 B for IPv4) | Short VC labels → less overhead |
| Typical use | Internet backbone | Telecom backbones, leased-line replacement |
| Connection termination | None | Explicit teardown required |
| Packets follow same path? | **No** | **Yes** |

## 2.3 The Optimality Principle — Mathematical Formulation

### Cost Function

Let $d(i, j)$ denote the cost of the link between adjacent routers $i$ and $j$. The cost of a path $P = (v_0, v_1, \dots, v_k)$ is:

$$
\text{cost}(P) = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} d(v_i, v_{i+1})
$$

### Bellman-Ford Optimality Equation (Dynamic Programming Form)

The shortest-path distance $D_x$ from any node $x$ to destination $t$ satisfies:

$$
