# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define Shortest Path Routing. List any two routing algorithms used to compute shortest paths in computer networks.**

**Model Answer:**

Shortest Path Routing is a routing technique in which the path selected between a source and destination is the one with the **minimum cumulative cost** as defined by a metric such as hop count, delay, or bandwidth. The network is modeled as a weighted graph $G = (V, E, W)$, and the algorithm seeks to minimize the total edge weight along the path.

Two routing algorithms based on shortest path computation are:

1. **Dijkstra's Algorithm** — used in Link State Routing protocols such as OSPF and IS-IS; finds shortest paths from a single source using a greedy approach with time complexity $O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)$ when a priority queue is used.
2. **Bellman–Ford Algorithm** — used in Distance Vector Routing protocols such as RIP; iteratively relaxes all edges for $|V| - 1$ iterations and handles negative weights.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the Count-to-Infinity problem? Which routing algorithm suffers from it and why?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Count-to-Infinity problem** is a routing anomaly in **Distance Vector Routing (Bellman–Ford based)** where, upon a link failure, routers gradually increment their distance to the failed destination in steps of 1 per iteration until reaching the maximum metric (e.g., 16 in RIP). This causes **slow convergence** of routing tables and temporarily incorrect forwarding decisions.

**Why it occurs:** Distance Vector routers only have a *neighbor's view* of the network. When a link fails, neighbors are unaware and continue to advertise the failed route with incrementally increasing costs, forming a **routing loop** until the cost saturates at infinity.

**Mitigations:** Split Horizon, Split Horizon with Poison Reverse, and Hold-Down Timers. Link State Routing (Dijkstra-based) does **not** suffer from this problem because each router maintains a complete topology database.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Explain Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm with a suitable example. Demonstrate the step-by-step computation of shortest paths from source node **A** in the given graph. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Dijkstra's algorithm with Bellman–Ford algorithm in terms of complexity, applicability, and routing protocol usage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Dijkstra's Algorithm Explanation:**

Dijkstra's algorithm computes the shortest path from a single source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with **non-negative edge weights**. It maintains two sets — *visited* (finalized shortest distances) and *unvisited* (tentative distances). At each iteration, the unvisited node with the smallest tentative distance is selected and its outgoing edges are *relaxed*.

**Example Graph (5 nodes):**

Edges with weights: $(A, B, 6)$, $(A, D, 1)$, $(D, B, 2)$, $(D, E, 4)$, $(B, E, 3)$, $(B, C, 5)$, $(E, C, 2)$

**Step-by-step computation from source A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial: } & d = \{A:0,\ B:6,\ C:\infty,\ D:1,\ E:\infty\} \\
\text{Iter 1 — visit A (d=0); relax A-B=6, A-D=1:} & d = \{A:0,\ B:6,\ C:\infty,\ D:1,\ E:\infty\} \\
\text{Iter 2 — visit D (d=1); relax D-B=2 →1+2=3 < 6, D-E=4 → 1+4=5:} & d = \{A:0,\ B:3,\ C:\infty,\ D:1,\ E:5\} \\
\text{Iter 3 — visit B (d=3); relax B-E=3 → 3+3=6 > 5, B-C=5 → 3+5=8:} & d = \{A:0,\ B:3,\ C:8,\ D:1,\ E:5\} \\
\text{Iter 4 — visit E (d=5); relax E-C=2 → 5+2=7 < 8:} & d = \{A:0,\ B:3,\ C:7,\ D:1,\ E:5\} \\
\text{Iter 5 — visit C (d=7); all visited, terminate:}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final shortest paths from A:**

- $A \rightarrow D$: cost = **1**
- $A \rightarrow D \rightarrow B$: cost = **3**
- $A \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$: cost = **5**
- $A \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow C$: cost = **7**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (as required for full 7 marks):**

| Parameter | Dijkstra's Algorithm | Bellman–Ford Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Greedy | Dynamic Programming |
| Time Complexity | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log\vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ |
| Negative Edge Weights | Not supported | Supported |
| Information Required | Full topology | Only neighbor info |
| Distributed Operation | No (centralized) | Yes |
| Convergence | Immediate (single pass) | Up to $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations |
| Routing Protocol Usage | OSPF, IS-IS (Link State) | RIP, IGRP (Distance Vector) |
| Count-to-Infinity Problem | Not affected | Affected |
| Negative Cycle Detection | No | Yes |

**Key Discussion Points:**

- Dijkstra is **faster** but needs complete topology; suitable for stable, hierarchical networks.
- Bellman–Ford is **slower** but works in fully distributed environments and tolerates negative weights; suitable for legacy / small networks like RIP.
- Modern hybrid protocols (EIGRP) use **DUAL algorithm** that combines the best of both.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Explain the Distance Vector Routing algorithm. How does it differ from Link State Routing? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a network with 5 routers A, B, C, D, E. Initially, all routers know only the cost to their direct neighbors. Apply the Bellman–Ford (Distance Vector) algorithm to compute the final routing table at router **A**. Use the link costs given: A-B=2, A-C=4, B-C=1, B-D=7, C-D=3, C-E=5, D-E=1. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Distance Vector Routing (DVR):**

Each router maintains a **routing table** containing one entry per destination, listing the *estimated distance* (cost) and the *next-hop router*. Routers periodically share their **entire routing table** with their **immediate neighbors only**, and update their own table using the Bellman–Ford equation:

$$
d_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left[ c(x, v) + d_v(y) \right]
$$

where $d_x(y)$ is router $x$'s estimate of the cost to $y$, $c(x, v)$ is the direct cost from $x$ to neighbor $v$, and $d_v(y)$ is $v$'s advertised distance to $y$.

**Difference from Link State Routing (LSR):**

| Aspect | Distance Vector | Link State |
|---|---|---|
| Information Shared | Entire routing table with neighbors only | Link state packets flooded to **all** routers |
| Network View | Neighbor's view only (partial) | Complete topology (full graph) |
| Algorithm Used | Bellman–Ford | Dijkstra |
| Update Trigger | Periodic (e.g., every 30 s in RIP) | Event-driven (on topology change) |
| Bandwidth Usage | Low (small periodic updates) | Higher (LSP flooding during changes) |
| Convergence Speed | Slow (count-to-infinity possible) | Fast |
| Scalability | Poor for large networks | Better |
| Example Protocols | RIP, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Initial Direct-Link Costs at Router A:** $d(A, B) = 2$, $d(A, C) = 4$, $d(A, D) = \infty$, $d(A, E) = \infty$.

**Bellman–Ford Iterative Updates at Router A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Through B (cost 2 to reach B):} \\
d(A, B) &= 2 \\
d(A, C) &= \min\{4, 2 + 1\} = \min\{4, 3\} = 3 \quad (\text{via B}) \\
d(A, D) &= \min\{\infty, 2 + 7\} = 9 \quad (\text{via B}) \\
d(A, E) &= \min\{\infty, 2 + d_B(E)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

As B updates (with help from C, D, E), assume after sufficient iterations: $d_B(E) = 4$ (B → D → E = 7 + 1 = 8 OR B → C → E = 1 + 5 = 6 OR B → C → D → E = 1 + 3 + 1 = 5; through Dijkstra the optimum at B is **5** via B-C-D-E: 1+3+1=5, so $d_B(E) = 5$).

Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(A, E) &= \min\{\infty, 2 + 5\} = 7 \quad (\text{via B})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Through C (cost 4 to reach C):} \\
d(A, C) &= \min\{3, 4 + 0\} = 3 \quad (\text{B remains next-hop}) \\
d(A, D) &= \min\{9, 4 + 3\} = 7 \quad (\text{via C}) \\
d(A, E) &= \min\{7, 4 + 5\} = 7 \quad (\text{no improvement})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Routing Table at Router A (after convergence):**

| Destination | Next-Hop | Cost | Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| B | B (direct) | **2** | A → B |
| C | B | **3** | A → B → C |
| D | C | **7** | A → B → C → D |
| E | B | **7** | A → B → C → D → E |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to write the **time complexity** formula for both algorithms — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. Always quote $O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)$ for Dijkstra and $O(|V| \cdot |E|)$ for Bellman–Ford.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In DVR questions, students compute only the *cost* but forget the **next-hop column**. Both columns are mandatory for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Conflating "hop count" with "shortest path." Hop count is only ONE metric; shortest path is **metric-agnostic**. State the metric explicitly in your answer.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing Dijkstra's steps without showing the **predecessor tracking** ($\pi[v]$). Without it, the path reconstruction step is incomplete — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Claiming Bellman–Ford handles negative cycles — it **detects** them but does **not** produce correct shortest paths in their presence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- ⭐ Shortest Path Routing minimizes $\sum w(v_i, v_{i+1})$ over all paths from source to destination.
- ⭐ Two foundational algorithms: **Dijkstra** (Link State) and **Bellman–Ford** (Distance Vector).
- ⭐ Dijkstra = greedy, **non-negative weights only**, faster $O((|V|+|E|)\log|V|)$.
- ⭐ Bellman–Ford = dynamic programming, **handles negative weights**, slower $O(|V| \cdot |E|)$.
- ⭐ Bellman–Ford needs **$|V| - 1$ iterations** for guaranteed convergence.
- ⭐ Negative cycle detection = run **one extra iteration**; if any $d[v]$ decreases, a negative cycle exists.
- ⭐ **Count-to-Infinity** affects only Distance Vector (Bellman–Ford) routing.
- ⭐ Routing table entry contains: **Destination, Next-Hop, Cost, Interface**.
- ⭐ DVR shares tables with **neighbors only**; LSR floods LSPs to **all routers** in the area.
- ⭐ OSPF uses Dijkstra; RIP uses Bellman–Ford; BGP uses path vector (extended Bellman–Ford).
- ⭐ The relaxation equation $d[v] = \min\{d[v],\; d[u] + w(u, v)\}$ is the **atomic operation** in both algorithms — memorize it.
- ⭐ Predecessor array $\pi[\cdot]$ is essential for **path reconstruction** — do not skip in exam answers.
- ⭐ Standard metrics in KTU syllabus: **hop count, delay, bandwidth, load, administrative weight**.
- ⭐ The Triangle Inequality $d(s,t) \leq d(s,u) + d(u,t)$ holds only for non-negative weights.
- ⭐ Split horizon and poison reverse are DVR-specific count-to-infinity mitigations — **not applicable** to LSR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flooding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flooding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Flooding** is a non-adaptive, brute-force **routing algorithm** (also classified as a packet-forwarding strategy) in which every incoming packet destined for a node is transmitted on **every outgoing link** of that node, with the sole exception of the link on which the packet originally arrived. The technique guarantees that the packet reaches *every reachable node* in the network, provided the topology contains no loops that have not been broken by a termination mechanism.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, flooding belongs to the family of **Static (Non-Adaptive) Routing Algorithms** along with *Shortest Path* and *Distance Vector* techniques. The distinguishing feature is that flooding does **not** maintain routing tables, and it requires **zero prior knowledge** of the network topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Flooding is examined as a comparison-benchmark against the Distance Vector and Link State algorithms. Expect conceptual questions on its variants (Pure, Controlled with Hop Count, Controlled with Sequence Numbers, and Spanning Tree Flooding) and Reverse Path Forwarding.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Rumour Spread"

Imagine a workplace rumour that a manager passes verbally to **every employee in the office except themselves**. Each of those employees, in turn, repeats the rumour to **every colleague they meet except the person who told them**. Within a very short time, the rumour reaches **every single employee** in the building. This is precisely how flooding works in a network:

| Rumour Scenario | Network Flooding |
| :--- | :--- |
| Office employees | Routers / Switches |
| Verbal communication channel | Network link |
| Rumour message | Data packet |
| Each employee tells all others | Each router forwards on all outgoing ports |
| Originating manager | Source node |

The rumour (packet) propagates *exponentially* until it saturates the office (network). In flooding, the corollary is that the destination is virtually guaranteed to receive the packet by **at least one path**—often the *shortest possible path*—and the message may even arrive **multiple times** (duplicates) and via **multiple routes**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two critical numerical parameters that govern flooding behaviour are:
> - **Hop Count Limit ($H_{max}$):** The maximum number of links a packet may traverse. Standard default values in legacy networks: **$H_{max} = 16$** (RIPv1) or **$H_{max} = 32$** (early ARPANET). Decremented by 1 at every hop.
> - **Time-To-Live ($TTL$):** Measured in **seconds** (originally), now commonly used as a *hop counter*. Initial value in IPv4: **64 / 128 / 255**, configurable at the operating system.

Flooding is also characterised by its **reliability ratio**, which in dense topologies approaches **$1$ (100 %)** because the destination is reachable through redundant paths.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown

When a router running a flooding algorithm receives a packet, the following sequence is executed:

1. **Step 1 — Reception:** Router $R$ receives a packet $P$ on the incoming interface $L_{in}$.
2. **Step 2 — Header Inspection:** $R$ examines the packet's destination address, $TTL$ (or hop count), and source identifier.
3. **Step 3 — Local Delivery Check:** If the destination address equals $R$'s own address, the packet is delivered to the upper layer (delivery to the host).
4. **Step 4 — Loop Prevention Test:** If the packet has been seen before (or its $TTL$ has reached zero), the packet is silently discarded.
5. **Step 5 — Decrement Counter:** If the packet is fresh and still alive, decrement $TTL$ by **1**.
6. **Step 6 — Fan-Out Broadcast:** The packet is **replicated** and queued on **every outgoing interface except $L_{in}$**.
7. **Step 7 — Repeat:** Every neighbouring router repeats Steps 1–6 until the packet either dies ($TTL = 0$) or has been delivered to all reachable nodes.

### 2.2 Variants of Flooding (KTU High-Priority)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to **compare** the four major variants. Memorise the *control mechanism* of each.

#### 2.2.1 Pure (Uncontrolled) Flooding
- **No control mechanism whatsoever.** Every node re-broadcasts unconditionally.
- **Fatal flaw:** Packet count grows **exponentially**—in a balanced binary tree of depth $d$, a single source packet produces $2^{d} - 1$ total packets.
- **Use case:** Only theoretical; never deployed in real networks.

#### 2.2.2 Hop-Count Controlled Flooding
- Each packet carries a **hop counter** initialised to the network **diameter** (or any safe upper bound).
- The counter is decremented at every hop; when it reaches **zero**, the packet is dropped.
- **Disadvantage:** Does **not** prevent duplicate packets reaching the destination from different paths.

#### 2.2.3 Sequence-Number Controlled Flooding
- The source attaches a **unique sequence number** (e.g., an $(S, k)$ tuple where $S$ is source ID and $k$ is a monotonically increasing counter).
- Each router maintains a **seen-list** (or a Source-Destination Sequence Number table).
- A packet is re-broadcast **only if** its sequence number is *new* to that router.
- **Disadvantage:** Memory overhead grows with traffic; for $N$ sources and $K$ retained sequence numbers, the table size is $O(N \times K)$.

#### 2.2.4 Spanning-Tree Flooding
- A **Spanning Tree** of the topology is pre-computed (e.g., via the *Spanning Tree Protocol*, IEEE 802.1D).
- Flooding is **restricted** to the edges of the spanning tree only.
- **Key benefit:** No duplicates, bounded packet count, and the tree can be re-rooted dynamically for load balancing.

#### 2.2.5 Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) — *KTU Favourite*
- Used specifically for **multicast** and **broadcast** traffic.
- When a packet arrives on interface $I$, the router checks whether $I$ lies on the **shortest path back to the source** (reverse of the unicast route).
  - ✅ **Yes** → forward on all other interfaces (the packet came from the *correct* upstream neighbour).
  - ❌ **No** → silently drop (the packet arrived via a non-shortest path; another copy will eventually arrive correctly).
- **Variants:** *Reverse Path Broadcasting (RPB)* and *Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM)*.
- **Modern deployment:** RPF is the basis of **multicast routing** in PIM (Protocol Independent Multicast).

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Why this step? | How it is implemented |
| :--- | :--- |
| Stop infinite looping | Decrement $TTL$ / Hop Count; drop at zero |
| Avoid duplicate forwarding | Maintain a sequence-number cache per source |
| Reduce redundant bandwidth | Restrict forwarding to a Spanning Tree |
| Avoid wrong-path duplicates | Check if arrival interface is on the *reverse* shortest path |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important** revision resource for this topic in the KTU ESE. Memorise every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Description / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Packets generated in pure flooding (balanced tree) | $P_{total} = \dfrac{d(d+1)}{2}$ | Where $d$ = number of hops, packets per link |
| 2 | Exponential growth (worst case, degree $\Delta$ tree) | $P_{total} = \Delta^{d}$ | $\Delta$ = node degree, $d$ = depth |
| 3 | TTL Decrement Rule | $TTL_{new} = TTL_{old} - 1$ | Per-hop decrement |
| 4 | Drop Condition | $TTL = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Drop}$ | Termination test |
| 5 | Sequence-No. Match Rule | $Seq_{new} \notin \text{Cache} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Forward}$ | Newness test |
| 6 | Hop-Count Limit Safety | $H_{max} \geq \text{diam}(G)$ | Must cover graph diameter |
| 7 | Bandwidth Usage Estimate | $B_{flood} = S \times P_{total}$ | $S$ = packet size in bits, $B$ in bit-hops |
| 8 | RPF Acceptance Rule | $\text{if } I = \text{ShortestPath}(R, src) \rightarrow \text{Forward}$ | Reverse path check |
| 9 | Network Diameter (max hops) | $D(G) = \max_{u,v \in V} d(u,v)$ | For $n$-node graph, $D \leq n - 1$ |
| 10 | Spanning Tree Edges | $\vert E_{ST} \vert = \vert V \vert - 1$ | Always acyclic |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Military & Tactical Networks:** Flooding's *inherent redundancy* is invaluable when links are intermittent or hostile. Messages reach the destination even if 50 % of nodes are destroyed.
- **Ad-Hoc Networks (MANETs):** Protocols such as **Directed Flooding**, **Gossip-based flooding**, and **Reliable Broadcast** adapt classical flooding for mobile scenarios.
- **Distributed Databases & Gossip Protocols:** Modern variants of flooding power *Cassandra*, *DynamoDB*, and *Bitcoin's* transaction propagation.
- **ARP (Address Resolution Protocol):** Uses **local broadcast flooding** within an Ethernet LAN to resolve IP→MAC mappings.
- **DHCP Discovery:** The `DHCPDISCOVER` message is flooded as a **limited broadcast** (255.255.255.255) to find a DHCP server.
- **Routing Convergence (OSPF/IS-IS):** Link-State Advertisements (LSAs) are flooded reliably across an *area* using sequence-number-controlled flooding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Packet Count in Pure Flooding (Binary Tree Topology)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *very common* 7-mark derivation in KTU ESE. Practice it until you can reproduce it from memory.

**Given:**
- A balanced binary tree of depth $d$ (root at depth 0, leaves at depth $d$).
- Each internal node has exactly 2 children.
- A single source packet originates at the root.

**To Derive:** Total number of packets transmitted across the network by the time flooding terminates.

**Step 1 — Count packets at each depth level.**

At depth $k$ (for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, d$), the number of nodes is:

$$
N_k = 2^{k}
$$

Each node at depth $k$ receives one packet from its parent and forwards it to its children. Therefore, the number of packet transmissions at level $k$ equals the number of *edges* from depth $k-1$ to depth $k$:

$$
T_k = 2^{k} \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, \ldots, d
$$

**Step 2 — Sum transmissions across all depths.**

$$
P_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{d} T_k = \sum_{k=1}^{d} 2^{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the geometric series.**

$$
P_{total} = 2^{1} + 2^{2} + 2^{3} + \cdots + 2^{d}
$$

Using the closed-form formula for a finite geometric series $\sum_{k=1}^{d} r^{k} = \dfrac{r(r^{d}-1)}{r-1}$ with $r = 2$:

$$
P_{total} = \dfrac{2(2^{d} - 1)}{2 - 1} = 2(2^{d} - 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to standard textbook form.**

$$
P_{total} = 2^{d+1} - 2
$$

For a more general tree where each non-leaf node has degree $\Delta$ (i.e., $\Delta - 1$ children), the total packets become:

$$
P_{total} = \dfrac{\Delta(\Delta^{d} - 1)}{\Delta - 1}
$$

**Numerical check:** For $d = 3, \Delta = 2$:

$$
P_{total} = 2^{3+1} - 2 = 16 - 2 = 14 \text{ packets}
$$

Manual verification: depth 1 has 2 packets, depth 2 has 4, depth 3 has 8. Total: $2 + 4 + 8 = 14$. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bandwidth Overhead of Pure Flooding

**Given:**
- A network with $n$ nodes and average degree $\bar{\Delta}$.
- A single source floods a packet of size $S$ bits.
- The diameter of the network is $D(G)$.

**To Derive:** The total bandwidth (in bit-hops) consumed by a single flood.

**Step 1 — Count edges traversed per packet.**

A single packet, in the worst case, traverses the entire diameter $D(G)$. In pure flooding, the total edge traversals equal the total packet count from Derivation 1, generalised:

$$
E_{traversed} = \sum_{k=1}^{D(G)} \bar{\Delta}^{k} = \dfrac{\bar{\Delta}(\bar{\Delta}^{D(G)} - 1)}{\bar{\Delta} - 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by packet size.**

The total bandwidth consumed (in bit-hops) is:

$$
B_{flood} = S \times E_{traversed} = S \cdot \dfrac{\bar{\Delta}(\bar{\Delta}^{D(G)} - 1)}{\bar{\Delta} - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

For a 100-node mesh network with $\bar{\Delta} = 4$ and $D(G) = 10$, sending a single 1 KB packet consumes:

$$
B_{flood} = 1024 \times 8 \times \dfrac{4(4^{10} - 1)}{3} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{10} \text{ bit-hops} \approx 1.25 \text{ GB of link traffic}
$$

This dramatic amplification is **why pure flooding is impractical** for large networks.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hop-Count Limit vs. Network Diameter

**To Prove:** Setting $H_{max} = \text{diam}(G) - 1$ may cause legitimate packets to be dropped.

**Step 1 — Define diameter.**

For a connected graph $G = (V, E)$, the diameter is:

$$
D(G) = \max_{u, v \in V} d(u, v)
$$

where $d(u, v)$ is the shortest-path hop count between $u$ and $v$.

**Step 2 — Critical observation.**

The *shortest* path between the two most-distant nodes is exactly $D(G)$ hops. Any packet destined for such a pair therefore requires **at least $D(G)$ decrements** of the hop counter.

**Step 3 — Consequence.**

If we set $H_{max} < D(G)$, the destination is unreachable. Therefore, the *minimum safe* value is:

$$
H_{max} = D(G)
$$

**Step 4 — Practical safety margin.**

To tolerate transient routing inconsistencies, set:

$$
H_{max} = D(G) + \delta, \quad \delta \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}
$$

where $\delta$ is a small safety buffer (typically $\delta = 2$ to $5$).

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Variant | Loop-Free? | Duplicate-Free? | Memory Overhead | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Pure Flooding | ❌ | ❌ | None | Theoretical benchmark |
| Hop-Count | ✅ | ❌ | None (just counter) | Small, stable networks |
| Sequence-No. | ✅ | ✅ | $O(N \cdot K)$ per router | ARP, OSPF LSAs |
| Spanning Tree | ✅ | ✅ | Tree edges only | Ethernet (802.1D) |
| Reverse Path Fwd. | ✅ | ✅ (eventually) | Per-source reverse path | Multicast (PIM) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Algorithmic Logic of Controlled Flooding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Packet Arrives at Router R]) --> B{Is destination == R?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Deliver packet to upper layer]
    B -- No --> D{Is TTL or Hop Count == 0?}
    D -- Yes --> E([Silently Drop Packet])
    D -- No --> F{Is sequence number already seen?}
    F -- Yes --> E
    F -- No --> G[Record sequence number in cache]
    G --> H[Decrement TTL by 1]
    H --> I[Replicate packet]
    I --> J[Queue copy on EVERY outgoing interface except arrival link]
    J --> K([Flood Continues Downstream])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flooding Variants Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flooding Family] --> B[Pure Flooding]
    A --> C[Controlled Flooding]
    A --> D[Selective Flooding]

    C --> C1[Hop Count Based]
    C --> C2[Sequence Number Based]
    C --> C3[Spanning Tree Based]
    C --> C4[Reverse Path Based]

    C4 --> C4a[Reverse Path Forwarding RPF]
    C4 --> C4b[Reverse Path Broadcasting RPB]
    C4 --> C4c[Reverse Path Multicasting RPM]

    D --> D1[Probabilistic Gossip]
    D --> D2[Directional Restriction]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P([Packet from source S arrives at Router R on interface I]) --> Q{Look up unicast route from R to S}
    Q --> R[Compute shortest path R to S]
    R --> S{Does the shortest path start with interface I?}
    S -- Yes, I is on reverse shortest path --> T[ACCEPT: forward on all interfaces except I]
    S -- No, I is not on reverse shortest path --> U([DROP: duplicate from wrong direction])
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Network Topology — Flooding Reach Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src((S)) --- r1((R1))
    r1 --- r2((R2))
    r1 --- r3((R3))
    r2 --- dst((D))
    r3 --- dst
    r2 --- r4((R4))
    r3 --- r4
    r4 --- dst
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Source `S` floods its packet. The packet reaches `D` via three independent paths: `S→R1→R2→D`, `S→R1→R3→D`, and `S→R1→R4→D`. In *pure flooding*, the destination receives the packet **three times** (duplicate problem). In *sequence-number-controlled* flooding, only the first arrival is processed; later copies are silently dropped. In *spanning-tree* flooding, only one of these paths is used because cycles are eliminated.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define flooding. List any two advantages and two disadvantages of flooding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Flooding is a static, non-adaptive packet-forwarding technique in which every incoming packet is transmitted on every outgoing link of a router except the one on which the packet arrived. It requires no routing tables and no prior topology knowledge.

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Robustness / High reliability:** The destination will receive the packet if any path exists, since the packet is sent on *all* links.
2. **Simplicity:** The algorithm is trivial to implement; no routing tables, no topology discovery, no metric computation.
3. **Optimal in some sense:** The packet always arrives via the *shortest path* (since all paths are tried).

**Disadvantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Bandwidth explosion:** Packet traffic grows exponentially with the network diameter.
2. **Duplicate packets:** The destination may receive the same packet many times.
3. **No scalability:** Unsuitable for large or congested networks.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Differentiate between pure flooding and controlled flooding. Mention the role of the hop counter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pure Flooding | Controlled Flooding |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Loop control | None | Implemented via counter / sequence number / tree |
| Duplicate packets | Yes, many | Eliminated (sequence-number variant) |
| Scalability | Extremely poor | Practical for small-to-medium networks |
| Use case | Theoretical only | ARP, OSPF, DHCP, multicast |

**Hop counter (1 mark):** The hop counter is initialised to the network diameter (or any safe upper bound) and decremented by 1 at every router. When the counter reaches zero, the packet is dropped. This bounds the lifetime of the packet and prevents indefinite circulation in loops.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`**

**a)** Explain the **flooding** routing technique in detail. Discuss its **two major variants** — *hop-count controlled* and *sequence-number controlled* — with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)

**b)** What is **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)**? How does it eliminate duplicate packet forwarding in a broadcast or multicast scenario? Illustrate with an example network of 6 nodes. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Flooding Definition (1 mark):**
Flooding is a packet-forwarding algorithm in which a router forwards every incoming packet on *all* of its outgoing interfaces except the arrival interface, irrespective of routing tables. The packet thus reaches every reachable node in the network.

**(ii) Hop-Count Controlled Flooding (3 marks):**

- Each packet carries a **hop counter** initialised to the network diameter $D(G)$.
- At every hop, the counter is decremented: $TTL_{new} = TTL_{old} - 1$.
- When $TTL$ reaches **zero**, the packet is dropped.
- **Limitation:** does not eliminate duplicates — the destination may still receive the same packet from multiple paths.
- **Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S((S)) --> A((A))
    A --> B((B))
    A --> C((C))
    B --> D((D))
    C --> D
    B --> E((E))
    C --> E
    D --> TGT((TGT))
    E --> TGT
```

Even with $TTL = 3$, the target `TGT` receives the packet twice (via B→D and via C→E).

**(iii) Sequence-Number Controlled Flooding (3 marks):**

- Each packet header contains $(SourceID, SequenceNumber)$.
- Each router maintains a **sequence-number cache** mapping $SourceID \to$ largest sequence number seen.
- A packet is forwarded **only if** its sequence number is *strictly greater* than the largest seen from that source.
- This **completely eliminates duplicates**.
- **Trade-off:** memory overhead is $O(N \cdot K)$ where $N$ is the number of sources and $K$ is the cache depth.

---

#### Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) RPF Definition (2 marks):**
Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) is a forwarding technique used in broadcast and multicast routing. When a packet arrives on interface $I$, the router checks whether $I$ lies on the **shortest path back to the source**. If yes, the packet is forwarded on all other interfaces; if no, the packet is dropped (it must have arrived via a non-optimal path, and a correct copy will arrive shortly).

**(ii) Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Receive packet from source $S$ on interface $I$.
2. Look up the unicast routing table to compute the *shortest path* from $R$ to $S$.
3. Let the *first interface* on that shortest path be $I^*$.
4. If $I = I^*$, forward on all other interfaces.
5. Else, drop silently.

**(iii) Worked Example on 6-node network (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S((S)) --- A((A))
    A --- B((B))
    A --- C((C))
    B --- D((D))
    C --- D
    B --- T((T))
    C --- T
    D --- T
```

Suppose the shortest path from any router back to `S` is via interface on the **left side** (toward A). When `B` receives the packet from `A` (the correct reverse path), it forwards to `D` and `T`. When `B` receives the *same* packet from `D` (wrong reverse path), RPF drops it. This eliminates duplicate forwarding without a sequence-number cache.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]`**

**a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **spanning-tree-based flooding** technique. Why is it preferred over pure flooding? (7 marks)

**b)** Consider a balanced binary tree network of depth 4 with a single source at the root. A pure-flooding algorithm is used. **Calculate the total number of packets** generated and the **bandwidth consumed** if each packet is 512 bytes. Comment on the scalability of pure flooding. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Spanning-Tree Flooding (3 marks):**
A **spanning tree** of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is a connected acyclic subgraph that includes every vertex of $G$. It has exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges.

In spanning-tree flooding:
- A spanning tree of the network is pre-computed (e.g., via the Spanning Tree Protocol, IEEE 802.1D).
- Flooding is **restricted to the edges of the spanning tree** only.
- Packets travel only along tree edges, which by definition contain no cycles.

**(ii) Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S((S)) --- A((A))
    S --- B((B))
    A --- C((C))
    A --- D((D))
    B --- E((E))
    B --- F((F))
    C --- G((G))
    D --- H((H))
    E --- I((I))
    F --- J((J))
```

In the above network, flooding is restricted to the tree edges (the lines shown). The non-tree edge between `H` and `I`, for example, is not used.

**(iii) Advantages over pure flooding (2 marks):**
1. **No duplicate packets** — the tree is acyclic, so there is only one path from the source to every destination.
2. **Bounded traffic** — at most $\vert V \vert - 1$ packet transmissions per flood.
3. **No sequence-number cache** required.
4. **Easy to reconfigure** — re-root the tree to balance load.

---

#### Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Setup (1 mark):**
- Depth $d = 4$, root is the source.
- Each internal node has degree 2 (balanced binary tree).
- Packet size $S = 512$ bytes $= 512 \times 8 = 4096$ bits.

**(ii) Packet count calculation (3 marks):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
P_{total} = 2^{d+1} - 2
$$

Substituting $d = 4$:

$$
P_{total} = 2^{4+1} - 2 = 2^{5} - 2 = 32 - 2 = 30 \text{ packets}
$$

**Verification by levels:** depth 1 → 2 packets, depth 2 → 4, depth 3 → 8, depth 4 → 16. Sum: $2+4+8+16 = 30$. ✅

**(iii) Bandwidth calculation (2 marks):**

$$
B_{flood} = P_{total} \times S = 30 \times 4096 \text{ bits} = 122{,}880 \text{ bits} \approx 120 \text{ KB}
$$

**(iv) Scalability comment (1 mark):**
Even for a *single* 512-byte packet, pure flooding generated 30 packets consuming 120 KB of link capacity. For $d = 10$, the count would be $2^{11} - 2 = 2046$ packets (over 8 MB for one tiny message). Pure flooding is **not scalable** beyond small networks of depth ≤ 6.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Confusing flooding with Distance-Vector routing.** Flooding is *non-adaptive* and *table-less*; Distance-Vector *is* table-based and adaptive. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Writing the formula $2^{d+1} - 2$ without stating the assumption** (balanced binary tree, degree = 2). Always state the topology before substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting to decrement $TTL$ / hop count** in algorithmic answers. Examiners expect an explicit `TTL = TTL - 1` step.
> 4. **Treating RPF as a separate algorithm from flooding.** RPF is a *variant* of controlled flooding. Always say "RPF is a controlled-flooding variant used for multicast."
> 5. **Spelling "flooding" as "floding"** in KTU answer scripts — automatic 0.5 mark deduction in strict valuation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- ☐ Flooding is a **static, non-adaptive, table-less** packet-forwarding technique.
- ☐ Every router forwards on **all outgoing interfaces except the arrival interface**.
- ☐ Pure flooding produces **exponential** traffic: $2^{d+1} - 2$ packets in a balanced binary tree of depth $d$.
- ☐ **Hop-count controlled** flooding uses a decrementing counter to limit lifetime but does **not** eliminate duplicates.
- ☐ **Sequence-number controlled** flooding uses a $(SourceID, SeqNo)$ tuple and a per-router cache — this **does** eliminate duplicates.
- ☐ **Spanning-tree flooding** restricts forwarding to tree edges: $\vert V \vert - 1$ transmissions, no cycles, no duplicates.
- ☐ **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** is the broadcast/multicast variant; it accepts a packet only if it arrived on the *reverse* of the unicast shortest path to the source.
- ☐ RPF underpins modern multicast protocols: **PIM-SM**, **PIM-SSM**, **DVMRP**.
- ☐ Real-world applications: **ARP**, **DHCP discovery**, **OSPF LSA flooding**, **gossip protocols**, **ad-hoc routing**.
- ☐ Key limitation: **bandwidth explosion**; pure flooding is impractical for networks of depth $> 6$.
- ☐ TTL values: IPv4 default $64$, max $255$; decrement by 1 at every hop; drop at zero.
- ☐ Hop-count limit must satisfy $H_{max} \geq D(G)$ where $D(G)$ is the network diameter.
- ☐ **Sequence-number cache size** grows as $O(N \cdot K)$ — a scalability concern for large backbones.
- ☐ Spanning tree has **exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges** — always acyclic by construction.
- ☐ Always mention **advantages AND disadvantages** in 3-mark definition questions; examiners reward both.
- ☐ For 7-mark derivations, **show every step**: state given, state to-derive, write the series, apply the geometric sum, substitute, verify.
- ☐ In KTU ESE, pair flooding with **Distance Vector** or **Link State** for comparison questions — both are in the same module.
- ☐ Memorise the **RPF decision rule** verbatim: *"Forward only if arrival interface is on the reverse shortest path to source."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Vector Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distance Vector Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — DISTANCE VECTOR ROUTING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Distance Vector Routing (DVR)** is a *dynamic, distributed, intra-domain routing algorithm* in which every router maintains a **routing table** containing, for every reachable destination, the **shortest distance (metric)** and the **next-hop identifier (outgoing line)**. Routers exchange their *complete* distance vectors with **directly connected neighbours only**, and recompute their own tables using the **Bellman–Ford equation** until convergence.

The metric used is typically the **hop count** in protocols like **RIP** (Routing Information Protocol), where each link costs **1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Terminology**
> - **Distance Vector** → Pair $\langle \text{destination}, \text{distance}, \text{next\_hop} \rangle$
> - **Neighbour** → A router sharing a common physical/logical link
> - **Convergence** → State when no router's table changes in an update cycle
> - **Metric** → Hop count, delay, or composite cost (KTU usually **hop count**)

---

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy — "The Village Rumour Network"

Imagine a chain of **6 hill villages (A–F)** connected by footpaths. Each villager (router) only knows the **length of path from his own village to every other village** via the village he directly talks to.

Every evening, each villager shouts his list of "best known distances" to his **immediate neighbours** only. After hearing the neighbour's list, he updates his own list:
- *"If my neighbour's path to X plus the 1-km path to that neighbour is shorter than my current path, I switch to that route."*

The rumour propagates *gradually*. If the bridge between B and C collapses:
- C initially still trusts B's old numbers and announces a *stale* distance to A.
- This fake distance propagates like a **whisper that grows** — the **count-to-infinity** problem — until the metric cap (say 16) stops it.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Distance Vector"?** Because each router advertises a **vector** (array) of **distances** to all destinations — but only sends it to **first-hop neighbours**, not to every router in the network. This makes DVR highly **bandwidth-efficient at the cost of slow convergence**.

---

### ⚙️ DVR vs. LSR — A Quick Contrast

| Property | Distance Vector (DVR) | Link State (LSR) |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge scope | **Neighbour only** | **Entire network** (LSP flood) |
| Information shared | Entire routing table | Link-state packet (LSP) |
| Algorithm | **Bellman–Ford** | **Dijkstra's SPF** |
| Convergence | Slow (cyclic updates) | Fast |
| Bandwidth usage | Low | High |
| Example Protocol | **RIP, IGRP** | **OSPF, IS-IS** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bellman–Ford Equation — The Heart of DVR

For every router $x$, the shortest distance to any destination $y$ is given by the **Bellman–Ford optimality principle**:

$$D_{x}(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left\{ c(x,v) + D_{v}(y) \right\}$$

Where:
- $D_{x}(y)$ → Current shortest distance from $x$ to $y$
- $N(x)$ → Set of directly connected neighbours of $x$
- $c(x,v)$ → **Cost of the link** between $x$ and neighbour $v$
- $D_{v}(y)$ → Distance advertised by neighbour $v$ to reach $y$

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Logic — Why this equation works**
> The optimal path from $x$ to $y$ must go through **some neighbour** $v$. Once $v$ is chosen, the sub-path from $v$ to $y$ must itself be optimal (principle of optimality). Hence the additive cost structure $c(x,v) + D_{v}(y)$.

---

## 2.2 Iterative Update Steps (KTU Board-Standard)

1. **Initialization**: Each router sets $D_{x}(y) = 0$ if $x = y$, $D_{x}(y) = c(x,y)$ for direct neighbours, and $D_{x}(y) = \infty$ otherwise.
2. **Sharing**: Every $\Delta T$ seconds (RIP uses **30 s**), each router sends its distance vector to all neighbours.
3. **Recomputation**: On receiving a neighbour's vector, router $x$ re-evaluates the Bellman–Ford equation for *every* destination.
4. **Replacement**: If a strictly shorter (or equal-cost) path is found, the **next-hop** field is updated.
5. **Termination**: When no router's table changes in an update cycle, the network has **converged**.

---

## 2.3 The Count-to-Infinity Problem

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Concept** — This is the most frequently asked DVR pitfall in KTU ESE. Memorize both the mechanism and the three classic mitigations.

When a link fails, the bad news propagates **one hop per update cycle**. If the true distance to a lost destination is $\infty$, routers may iteratively increment the metric (1, 2, 3, 4 …) up to the protocol's maximum (**RIP caps at 16**), wasting time and creating **routing loops**.

### The Three Mitigations (Board-Favourite)

| Technique | Mechanism | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| **Split Horizon** | Never advertise a route back to the neighbour from whom it was learned | Stops 2-node loops |
| **Poison Reverse** | Advertise the learned route back with metric **16 (∞)** | Stronger than split horizon |
| **Hold-Down Timers** | Ignore updates about a failed route for a fixed period (e.g., 180 s in RIP) | Limits propagation time |
| **Triggered Updates** | Send an update *immediately* on detecting a change, not wait for the timer | Faster bad-news spread |

---

## 2.4 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| $D_{x}(y) = \min_{v} \{ c(x,v) + D_{v}(y) \}$ | Bellman–Ford shortest-path equation | Hop count |
| $c(x,v)$ | Link cost (hop count = 1) | $\geq 1$ |
| $\infty$ (DVR cap) | Maximum metric (RIP = **16**, Cisco IGRP = 255) | Integer |
| Convergence time | $\leq O(d)$ update cycles | $d$ = network diameter |
| Update interval (RIP) | **30 s** | Timer |
| Hold-down timer (RIP) | **180 s** | Timer |
| Garbage-collection timer (RIP) | **120 s** after route marked invalid | Timer |
| Route timeout (RIP) | **180 s** without refresh | Timer |
| Maximum packet size (RIP) | 512 bytes (up to 25 routes) | Bytes |

> [!TIP]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in tables for absolute value** — never raw `\vert` to avoid breaking KTU markdown rendering. Example: $\vert D_x(y) \vert$ should be written as $\lvert D_x(y) \rvert$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DVR is the engine behind:
- **RIP v1 / RIP v2** — legacy small enterprise networks
- **IGRP / EIGRP** (hybrid — Cisco proprietary)
- **Babel** — modern wireless mesh routing

It is preferred when the network is **small**, **flat**, and **bandwidth-constrained**, such as in **embedded IoT meshes** and **early-stage ad-hoc networks**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Bellman–Ford Table Construction (KTU Board Standard)

Consider a 4-router topology:

```
        (cost 1)
   A ─────────── B
   │ \           │
   │  \          │ (cost 2)
 1 │   \ 4       │
   │    \        │
   C ─── D ──────┘
   │  1     (cost 1)
   │  (cost 3)
   E
   (cost 5 from A to E via C)
```

**Link costs:**
- $c(A,B) = 1$, $c(A,C) = 1$, $c(A,D) = 4$
- $c(B,D) = 2$, $c(C,D) = 1$, $c(C,E) = 3$
- $c(D,E) = 1$

We shall compute the distance table for **Router A** iteratively.

---

### 🔢 Step 0 — Initial Tables (Direct Neighbours Only)

Each router knows only its directly attached links. For **Router A**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{A}(A) &= 0 \\
D_{A}(B) &= c(A,B) = 1, \quad \text{next-hop} = B \\
D_{A}(C) &= c(A,C) = 1, \quad \text{next-hop} = C \\
D_{A}(D) &= c(A,D) = 4, \quad \text{next-hop} = D \\
D_{A}(E) &= \infty, \quad \text{next-hop} = \text{none}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 🔢 Step 1 — A Receives Vectors from B, C, D

Suppose A receives these vectors from its three neighbours:
- From B: $D_{B}(A)=0, D_{B}(B)=0, D_{B}(C)=3, D_{B}(D)=2, D_{B}(E)=\infty$
- From C: $D_{C}(A)=0, D_{C}(B)=3, D_{C}(C)=0, D_{C}(D)=1, D_{C}(E)=3$
- From D: $D_{D}(A)=0, D_{D}(B)=2, D_{D}(C)=1, D_{D}(D)=0, D_{D}(E)=1$

### 🔢 Step 2 — Apply Bellman–Ford for Each Destination

For destination **E** from A:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{via B:} \quad & c(A,B) + D_{B}(E) = 1 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{via C:} \quad & c(A,C) + D_{C}(E) = 1 + 3 = 4 \\
\text{via D:} \quad & c(A,D) + D_{D}(E) = 4 + 1 = 5 \\
\therefore D_{A}(E) &= \min\{\infty, 4, 5\} = 4, \quad \text{next-hop} = C
\end{aligned}
$$

For destination **D** from A:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{via B:} \quad & 1 + 2 = 3 \\
\text{via C:} \quad & 1 + 1 = 2 \\
\text{via D:} \quad & 4 + 0 = 4 \\
\therefore D_{A}(D) &= 2, \quad \text{next-hop} = C
\end{aligned}
$$

For destination **B** from A:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{via B:} \quad & 1 + 0 = 1 \\
\text{via C:} \quad & 1 + 3 = 4 \\
\text{via D:} \quad & 4 + 2 = 6 \\
\therefore D_{A}(B) &= 1, \quad \text{next-hop} = B
\end{aligned}
$$

For destination **C** from A:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{via B:} \quad & 1 + 3 = 4 \\
\text{via C:} \quad & 1 + 0 = 1 \\
\text{via D:} \quad & 4 + 1 = 5 \\
\therefore D_{A}(C) &= 1, \quad \text{next-hop} = C
\end{aligned}
$$

### 🔢 Step 3 — Final A's Routing Table (After One Round)

| Destination | Distance (hops) | Next Hop |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 0 | — |
| B | 1 | B |
| C | 1 | C |
| D | **2** | C |
| E | **4** | C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Criterion**: Repeat this cycle until no row changes. In this topology, convergence is reached in **2 update cycles**.

---

## 3.2 Count-to-Infinity — Numerical Trace

Suppose the link **B–D fails** (cost becomes $\infty$).

| Cycle | B's table $D_B(D)$ | D's table $D_D(B)$ | Comment |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 | 2 (via D) | 2 (via B) | Old state |
| 1 | 3 (via B→C→D) | 3 (via D→C→B) | Bad news begins |
| 2 | 4 | 4 | Each cycle adds 1 |
| 3 | 5 | 5 | Metric "climbs" |
| … | … | … | … |
| 15 | 16 | 16 | **RIP cap — route poisoned** |
| 16 | ∞ | ∞ | Route declared **unreachable** |

**Total time** ≈ $15 \times 30\text{ s} = 7.5 \text{ min}$ for RIP. This is why RIP is unsuitable for large networks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use DVR

| Network Property | DVR Suitability | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Diameter < 15 hops | ✅ Excellent | RIP's 16-hop cap not a problem |
| Diameter > 20 hops | ❌ Poor | Need OSPF / IS-IS |
| Bandwidth-limited links | ✅ Excellent | Small periodic updates only |
| Mission-critical paths | ❌ Risky | Slow convergence → loops |
| Hierarchical topology | ❌ No native hierarchy | Need OSPF areas |
| Homogeneous admin control | ✅ Good | Single routing domain |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bellman–Ford Update Cycle — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Router X starts update cycle]) --> B[Send current Distance Vector to all neighbours]
    B --> C{Receive neighbour's vector}
    C --> D[Extract D_v(y) for every destination y]
    D --> E[Apply Bellman-Ford for each y]
    E --> F[Compute c X,v plus D_v y]
    F --> G{Minimum found}
    G -- Yes, new path shorter --> H[Update distance and next-hop fields]
    G -- No change --> I[Keep existing entry]
    H --> J{More destinations}
    I --> J
    J -- Yes --> E
    J -- No --> K{Any table change this cycle}
    K -- Yes --> B
    K -- No --> L([Network Converged])
```

## 4.2 Count-to-Infinity Propagation — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stable
    Stable --> Detected: Link B-D fails
    Detected --> Metric1: B hears stale 1 from D
    Metric1 --> Metric2: Next cycle, D hears 2 from B
    Metric2 --> Metric3: Next cycle
    Metric3 --> MetricN: Cycle continues
    MetricN --> Poisoned: Metric equals 16
    Poisoned --> Unreachable: Route deleted
    Unreachable --> [*]
```

## 4.3 DVR Information Exchange Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph AutonomousSystem
        R1[Router R1\nDV: A=0 B=2 C=1] 
        R2[Router R2\nDV: A=2 B=0 C=3]
        R3[Router R3\nDV: A=1 B=3 C=0]
        R4[Router R4\nDV: A=4 B=1 C=2]
    end
    R1 <-->|Periodic\n30s| R2
    R1 <-->|Periodic\n30s| R3
    R2 <-->|Periodic\n30s| R4
    R3 <-->|Periodic\n30s| R4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Observation from the Graph**: Each router (e.g., R1) only exchanges vectors with **adjacent nodes** — not the entire AS. This is what makes DVR *bandwidth-efficient* but *slow to converge* compared to Link State Routing.

## 4.4 Split Horizon vs. Poison Reverse — Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Router B learns route to X via C] --> B{Update type?}
    B -- Split Horizon --> C[Do NOT advertise route to X back to C]
    B -- Poison Reverse --> D[Advertise route to X with metric 16 back to C]
    C --> E[C never uses B for X, breaking 2-node loop]
    D --> F[C knows B's route is poisoned, prevents stale use]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Distance Vector Routing and Link State Routing with respect to information sharing, algorithm, and convergence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Distance Vector | Link State |
|---|---|---|
| Information shared | Routing table to neighbours only | LSPs flooded to *all* routers |
| Algorithm | **Bellman–Ford** | **Dijkstra's SPF** |
| Convergence | Slow; prone to loops | Fast; loop-free after SPF |
| Bandwidth | Low | High during flooding |
| Memory | Smaller tables | Full topology in LSDB |

(1 mark per correct contrasting point — full 3 marks for all four covered.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the count-to-infinity problem in DVR? Mention any two solutions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Count-to-infinity (1 mark):** When a link fails, routers iteratively advertise *incrementally increasing* distances to a now-unreachable destination, because they trust neighbours' stale information. The metric climbs 1, 2, 3 … until the protocol's cap.
- **Solution 1 — Split Horizon (1 mark):** A router never advertises a route back to the neighbour it was learned from.
- **Solution 2 — Poison Reverse (1 mark):** The route is advertised back with metric 16 (in RIP), explicitly marking it as unreachable.

---

## 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Bellman–Ford algorithm** used in Distance Vector Routing. State the optimality equation and describe its iterative operation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Construct the **distance vector tables for all routers** in the given 4-node network, showing two complete update cycles until convergence. Use hop count as the metric. **(7 marks)**

**Network:**
```
       1        2
   A ------ B ------ C
   |        |        |
   3        4        1
   |        |        |
   D ------ E ------ F
       1        5
```

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bellman–Ford Algorithm (7 marks):**

The algorithm maintains at each router $x$ a distance $D_x(y)$ to every destination $y$. The **optimality equation** is:

$$D_{x}(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{ c(x,v) + D_{v}(y) \}$$

**Steps:** (1 mark per step)
1. **Initialize** $D_x(y) = 0$ for $x=y$; $D_x(y) = c(x,y)$ for direct neighbours; $D_x(y) = \infty$ for all others. *(1 mark)*
2. **Share** the distance vector with all immediate neighbours periodically. *(1 mark)*
3. **Receive** neighbour's vector and apply the Bellman–Ford equation for each destination $y$. *(1 mark)*
4. **Compare** with current entry — if the new path is shorter, update the distance and next-hop. *(1 mark)*
5. **Repeat** until no table changes occur in a complete cycle. *(1 mark)*
6. **Termination** — network is said to have **converged**. *(1 mark)*
7. **Convergence time** is bounded by the network diameter (in hops). *(1 mark)*

**(b) Distance Vector Construction (7 marks):**

**Initial Tables (Step 0):**

| Router | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A** | 0,– | 1,B | ∞ | 3,D | ∞ | ∞ |
| **B** | 1,A | 0,– | 2,C | ∞ | 4,E | ∞ |
| **C** | ∞ | 2,B | 0,– | ∞ | ∞ | 1,F |
| **D** | 3,A | ∞ | ∞ | 0,– | 1,E | ∞ |
| **E** | ∞ | 4,B | ∞ | 1,D | 0,– | 5,F |
| **F** | ∞ | ∞ | 1,C | ∞ | 5,E | 0,– |

**After Cycle 1 — Update via Bellman–Ford (e.g., for Router A):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(E) &= \min\{c(A,B)+D_B(E),\ c(A,D)+D_D(E)\} \\
       &= \min\{1+4,\ 3+1\} = \min\{5,4\} = 4, \text{ next-hop} = D
\end{aligned}
$$

Following similar computation for all destinations: *(3 marks for full table)*

| Router | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A** | 0,– | 1,B | 3,B | 3,D | 4,D | ∞ |
| **B** | 1,A | 0,– | 2,C | 4,A | 4,E | 3,C |
| **C** | 3,B | 2,B | 0,– | 6,B | 7,B | 1,F |
| **D** | 3,A | 4,A | ∞ | 0,– | 1,E | 6,E |
| **E** | 4,D | 4,B | 6,B | 1,D | 0,– | 5,F |
| **F** | ∞ | 3,C | 1,C | 6,E | 5,E | 0,– |

**After Cycle 2:** New shorter paths exist; recompute. Convergence reached in **3 cycles** for this topology. *(1 mark for convergence statement)*

**Final answer** — write out the converged table for all six routers. *(1 mark)*

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **count-to-infinity problem** in distance vector routing. Show the metric evolution when a link fails. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe any **three techniques** to alleviate the count-to-infinity problem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Count-to-Infinity (7 marks):**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A((A)) --1--> B((B))
    B --1--> C((C))
    C --1--> D((D))
    style A fill:#ffe5e5
    style D fill:#ffe5e5
```

**Mechanism (4 marks):**
1. Initially, B's distance to D = 3, via C. *(1 mark)*
2. Link **C–D** fails. C detects and sets $D_C(D) = \infty$, but this takes one cycle to propagate. *(1 mark)*
3. B's next update from C is *stale*: B's old table says $D_C(D) = 2$ (cached). B recomputes: $D_B(D) = 1 + 2 = 3$ (unchanged — bad!). B *re-advertises* this stale value. *(1 mark)*
4. Over successive cycles, the metric **climbs**: 3, 4, 5, … , 16 in RIP. This phenomenon is called **count-to-infinity**, lasting up to 15 cycles × 30 s = 7.5 min. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Mitigation Techniques (7 marks):**

**1. Split Horizon with Poison Reverse (2 marks):**
A router advertises a route back to the source it learned it from with metric **16 (infinity)**. This explicitly signals the route is unusable, breaking 2-node loops.

**2. Hold-Down Timers (2 marks):**
When a route becomes invalid, the router ignores any updates about that destination for **180 seconds** (RIP default). This prevents accepting potentially stale information.

**3. Triggered / Flash Updates (2 marks):**
Instead of waiting for the periodic 30-second timer, the router sends an update **immediately** when it detects a topology change. This accelerates the propagation of both good and bad news.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
None of these techniques *eliminate* count-to-infinity; they only **bound** its duration. The only true cure is to use **link-state routing** (OSPF) or **path-vector routing** (BGP).

---

> [!WARNING]
> 🚨 **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Confusing next-hop with destination** in tables — next-hop is the *neighbour*, not the destination itself. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the convergence cycle count** — always state "Convergence reached after **N** cycles". (–1 mark)
> 3. **Missing the cap value of 16** for RIP when explaining count-to-infinity — board examiners *expect* this number. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Not drawing arrows in diagrams** — unlabelled diagrams fetch only 1 of 3 marks.
> 5. **Writing RIP timers incorrectly** — 30 s update, 180 s timeout, 180 s hold-down, 120 s garbage — memorize.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 DVR uses the **Bellman–Ford** equation $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$.
- 🎯 Routers share vectors with **neighbours only**, periodically (every **30 s** in RIP).
- 🎯 **Convergence** = no table changes in a full update cycle.
- 🎯 **Count-to-infinity** = bad news propagates slowly; metric climbs 1 per cycle up to the cap.
- 🎯 RIP cap = **16** (≥ 16 = unreachable).
- 🎯 Three mitigations: **Split Horizon**, **Poison Reverse**, **Hold-Down Timers** (+ Triggered Updates).
- 🎯 DVR is suitable for **small, flat networks** (< 15 hops); use **OSPF** for larger topologies.
- 🎯 Compared to LSR, DVR trades **bandwidth efficiency** for **slow convergence** — a classic engineering compromise.
- 🎯 RIP v1 is **classful**; RIP v2 adds **subnet masks** and **multicast** (224.0.0.9).
- 🎯 In KTU ESE, always show **at least two complete update cycles** in numerical DVR problems.

> **End of Module 3 — Distance Vector Routing Notes** 📚
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Link State Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Link State Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Link State Routing (LSR)** is a dynamic, intra-domain routing algorithm in which every router independently constructs a complete topological map (graph) of the entire autonomous system by collecting **Link State Advertisements (LSAs)** flooded from all other routers, and then computes the shortest path to every destination using **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, LSR belongs to the class of **table-driven, globally-informed routing protocols** that fall under the category of *Interior Gateway Protocols (IGP)*. The reference real-world implementation of LSR is the **Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)** protocol standardized in **RFC 2328**.

The fundamental operational philosophy of LSR can be summarized in three axioms:

1. **Each router = local authority on its own links.** Every router knows the state (up/down) and cost (metric) of *only* its directly connected neighbors.
2. **Flooding = universal knowledge.** The router shares this local knowledge with **every other router in the AS** using controlled, reliable flooding.
3. **Independent computation = consistent routing table.** Each router runs **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm locally** on the identical link-state database, producing loop-free, optimal paths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction from Distance Vector**: In Distance Vector (RIP), routers share their *entire routing table* with only their *neighbors*. In Link State, routers share *only their local link costs* with *every router in the network*. This trade-off trades **bandwidth** for **convergence speed and loop-freedom**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Master Map" Mental Model)

Imagine a **chain of 10 hill stations** connected by mountain roads. Each station manager knows only:

- Which neighboring stations are directly connected to them.
- The **traffic congestion** (cost) on those local roads.

Now, the **Link State Routing** approach is like every manager sending a "**Road Condition Report**" to **every other manager** in the chain, not just their neighbors. After receiving reports from all 9 other managers, **each manager independently draws the entire mountain map** and calculates the best route from their station to any other.

Compare this to **Distance Vector**, where each manager only asks their **adjacent neighbor** for the "best route to Station X," and the neighbor asks *their* neighbor, and so on — like asking for directions one person at a time.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick**: *Link State = "I tell the WORLD about my LINKS"*. *Distance Vector = "I tell my NEIGHBOR about my DISTANCES"*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded Standard KTU Vocabulary**:
> - **LSA (Link State Advertisement)** — A small packet containing router ID, neighbor list, and link costs.
> - **LSDB (Link State Database)** — The complete topological map stored identically in every router.
> - **SPF Tree** — The shortest-path tree generated by Dijkstra's algorithm from a given source.
> - **Flooding** — The process of reliably distributing LSAs to every router in the AS.
> - **Sequence Number** — A 32-bit counter to ensure LSP freshness and prevent loops during flooding.
> - **Age / MaxAge** — A 16-bit timer; an LSA is considered stale if not refreshed within **MaxAge (3600 seconds in OSPF)**.
> - **Cost Metric** — In OSPF, inversely proportional to bandwidth: `Cost = Reference_BW / Interface_BW`, where `Reference_BW = 10^8 bps = 100 Mbps`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Operational Steps of Link State Routing

KTU examiners specifically test these **five sequential stages**. Memorize them in order:

#### Step 1 — Discovery of Neighbors
- Each router boots up and sends **HELLO packets** out of every interface.
- Upon receiving a HELLO reply, the router establishes an **adjacency** and records the neighbor's Router ID and the **cost of the link** connecting them.
- In OSPF, the **HelloInterval = 10 seconds** and **DeadInterval = 40 seconds** (4× Hello).

#### Step 2 — Measurement of Link Cost (Metric)
- The cost can be statically assigned by the administrator or dynamically measured.
- Common metrics: **delay, bandwidth, hop count, reliability, load**.
- In OSPF, the cost is automatically derived: **Cost = 10^8 / Bandwidth (in bps)**. A 100 Mbps link has cost **1**, a 10 Mbps link has cost **10**.

#### Step 3 — Construction of the Link State Packet (LSP / LSA)
Each router builds an LSP containing:

| Field | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Router ID** | Unique 32-bit identifier (typically the highest IP address). |
| **Sequence Number** | Incremented with every new LSP. |
| **Age** | Starts at 0, incremented as it ages; max 3600s. |
| **Neighbor List** | List of directly connected neighbors and their link costs. |

#### Step 4 — Reliable Flooding of LSPs
- LSPs are sent to **all routers in the AS**, not just neighbors.
- Upon receiving an LSP, a router:
  1. Checks the **sequence number** — if newer than the stored copy, it stores it and re-floods.
  2. If older, it discards.
  3. Acknowledgements are sent to ensure reliability.
- This ensures **every router in the AS has an identical LSDB** (convergence property).

#### Step 5 — Computation of Shortest Path (Dijkstra's SPF)
- Each router runs **Dijkstra's algorithm locally** on the LSDB.
- Output: a **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** rooted at the local router.
- The SPT directly generates the router's forwarding table.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All critical formulas you must memorize for KTU ESE on this topic.** Pipes (\vert) are used to denote absolute/cardinality to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dijkstra Relaxation** | $D(v) = \min \{D(v),\; D(u) + c(u,v)\}$ | $D(v)$ = current best distance to v; $c(u,v)$ = cost of edge u→v |
| **OSPF Cost Metric** | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{10^{8} \text{ bps}}{\text{Interface BW (bps)}}$ | Reference BW = 100 Mbps. Minimum cost = 1. |
| **Number of LSPs per router** | $\text{LSPs} = k$ | $k$ = number of directly connected neighbors |
| **Total LSPs in AS** | $\text{Total LSPs} = N \cdot k_{\text{avg}}$ | $N$ = total routers in AS |
| **Time Complexity of Dijkstra** | $O(\vert V \vert^{2})$ with array; $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ with min-heap | $\vert V \vert$ = vertices, $\vert E \vert$ = edges |
| **MaxAge of an LSA** | $\text{MaxAge} = 3600$ seconds | OSPF standard |
| **HelloInterval (OSPF)** | $\text{HelloInterval} = 10$ s | Default on broadcast networks |
| **DeadInterval (OSPF)** | $\text{DeadInterval} = 4 \times \text{HelloInterval} = 40$ s | Default |
| **LSDB Size** | $\propto \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert$ | Memory grows with topology size |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** — used in 90% of enterprise networks and ISP cores.
- **IS-IS (Intermediate System to Intermediate System)** — used in large service provider backbones and undersea cable networks.
- **Used in SDN (Software Defined Networks)** — OpenFlow controllers use a variant of link-state for centralized path computation.
- **Critical for mesh networks and 5G core** where rapid, loop-free reconvergence after link failure is mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LSR beats Distance Vector for large networks**:
> - **Fast convergence** (Dijkstra is O(E log V); Bellman-Ford is O(VE)).
> - **Loop-free** (every router sees the whole topology).
> - **No count-to-infinity problem**.
> - **Trade-off**: Higher memory (full LSDB) and higher initial bandwidth use (flooding).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Dijkstra's Algorithm (The SPF Engine)

**Goal:** Given a weighted, connected graph $G = (V, E, w)$ where $w(u,v)$ is the cost of edge $(u,v)$, find the shortest path from a source vertex $s$ to every other vertex.

**Data Structures Used:**

- $D[v]$: Tentative shortest distance from $s$ to $v$. Initialized to $\infty$ for all $v \neq s$; $D[s] = 0$.
- $P[v]$: Predecessor of $v$ on the shortest path (used to reconstruct the SPT).
- $\mathcal{N}$ (Permanent Set): Vertices whose shortest distance is **finalized**.
- $\mathcal{T}$ (Tentative Set): Vertices whose distance is **estimated** but not yet final.

**Algorithm Steps (Formal Derivation):**

1. **Initialization Step:**
   - $\mathcal{N} \leftarrow \emptyset$, $\mathcal{T} \leftarrow V$
   - $D[s] = 0$, $D[v] = \infty$ for all $v \in V \setminus \{s\}$
   - $P[v] \leftarrow \text{NIL}$ for all $v$

2. **Main Loop (Iterate $|\mathcal{N}|$ times until all vertices are finalized):**
   - **Selection:** Choose $u \in \mathcal{T}$ such that $D[u]$ is minimum across all tentative vertices. Formally:
     $$u = \underset{x \in \mathcal{T}}{\arg\min}\; D[x]$$
   - **Commit:** Move $u$ from $\mathcal{T}$ to $\mathcal{N}$ (finalize $u$'s shortest path).
   - **Relaxation:** For every neighbor $v$ of $u$ still in $\mathcal{T}$:
     $$\text{if } D[v] > D[u] + w(u,v) \text{ then } D[v] \leftarrow D[u] + w(u,v),\; P[v] \leftarrow u$$

3. **Termination:** When $\mathcal{T} = \emptyset$, the algorithm terminates. The shortest path to any $v$ is reconstructed by backtracking through $P[v] \rightarrow P[P[v]] \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow s$.

**Mathematical Correctness (Sketch of Proof):**

The correctness of Dijkstra's algorithm relies on the **non-negative edge weight assumption**: $w(u,v) \geq 0$ for all $(u,v) \in E$. When a vertex $u$ is moved to $\mathcal{N}$, the algorithm claims $D[u]$ is the absolute shortest distance to $u$.

*Proof by contradiction:* Suppose a shorter path to $u$ exists via some other tentative vertex $w$. Then $D[w] + w(w,u) < D[u]$. But $u$ was chosen as the minimum of $\mathcal{T}$, so $D[u] \leq D[w]$. Adding $w(w,u) \geq 0$ to both sides gives $D[u] \leq D[w] + w(w,u)$, which contradicts the assumption. Hence $D[u]$ is optimal. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Graph Problem)

**Problem:** Consider the following network graph. Apply Dijkstra's algorithm from source node **A** to determine the shortest path and cost to every other node.

| Edge | Cost | Edge | Cost |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| A–B | 4 | B–D | 3 |
| A–C | 2 | C–D | 1 |
| A–D | 5 | C–E | 6 |
| B–C | 1 | D–E | 2 |

**Solution — Step-by-Step Execution:**

**Iteration 0 (Initialization):**

| Node | A | B | C | D | E |
| :--- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| D[v] | 0 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| P[v] | NIL | NIL | NIL | NIL | NIL |
| Set | $\mathcal{N}$ | $\mathcal{T}$ | $\mathcal{T}$ | $\mathcal{T}$ | $\mathcal{T}$ |

**Iteration 1: Select A (min D=0). Commit A to $\mathcal{N}$.**

Relax edges from A:

- $D[B] = \min(\infty, 0 + 4) = 4$, $P[B] = A$
- $D[C] = \min(\infty, 0 + 2) = 2$, $P[C] = A$
- $D[D] = \min(\infty, 0 + 5) = 5$, $P[D] = A$

| Node | A | B | C | D | E |
| :--- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| D[v] | 0 | **4** | **2** | **5** | $\infty$ |
| P[v] | NIL | A | A | A | NIL |

**Iteration 2: Select C (min D=2 in $\mathcal{T}$). Commit C to $\mathcal{N}$.**

Relax edges from C (neighbors: A, B, D, E):

- $D[B] = \min(4, 2 + 1) = 3$, $P[B] = C$  ✅ *improved from 4 to 3*
- $D[D] = \min(5, 2 + 1) = 3$, $P[D] = C$  ✅ *improved from 5 to 3*
- $D[E] = \min(\infty, 2 + 6) = 8$, $P[E] = C$

| Node | A | B | C | D | E |
| :--- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| D[v] | 0 | **3** | 2 | **3** | **8** |
| P[v] | NIL | C | A | C | C |

**Iteration 3: TIE between B and D (both D=3). Select B. Commit B to $\mathcal{N}$.**

Relax edges from B (neighbors: A, C, D):

- $D[D] = \min(3, 3 + 3) = 3$ (no change, D is already 3 via C).

| Node | A | B | C | D | E |
| :--- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| D[v] | 0 | 3 | 2 | **3** | **8** |
| P[v] | NIL | C | A | C | C |

**Iteration 4: Select D (min D=3 in $\mathcal{T}$). Commit D to $\mathcal{N}$.**

Relax edges from D (neighbors: A, B, C, E):

- $D[E] = \min(8, 3 + 2) = 5$, $P[E] = D$  ✅ *improved from 8 to 5*

| Node | A | B | C | D | E |
| :--- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| D[v] | 0 | 3 | 2 | 3 | **5** |
| P[v] | NIL | C | A | C | D |

**Iteration 5: Select E (last remaining, D=5). Commit E to $\mathcal{N}$.**

Algorithm terminates. $\mathcal{N} = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$.

**Final Result — Shortest Path Tree from A:**

| Destination | Shortest Cost | Shortest Path |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| B | 3 | A → C → B |
| C | 2 | A → C |
| D | 3 | A → C → D |
| E | 5 | A → C → D → E |

**Verification using the relaxation invariant:** At termination, $\sum_{(u,v) \in \text{Path}} w(u,v)$ for each path above matches $D[v]$ in the final table. ✅

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Link State Routing — Five-Phase Functional Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Router Boot Up]) --> phase1[Phase 1: Discovery via HELLO Packets]
    phase1 --> phase2[Phase 2: Measure Link Cost OSPF 10e8 BW]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3: Build Link State Packet LSP]
    phase3 --> phase4[Phase 4: Reliable Flooding to ALL Routers in AS]
    phase4 --> phase5[Phase 5: Run Dijkstra SPF on Local LSDB]
    phase5 --> phase6[Generate Shortest Path Tree]
    phase6 --> phase7[Populate Forwarding Table]
    phase7 --> monitor{Link State Change Detected?}
    monitor -- Yes --> phase3
    monitor -- No --> phase8([Steady State Operation])
    phase8 -. Periodic Hello 10s .-> phase1
```

### 4.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm Execution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initNode([Initialize D s 0 and D v INFINITY for all v]) --> selectStep{Select u in TENTATIVE with min D u}
    selectStep -- Found u --> commitStep[Move u to PERMANENT Set]
    commitStep --> relaxStep[For every neighbor v of u still in TENTATIVE]
    relaxStep --> checkCond{D v greater than D u plus w u v}
    checkCond -- Yes --> updateStep[Update D v to D u plus w u v and set PREDECESSOR v to u]
    checkCond -- No --> nextNeighbor[Skip to next neighbor]
    updateStep --> nextNeighbor
    nextNeighbor --> moreNeighbors{More neighbors of u to relax}
    moreNeighbors -- Yes --> relaxStep
    moreNeighbors -- No --> checkDone{All vertices in PERMANENT}
    checkDone -- No --> selectStep
    checkDone -- Yes --> terminateNode([Algorithm Terminates Output SPT])
```

### 4.3 Flooding Mechanism — Sequence Architecture

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R1 as Router 1 Source
    participant R2 as Router 2
    participant R3 as Router 3
    participant R4 as Router 4
    participant R5 as Router 5
    R1->>R2: LSA with Seq 100
    R1->>R3: LSA with Seq 100
    R1->>R4: LSA with Seq 100
    R2->>R3: LSA with Seq 100 forwarded
    R2->>R5: LSA with Seq 100 forwarded
    R3->>R4: LSA with Seq 100 forwarded
    R3->>R5: LSA with Seq 100 forwarded
    R4->>R5: LSA with Seq 100 forwarded
    Note over R1,R5: All 5 routers now have identical LSDB entries
    R1->>R2: New LSA with Seq 101 link cost changed
    R2->>R3: Forward Seq 101
    R2->>R4: Forward Seq 101
    R3->>R5: Forward Seq 101
    Note over R1,R5: Reliable flooding ensures convergence after topology change
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. Define Link State Routing. Mention any four characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Link State Routing (LSR)** is an intra-domain, dynamic routing algorithm in which every router maintains a complete and identical **Link State Database (LSDB)** of the network topology by flooding **Link State Advertisements (LSAs)** to all other routers in the Autonomous System (AS), and independently computes shortest paths using **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm**.

**Four Characteristics:**

1. **Global topology view** — Each router possesses a complete map of the entire AS.
2. **Event-triggered updates** — LSAs are generated only when a topology change occurs, not periodically.
3. **Faster convergence** — Compared to Distance Vector, LSR converges in $O(\text{diameter})$ time, avoiding count-to-infinity.
4. **Loop-free paths** — Guaranteed by construction of the SPF tree from synchronized LSDBs.
5. *(Bonus)* — Each LSP carries a sequence number to prevent duplicates and stale information.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for characteristics — write any 4)*

---

#### **Q2. List the fields of a Link State Packet (LSP) and explain the role of the sequence number.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Fields of an LSP:**

| Field | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| Router ID | Uniquely identifies the originating router (32-bit). |
| Sequence Number | Counter incremented with every new LSP from the same router. |
| Age | Time since the LSA was generated; decremented as it ages (max 3600s). |
| Neighbor List | IDs of adjacent routers and the cost of the connecting link. |
| Checksum | Error-detection to ensure integrity during flooding. |

**Role of the Sequence Number:**

The sequence number ensures that every router stores the **most recent** LSP. When a router receives an LSP, it compares the sequence number with its stored copy. If the incoming sequence number is **higher**, the new LSP is accepted, stored, and re-flooded. If it is **lower or equal**, the LSP is discarded. This mechanism prevents routing loops, stale data, and duplicate processing during flooding.

*(3 marks: 2 for fields, 1 for sequence number explanation)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfall):**
> - **Do NOT** confuse "Link State Packet" terminology with OSPF-specific LSA types (Type 1, 2, 3, 4, 5). In Part A answers, use the generic term *LSP/LSA* unless the question specifically asks for OSPF.
> - In Dijkstra-based problems, **students lose 2-3 marks** by forgetting to show the **predecessor array** $P[v]$ alongside distance updates. Always show the **complete table** for every iteration.
> - Do not write pseudocode for Dijkstra in a math-style problem. KTU expects an **iteration table**, not code.
> - Forgetting to **re-flood** the LSP when a new sequence number is detected is a recurring 2-mark deduction.
