### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain the five steps of Link State Routing with a neat diagram.** *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

Link State Routing operates in five sequential phases, described below:

**Step 1 — Neighbor Discovery:**
Each router, upon boot-up, sends **HELLO packets** out of all interfaces. Neighbors reply with their Router ID and negotiated parameters (HelloInterval, DeadInterval). This forms the **adjacency table**. In OSPF, Hello packets are sent every **10 seconds**; a neighbor is declared dead if no Hello is received for **40 seconds**.

**Step 2 — Link Cost Measurement:**
The router measures or assigns a **cost** to each of its links. In OSPF, cost is computed as:
$$\text{Cost} = \frac{10^{8} \text{ bps}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth (bps)}}$$
This provides an automatic, inverse-bandwidth metric. A 1 Gbps link has cost **1**, while a 100 Mbps link has cost **10**.

**Step 3 — Construction of LSP:**
The router builds a **Link State Packet** containing:
- Its Router ID
- A list of neighbors and their link costs
- A **Sequence Number** (incremented with every new LSP)
- An **Age** field
- A **Checksum**

**Step 4 — Reliable Flooding:**
The LSP is flooded to **all routers in the AS**, not just adjacent ones. Each receiving router:
1. Compares the sequence number with its stored copy.
2. Accepts if newer; discards if older or duplicate.
3. Sends an **acknowledgement** to the sender.
4. Re-floods the LSP out of all other interfaces (except the one it arrived on).

**Step 5 — Shortest Path Computation:**
Each router independently executes **Dijkstra's algorithm** on the identical LSDB. The output is a **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** rooted at the local router, from which the forwarding table is populated.

*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual flow.)*

**Block diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
   [HELLO] --> [Adjacency Table] --> [Measure Cost] --> [Build LSP]
        --> [FLOOD to all routers] --> [Update LSDB] --> [Run Dijkstra]
        --> [SPT] --> [Forwarding Table]
```

*(7 marks: 1 per step × 5 = 5 marks, 2 marks for diagram/flow)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Compare Distance Vector Routing and Link State Routing.** *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Distance Vector (RIP) | Link State (OSPF) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Algorithm Used** | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra's SPF |
| **Information Shared** | Entire routing table | Local link costs only |
| **Shared With** | Directly connected neighbors only | All routers in the AS (flooding) |
| **Topology View** | Local / partial | Global / complete |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slow (count-to-infinity) | Fast (event-triggered) |
| **Routing Loops** | Possible (temporary) | Not possible |
| **Bandwidth Usage** | Low (periodic) | High during convergence, low after |
| **Memory Requirement** | Low (only routing table) | High (full LSDB) |
| **CPU Usage** | Low | High (Dijkstra on every change) |
| **Update Trigger** | Periodic (every 30s in RIP) | Event-triggered + periodic refresh (30 min) |
| **Metric** | Hop count (max 15) | Cost (based on bandwidth) |
| **Scalability** | Limited (max 15 hops) | Highly scalable (hierarchy with areas) |
| **Real-world Example** | RIP, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(VE)$ | $O((V+E)\log V)$ with min-heap |

**Conclusion:** Link State Routing is preferred for **large, hierarchical, mission-critical networks** (enterprise WANs, ISP backbones) due to fast convergence and loop-freedom. Distance Vector is still used in **small, flat networks** where simplicity outweighs convergence speed.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per row for the most important 6-7 rows; 1 mark for conclusion)*

#### **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the working of OSPF protocol. Mention any four features.** *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** is a link-state, intra-domain (IGP) routing protocol standardized in **RFC 2328**. It is the most widely deployed LSR implementation in enterprise and ISP networks.

**Working of OSPF — Stepwise:**

1. **Router boots and sends HELLO packets** on all OSPF-enabled interfaces. Neighboring OSPF routers reply, forming **adjacencies**.
2. **Designated Router (DR) and Backup DR (BDR)** are elected on multi-access networks (e.g., Ethernet) to reduce LSA flooding overhead — all other routers form **full adjacencies** only with the DR/BDR.
3. **Link State Advertisements (LSAs)** are generated by each router describing its local links and flooded reliably through the DR to the entire OSPF area.
4. **All routers in the same area** synchronize their **LSDBs** (flooding ensures database consistency).
5. **Each router runs Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** on the LSDB to build its own SPT and forwarding table.
6. **Hierarchical design:** Large networks are divided into **areas** (Area 0 = backbone; others = non-backbone). Area Border Routers (ABRs) summarize routes between areas.

**Block Diagram (Hierarchical OSPF Architecture):**

```
              ┌─────────── AREA 0 (Backbone) ───────────┐
              │                                          │
              │   ┌────┐    ┌────┐    ┌────┐             │
              │   │ R1 │----│ R2 │----│ R3 │             │
              │   └─┬──┘    └─┬──┘    └──┬─┘             │
              └─────┼─────────┼──────────┼───────────────┘
              ┌─────┼─────────┼──────────┼──────────┐
         ┌────▼─┐ ┌─▼──┐  ┌───▼┐  ┌─────▼─┐
         │ R4   │ │ R5 │  │ R6 │  │  R7   │      AREA 1
         │      │ │    │  │    │  │       │    (Stub Area)
         └──────┘ └────┘  └────┘  └───────┘
              │       │      │       │
         ┌────▼───────▼──────▼───────▼────┐
         │         Internal LANs           │
         └─────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Four Features of OSPF:**

1. **Hierarchical design with areas** — Reduces routing table size and LSDB per router.
2. **Classless routing (CIDR & VLSM support)** — Efficient IP address utilization.
3. **Fast, loop-free convergence** — Due to synchronized LSDBs and Dijkstra's SPF.
4. **Type-of-Service (TOS) routing** — Multiple metrics for different service classes.
5. *(Bonus)* — Supports **multicast** and **unicast** routing.
6. *(Bonus)* — Authentication support (MD5 / SHA) for secure updates.

*(7 marks: 3 for working steps, 2 for diagram, 2 for features)*

- [x] **OSPF HelloInterval = 10s, DeadInterval = 40s** (4× Hello).
- [x] **OSPF cost formula**: $10^8 / \text{Bandwidth (bps)}$.
- [x] **Dijkstra's relaxation**: $D(v) = \min \{D(v), D(u) + w(u,v)\}$.
- [x] **Dijkstra requires non-negative edge weights** (else incorrect).
- [x] **Time complexity**: $O((V+E)\log V)$ with min-heap; $O(V^2)$ with array.
- [x] **Real-world LSR protocols**: OSPF (RFC 2328), IS-IS.
- [x] **OSPF real-world packet overhead**: Each LSA flooded = $O(N)$ messages in worst case.
- [x] **Advantages of LSR**: Fast convergence, loop-free, no count-to-infinity, scalable via areas.
- [x] **Disadvantages of LSR**: High memory (full LSDB), high initial CPU, complex implementation.
- [x] **Hierarchical OSPF**: Area 0 (backbone) mandatory; non-backbone areas connect via ABRs.
- [x] **DR/BDR election** on multi-access networks reduces $O(N^2)$ adjacencies to $O(N)$.
- [x] **Dijkstra's correctness** requires non-negative edge weights — always state this assumption.
- [x] **In iteration tables**, always include three rows: $D[v]$, $P[v]$, and Status (PERM/TENT).
- [x] **Most common KTU mistake**: Forgetting predecessor array $P[v]$ in Dijkstra iterations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multicast Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multicast Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multicast Routing** is a specialized networking technique that enables a single source node to efficiently transmit a single data stream to a *selected subset* of interested receivers (called a **multicast group**) across an IP network, using a single transmission from the source. Unlike unicast (one-to-one) or broadcast (one-to-all), multicast operates on a **one-to-many** (or many-to-many) communication paradigm.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Computer Networks (Module 3), multicast routing is studied as the mechanism by which routers cooperate using specialized protocols to construct and maintain **distribution trees** that minimize bandwidth consumption while delivering identical data packets to all group members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Multicast routing is the set of protocols and algorithms (such as DVMRP, PIM, CBT, MOSPF) used by routers to forward a single IP datagram from a source to multiple interested receivers, identified by a Class D IP address (224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255), by building and pruning optimal distribution trees across the network.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a college professor in Kerala who wants to send a single notice about an upcoming tech fest to *only those students who opted for the coding club* — not the entire college.

- **Unicast Analogy**: You call each interested student one-by-one on the phone. Wasteful, repetitive, and time-consuming.
- **Broadcast Analogy**: You shout the message on a loudspeaker across the entire campus. Everyone hears it, even those who don't care (and the canteen staff gets annoyed).
- **Multicast Analogy**: You post the notice on a single shared WhatsApp group called "Coding Club KTU 2024." You send **one** message, and WhatsApp intelligently delivers it only to the group members, using the least possible network paths. This is **multicast routing** — one sender, one message, smart distribution to only interested receivers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Multicast Matters (Real-World Use Cases):**
> - **IPTV / Live Streaming** (e.g., Asianet News live)
> - **Stock market data feeds** (one source, thousands of traders)
> - **Software updates** distributed to multiple corporate branches
> - **Video conferencing** in enterprise networks
> - **Distance learning** platforms (KTU online lectures)

## 1.3 Key Multicast IP Address Range (Bold Highlights)

The Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA) reserves the **Class D address block: 224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255** exclusively for multicast group identification.

| Range | Scope | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **224.0.0.0 – 224.0.0.255** | Link-Local | Network protocol control (e.g., OSPF, EIGRP) |
| **224.0.1.0 – 224.0.1.255** | Internetwork | Internet-wide multicast |
| **239.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255** | Administratively Scoped | Private multicast (organizational use) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Point:** A multicast IP address does NOT refer to a specific host. It refers to a *logical group identifier*. Any host that wishes to receive the traffic joins this logical group using **IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: How Multicast Routing Works (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1 — Group Membership Management (Host-to-Router)
Hosts use **IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol)** to inform their *directly connected* local router that they wish to join a specific multicast group identified by a Class D address.

### Step 2 — Distribution Tree Construction
Routers coordinate with one another to build a **distribution tree** rooted at either:
- The **source** (called a *Source-Specific Tree* or *Shortest Path Tree / SPT*)
- A **rendezvous point / core** (called a *Shared Tree* or *RP-based Tree*)

### Step 3 — Packet Forwarding Along the Tree
When a source sends a multicast packet, each router on the tree forwards a *single copy* out of every interface that leads to at least one group member — **except the interface on which the packet arrived** (the *reverse path forwarding* rule, explained later).

### Step 4 — Pruning (For Dense Mode Protocols)
If a router determines that none of its downstream branches contain active group members, it sends a **PRUNE message** upstream so that unnecessary sub-trees are trimmed off.

### Step 5 — Grafting (Re-joining)
If a host later joins a previously pruned branch, a **GRAFT message** is sent upstream to re-attach that branch to the distribution tree.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why"

- **Step 1 is needed** because routers cannot know which hosts care without an explicit signal.
- **Step 2 prevents flooding** the entire internet with every packet.
- **Step 3's reverse-path rule** prevents routing loops (a critical KTU board favorite).
- **Steps 4 & 5** keep the tree efficient — bandwidth isn't wasted on silent branches.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Number of copies on a link (SPT) | $N_{copies} = N_{leaves} - 1$ | In a shortest-path tree with $N_{leaves}$ group members |
| Bandwidth saved by multicast vs unicast | $BW_{saved} = (N-1) \times S - S = (N-2) \times S$ | Where $N$ = number of receivers, $S$ = single stream size |
| Multicast IP range | $[224.0.0.0, \; 239.255.255.255]$ | All Class D addresses |
| TTL threshold for local multicast | $TTL \ge 1$ | Must be at least 1 to leave host |
| Administrative scope (private) | $[239.0.0.0, \; 239.255.255.255]$ | Reserved for organizations |
| RPF check | Interface on which packet arrived = interface on unicast route back to source | Prevents loops |
| Prune timer (typical) | $T_{prune} \approx 3$ minutes | DVMRP default |
| IGMP report suppression | One report per LAN per group | Optimization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Multicast routing is the backbone of modern CDN (Content Delivery Network) live-streaming infrastructure. Without multicast, streaming the IPL final to 50 million viewers would require 50 million unicast streams, saturating backbone links. Multicast reduces this to a single tree.

## 2.4 Classification of Multicast Routing Protocols (KTU Board Table)

| Category | Protocols | Tree Type | Topology Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Distance Vector** | **DVMRP** (Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol) | Source-specific (SPT) | Dense receivers |
| **Link State** | **MOSPF** (Multicast OSPF) | Source-specific (SPT) | Dense receivers, OSPF-aware |
| **Core-Based** | **CBT** (Core-Based Trees) | Shared (Core) | Sparse receivers |
| **Protocol Independent** | **PIM-DM** (Protocol Independent Multicast – Dense Mode) | Source-specific (SPT) | Dense receivers |
| **Protocol Independent** | **PIM-SM** (PIM – Sparse Mode) | Shared (RP) → SPT switchover | Sparse receivers |
| **Group Management** | **IGMPv1, IGMPv2, IGMPv3** | N/A | Host-to-router signaling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Algorithm Analysis — Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)

**Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** is the *fundamental building block* of all dense-mode multicast routing protocols (DVMRP, PIM-DM, etc.). It uses the router's existing **unicast routing table** to make forwarding decisions.

### Algorithm Logic

A multicast packet arriving on interface $I_{in}$ from source $S$ is forwarded out of *all other interfaces* **IF AND ONLY IF** $I_{in}$ is the same interface that the router would use to send a unicast packet back to $S$. In other words, $I_{in}$ must lie on the *reverse* of the shortest path from the router to $S$.

### Formal Mathematical Representation

Let the unicast routing table at router $R$ define a function:
$$f(R, S) \rightarrow I_{R \to S}$$
which returns the outgoing interface for forwarding a unicast packet from $R$ to source $S$.

A multicast packet arriving at $R$ on interface $I_{in}$ with source address $S$ satisfies the **RPF Check** if and only if:
$$I_{in} \equiv f(R, S)$$

If the check passes, $R$ forwards the packet on all interfaces in the set:
$$\mathcal{F} = \{I_j : I_j \neq I_{in}\} \setminus \{I_{pruned}\}$$
where $\{I_{pruned}\}$ represents interfaces for which prune state exists.

### Worked Example: RPF Loop Prevention

Consider the topology where Router $A$ is the source, Router $B$ is in the middle, and Router $C$ is another peer. Suppose $A$ sends a multicast packet to group $G$. $B$'s unicast routing table says: "To reach $A$, use interface $I_1$." $C$'s table says: "To reach $A$, use interface $I_3$."

If the packet somehow arrives at $B$ on interface $I_2$ (not $I_1$), the RPF check **fails**, and $B$ drops the packet. This guarantees that no packet can loop back over a non-reverse path.

## 3.2 Detailed Algorithm Analysis — Reverse Path Broadcasting (RPB)

RPB improves RPF by ensuring each packet is forwarded **exactly once per link** across the entire broadcast network. It uses the concept of **parent** and **child** routers on the SPT.

For each source $S$ and group $G$, each router $R_i$ in the network computes:
$$d(R_i, S) = \text{shortest unicast distance from } R_i \text{ to } S$$

A router $R_i$ is designated as the **parent** of $R_j$ on the tree rooted at $S$ if:
$$d(R_i, S) < d(R_j, S)$$
and $R_i$ lies on the shortest path from $R_j$ back to $S$.

The packet is forwarded only from parent to child, never from child to parent, eliminating duplicate packet delivery on shared broadcast segments.

## 3.3 Detailed Algorithm Analysis — Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM) = RPF + Prune + Graft

RPM is the complete dense-mode multicast algorithm used by DVMRP and PIM-DM. It adds two control messages to RPB:

**Prune Message Format (Conceptual):**
$$\text{PRUNE} : (Source_{IP}, Group_{IP}, Prune_{TTL}, Prune_{Timeout})$$

**Graft Message Format (Conceptual):**
$$\text{GRAFT} : (Source_{IP}, Group_{IP})$$

### Step-by-Step Operation

**Step 1 — Flooding Phase:**
When source $S$ starts transmitting to group $G$, the packet is flooded across the entire network via RPB. Every router initially forwards it on all interfaces except the RPF interface.

**Step 2 — Prune Phase:**
Any router $R$ that has no local members for group $G$ and no downstream neighbors with members sends a PRUNE message out its RPF interface toward $S$. The upstream router marks that interface as pruned and stops forwarding.

**Step 3 — Steady State:**
Only routers on branches leading to active group members continue receiving the multicast stream.

**Step 4 — Re-join (Graft):**
When a host later joins group $G$ on a previously pruned branch, the local router sends a GRAFT message hop-by-hop upstream until it reaches a router with active forwarding state, restoring the branch.

### Mathematical Model of RPM Bandwidth Efficiency

Let:
- $V$ = total number of routers in the network
- $M$ = number of routers with at least one group member
- $L$ = total number of links in the SPT

During flooding phase, total copies of packet transmitted = $L$
After pruning converges, copies transmitted = $L_{active}$, where $L_{active} \subseteq L$

Pruning efficiency:
$$\eta_{prune} = \frac{L - L_{active}}{L} \times 100\%$$

For an efficient multicast protocol with sparse receivers:
$$\eta_{prune} \approx \frac{V - M}{V} \times 100\%$$

## 3.4 Detailed Algorithm Analysis — Core-Based Trees (CBT)

Unlike source-based trees, CBT builds **a single shared tree per group**, rooted at a special router called the **Core** (or Rendezvous Point).

### Construction Steps

**Step 1 — Core Election:** A Core router is manually configured or auto-elected for each multicast group.

**Step 2 — Join Request:** When a router learns that a downstream host wants to join group $G$, it sends a unicast **JOIN** message hop-by-hop toward the Core.

**Step 3 — Acknowledge:** The Core (or an intermediate on-tree router) sends back a **JOIN-ACK**, which installs forwarding state on every router along the path. The branch becomes part of the shared tree.

**Step 4 — Forwarding:** Any source sending to group $G$ first unicasts the packet to the Core, which then distributes it down the shared tree.

### Key CBT Trade-off

| Aspect | Source Tree (SPT) | Shared Tree (CBT / PIM-SM) |
|---|---|---|
| Optimal paths from source to receivers | ✅ Yes (shortest) | ❌ No (may be sub-optimal) |
| State per (S, G) | High (one entry per source) | Low (one entry per group) |
| Bandwidth on shared tree | Lower per group | Higher per packet |
| Suitable for | Few sources, many receivers | Many sources, sparse receivers |

## 3.5 IGMP Versions — Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Feature | **IGMPv1** | **IGMPv2** | **IGMPv3** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Query types | General query only | General + Group-specific | General + Group-and-source-specific |
| Leave mechanism | Silent leave (router times out) | Explicit **LEAVE** message | Explicit leave via GROUP-AND-SOURCE records |
| Source filtering | No (ASM only) | No (ASM only) | Yes (**SSM** – Source-Specific Multicast) |
| Max response time field | Fixed | Variable (tunable) | Variable (tunable) |
| Compatibility | Baseline | Backward-compatible w/ v1 | Backward-compatible w/ v1, v2 |

**IGMP Message Types (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Membership Query** (Type = 0x11): Router → Hosts ("Who wants groups?")
- **Membership Report** / **Join** (Type = 0x12 or 0x16): Host → Router ("I want group G")
- **Leave Group** (Type = 0x17, v2/v3 only): Host → Router ("I'm leaving group G")

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Multicast Routing System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC["SOURCE DOMAIN"]
        SRCSRV["Multicast Source Host"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE / ACCESS NETWORK"]
        AR1["Access Router R1"]
        H1["Host A - Member of Group 224.1.1.1"]
        H2["Host B - NOT a member"]
        H3["Host C - Member of Group 224.1.1.1"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["MULTICAST-AWARE CORE NETWORK"]
        DR["Designated Router DR"]
        RPM["Reverse Path Multicasting Engine"]
        TREEBLD["Distribution Tree Builder"]
        PRUNE["Prune/Graft State Machine"]
    end

    subgraph DEST["RECEIVER DOMAIN"]
        RR["Receiver Router"]
        HM1["Receiver Host 1"]
        HM2["Receiver Host 2"]
    end

    SRCSRV -->|Single multicast flow| AR1
    AR1 -->|"IGMP Query (who joins?)"| H1
    AR1 -->|"IGMP Query (who joins?)"| H2
    AR1 -->|"IGMP Query (who joins?)"| H3
    H1 -->|"IGMP Report - Join 224.1.1.1"| AR1
    H3 -->|"IGMP Report - Join 224.1.1.1"| AR1

    AR1 -->|"PIM Join / DVMRP Probe"| DR
    DR --> RPM
    DR --> TREEBLD
    DR --> PRUNE
    DR -->|"Tree Branch only to active group members"| RR
    RR --> HM1
    RR --> HM2
```

## 4.2 Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PKT["Multicast Packet Arrives on Interface Iin from Source S"]
    CHECK{"Does Iin match unicast route back to S? (RPF Check)"}
    DROP["DROP Packet - RPF Failure - Prevents Loops"]
    PRUNECHK{"Any active PRUNE state on outgoing interfaces?"}
    FWD["Forward out all non-RPF, non-pruned interfaces"]
    QUIET["Packet absorbed - no downstream members"]

    PKT --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|"NO"| DROP
    CHECK -->|"YES"| PRUNECHK
    PRUNECHK -->|"Some interfaces pruned"| FWD
    PRUNECHK -->|"All downstream pruned"| QUIET
```

## 4.3 DVMRP — Three-Phase Operation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PHASE 1: FLOODING via RPB - Reach all routers"]
    P2["PHASE 2: PRUNE - Leaf routers without members send PRUNE upstream"]
    P3["PHASE 3: GRAFT - Re-attach branches when new members appear"]

    P1 -->|"TTL expiry or duplicate detection triggers prune"| P2
    P2 -->|"Host joins group again"| P3
    P3 -->|"New branch added to SPT"| P1
```

## 4.4 Source Tree vs Shared Tree — Comparative Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ST["SOURCE-BASED TREE (SPT) - One tree per source"]
        S1["Source S1"]
        S2["Source S2"]
        ST1["Router T1"]
        ST2["Router T2"]
        ST3["Router T3"]
        SR1["Receiver R1"]
        SR2["Receiver R2"]
        SR3["Receiver R3"]
        S1 --> ST1
        S1 --> ST2
        S1 --> ST3
        ST1 --> SR1
        ST2 --> SR2
        ST3 --> SR3
        S2 -.->|"Separate tree for S2"| ST1
    end

    subgraph CT["SHARED TREE (CBT / PIM-SM) - One tree per group"]
        CORE["CORE / RP"]
        CT1["Branch Router A"]
        CT2["Branch Router B"]
        CT3["Branch Router C"]
        CR1["Receiver R1"]
        CR2["Receiver R2"]
        CR3["Receiver R3"]
        CORE --> CT1
        CORE --> CT2
        CORE --> CT3
        CT1 --> CR1
        CT2 --> CR2
        CT3 --> CR3
    end
```

## 4.5 IGMP Host-to-Router State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> DelayTimer: "Receive IGMP Query"
    DelayTimer --> Reporting: "Timer expires for group G"
    Reporting --> Idle: "Send IGMP Membership Report"
    Reporting --> Suppressed: "Hear another host's report (Report Suppression)"
    Suppressed --> Idle: "Suppression timer expires"
    Idle --> Leaving: "User application leaves group (v2/v3)"
    Leaving --> Idle: "Send IGMP Leave to Router"
    Leaving --> Idle: "Router sends Group-Specific Query, no reply"
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]
**Differentiate between unicast, broadcast, and multicast communication with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Delivery Scope | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Unicast** | One source → One specific destination | A user opening `https://www.ktu.edu.in` |
| **Broadcast** | One source → All hosts on the LAN (255.255.255.255) | ARP Request from a host |
| **Multicast** | One source → Selected group of hosts (Class D address) | IPTV stream to subscribed viewers |

*(1 mark for unicast definition + example, 1 mark for broadcast + example, 1 mark for multicast + example)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**What is the purpose of IGMP in multicast routing? List any two IGMP message types.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Purpose:** IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol) operates between a host and its directly connected (local) multicast router. It allows hosts to **join** or **leave** a multicast group and informs the local router of group membership so that the router can correctly forward multicast traffic only onto LAN segments that have at least one interested host. (2 marks)

**Two IGMP Message Types:** (1 mark)
1. **Membership Query** (Type 0x11) — sent by the router to discover which groups have members on the attached network.
2. **Membership Report / Join** (Type 0x12) — sent by a host to declare its interest in joining a specific multicast group.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often confuse the **multicast MAC address** mapping (low-order 23 bits of IP) — leave it blank and you lose 2 marks.
> 2. When drawing distribution trees, **forgetting to mark the source/root** explicitly costs 1 mark.
> 3. Mixing up **RPF (checks unicast table)** with **RPB (parent-child on tree)** is a 3-mark deduction.
> 4. Never write "multicast IP address of host" — it is a **group identifier**, not a host address.
> 5. In DVMRP questions, students forget the **graft mechanism** — both prune AND graft must be explained for full marks.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**Q.A (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** algorithm. How does it prevent looping of multicast packets? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) is a fundamental multicast forwarding algorithm in which a router accepts a multicast packet on an interface only if that interface is the same one the router would use to send a unicast packet back to the source. It is the foundational mechanism of dense-mode multicast protocols like DVMRP and PIM-DM.

**Operation (4 marks):**
1. Each router maintains (or reuses) a standard unicast routing table that maps every destination (including sources) to an outgoing interface.
2. When a multicast packet from source $S$ arrives on interface $I_{in}$ at router $R$, the router performs the **RPF check**:
   $$I_{in} \stackrel{?}{=} f(R, S)$$
   where $f(R, S)$ is the unicast forwarding function from $R$ toward $S$.
3. If the check succeeds, the router forwards a copy of the packet out of **every other interface** (except $I_{in}$). If it fails, the packet is silently dropped.
4. To prevent the same packet from circulating forever, the router also runs a **duplicate-detection** mechanism based on (Source, Group) pair and a short time window.

**Loop Prevention Explanation (2 marks):**
Because a packet is forwarded only out of interfaces that lie *along* the path back to the source, any packet that has wandered off the correct shortest path will arrive on a *non-RPF* interface. The RPF check fails, and the router drops it. Since the unicast routing table is itself loop-free (assuming a stable topology), RPF inherits this property, and multicast loops are mathematically impossible.

**Supporting Diagram (simple sketch expected — 1 mark):**
```
    [Source S]
        |
       (I_RPF)
   [Router R1]
   /          \
 (I2)        (I3)
[Router R2] [Router R3]
```
A packet from $S$ arriving at $R_1$ on $I_{RPF}$ is forwarded to $R_2$ and $R_3$. If a stray copy somehow returns to $R_1$ via $I_2$, the RPF check fails (since $I_2 \neq I_{RPF}$) and the packet is dropped.

---

**Q.A (b)** Explain the **Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol (DVMRP)** in detail. Mention the role of Prune and Graft messages. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Overview (1 mark):**
DVMRP is a dense-mode multicast routing protocol defined in RFC 1075. It is essentially an extension of the unicast RIP (Distance Vector) protocol with multicast forwarding capabilities added. It is suitable for environments where multicast group members are densely distributed across the network.

**Three Phases of DVMRP (4 marks):**

**Phase 1 — Neighbor Discovery / Probe:**
Routers exchange **Probe** messages to identify which neighbors are DVMRP-capable and to learn the metrics of the links.

**Phase 2 — Flooding via RPF + RPB:**
When a source $S$ begins transmitting to group $G$, DVMRP uses Reverse Path Forwarding combined with Reverse Path Broadcasting to flood a copy of each packet to *every* router in the network along the shortest-path tree.

**Phase 3 — Pruning:**
Each leaf router with no local members for group $G$ sends a **PRUNE** message upstream toward $S$ on its RPF interface. The upstream router marks that interface as pruned and stops forwarding $G$'s traffic on it. This continues hop-by-hop until only the branches that actually lead to group members remain active.

**Role of PRUNE message (1 mark):**
- Format (conceptual): `(Source IP, Multicast Group IP, TTL, Timeout)`
- Sent by a router to its upstream neighbor to inform it that no downstream members exist for a particular (S, G) pair.
- Has a finite lifetime (typically **3 minutes**) — after which pruning expires and flooding reoccurs unless another prune is issued. This handles silent hosts that become active again.

**Role of GRAFT message (1 mark):**
- Format (conceptual): `(Source IP, Multicast Group IP)`
- Sent by a router upstream toward $S$ when a new local host joins group $G$ on a previously pruned branch.
- Unlike prune (which is unreliable), graft is **reliably acknowledged hop-by-hop**, rapidly restoring the multicast flow without waiting for prune timeouts.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B (a)** Compare **Source-Based Tree (SPT)** and **Shared Tree (Core-Based Tree)** approaches in multicast routing. Which is more suitable for sparse-receiver scenarios? Justify. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Source-Based Tree (SPT) — 3 marks:**
- A *separate* shortest-path tree is constructed **for each (Source, Group)** pair.
- Tree is rooted at the **source**.
- Provides **optimal path** from source to every receiver.
- Drawback: requires forwarding state for every (S, G) pair → high memory consumption at core routers.
- Used by: DVMRP, MOSPF, PIM-DM, and PIM-SM after SPT switchover.

**Shared Tree (CBT) — 3 marks:**
- A **single** tree is constructed **per group**, rooted at a **Core / Rendezvous Point (RP)**.
- All sources send traffic first toward the Core, which then distributes down the shared tree.
- Provides **sub-optimal paths** (extra hop via core).
- Advantage: requires forwarding state for only **(∗, G)** pairs → very low memory consumption, scales to many sources.
- Used by: CBT (RFC 2201), PIM-SM in shared-tree mode.

**Comparison Table (1 mark — implied within marks above):**

| Aspect | SPT | Shared Tree |
|---|---|---|
| State entries | O(S × G) | O(G) |
| Path optimality | Optimal | Sub-optimal |
| Scalability (many sources) | Poor | Excellent |
| Convergence time | Fast | Slower (Core dependency) |

**Suitability for Sparse Receivers (1 mark):**
**Shared Tree (CBT / PIM-SM)** is more suitable for sparse-receiver scenarios because:
1. Building a full source-based tree for every source is wasteful when receivers are few and scattered.
2. Shared tree requires only one tree per group, drastically reducing router state.
3. Pruning in DVMRP-style dense-mode protocols is inefficient when 99% of the network must prune — bandwidth is wasted on the initial flood.
4. In sparse mode, sources explicitly send JOIN toward the RP, and only interested receivers establish branches → no global flooding occurs at all.

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the **Internet Group Management Protocol (IGMP)** with its three versions. How does IGMPv2 improve upon IGMPv1? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**What is IGMP (1 mark):**
IGMP is a **host-to-local-router** signaling protocol defined for IPv4 (its IPv6 counterpart is **MLD — Multicast Listener Discovery**). It enables hosts to dynamically register their interest in receiving multicast traffic for specific groups, and enables routers to track group membership on each of their directly attached LANs.

**Working Principle (1 mark):**
- The local multicast router periodically sends an **IGMP Membership Query** (default every 125 s) onto each attached LAN.
- Any host that wishes to continue receiving group $G$ responds with an **IGMP Membership Report** (join).
- If no host responds within the response window, the router concludes that there are no members for $G$ on that LAN and stops forwarding that group's traffic there.

**IGMPv1 (1 mark):**
- Original RFC 1112 specification.
- Supports only **general membership queries** (no group-specific queries).
- **No explicit leave mechanism** — when the last host leaves, the router simply waits for its membership timer (typically 3 minutes) to expire before pruning the LAN.
- Uses **report suppression** to reduce duplicate reports on a LAN.

**IGMPv2 (RFC 2236) — 1.5 marks:**
- Introduces **Group-Specific Query** — router can ask "any hosts for group 224.1.1.1?" directly, instead of querying all groups.
- Introduces **explicit Leave Group message** — the last host leaving sends an IGMP Leave to 224.0.0.2 (All-Routers), prompting the router to immediately send a Group-Specific Query. If no report comes back, the LAN is pruned **within seconds** instead of waiting 3 minutes.
- Adds **Max Response Time field** in queries → allows tuning of the leave latency.

**IGMPv3 (RFC 3376) — 1.5 marks:**
- Adds **source filtering** — host can say "I want traffic from source $S_1$ but NOT from $S_2$."
- Enables **Source-Specific Multicast (SSM)** — a host joins (S, G) directly rather than just G.
- Supports **INCLUDE** and **EXCLUDE** modes in membership reports.

**Improvement Summary (1 mark):**
IGMPv2 improves upon IGMPv1 primarily by introducing the **explicit Leave mechanism** and **group-specific queries**, which together reduce the leave latency from ~3 minutes (waiting for timeouts in IGMPv1) to a few seconds — a critical improvement for bandwidth-sensitive applications like IPTV.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Multicast = one-to-many.** Class D IP range is **224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255**.
- **IGMP** is host-to-router only. **Multicast routing protocols** (DVMRP, PIM, MOSPF, CBT) are router-to-router.
- **RPF** uses the **unicast routing table** to prevent loops — it is the backbone of dense-mode multicast.
- **DVMRP = RPF + RPB + Prune + Graft**. Dense mode. SPT-based.
- **PIM-DM** = same dense-mode logic as DVMRP but **independent of the unicast protocol**.
- **PIM-SM** = sparse mode, uses a **Rendezvous Point (RP)**, builds a shared tree first, optionally switches to SPT.
- **CBT** = shared tree rooted at a **Core**. Good for sparse, many-source scenarios.
- **MOSPF** = multicast extension of OSPF. Requires OSPF link-state database. Computes SPT using Dijkstra per (S, G).
- **Prune** stops upstream forwarding; **Graft** re-attaches a pruned branch. Graft is **reliable**; prune is best-effort with a timeout.
- **Report suppression** in IGMP prevents multiple hosts on the same LAN from sending duplicate joins.
- **Source-specific tree (SPT)** = optimal paths, high state. **Shared tree** = sub-optimal paths, low state.
- **TTL field** in multicast packets must be ≥ 1 to leave the host; otherwise it never reaches the router.
- For KTU 3-mark questions: always draw a small diagram showing source, router, group members, and indicate the tree path.
- For KTU 14-mark questions: structure your answer as Definition → Working → Diagram → Comparison → Conclusion.
- Administrative scope address range **239.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255** is *not* routable on the public internet — reserved for private use.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing for Mobile Hosts. Congestion Control Algorithms. Quality of Service (QoS) - Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing for Mobile Hosts. Congestion Control Algorithms. Quality of Service (QoS) - Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Routing for Mobile Hosts (Mobile IP)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
*Mobile IP* is a communication protocol standardized by the **IETF (RFC 3344 / RFC 6275)** that enables **transparent routing of IPv4 datagrams** to mobile nodes (hosts) which move from one network to another without changing their permanent **Home IP Address**. It preserves ongoing communication sessions despite topological re-location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Entities of Mobile IP:**
> - **Home Agent (HA):** A router on the home network that tunnels datagrams destined to the mobile node when it is away.
> - **Foreign Agent (FA):** A router on the visited (foreign) network that helps deliver tunneled packets to the Mobile Node.
> - **Mobile Node (MN):** The host that changes its point of attachment.
> - **Home Address (HoA):** Permanent IP address, topologically valid at the home network.
> - **Care-of Address (CoA):** Temporary address assigned by the Foreign Agent (or acquired via DHCP) valid on the foreign network.
> - **Correspondent Node (CN):** The peer host communicating with the MN.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Forwarding Postal Address":**
> Imagine you move from Kerala to Bengaluru for a job, but you do **not** want to tell your bank, friends, or Amazon to update your address. You simply leave behind a *trusted relative* (Home Agent) at your old house. Anyone who sends mail to your old address will have that relative **forward it** to your new Bengaluru address. Mobile IP works exactly like this — the HA forwards (tunnels) packets to the MN's new CoA. The original sender (CN) never needs to know you moved!

---

### 1.2 Congestion Control Algorithms

**Formal Definition:**
*Congestion* in a network is the state where the **demand for network resources (buffer space, link bandwidth) exceeds the available capacity**, causing packet loss, queueing delays, and throughput collapse. *Congestion Control Algorithms* are the distributed mechanisms used by transport protocols (principally TCP) to **prevent, detect, and recover** from this state.

**The Four Canonical TCP Congestion-Control Phases:**
1. **Slow Start**
2. **Congestion Avoidance**
3. **Fast Retransmit**
4. **Fast Recovery**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Quantitative Anchors (Memorize for KTU 2024):**
> - **Initial Congestion Window (cwnd):** **1 Maximum Segment Size (MSS)**
> - **Slow Start Threshold (ssthresh):** **65,535 bytes** (default initial)
> - **Increment rule (Slow Start):** cwnd grows by **1 MSS per ACK received** → exponential growth.
> - **Increment rule (Congestion Avoidance):** cwnd grows by **1 MSS per RTT** → linear growth.
> - **Loss detection via 3 duplicate ACKs:** triggers **Fast Retransmit + Fast Recovery** (no Slow Start reset).
> - **Loss detection via Timeout:** triggers **Slow Start** from cwnd = 1 MSS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Driving a Car in Rush Hour Traffic":**
> Imagine driving a car down a busy road. **Slow Start** is like cautiously accelerating from a stop — you slowly press the gas (doubling speed each second) until you find cars ahead. **Congestion Avoidance** is like maintaining a safe distance once you have matched traffic flow — you creep forward by **just 1 car length per RTT**. **Fast Retransmit/Recovery** is like noticing a *slight tap* on your bumper (3 duplicate ACKs) and gently easing off the gas — you do **not** slam the brakes to a full stop (no Slow Start) because the situation is not catastrophic.

---

### 1.3 Quality of Service (QoS) — Requirements

**Formal Definition:**
*Quality of Service (QoS)* is the **collective set of service-level performance guarantees** that a network must satisfy for a given traffic flow. It is measured against the four canonical QoS parameters defined below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Canonical QoS Requirements (KTU 2024 Board Examination Favourite):**
>
> | # | Metric | Definition | Typical Unit |
> |---|--------|------------|--------------|
> | 1 | **Throughput** | Rate of successful data delivery over a channel | bits per second (bps) |
> | 2 | **Delay / Latency** | Time taken for a packet to traverse from source to destination | milliseconds (ms) |
> | 3 | **Jitter** | Variation in inter-packet arrival times | ms (standard deviation) |
> | 4 | **Packet Loss / Reliability** | Fraction of packets dropped or lost in transit | % loss / BER |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Service Contract":**
> Picture four contract clauses between you and your internet provider:
> 1. **Throughput** = "My data must travel at least 100 Mbps."
> 2. **Delay** = "A video packet must reach me within 50 ms."
> 3. **Jitter** = "Packets must arrive at a steady rhythm — not in bursts and gaps."
> 4. **Packet Loss** = "No more than 0.1% of packets may be lost."
>
> These four promises together form the **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** that defines QoS. VoIP and video streaming are **delay-sensitive**; bulk file transfer is **loss-sensitive**; both want high throughput.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mobile IP — Operational Phases (Deep Dive)

The Mobile IP protocol operates in **three operational phases**:

**Phase 1 — Agent Discovery (HA & FA discovery)**
- HA and FA periodically broadcast **Agent Advertisement** messages (ICMP Router Advertisement extension).
- MN listens; if away from home, it obtains a **CoA** from the FA.
- If no FA exists, MN acquires a **co-located CoA** via DHCP.

**Phase 2 — Registration (Binding Update)**
- MN sends a **Registration Request** to FA → forwarded to HA.
- HA authenticates the MN and installs a **mobility binding**: `HoA ↔ CoA`.
- Registration lifetime is granted; must be renewed before expiry.

**Phase 3 — Tunneling & Data Delivery**
- CN sends packet to MN's **HoA** → routed to home network.
- HA intercepts → encapsulates original packet inside an outer IP header with destination = **CoA** (tunneling).
- FA decapsulates → delivers inner packet to MN.
- Reply from MN is sent **directly** to CN (reverse tunneling optional but recommended to traverse NATs).

> [!WARNING]
> **Triangle Routing Problem:** When CN sends packets via HA → FA → MN, the route forms a **triangle**. This is inefficient when CN and MN are topologically close but HA is far. Optimization is achieved via **Route Optimization** (RFC 3775 / 6275), where CN learns the CoA directly via a Binding Update and tunnels directly to the FA.

---

### 2.2 Congestion Control — Detailed Algorithmic Steps

#### A. Slow Start (cwnd < ssthresh)
- **Trigger:** Initial connection OR Timeout-based loss recovery.
- **Rule:**
$$\text{cwnd}_{t+1} = \text{cwnd}_t + \text{MSS} \cdot (\text{ACK\_count} / \text{cwnd})$$
- For each ACK: cwnd += 1 MSS → **effective doubling per RTT** (exponential growth).

#### B. Congestion Avoidance (cwnd ≥ ssthresh)
- **Trigger:** cwnd reaches or exceeds ssthresh.
- **Rule (AIMD — Additive Increase):**
$$\text{cwnd}_{t+1} = \text{cwnd}_t + \frac{\text{MSS} \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{cwnd}_t}$$
- Approximately **+1 MSS per RTT** (linear growth).

#### C. Fast Retransmit
- **Trigger:** Receiver receives **3 duplicate ACKs** for the same segment.
- **Action:** Sender **immediately retransmits** the lost segment — does **not** wait for timeout.

#### D. Fast Recovery
- **Rule:** ssthresh = cwnd / 2; cwnd = ssthresh + 3·MSS.
- Inflates cwnd by 3 MSS to account for the 3 buffered segments at the receiver.
- On next ACK that advances the window: cwnd = ssthresh (enter Congestion Avoidance).

#### E. Timeout-Based Loss (Multiplicative Decrease — extreme)
- ssthresh = cwnd / 2; cwnd = 1 MSS → re-enter **Slow Start**.

---

### 2.3 AIMD — The Master Equation

TCP congestion control is formally an **AIMD** process:

$$\boxed{\text{AIMD: Additive Increase, Multiplicative Decrease}}$$

- **Additive Increase:** cwnd ← cwnd + α (α = 1 MSS/RTT)
- **Multiplicative Decrease:** cwnd ← cwnd × β (β = 0.5)

**Why AIMD?**
- **Additive Increase** ensures fairness and gentle probing of available bandwidth.
- **Multiplicative Decrease** ensures rapid back-off upon congestion and **convergence to fairness** across competing flows (proved by Chiu & Jain, 1989).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Value |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Slow Start growth | $\text{cwnd}_{t+1} = \text{cwnd}_t + \text{MSS}$ per ACK | Doubles per RTT |
| 2 | Congestion Avoidance growth | $\text{cwnd}_{t+1} = \text{cwnd}_t + \frac{\text{MSS}^2}{\text{cwnd}_t}$ | +1 MSS per RTT |
| 3 | Timeout response | ssthresh = cwnd / 2; cwnd = 1 MSS | Both in MSS |
| 4 | 3-DupACK response (Tahoe/reno) | ssthresh = cwnd / 2 | — |
| 5 | 3-DupACK response (Reno) | ssthresh = cwnd / 2; cwnd = ssthresh + 3·MSS | — |
| 6 | Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) | $\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT}$ | bits |
| 7 | Max window for full utilization | $W_{\max} \geq B \times \text{RTT} / \text{MSS}$ | MSS units |
| 8 | Throughput (steady-state AIMD) | $T \approx \frac{\text{MSS} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot p}{3}}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p \cdot \frac{3}{8}}}$ ≈ $\frac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}$ | bps (p = loss prob.) |
| 9 | Jitter | $\sigma_{\text{jitter}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(D_i - \bar{D})^2}$ | ms |
| 10 | Packet Loss Ratio | $L = \frac{\text{Packets Lost}}{\text{Packets Sent}} \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Triangular routing extra hops | Extra_hops = $D_{CN,HA} + D_{HA,FA}$ vs Direct $D_{CN,FA}$ | hops |
| 12 | Encapsulation overhead | $\text{Overhead} = \frac{\text{Outer\_Header}}{\text{Outer + Inner}} \times 100$ | % |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile IP** is the *direct ancestor* of **Mobile IPv6 (RFC 6275)** and is conceptually embedded in **5G handover procedures**, **LTE X2/S1 handover**, and **SDN-based mobility** (e.g., Locator/ID Separation Protocol — LISP).
- **TCP Congestion Control** governs **every** internet download — video streaming (DASH), web (HTTP/3 over QUIC), file transfer (BBR), and bulk data sync.
- **QoS Requirements** underpin **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** of cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP), ISP traffic shaping (DiffServ), and real-time applications (VoIP, telemedicine, autonomous vehicles).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throughput of an AIMD-Controlled TCP Flow

We derive the classic **square-root throughput formula** for a TCP Reno flow under random (independent) packet loss.

**Step 1: Loss probability assumption.**
Let $p$ = probability that a single packet is lost (independent, uniform).

**Step 2: Window evolution in steady state.**
TCP Reno operates in a periodic sawtooth between $W_{\max}$ and $W_{\max}/2$:

- After a loss event, cwnd = $W_{\max}/2$.
- cwnd grows by 1 MSS per RTT until reaching $W_{\max}$ again.
- Approximate **average window** $\bar{W}$:
$$\bar{W} \approx \frac{3}{4} W_{\max}$$

**Step 3: Number of packets sent per cycle.**
The cycle (one loss event) sends approximately:
$$N_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{W_{\max}}{2} + W_{\max}\right) \cdot \frac{W_{\max}}{2} = \frac{3}{8} W_{\max}^2$$

(This is the area of a triangle with base $W_{\max}/2$ and height $W_{\max}/2$.)

**Step 4: Relate $W_{\max}$ to $p$.**
Since one loss occurs per cycle:
$$p \approx \frac{1}{N_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{8}{3 W_{\max}^2}$$

Solve for $W_{\max}$:
$$W_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3p}}$$

**Step 5: Average throughput $B$.**
$$B = \frac{\bar{W} \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT}} = \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{\text{MSS}}{\text{RTT}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8}{3p}}$$

Simplify:
$$B = \frac{\text{MSS}}{\text{RTT}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3}{2p}}$$

$$\boxed{B \approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}}$$

> **Textual Interpretation:** Throughput is **inversely proportional to $\sqrt{p}$** — halving the loss probability increases throughput by $\sqrt{2} \approx 41\%$. This is the famous *square-root law of TCP throughput*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Slow Start Duration to Reach ssthresh

**Given:** Initial cwnd = 1 MSS; target = ssthresh = $S$ MSS; each RTT doubles cwnd.

**Step 1:** After $k$ RTTs, cwnd = $2^k$ MSS.

**Step 2:** Set $2^k = S$:
$$\boxed{k = \lceil \log_2(S) \rceil \text{ RTTs}}$$

**Step 3:** Total time = $k \cdot \text{RTT}$ seconds.
Total bytes sent before exit = $2^k - 1$ MSS (sum of geometric series $1 + 2 + 4 + \ldots + 2^{k-1}$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Triangle Routing Overhead Quantification

Let:
- $D_{CN \to HA}$ = distance CN↔HA
- $D_{HA \to FA}$ = distance HA↔FA
- $D_{CN \to FA}$ = direct distance CN↔FA (ideal optimized path)

**Step 1: Cost of Mobile IP without Route Optimization.**
$$L_{\text{indirect}} = D_{CN \to HA} + D_{HA \to FA}$$

**Step 2: Cost with Route Optimization.**
$$L_{\text{direct}} = D_{CN \to FA}$$

**Step 3: Overhead ratio.**
$$\boxed{R_{\text{overhead}} = \frac{L_{\text{indirect}} - L_{\text{direct}}}{L_{\text{direct}}} \times 100\%}$$

If $D_{CN \to HA} = D_{HA \to FA} = 2 \cdot D_{CN \to FA}$ (symmetric topology), then $L_{\text{indirect}} = 4 \cdot D_{CN \to FA}$ and overhead = **300%** — i.e., the indirect path is **4× longer**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Congestion Window Trajectory (TCP Reno — Worked Example)

**Scenario:** MSS = 1 KB, RTT = 100 ms, ssthresh = 8 KB. A single segment is dropped, detected by 3 duplicate ACKs at cwnd = 12 KB.

**Step 1: Fast Retransmit triggered at cwnd = 12 KB.**
**Step 2: Fast Recovery:**
$$\text{ssthresh} = \frac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ KB}$$

$$\text{cwnd} = \text{ssthresh} + 3 \cdot \text{MSS} = 6 + 3 = 9 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3: Next ACK advances the window → set cwnd = ssthresh = 6 KB.**
**Step 4: Enter Congestion Avoidance.** Each RTT adds 1 MSS:

| RTT | cwnd (KB) |
|-----|-----------|
| 0 (recovery) | 6 |
| 1 | 7 |
| 2 | 8 |
| 3 | 9 |
| ... | ... |

Reaches cwnd = 12 KB again after 6 RTTs = **600 ms**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — TCP Tahoe vs Reno vs NewReno

| Feature | Tahoe | Reno | NewReno |
|---------|-------|------|---------|
| Slow Start after timeout | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Fast Retransmit | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Fast Recovery | No | Yes (partial) | Yes (full) |
| Handles multiple losses in one window | No (Slow Start) | Partial (exits early) | Yes (waits for full ACK) |
| Congestion Window post-3dupACK | Reset to 1 MSS | Reset to ssthresh + 3 | Reset to ssthresh |
| KTU Exam Frequency | High | Very High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mobile IP — Functional Block Diagram (Triangle Routing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CN["Correspondent Node CN"] -->|1 sends to HoA| R1["Internet Router R1"]
    R1 -->|2 routed to home net| HA["Home Agent HA on Home Network"]
    HA -->|3 tunnels to CoA| FA["Foreign Agent FA on Visited Network"]
    FA -->|4 decapsulates and delivers| MN["Mobile Node MN with HoA plus CoA"]
    MN -->|5 reply direct to CN| CN

    subgraph HomeNet["Home Network 10.0.0.0/24"]
        HA
    end

    subgraph ForeignNet["Foreign Network 172.16.0.0/24"]
        FA
        MN
    end

    HA -.->|Registration Request via FA| FA
    FA -.->|Registration Reply via FA| HA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** CN → HA (long path 1) → FA (tunnel 2) → MN. The dashed lines represent the *control plane* (mobility binding updates), and the solid arrows represent the *data plane* (encapsulated packet forwarding).

---

### 4.2 TCP Congestion Control — State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SlowStart: connection begins cwnd equals 1 MSS
    SlowStart --> CongestionAvoidance: cwnd greater or equal ssthresh
    SlowStart --> SlowStart: Timeout detected cwnd back to 1 MSS
    CongestionAvoidance --> CongestionAvoidance: 3 DupACK Fast Recovery then linear growth
    CongestionAvoidance --> SlowStart: Timeout ssthresh updated cwnd back to 1 MSS
    CongestionAvoidance --> FastRecovery: 3 DupACK received
    FastRecovery --> CongestionAvoidance: New ACK cwnd set to ssthresh
```

---

### 4.3 TCP Congestion Window Evolution — Sawtooth Plot (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1["Phase 1 Slow Start Exponential"]
        A1["cwnd equals 1 MSS"] -->|RTT 1| A2["cwnd equals 2 MSS"]
        A2 -->|RTT 2| A3["cwnd equals 4 MSS"]
        A3 -->|RTT 3| A4["cwnd equals 8 MSS equals ssthresh"]
    end

    subgraph P2["Phase 2 Congestion Avoidance Linear AIMD"]
        A4 --> B1["cwnd equals 9 MSS"]
        B1 --> B2["cwnd equals 10 MSS"]
        B2 --> B3["cwnd equals N MSS"]
    end

    subgraph P3["Phase 3 Loss Event"]
        B3 --> C1["3 DupACK detected"]
        C1 --> C2["ssthresh equals N divided by 2"]
        C2 --> C3["cwnd set to ssthresh"]
    end

    C3 --> D1["Cycle repeats sawtooth pattern"]
```

---

### 4.4 QoS Architecture — Layered Service Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["Application Layer VoIP Video HTTP FTP"]
    L1["Transport Layer TCP UDP"]
    L2["Network Layer IP with DiffServ RSVP MPLS"]
    L3["Data Link Layer 802.1p ATM QoS classes"]
    L4["Physical Layer Bandwidth and BER"]

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4

    L2 -.->|"Throughput metric"| M1["bits per second"]
    L2 -.->|"Delay metric"| M2["milliseconds"]
    L2 -.->|"Jitter metric"| M3["ms variance"]
    L2 -.->|"Loss metric"| M4["percentage"]
```

---

### 4.5 Mobile IP Encapsulation — Packet Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Outer["Outer IP Header 20 bytes"]
        OH1["Src equals HA"]
        OH2["Dst equals CoA of FA or MN"]
        OH3["Protocol equals 4 IP in IP"]
    end

    subgraph Inner["Inner IP Header 20 bytes"]
        IH1["Src equals CN"]
        IH2["Dst equals HoA of MN"]
        IH3["Original payload"]
    end

    Outer --> Inner
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define Mobile IP. List its three main functional entities and explain the role of the Home Agent.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Mobile IP is an IETF-standardized protocol (RFC 3344) that enables a mobile node to roam across IP networks while retaining its permanent Home Address, ensuring uninterrupted communication.
>
> **Three main functional entities (1½ mark):**
> 1. **Mobile Node (MN)** — the roaming host.
> 2. **Home Agent (HA)** — a router on the home network.
> 3. **Foreign Agent (FA)** — a router on the visited network.
>
> **Role of Home Agent (1½ mark):** The HA intercepts all packets destined to the MN's Home Address when the MN is away from its home network. It maintains the **mobility binding table** (HoA ↔ CoA) and **tunnels** (encapsulates) each intercepted packet inside an outer IP header addressed to the MN's Care-of Address on the foreign network.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the four key Quality of Service (QoS) requirements with one example application each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| QoS Metric | Definition | Example Application |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Throughput** | Rate of successful data delivery (bps) | Bulk file download, FTP |
| **Delay / Latency** | End-to-end packet traversal time | VoIP, online gaming |
| **Jitter** | Variation in inter-packet arrival times | Video conferencing (Zoom) |
| **Packet Loss / Reliability** | Fraction of packets lost in transit | Email, banking transactions |

(1 mark for the table; 1 mark for correctly linking each metric to an example; 1 mark for clear definitions.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Mobile IP architecture**, including the roles of Home Agent, Foreign Agent, Mobile Node, and Correspondent Node. Describe the **three operational phases**: Agent Discovery, Registration, and Tunneling with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **TCP Slow Start and Congestion Avoidance algorithms** with cwnd vs RTT graph. Explain the **AIMD principle** with formulas. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mobile IP Architecture (7 marks):**

*Mobile IP Architecture Diagram:*
> *(Draw a diagram with three sub-networks — Home Network containing HA, Foreign Network containing FA and MN, and Internet containing CN. Show registration request/response via FA, and data flow via HA → FA → MN tunneling. Caption: "Figure 1 — Mobile IP Triangle Routing.")*

**Entities (2 marks):**
- **Home Agent (HA):** Router on home network. Maintains mobility binding `HoA ↔ CoA`. Intercepts and tunnels packets.
- **Foreign Agent (FA):** Router on visited network. Provides CoA. Decapsulates tunneled packets. Forwards to MN.
- **Mobile Node (MN):** Roaming host. Detects movement via Agent Advertisements.
- **Correspondent Node (CN):** Peer host; unaware of mobility; sends to HoA.

**Three Phases (4 marks):**
1. **Agent Discovery:** HA and FA broadcast *ICMP Agent Advertisement* messages periodically. MN detects it is on a foreign network and obtains a **CoA** from FA.
2. **Registration:** MN sends *Registration Request* → FA → HA. HA authenticates and registers binding (lifetime usually 1 hour). HA replies *Registration Reply*.
3. **Tunneling (Data Delivery):** CN → IP packet to HoA → routed to home network → HA intercepts → encapsulates inside new IP header with destination CoA → FA decapsulates → delivers to MN. Reverse path: MN → CN (direct).

> **Triangle Routing Limitation (1 mark extra):** Mention that Route Optimization (RFC 3775) eliminates triangle routing via Binding Updates to CN.

---

**(b) TCP Slow Start and Congestion Avoidance (7 marks):**

**Slow Start (3 marks):**
- **Initial state:** cwnd = 1 MSS; ssthresh = 65,535 bytes (default).
- **Per-ACK rule:** cwnd ← cwnd + 1 MSS → **effective doubling per RTT** (exponential).
- **Exit condition:** cwnd ≥ ssthresh → switch to Congestion Avoidance.

$$\text{cwnd}_k = 2^{k-1} \cdot \text{MSS} \text{ after } k \text{ RTTs}$$

**Congestion Avoidance (3 marks):**
- **Per-RTT rule:** cwnd ← cwnd + 1 MSS → **linear growth**.
- **Equivalent per-ACK rule:** cwnd ← cwnd + MSS²/cwnd.
- **Exit conditions:**
  - 3 duplicate ACKs → Fast Retransmit + Fast Recovery.
  - Timeout → Slow Start (cwnd ← 1 MSS).

**AIMD Principle (1 mark):**
- **Additive Increase:** cwnd ← cwnd + α (α = 1 MSS/RTT) during Congestion Avoidance.
- **Multiplicative Decrease:** cwnd ← cwnd / 2 on loss detection.

> **cwnd vs RTT Graph:** Draw a sawtooth waveform — exponential rise in Slow Start phase, linear rise in Congestion Avoidance, sharp drop to ssthresh at loss event, repetition. (Caption: "Figure 2 — TCP Congestion Window Sawtooth.")

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Congestion Control problem** in computer networks. Describe the **four phases of TCP congestion control**: Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, and Fast Recovery. Compare **TCP Tahoe and TCP Reno** in a tabular form. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Quality of Service (QoS)** requirements in detail. Describe the **techniques to achieve QoS**: Overprovisioning, Buffering, Traffic Shaping, Resource Reservation, and Scheduling. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Congestion Control (7 marks):**

**Problem Statement (1 mark):**
Congestion occurs when the sum of arrival rates at a router exceeds the outgoing link capacity. Symptoms: packet loss, increased queueing delay, throughput collapse. Solved by distributed end-to-end control via TCP.

**Slow Start (1½ marks):**
- cwnd = 1 MSS; doubles per RTT until cwnd ≥ ssthresh.
- Exits on loss (timeout → reset cwnd to 1).

**Congestion Avoidance (1½ marks):**
- Linear increase: +1 MSS per RTT.
- Exits on loss (3 dupACK → halve cwnd; timeout → reset to 1).

**Fast Retransmit (1 mark):**
- On 3 duplicate ACKs: immediately retransmit lost segment (do not wait for timeout).

**Fast Recovery (1½ marks):**
- ssthresh = cwnd / 2; cwnd = ssthresh + 3·MSS.
- Inflates for 3 buffered segments at receiver.
- Returns to Congestion Avoidance on next new ACK.

**TCP Tahoe vs Reno (1 mark):**

| Feature | Tahoe | Reno |
|---------|-------|------|
| 3-dupACK response | cwnd ← 1; Slow Start | cwnd ← ssthresh; Fast Recovery |
| Throughput efficiency | Lower (over-reacts) | Higher |
| Multiple losses in one window | Slow Start every loss | Partial recovery only |

---

**(b) QoS Requirements and Techniques (7 marks):**

**QoS Requirements (3 marks):**

| Metric | Definition | Sensitive Apps |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| Throughput | Data rate (bps) | FTP, video streaming |
| Delay | End-to-end latency | VoIP, gaming |
| Jitter | Delay variation | Video conf, real-time |
| Loss | % packets dropped | Email, banking |

**QoS Techniques (4 marks):**

1. **Overprovisioning:** Provision bandwidth >> peak demand. Simple but expensive.
2. **Buffering:** Absorb bursts at receiver. Adds delay; helps with jitter.
3. **Traffic Shaping:** Smooth outgoing traffic using **Leaky Bucket** (constant output rate) or **Token Bucket** (allows bursts up to bucket capacity).
4. **Resource Reservation:** Protocols like **RSVP** reserve bandwidth for flows in advance (IntServ).
5. **Scheduling:** Algorithms like **FIFO, Priority Queuing, Weighted Fair Queuing (WFQ)** decide packet transmission order at routers.

> **Application Class Mapping (extra):** Real-time traffic → strict priority; elastic traffic → WFQ.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HoA and CoA (lose 1–2 marks):** Home Address is *permanent*, topologically valid only on home net. Care-of Address is *temporary*, valid on foreign net. Do **not** interchange.
> 2. **Forgetting ssthresh update in Tahoe:** Many students write only "cwnd ← 1" on timeout but forget `ssthresh ← cwnd/2`. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this omission.
> 3. **Confusing Fast Retransmit with Fast Recovery:** Fast Retransmit *detects* and *re-sends*. Fast Recovery *adjusts the window without returning to Slow Start*. They are two distinct phases.
> 4. **Mixing Jitter with Delay:** Jitter = *variation* in delay, not the delay itself. A constant 50 ms delay has **zero** jitter.
> 5. **In AIMD derivation, missing the $\sqrt{p}$ justification:** Examiners expect mention of Chiu-Jain convergence proof for fairness.
> 6. **In Mobile IP diagram, omitting Foreign Agent:** Students often draw only HA and MN — FA is mandatory for full marks.
> 7. **TCP Reno vs NewReno confusion:** NewReno does not exit Fast Recovery until *all* lost segments in a window are recovered; Reno exits on the first new ACK.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Mobile IP:**
> - ⭐ RFC 3344 / 6275; preserves HoA across networks.
> - ⭐ Three entities: **HA, FA, MN**; **CN** is the peer.
> - ⭐ Two addresses: **Home Address (HoA) + Care-of Address (CoA)**.
> - ⭐ Three phases: **Discovery → Registration → Tunneling**.
> - ⭐ **Triangle Routing Problem** → solved by **Route Optimization**.
> - ⭐ Encapsulation uses **IP-in-IP tunneling** (Protocol 4).

> **Congestion Control:**
> - ⭐ Two classic TCP variants: **Tahoe** (resets to 1 MSS on any loss) vs **Reno** (Fast Recovery).
> - ⭐ **Slow Start:** cwnd doubles per RTT; exits at ssthresh.
> - ⭐ **Congestion Avoidance:** cwnd grows linearly by +1 MSS/RTT.
> - ⭐ **Fast Retransmit:** triggered by **3 duplicate ACKs**.
> - ⭐ **Fast Recovery:** ssthresh = cwnd/2; cwnd = ssthresh + 3·MSS.
> - ⭐ **AIMD:** Additive Increase + Multiplicative Decrease → converges to fairness.
> - ⭐ **Square-root throughput law:** $B \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{p}}$.

> **QoS:**
> - ⭐ Four metrics: **Throughput, Delay, Jitter, Loss**.
> - ⭐ Two traffic-conditioning tools: **Leaky Bucket** (smooth) vs **Token Bucket** (bursty).
> - ⭐ Two architecture models: **IntServ (RSVP per-flow)** vs **DiffServ (ToS/CoS field per-class)**.
> - ⭐ Schedulers: **FIFO, PQ, WFQ, RR**.
> - ⭐ Real-time apps = delay + jitter sensitive; elastic apps = loss + throughput sensitive.

> **Cross-Topic Linkage (Frequently asked):**
> - ⭐ Mobile IP is the *application-layer-aware* cousin of **TCP mobility** (MPTCP).
> - ⭐ Congestion control interacts with **QoS** through **ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification)** — RFC 3168.
> - ⭐ **cwnd** in TCP essentially *enforces* a per-flow throughput guarantee — a QoS mechanism at the transport layer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Achieving Good QoS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Achieving Good QoS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of QoS
**Quality of Service (QoS)** is the collective set of mechanisms, policies, and engineering techniques employed in a network to guarantee a certain level of packet delivery performance in terms of measurable metrics such as **bandwidth**, **delay (latency)**, **jitter (delay variation)**, and **packet loss ratio**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, QoS is treated as the *application-layer and transport-layer response to the limitations of best-effort IP networks*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Language):**
> *QoS refers to the ability of a network to provide differentiated service guarantees to specific traffic flows, ensuring that critical applications (voice, video, real-time control) receive predictable and consistent performance despite network congestion.*

## 1.2 The Five Pillars of QoS Techniques
The KTU syllabus categorizes techniques for achieving good QoS into **five fundamental categories**:

1. **Overprovisioning** – Engineering a network with far more capacity than peak demand.
2. **Buffering** – Temporarily storing bursts of packets at intermediate nodes.
3. **Traffic Shaping** – Regulating the *average* and *burst* rate of a traffic flow (Leaky Bucket & Token Bucket).
4. **Resource Reservation** – Pre-allocating bandwidth, buffer space, and CPU cycles along a path (e.g., **RSVP**).
5. **Traffic Scheduling / Policing** – Deciding the *order* in which queued packets are forwarded (FIFO, Priority, WFQ).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Multi-Lane Smart Highway"**
> Imagine a national highway with limited lanes carrying cars (packets) of vastly different urgency — ambulances (voice packets), VIP convoys (video packets), and regular commuters (email/bulk data). The highway authority uses:
> - **Extra lanes** (overprovisioning),
> - **Lay-bys / rest areas** (buffering),
> - **Toll booths that meter entry** (traffic shaping),
> - **Pre-booked VIP passes** (resource reservation), and
> - **Traffic policemen controlling merging order** (scheduling).
> Together, these ensure the *ambulance always reaches the hospital on time*, even during peak congestion. This is exactly what QoS does in a packet-switched network.

## 1.3 Why Best-Effort Is Not Enough
The traditional Internet provides **best-effort delivery** — packets are forwarded as soon as possible, with no guarantees. For:
- **Real-time VoIP** → requires delay < 150 ms, jitter < 30 ms.
- **Video conferencing** → requires sustained 1–5 Mbps bandwidth.
- **Online gaming / IoT control** → requires low loss and low jitter.

Hence, **techniques for achieving good QoS become essential**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Technique 1 — Overprovisioning
- **Concept:** Provide far more link capacity (e.g., 10× the peak load) so that queues never build up and congestion rarely occurs.
- **Pros:** Trivially simple, no protocol changes.
- **Cons:** Extremely expensive; wasteful; "just throw bandwidth" is rarely scalable.
- **KTU Verdict:** Mentioned but **rarely marks-bearing**.

## 2.2 Technique 2 — Buffering
- **Concept:** Routers use **finite queues (buffers)** to absorb short-term bursts of traffic.
- **Behavior:** When buffer fills, arriving packets are **dropped** (Tail Drop) or marked using **RED (Random Early Detection)**.
- **Trade-off:** Larger buffers → lower loss but **higher delay (bufferbloat)**.
- **Buffer Size Rule of Thumb:** Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) = $C \times RTT$ (bytes).

## 2.3 Technique 3 — Traffic Shaping (★ Most-Marked Topic ★)

### 2.3.1 Leaky Bucket Algorithm
The **leaky bucket** is an **abstract meter** that converts an *uneven* flow of packets into a *smooth, constant* output stream — just like a bucket with a small hole at the bottom.

**Operational Rules:**
- The bucket has a fixed capacity $C$ (in bytes or packets).
- It drains at a constant rate $\rho$ (leak rate / output rate).
- If the bucket is **full** and a new packet arrives, the packet is **discarded**.
- The output rate is *always* $\rho$, regardless of the input burst.

### 2.3.2 Token Bucket Algorithm
The **token bucket** allows a *controlled amount of burstiness* by accumulating "permission tokens".

**Operational Rules:**
- Tokens are generated at rate $r$ tokens/second and added to a bucket of capacity $b$.
- A packet of size $L$ consumes $L$ tokens before transmission.
- If tokens are **insufficient** ($< L$), the packet must **wait** (or is dropped, depending on policy).
- The bucket can hold at most $b$ tokens → allows burst of up to $b$ bytes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Leaky Bucket vs Token Bucket — Board-Examiner Favourite**
> *Leaky Bucket enforces a constant output rate (shaping) — Token Bucket allows controlled bursts but enforces a long-term average rate.*

## 2.4 Technique 4 — Resource Reservation (RSVP)
- **Protocol:** **Resource Reservation Protocol (RSVP)** is a signalling protocol used in the **IntServ (Integrated Services)** model.
- **Mechanism:** The sender issues a `PATH` message; the receiver replies with a `RESV` message that *reserves* bandwidth and buffer along every router on the path.
- **Drawback:** State must be maintained at *every router* (scalability issue).
- **IntServ Classes:**
  - **Guaranteed Service** → strict upper bound on delay.
  - **Controlled Load Service** → "as-if unloaded" best-effort with high probability.

## 2.5 Technique 5 — Traffic Scheduling
Decides **the order in which packets are dequeued** for transmission.

| Scheduler | Logic | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **FIFO** | First-In-First-Out, no differentiation | Default in simple routers |
| **Priority Queuing (PQ)** | Multiple queues, strict priority for higher class | Real-time traffic |
| **Round Robin (RR)** | Each queue gets equal turn | Fairness among flows |
| **Weighted Fair Queuing (WFQ)** | Each flow gets a *weighted* share of bandwidth | Multi-class QoS |

### 2.6 Technique 6 — DiffServ (Differentiated Services) — Modern Internet QoS
- **Mechanism:** Packets carry a **DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point)** in the IP header.
- Routers apply **PHBs (Per-Hop Behaviors)** such as *EF (Expedited Forwarding)* and *AF (Assured Forwarding)*.
- **Advantage:** **Stateless** core routers — scalable.
- **EF Class** ≈ virtual leased line; **AF Class** ≈ better-than-best-effort with priorities.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (★ Print This ★)

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buffer Size (BDP) | $B = C \times RTT$ | Bytes; $C$ in B/s, RTT in s |
| 2 | Leaky Bucket Output Rate | $Out = \rho$ | Constant, in bytes/sec |
| 3 | Leaky Bucket Capacity | $0 \le Q \le C$ | $C$ = bucket size (bytes) |
| 4 | Token Bucket Token Arrival | $T(t) = r \cdot t$ | $r$ = token rate (tokens/s) |
| 5 | Max Burst (Token Bucket) | $B_{max} = b$ | Bytes; $b$ = bucket capacity |
| 6 | Long-term Avg Rate | $\le r$ | Bytes/sec |
| 7 | WFQ Bandwidth Share | $W_i = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum w_j} \times C$ | $w_i$ = weight of flow $i$ |
| 8 | Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{useful output}}{\text{max capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| 9 | Total Delay | $D = D_{prop} + D_{trans} + D_{queue} + D_{proc}$ | Seconds |
| 10 | Jitter | $J = \vert D_{max} - D_{min} \vert$ | Seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Always state the **units** of $\rho$, $r$, $C$, $b$ explicitly. Use **bytes/sec** for $r$ and $\rho$ consistently to avoid losing the "correctness" mark.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Telephony (VoLTE / 5G):** Uses token-bucket traffic shaping + WFQ for voice bearers.
- **Data Centers (RDMA / RoCE):** Use **PFC (Priority Flow Control)** — a Layer-2 QoS mechanism.
- **Enterprise WANs (SD-WAN):** Implement DiffServ DSCP tagging end-to-end.
- **Streaming (Netflix, YouTube):** Adaptive Bitrate (ABR) sits *above* QoS shaping at the application layer.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Leaky Bucket Maximum Waiting Time

Let:
- $C$ = bucket capacity (bytes)
- $\rho$ = output rate (bytes/sec)
- $P$ = size of the packet that just arrived to a *full* bucket (will be dropped after waiting)

**Step 1 — Bucket fills at a rate of $\rho$.**
The bucket loses $\rho$ bytes worth of water every second, even when empty.

**Step 2 — For a packet of size $L$ to be transmitted from a *full* bucket, the wait time is:**

$$
T_{wait} = \frac{L}{\rho}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum queue delay (worst case, bucket just became full, all $C$ bytes must drain before the new packet enters):**

$$
D_{max} = \frac{C + L}{\rho}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- If $C = 0$ (no buffering), $D_{max} = \dfrac{L}{\rho}$ → pure serialization delay.
- If $C \to \infty$, the bucket is *never* a bottleneck — delay grows indefinitely.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Token Bucket Burst Tolerance

**Step 1 — Token count at time $t$ (assuming bucket initially full at $t=0$):**

$$
T_{count}(t) = \min\Big(b,\; b + r \cdot t - \text{consumed}\Big)
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum number of bytes that can be transmitted in a *single burst*:**

$$
B_{max} = b \quad (\text{bytes})
$$

Because the bucket can hold at most $b$ tokens, and each token corresponds to 1 byte of transmission right.

**Step 3 — Long-term sustained throughput (over a window $T \gg b/r$):**

$$
\text{Avg Rate} = r \quad (\text{bytes/sec})
$$

**Step 4 — Proof of "burst then idle" pattern:**
Consider a flow that is silent for time $T_s$. Tokens accumulate:
$$
T_{acc} = r \cdot T_s \quad \text{(capped at } b\text{)}
$$
When the flow resumes, it can send a burst of up to $b$ bytes *instantly* — this is the **burst-tolerance** property that pure leaky-bucket cannot provide.

## 3.3 Solved Numerical — Leaky Bucket (★ Board-Exam Style ★)

**Problem:** A host generates traffic at an average rate of **6 Mbps**, with bursts of up to **1 MB**. A leaky bucket regulator is configured with a bucket size of **1 MB** and a drain rate of **4 Mbps**.
**(a)** Will any packets be dropped?
**(b)** If the host suddenly bursts 1.5 MB in 1 sec, what is the drain time?
**(c)** Maximum waiting time for a 1 KB packet?

### Solution

**(a) Drop Analysis:**
- Average input rate $= 6$ Mbps.
- Output (drain) rate $\rho = 4$ Mbps.
- Since input rate **>** output rate, the bucket will *eventually* fill up; **burst packets will be dropped** once bucket reaches $C = 1$ MB.

**(b) Drain Time for 1.5 MB burst:**
- The bucket can hold only 1 MB; **0.5 MB is dropped** immediately.
- Time to drain 1 MB at 4 Mbps:

$$
T = \frac{\text{Size}}{\text{Rate}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{4 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/sec}} = 2 \text{ seconds}
$$

**(c) Maximum Waiting Time for 1 KB packet (worst case, full bucket):**

$$
D_{max} = \frac{C + L}{\rho} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{6} \text{ B} + 1024 \text{ B}) \times 8}{4 \times 10^{6}} \approx 2.002 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that the 1 KB packet waits *almost* 2 seconds — illustrating that leaky bucket trades **delay** for **smoothness**. This is a favourite 7-mark analytical question.

## 3.4 Solved Numerical — Token Bucket

**Problem:** A token bucket has:
- Token rate $r = 2$ MBps
- Bucket capacity $b = 1$ MB
- Each packet consumes 1 token regardless of size (assume 1 token = 1 packet for simplicity).

**(a)** If the bucket starts full, what is the **maximum number of packets** that can be sent in a single burst?
**(b)** If the source sends 0.5 MBps average but bursts to 2 MBps for 3 seconds, are any packets dropped?
**(c)** Long-term maximum throughput?

### Solution

**(a)** Max burst packets $= \dfrac{b}{\text{tokens per packet}} = \dfrac{1 \text{ MB}}{1 \text{ token per packet (assuming 1 packet = 1 token-equivalent)}} = 1$ MB worth → 1000 packets (if 1 KB each).

**(b)** Over 3 seconds at 2 MBps, source sends $6$ MB. Bucket starts with $b = 1$ MB; tokens earned in 3 s = $r \times 3 = 2 \times 3 = 6$ MB. **Total allowed** = $1 + 6 = 7$ MB $\ge 6$ MB → **No drops**.
