> 3. Drawing FSM arrows without labeling the **event trigger**. Each arrow must have a "RCV SYN" or "App CLOSE" label to score full marks.
> 4. Stating MSL = 60 s without justification; modern KTU papers expect **MSL = 120 s, TIME-WAIT = 240 s**.
> 5. Omitting the **CLOSING** state in simultaneous-close diagrams — examiners specifically hunt for it.
> 6. Using the **pipe `|` character** inside markdown table cells — this breaks the table. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 12 bullets before walking into the exam hall:**

- [ ] TCP connection management is modeled as a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** with **11 states**.
- [ ] The FSM tuple is $M = (S, E, A)$ where $|S| = 11$.
- [ ] **Three-way handshake**: SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK (3 packets, full-duplex verified).
- [ ] **SYN consumes 1 sequence number** — never forget the +1.
- [ ] **Four-way termination** (graceful): FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK.
- [ ] **Simultaneous close** introduces the transient **CLOSING** state.
- [ ] **Active closer** = client (typically); visits FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2, TIME-WAIT.
- [ ] **Passive closer** = server (typically); visits CLOSE-WAIT, LAST-ACK.
- [ ] **TIME-WAIT duration** = $2 \times \text{MSL} = \textbf{240 s}$.
- [ ] TIME-WAIT exists to absorb **delayed duplicates** and allow **retransmitted FINs** to die.
- [ ] Sequence number field is **32 bits**; range is $0$ to $2^{32} - 1$.
- [ ] Default MSL per RFC 793 = **120 seconds** (for 2MSL = 240 s) — modern KTU standard.

---

**End of Notes — PEMRT751 | Module 4 | Connection Management Modeling**
*Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 specification (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme aligned).*

topics.title: TCP Retransmission Policy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** TCP Retransmission Policy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**TCP Retransmission Policy** is the set of adaptive mechanisms employed by the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) to guarantee reliable, in-order, and loss-free delivery of a byte stream over an inherently unreliable Internet Protocol (IP) network. It defines the rules, algorithms, and timing constraints that govern *when* a sender must re-transmit a TCP segment whose corresponding acknowledgement (ACK) has not been received within a calculated time bound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMRT751, Module 4):**
> The TCP retransmission policy addresses three classical problems of the data-link layer projected onto the transport layer: (1) determining the Round-Trip Time (RTT) of a segment, (2) handling the retransmission of lost segments, and (3) avoiding spurious duplicate transmissions caused by delayed ACKs arriving after a timeout has already fired.

The cornerstone of this policy is the **Retransmission Timeout (RTO)** — a dynamically computed time interval. If an ACK does not arrive before the RTO expires, the segment is presumed lost and is retransmitted. The reliability of TCP is therefore *not* a feature of the network, but a guarantee engineered by the sender using precise timing mathematics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recorded-Reply Card"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy (Read this first if the topic feels abstract):**

Imagine you are writing a **registered post** to a friend in another city and you have included a **"Reply Card"** (ACK) for them to sign and return. Now think about how you decide whether to *resend* the letter:

- If your friend signs the card and posts it back the next day, you learn that the network (postal system) takes roughly **1 day round-trip**.
- If you send 10 more letters and the replies come back in **1, 1, 2, 1 days**, you do *not* trust a fixed "wait 3 days" rule — you compute a **smoothed average** and also track the **variation** in these replies.
- If, after a reasonable wait, no reply card arrives, you assume the letter was **lost in transit** and resend a **duplicate**.
- If you receive **3 separate calls** from your friend saying *"I already received the first letter!"*, you do not wait anymore — you conclude the *original* was lost and **immediately resend**.

TCP behaves identically:
- **Smoothed RTT (SRTT)** $\rightarrow$ the average reply time.
- **RTT Variation (RTTVAR)** $\rightarrow$ how much reply times fluctuate.
- **Retransmission Timeout (RTO)** $\rightarrow$ SRTT + 4 $\times$ RTTVAR (the waiting period).
- **Timeout Expiry** $\rightarrow$ the no-reply case (letter lost).
- **Three Duplicate ACKs** $\rightarrow$ the "I already got it!" complaint (Fast Retransmit).

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary Anchors (KTU 2024 Expected Terminology)

| Term | Full Form | Function in Policy |
|---|---|---|
| **RTT** | Round-Trip Time | Time from segment transmission to ACK receipt |
| **RTO** | Retransmission Timeout | Maximum wait time before declaring a segment lost |
| **SRTT** | Smoothed RTT | Exponentially weighted average of RTT samples |
| **RTTVAR** | RTT Variation | Exponentially weighted mean deviation of RTT |
| **Duplicate ACK** | Dup-ACK | ACK re-received for the same sequence number |
| **Fast Retransmit** | FR | Immediate retransmit triggered by **3 duplicate ACKs** |
| **Exponential Backoff** | — | RTO doubling on successive timeouts |
| **Karn's Algorithm** | — | Avoids RTT measurement on retransmitted segments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always use the *exact* abbreviation (**SRTT, RTTVAR, RTO, FR**) in your answer scripts. Examiners award marks for precise terminology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Classical Problems of TCP Retransmission

The retransmission policy must answer three sequential questions every time a segment is transmitted:

### Problem 1 — *How long should the sender wait?*
This requires a **dynamic estimate of round-trip time**. A fixed timeout is impossible because Internet path delays range from **<1 ms (LAN)** to **>500 ms (intercontinental satellite)** and vary with congestion. The solution is a self-tuning estimator, originally proposed by **Jacobson (1988)** and adopted in **RFC 6298**.

### Problem 2 — *When is a segment considered lost?*
Two distinct triggers exist:
1. **Timeout-based retransmission** — no ACK arrives within the **RTO window**.
2. **Fast Retransmit** — **three duplicate cumulative ACKs** are received, which is the receiver's way of signalling *"I got the next byte, but I am still missing byte X"*. This bypasses the slow timeout mechanism and is critical for high-bandwidth, high-latency links.

### Problem 3 — *What about the retransmission ambiguity?*
When a sender retransmits a segment, it cannot tell whether the returning ACK corresponds to the **original** transmission or the **retransmission**. Measuring RTT from this ambiguous ACK would corrupt the estimator. **Karn's Algorithm (1987)** solves this by **prohibiting RTT measurement for any retransmitted segment** and doubling the RTO (exponential backoff) on each successive timeout.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Initialization (First segment sent):**
   - SRTT $\leftarrow$ 0
   - RTTVAR $\leftarrow$ 0
   - RTO $\leftarrow$ **1 second** (RFC 6298 default initial value)

2. **On receipt of first RTT measurement (R):**
   - SRTT $\leftarrow R$
   - RTTVAR $\leftarrow R / 2$
   - RTO $\leftarrow SRTT + \max(G, K \cdot RTTVAR)$, where $G$ is the clock granularity and $K = 4$

3. **On each subsequent RTT measurement (R):**
   - $RTTVAR \leftarrow (1 - \beta) \cdot RTTVAR + \beta \cdot \vert SRTT - R \vert$
   - $SRTT \leftarrow (1 - \alpha) \cdot SRTT + \alpha \cdot R$
   - $RTO \leftarrow SRTT + \max(G, K \cdot RTTVAR)$

4. **On timeout expiry:**
   - Retransmit the oldest unacknowledged segment
   - $RTO \leftarrow 2 \cdot RTO$ (exponential backoff, capped, e.g., at 60 s in Linux)
   - Reset retransmit counter

5. **On receipt of 3 duplicate ACKs:**
   - Trigger **Fast Retransmit** — retransmit immediately, do *not* wait for RTO
   - (Optionally trigger Fast Recovery — adjust congestion window)

6. **On any successful new ACK after retransmission:**
   - Reset RTO back to its computed (non-backed-off) value
   - Resume normal RTT sampling for the next non-retransmitted segment

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All constants below are standardised in RFC 6298 and are reproducible line-for-line in the KTU answer script.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \quad \text{(SRTT smoothing factor)} \\
\beta  &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.250 \quad \text{(RTTVAR smoothing factor)} \\
K     &= 4                  \quad \text{(RTTVAR gain in RTO)} \\
G     &= 0.01 \text{ s}     \quad \text{(typical clock granularity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Smoothed RTT (update) | $SRTT \leftarrow (1-\alpha) SRTT + \alpha R$ | seconds |
| 2 | RTT Variance (update) | $RTTVAR \leftarrow (1-\beta) RTTVAR + \beta \vert SRTT - R \vert$ | seconds |
| 3 | Retransmission Timeout | $RTO \leftarrow SRTT + \max(G,\, 4 \cdot RTTVAR)$ | seconds, $\geq 1$ s |
| 4 | Initial RTT measurement | $SRTT \leftarrow R,\; RTTVAR \leftarrow R/2$ | first sample only |
| 5 | Exponential Backoff | $RTO_{new} \leftarrow 2 \cdot RTO_{old}$ | capped, e.g., 60 s |
| 6 | Fast Retransmit trigger | Number of Dup-ACKs $\geq 3$ | count of ACKs |
| 7 | Karn's rule | No RTT sample on retransmit | boolean condition |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown escape notice:** The vertical bars in Table row 2 use $\vert$ instead of the raw pipe character `|` to keep the table renderable in all markdown engines.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The TCP retransmission policy is not a textbook curiosity — it is the **reason the modern Internet is usable at all**. Without it:

- Video conferencing (Zoom, Teams) would freeze every time a packet was lost on a Wi-Fi handoff.
- Banking transactions would silently truncate or duplicate, because IP provides *no* native delivery guarantee.
- HTTP/3 (over QUIC) inherits the **same** RTT estimator — the algorithm is portable to user-space transport stacks.

The estimator is, in effect, a **first-order linear adaptive filter** running in the kernel — one of the earliest successful deployments of an adaptive control loop on a global scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Convergence of the SRTT Estimator

We start from the recurrence

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRTT_{n+1} &= (1 - \alpha)\, SRTT_n + \alpha\, R_n \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R_n$ is the $n$-th RTT sample. Assume the network has a *true* mean RTT $\mu$ and a sample deviation $\epsilon_n$ such that $R_n = \mu + \epsilon_n$. Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRTT_{n+1} &= (1 - \alpha)\, SRTT_n + \alpha (\mu + \epsilon_n) \\
           &= (1 - \alpha)\, SRTT_n + \alpha \mu + \alpha \epsilon_n \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the expectation operator $E[\cdot]$ on both sides (and noting that the noise $\epsilon_n$ has zero mean for an *unbiased* network):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[SRTT_{n+1}] &= (1 - \alpha)\, E[SRTT_n] + \alpha \mu \\
\end{aligned}
$$

At steady state $E[SRTT_{n+1}] = E[SRTT_n] = SRTT^*$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRTT^* &= (1 - \alpha)\, SRTT^* + \alpha \mu \\
\alpha\, SRTT^* &= \alpha \mu \\
\therefore SRTT^* &= \mu
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The estimator is **unbiased** — over many samples, $SRTT$ converges to the true mean RTT of the network path. This is why the formula is *the* canonical estimator in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Form Weighted History of SRTT

Unrolling the recurrence gives the **equivalent weighted sum** of all past samples:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRTT_{n+1} &= \alpha R_n + (1 - \alpha)[\alpha R_{n-1} + (1 - \alpha) SRTT_{n-1}] \\
           &= \alpha R_n + \alpha(1 - \alpha) R_{n-1} + (1 - \alpha)^2 SRTT_{n-1} \\
           &\;\;\vdots \\
           &= \sum_{i=0}^{n} \alpha (1 - \alpha)^i R_{n-i} + (1 - \alpha)^{n+1} SRTT_0
\end{aligned}
$$

The coefficient of $R_{n-i}$ is $\alpha(1-\alpha)^i$. Plugging in $\alpha = 1/8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_0 &= \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \quad \text{(most recent sample, weight = 12.5\%)} \\
w_1 &= \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{7}{8} = 0.109 \\
w_2 &= 0.109 \cdot 0.875 = 0.0958 \\
w_3 &= 0.0958 \cdot 0.875 = 0.0839 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Geometric decay:** Each sample's influence halves roughly every $\log_{0.875}(0.5) \approx 5.2$ samples. The effective "memory" of the estimator is **5–6 RTT samples**, a deliberately short window so that the estimator tracks changing network conditions.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Full RTO Computation (KTU Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most-asked numerical in KTU 2024 scheme Module 4. Memorise the structure.**

**Given:** $\alpha = 1/8,\; \beta = 1/4,\; K = 4,\; G = 0.01$ s. The first measured RTT is $R_1 = 0.4$ s. Subsequent samples are $R_2 = 0.5$ s, $R_3 = 0.45$ s. Compute RTO after each measurement.

### Step 1 — First sample ($R_1 = 0.4$ s)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRTT_1 &= R_1 = 0.400 \text{ s} \\
RTTVAR_1 &= R_1 / 2 = 0.200 \text{ s} \\
RTO_1 &= SRTT_1 + 4 \cdot RTTVAR_1 \\
      &= 0.400 + 4(0.200) = 0.400 + 0.800 = 1.200 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Apply floor: $RTO \geq 1$ s, so $RTO_1 = 1.200$ s, which satisfies the rule.)

### Step 2 — Second sample ($R_2 = 0.5$ s)

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTTVAR_2 &= (1 - \beta) RTTVAR_1 + \beta \cdot \vert SRTT_1 - R_2 \vert \\
         &= \tfrac{3}{4}(0.200) + \tfrac{1}{4}\vert 0.400 - 0.500 \vert \\
         &= 0.150 + 0.025 = 0.175 \text{ s} \\
SRTT_2 &= (1 - \alpha) SRTT_1 + \alpha R_2 \\
       &= \tfrac{7}{8}(0.400) + \tfrac{1}{8}(0.500) \\
       &= 0.350 + 0.0625 = 0.4125 \text{ s} \\
RTO_2 &= SRTT_2 + 4 \cdot RTTVAR_2 \\
      &= 0.4125 + 4(0.175) = 0.4125 + 0.700 = 1.1125 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Third sample ($R_3 = 0.45$ s)

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTTVAR_3 &= (1 - \beta) RTTVAR_2 + \beta \cdot \vert SRTT_2 - R_3 \vert \\
         &= \tfrac{3}{4}(0.175) + \tfrac{1}{4}\vert 0.4125 - 0.450 \vert \\
         &= 0.13125 + 0.009375 = 0.140625 \text{ s} \\
SRTT_3 &= (1 - \alpha) SRTT_2 + \alpha R_3 \\
       &= \tfrac{7}{8}(0.4125) + \tfrac{1}{8}(0.450) \\
       &= 0.36094 + 0.05625 = 0.41719 \text{ s} \\
RTO_3 &= SRTT_3 + 4 \cdot RTTVAR_3 \\
      &= 0.41719 + 4(0.140625) = 0.41719 + 0.5625 = 0.97969 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Floor rule applies:** $RTO \geq 1$ s $\Rightarrow RTO_3 = 1.000$ s (round up).

### Analytical Summary Table

| Sample | $R$ (s) | $SRTT$ (s) | $RTTVAR$ (s) | $RTO$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.40 | 0.4000 | 0.2000 | 1.200 |
| 2 | 0.50 | 0.4125 | 0.1750 | 1.1125 |
| 3 | 0.45 | 0.4172 | 0.1406 | **1.000** (floored) |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Why the RTO Multiplier is 4

The RTO formula $RTO = SRTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR$ uses a multiplier of **4** as a conservative safety margin. From Chebyshev's inequality, for *any* probability distribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\vert X - \mu \vert \geq k\sigma) \leq \frac{1}{k^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

With $k = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\vert X - \mu \vert \geq 4\sigma) \leq \frac{1}{16} = 6.25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the probability that a single RTT sample exceeds $SRTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR$ is bounded by **6.25%** for *any* network jitter distribution. This statistical safety margin is why $K=4$ is the universally adopted constant — it makes spurious timeouts vanishingly rare without wasting too much time on a genuinely lost segment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the TCP Retransmission Decision Engine

The following block diagram captures the complete control loop inside a TCP sender. It is the *exact* flow traced by Linux kernel's `tcp_retransmit_timer()` and `tcp_ack()` handlers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Segment sent: start retransmission timer] --> B{ACK received?}
    B -- No, timer expired --> C[Timeout Event]
    C --> D[Retransmit oldest unACKed segment]
    D --> E[RTO = 2 x RTO exponential backoff]
    E --> F[Reset Karn's rule: no RTT sampling for this segment]
    F --> A

    B -- Yes, new cumulative ACK --> G[Reset RTO to computed value]
    G --> H[Update SRTT and RTTVAR with new R sample]
    H --> A

    B -- Yes, duplicate ACK --> I{Dup ACK count}
    I -- count less than 3 --> J[Increment dup ACK counter]
    J --> A
    I -- count equals 3 --> K[Fast Retransmit triggered]
    K --> L[Retransmit immediately do not wait for RTO]
    L --> M[Optional: Fast Recovery adjust cwnd]
    M --> A

    styleA fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
    styleC fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    styleD fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    styleK fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600
    styleL fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600
    styleH fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.2 State Machine — Lifecycle of a Single TCP Segment

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sent: transmitter writes to socket
    Sent --> Acked: cumulative ACK received
    Sent --> DupAck: duplicate ACK count increments
    DupAck --> DupAck: count = 1
    DupAck --> DupAck: count = 2
    DupAck --> FastRetransmit: count reaches 3
    FastRetransmit --> Retransmitted: segment re-queued
    Sent --> Timeout: RTO expires
    Timeout --> Retransmitted: Karn rule applied RTO doubled
    Retransmitted --> Acked: matching ACK arrives
    Acked --> [*]
    Retransmitted --> Timeout: RTO expires again
    Timeout --> Retransmitted: exponential backoff continues
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — RTT Estimator Dataflow (Jacobson/Karn)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        R[RTT sample R]
    end
    subgraph Estimator
        SR[SRTT register]
        RV[RTTVAR register]
        ALPHA[alpha = 1 by 8]
        BETA[beta = 1 by 4]
    end
    subgraph KarnGuard
        KARN{Retransmitted segment?}
    end
    subgraph RTO
        RTOreg[RTO register]
        MAX[max G 4 x RTTVAR]
    end

    R --> KARN
    KARN -- Yes --> DROP[Discard sample: ambiguous ACK]
    KARN -- No --> SR
    KARN -- No --> RV
    SR --> ALPHA --> SR
    RV --> BETA --> RV
    SR --> RTOreg
    RV --> MAX --> RTOreg

    styleKARN fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600
    styleDROP fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    styleRTOreg fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Retransmission Timeout (RTO) in TCP. Why must it be dynamic and not a fixed value? [3 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
The **Retransmission Timeout (RTO)** is the maximum time interval a TCP sender waits for an acknowledgement (ACK) after transmitting a segment. If the ACK does not arrive within the RTO, the segment is presumed lost and is retransmitted.

It must be **dynamic** because:
1. **Network path delays vary widely** — from $<1$ ms on a LAN to $>500$ ms on a satellite link, and even along the *same* path, queuing delay fluctuates with congestion.
2. A fixed short RTO causes **spurious retransmissions** (wasting bandwidth), while a fixed long RTO causes **excessive idle time** after genuine loss.
3. RTO is therefore computed as $RTO = SRTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR$, where SRTT and RTTVAR are updated adaptively from each ACK.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for dynamic justification, 1 mark for the formula.)*

### Q2. State Karn's Algorithm. What problem does it solve? [3 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**
**Karn's Algorithm** states that for any **retransmitted segment**, the sender must *not* measure a new RTT sample from the returning ACK, because the sender cannot determine whether the ACK corresponds to the **original** transmission or the **retransmission**. This ambiguity, if uncorrected, would corrupt the RTT estimator. As a complementary rule, on each successive timeout the RTO is **doubled (exponential backoff)** until a valid (non-retransmitted) ACK is received, at which point normal RTT sampling resumes and RTO returns to its computed value.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark ambiguity, 1 mark no-sample rule, 1 mark backoff.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Detailed Numerical + Theory*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of the **TCP Retransmission Policy**. Clearly state the role of RTT, SRTT, RTTVAR, and RTO. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** Given $\alpha = 1/8$, $\beta = 1/4$, and $K = 4$, the first RTT measurement is $R_1 = 0.3$ s. Subsequent samples are $R_2 = 0.6$ s and $R_3 = 0.45$ s. Compute the RTO after each measurement. **[(7 Marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define the four quantities (2 marks):**
- **RTT (Round-Trip Time):** Time elapsed between sending a segment and receiving its ACK. Measured per non-retransmitted segment.
- **SRTT (Smoothed RTT):** Exponentially weighted moving average of past RTT samples — the *current best estimate* of the network's mean delay.
- **RTTVAR (RTT Variation):** Exponentially weighted mean deviation of RTT samples from SRTT — measures *jitter*.
- **RTO (Retransmission Timeout):** Computed waiting time; if no ACK arrives in RTO seconds, the segment is retransmitted. Formula: $RTO = SRTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR$.

**Step 2 — Block diagram (3 marks):**
The estimator receives the RTT sample, applies Karn's rule (drops the sample if the segment is a retransmission), updates SRTT and RTTVAR using $\alpha$ and $\beta$, and outputs the new RTO. The RTO drives the retransmission timer that arms alongside the segment in the send buffer. On timeout, the segment is retransmitted and the RTO is doubled. On 3 duplicate ACKs, Fast Retransmit fires immediately, bypassing the timer.

**Step 3 — Why $K = 4$ (2 marks):**
The factor 4 is a safety margin based on Chebyshev's inequality, bounding the probability of a single RTT exceeding $SRTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR$ to under $1/16$ ($\approx 6.25\%$) for *any* jitter distribution. This makes spurious timeouts statistically rare while still being aggressive enough for loss recovery.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — After $R_1 = 0.3$ s (first sample, special rule):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRTT_1 &= R_1 = 0.300 \text{ s} \\
RTTVAR_1 &= R_1 / 2 = 0.150 \text{ s} \\
RTO_1 &= SRTT_1 + 4 \cdot RTTVAR_1 = 0.300 + 0.600 = 0.900 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply floor rule: $RTO \geq 1$ s, therefore $RTO_1 = 1.000$ s.

**Step 2 — After $R_2 = 0.6$ s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTTVAR_2 &= (1 - \tfrac{1}{4}) \cdot 0.150 + \tfrac{1}{4} \cdot \vert 0.300 - 0.600 \vert \\
         &= 0.75(0.150) + 0.25(0.300) \\
         &= 0.1125 + 0.0750 = 0.1875 \text{ s} \\
SRTT_2 &= (1 - \tfrac{1}{8}) \cdot 0.300 + \tfrac{1}{8} \cdot 0.600 \\
       &= 0.875(0.300) + 0.125(0.600) \\
       &= 0.2625 + 0.0750 = 0.3375 \text{ s} \\
RTO_2 &= 0.3375 + 4(0.1875) = 0.3375 + 0.750 = 1.0875 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — After $R_3 = 0.45$ s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTTVAR_3 &= 0.75(0.1875) + 0.25 \cdot \vert 0.3375 - 0.450 \vert \\
         &= 0.140625 + 0.25(0.1125) \\
         &= 0.140625 + 0.028125 = 0.16875 \text{ s} \\
SRTT_3 &= 0.875(0.3375) + 0.125(0.450) \\
       &= 0.29531 + 0.05625 = 0.35156 \text{ s} \\
RTO_3 &= 0.35156 + 4(0.16875) = 0.35156 + 0.675 = 1.02656 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table (must be drawn in exam, 1 mark):**

| Sample | $R$ (s) | $SRTT$ (s) | $RTTVAR$ (s) | $RTO$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.30 | 0.3000 | 0.1500 | **1.000** (floored) |
| 2 | 0.60 | 0.3375 | 0.1875 | 1.0875 |
| 3 | 0.45 | 0.3516 | 0.1688 | 1.0266 |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Theory-Heavy Alternative*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the **TCP Timer Management** mechanism. Discuss how **Karn's Algorithm** and the **Exponential Backoff** rule together solve the retransmission ambiguity and timer inflation problems. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** Explain the **Fast Retransmit** mechanism in detail. Why is it preferred over plain timeout-based retransmission on high-bandwidth-delay-product (BDP) links? **[(7 Marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. The Need for Timer Management (2 marks):**
TCP uses a *single* retransmission timer per connection (not per segment) because a typical sender has multiple unacknowledged segments in flight (sliding window). The timer is re-armed on each new ACK and tracks the *oldest* unacknowledged segment. When it fires, only that oldest segment is retransmitted, after which the timer is re-armed for the next-oldest unACKed segment.

**2. Karn's Algorithm (3 marks):**
When a segment is retransmitted, the sender cannot disambiguate the source of the returning ACK — it could acknowledge either the original or the duplicate. Measuring RTT from this ACK would either over-estimate (if ACK is for original) or under-estimate (if for retransmit) the true RTT, destabilising the estimator. **Karn's rule** therefore **discards RTT samples from all retransmitted segments**. RTT measurement resumes only when a fresh (non-retransmitted) segment is acknowledged.

**3. Exponential Backoff (2 marks):**
To compensate for the lost RTT update, the sender **doubles the RTO** on each successive timeout. This backoff continues until either (a) an ACK arrives, after which RTO is reset to its computed value, or (b) a maximum cap is reached (typically 60 s in modern stacks). The doubling pattern mirrors the Ethernet CSMA/CD backoff and prevents network meltdown during persistent congestion.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**1. Duplicate ACKs as a Loss Signal (3 marks):**
Whenever the receiver gets an out-of-order segment, it **re-sends a cumulative ACK for the last in-order byte** received. This is a *duplicate* ACK because it carries the same acknowledgement number as the previous one. A single duplicate ACK may be caused by mere reordering. However, if the sender observes **3 such duplicates in succession**, it can confidently conclude that the segment *after* the duplicated ACK number has been lost (the receiver keeps ACKing the same byte because it is still missing the next one). The sender **retransmits immediately** without waiting for the RTO to expire — this is **Fast Retransmit**, defined in **RFC 2581 / RFC 5681**.

**2. Why Fast Retransmit is Essential for High-BDP Links (4 marks):**
On a high-BDP link (e.g., 1 Gbps transcontinental link, RTT $\approx 100$ ms, BDP $\approx 12.5$ MB), a single timeout of even 1 second wastes the equivalent of **10 Mbit of idle capacity** — capacity that could have carried more data. Fast Retransmit cuts loss-recovery latency from *one RTO* to roughly *one RTT*, a ~10× improvement. Modern stacks pair Fast Retransmit with **Fast Recovery** (inflate cwnd by 3 MSS for the 3 dup-ACKs, then deflate to ssthresh on retransmit), which keeps the pipe full during recovery and is the foundation of **TCP Reno / TCP NewReno** performance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the floor rule** $RTO \geq 1$ s. The first computed RTO is often $< 1$ s and *must* be clamped; not doing so loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong order of operations** — update $RTTVAR$ *before* $SRTT$ in the recurrence. The new RTTVAR uses the *old* SRTT.
> 3. **Confusing Fast Retransmit with Fast Recovery** — they are *two separate* mechanisms, often co-deployed but independently defined.
> 4. **Omitting Karn's rule from the Fast Retransmit discussion** — Karn's rule applies to *all* retransmissions, including Fast Retransmit.
> 5. **Writing the formula with raw `|` pipes** in tabular answers — examiners in strict KTU valuation rounds deduct for broken table syntax; always use $\vert$ or $\mid$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print or screenshot this section the night before the exam.**

- **TCP Retransmission Policy** = rules for *when* a sender retransmits a segment.
- **Three triggers for retransmission:** (i) RTO timeout, (ii) Fast Retransmit at 3 duplicate ACKs, (iii) sender-side explicit zero-window probe.
- **RTT** is measured only for *non-retransmitted* segments (**Karn's Algorithm**).
- **SRTT update:** $SRTT = (1 - \alpha) SRTT + \alpha R$, with $\alpha = 1/8$.
- **RTTVAR update:** $RTTVAR = (1 - \beta) RTTVAR + \beta \vert SRTT - R \vert$, with $\beta = 1/4$.
- **RTO formula:** $RTO = SRTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR$, clamped to $\geq 1$ s.
- **First-sample special case:** $SRTT = R$, $RTTVAR = R/2$.
- **Exponential backoff:** $RTO \leftarrow 2 \cdot RTO$ on each timeout; reset on a fresh ACK.
- **Fast Retransmit:** triggered by **3 duplicate cumulative ACKs**; bypasses the RTO timer.
- **Chebyshev bound:** with $K=4$, probability of spurious timeout $\leq 1/16 = 6.25\%$.
- **Memory of estimator:** ~5–6 samples (geometric decay at $\alpha = 1/8$).
- **Estimator is unbiased:** $E[SRTT^*] = \mu$, the true network mean RTT.
- **RTT measurement is taken at the sender** when the ACK arrives — not at the receiver.
- **Karn + Backoff** together solve *ambiguity* and *timer inflation* — never quote one without the other in 14-mark answers.
- **Draw the summary table** in every numerical — it earns the presentation mark that lifts a 12/14 to 14/14.
- **Units are mandatory** in every line of the numerical — $s$ for time, no naked decimals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TCP Congestion Control. Application Layer - File Transfer Protocol (FTP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** TCP Congestion Control. Application Layer - File Transfer Protocol (FTP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 TCP Congestion Control — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TCP Congestion Control** is the set of distributed, end-to-end algorithms implemented within the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) that allow a sender to regulate the rate at which it injects data into the network so as to **prevent the network from becoming overloaded (congested)** while still aggressively utilizing available bandwidth.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, congestion control is positioned as part of the **transport layer's role in ensuring Quality of Service (QoS)**, distinct from but complementary to **flow control** (which is end-to-end receiver protection).

**Key Distinction Table:**

| Aspect | Flow Control | Congestion Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Prevent sender from overwhelming the *receiver* | Prevent sender from overwhelming the *network* |
| **Scope** | End-to-end (single TCP connection) | Network-wide (shared routers/links) |
| **Controlled by** | Receiver's advertised window (`rwnd`) | Sender's congestion window (`cwnd`) |
| **Feedback signal** | ACKs carrying `rwnd` | Packet loss + duplicate ACKs + RTT variations |
| **KTU Line of Thought** | "Don't send faster than the receiver can absorb." | "Don't send faster than the network can deliver." |

> [!NOTE]
> **The effective TCP window** at any instant is given by:
> $$\text{Effective Window} = \min(\text{rwnd},\ \text{cwnd})$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **multi-lane highway (the network)** feeding cars (TCP segments) toward a destination city (receiver). The destination has only so many parking spots (`rwnd`), but the highway itself can also jam up at narrow bridges (router buffers). 

- **Flow control** = ensuring you don't crash into the parking lot of your destination.
- **Congestion control** = ensuring you don't pour 100,000 cars per minute onto a 2-lane tunnel. When you sense traffic slowing (RTT increases) or an accident (packet loss), you ease off the gas pedal.

A practical real-world analogy is the **TCP "Slow Start" behaves like a new driver gently pressing the accelerator**, doubling speed every second until they hit the speed limit (cwnd reaches ssthresh), after which they cruise carefully adding small increments (Congestion Avoidance), and brake hard if they see brake lights ahead (3 duplicate ACKs → Fast Retransmit) or hit a wall (timeout → back to Slow Start).

### 1.3 FTP — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **File Transfer Protocol (FTP)** is an application-layer, client-server protocol defined originally in **RFC 959 (1985)** that provides a reliable, connection-oriented service for transferring files between hosts over TCP, using **two parallel TCP connections** — a *control connection* and a *data connection*.

The protocol assumes an **always-on server (daemon)** listening on the well-known port **21**, with the client dynamically establishing a data channel (either on port 20 in active mode, or an ephemeral port in passive mode).

### 1.4 FTP Conceptual Analogy

Think of FTP like a **telephone call between a librarian and a researcher**:
- They first dial the library's main number (port 21) → this is the **control connection**, used to discuss *what* books are needed and *how many* copies.
- Once agreed, a **separate dedicated courier** (the data connection) is dispatched to physically carry the books. If the researcher asks for 10 books, 10 separate courier trips might occur.
- The control line stays open the entire time to coordinate future requests.

This is why FTP uses **two channels** — separating command dialogue from bulk data transfer improves efficiency and allows simultaneous browsing-and-downloading.

### 1.5 High-Yield Constants & Standards

- **Well-known port (control):** **TCP 21**
- **Well-known port (data, active mode):** **TCP 20**
- **TCP MSS default:** **536 bytes**
- **TCP initial cwnd (RFC 6928):** **10 MSS ≈ 15 KB** (raised from 1 MSS in RFC 2581)
- **ssthresh initial value:** **65535 bytes** (often)
- **Duplicate ACK threshold for Fast Retransmit:** **3 duplicate ACKs**
- **Standard Round-Trip Time (RTT) granularity:** **500 ms** (RFC 6298)
- **Karn's Algorithm rule:** Never measure RTT on retransmitted segments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Sub-Problems of Network Congestion

1. **Network-Assisted Congestion Control:**
   - Routers explicitly signal congestion to senders (e.g., via ECN — Explicit Congestion Notification, or DECbit, RED).
   - Not widely deployed on the public Internet.

2. **End-to-End Congestion Control (TCP's approach):**
   - The network is treated as a black box.
   - Senders infer congestion from observed loss, duplicate ACKs, and RTT inflation.
   - **TCP uses this model** — making it the focus of KTU Module 4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is congestion control needed?**
> Because **routers have finite buffers**. When arrival rate exceeds service rate, queues grow unboundedly, leading to:
> - **Increased delay (queuing delay → 0)**
> - **Buffer overflow → packet loss**
> - **Retransmissions → further congestion (positive feedback loop)**
> - Eventual **congestion collapse** (Kleinrock's classical result: throughput → 0 as load → ∞)

### 2.2 The TCP Sender's Three Variables

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $cwnd$ | Congestion Window (sender's estimate of network capacity) | bytes or MSS |
| $ssthresh$ | Slow-Start Threshold (boundary between SS and CA) | bytes or MSS |
| $rwnd$ | Receiver's advertised window (flow control) | bytes |
| $\text{RTT}$ | Round-Trip Time | seconds |

### 2.3 The Four Canonical TCP Congestion-Control Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks about these as state machines. Memorize the **transitions** and **multipliers**.

#### (a) Slow Start (SS)
- **Goal:** Quickly probe for available bandwidth when connection begins or after a timeout.
- **Phase:** `cwnd < ssthresh`
- **Behavior:** For each ACK received, increase $cwnd$ by **1 MSS**.
- **Effective growth:** $cwnd$ **doubles every RTT**.
- **Initial cwnd:** $\text{IW} = 10$ MSS (RFC 6928).

#### (b) Congestion Avoidance (CA)
- **Goal:** Carefully probe for additional bandwidth once near capacity.
- **Phase:** `cwnd ≥ ssthresh`
- **Behavior:** For each ACK, increase $cwnd$ by $\frac{\text{MSS}^2}{cwnd}$ (approximately **+1 MSS per RTT**).
- **Effective growth:** **Linear (Additive Increase)**.

#### (c) Fast Retransmit
- **Trigger:** **3 duplicate ACKs** received (rapid retransmission rule).
- **Action:** Retransmit the missing segment **without waiting for timeout**.
- **Why 3?** Tolerates small reordering; 3 is a strong signal of packet loss.

#### (d) Fast Recovery
- **Trigger:** After Fast Retransmit.
- **Action:** 
  - Set $ssthresh = \frac{cwnd}{2}$
  - Set $cwnd = ssthresh + 3 \cdot \text{MSS}$ (inflation for the 3 dupACKs already in flight)
  - Enter Congestion Avoidance directly (skip Slow Start).
- **Significance:** Avoids the drastic cwnd reset that a timeout would trigger.

### 2.4 The AIMD Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **AIMD = Additive Increase, Multiplicative Decrease** — the canonical congestion control principle.

| Phase | Formula | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Additive Increase** | $cwnd \leftarrow cwnd + \frac{\text{MSS}^2}{cwnd}$ | +1 MSS per RTT (linear) |
| **Multiplicative Decrease** | $cwnd \leftarrow \frac{cwnd}{2}$ | Halve on loss (geometric) |

**Why AIMD?** It is provably **convergent and fair** (Chiu & Jain, 1989) — multiple AIMD streams competing for a shared bottleneck converge to an **equal-share, efficient operating point**.

### 2.5 Loss Detection Methods

| Event | Reaction | $ssthresh$ update | $cwnd$ update |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Timeout (no ACK)** | Back to **Slow Start** | $ssthresh = \frac{cwnd}{2}$ | $cwnd = 1$ MSS |
| **3 Duplicate ACKs** | **Fast Retransmit + Fast Recovery** | $ssthresh = \frac{cwnd}{2}$ | $cwnd = ssthresh + 3\cdot\text{MSS}$ → CA |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the **multipliers** — examiners love asking "what happens at step $n$?"

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective window | $\min(rwnd, cwnd)$ | Sender's actual send limit |
| 2 | Slow Start growth | $cwnd_n = 2^n \cdot \text{MSS}$ | After $n$ RTTs |
| 3 | Congestion Avoidance growth | $cwnd_n = cwnd_0 + n \cdot \text{MSS}$ | Linear in $n$ |
| 4 | Multiplicative decrease | $cwnd_{\text{new}} = \frac{cwnd_{\text{old}}}{2}$ | On loss |
| 5 | Throughput (steady-state, loss probability $p$) | $\text{TP} \approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}$ | **Mathis formula** — important for KTU derivations |
| 6 | Initial Window (RFC 6928) | $\text{IW} = \min(10 \cdot \text{MSS},\ 14600)$ bytes | |
| 7 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \cdot \text{RTT}$ | In bits/bytes; defines pipe size |
| 8 | ssthresh after timeout | $ssthresh = \max\left(\frac{cwnd}{2},\ 2\cdot\text{MSS}\right)$ | Never less than 2 MSS |

> ⚠️ **Pipe character avoidance:** Note the use of `\cdot` and `\min` instead of `|` to keep tables intact.

### 2.7 FTP — Deep Operational Theory

FTP operates above TCP and follows a strict **telnet-like command/response model**. Two channels:

#### Channel 1: Control Connection (Persistent)
- **Port:** 21 (server-side well-known).
- **Purpose:** Exchange of FTP commands (`USER`, `PASS`, `LIST`, `RETR`, `STOR`) and replies (3-digit codes like `220`, `331`, `226`).
- **Persists** for the entire FTP session.
- Uses **NVT ASCII** (similar to Telnet).

#### Channel 2: Data Connection (Ephemeral)
- **Active Mode:** Server initiates data connection **from port 20** to client's ephemeral port.
- **Passive Mode:** Client initiates data connection to a server-side ephemeral port (the server tells the client which port via `PASV` response).
- **Closed** after each file transfer.
- Used for actual file payload transfer or directory listings.

### 2.8 FTP Commands — KTU High-Yield List

| Command | Function | Example Reply Code |
|---|---|---|
| `USER username` | Send user ID | `331` Username OK, need password |
| `PASS password` | Send password | `230` User logged in |
| `LIST` | List files in current dir | `125` Connection opened; `226` Transfer complete |
| `RETR filename` | Retrieve (download) a file | `226` Transfer complete |
| `STOR filename` | Store (upload) a file | `226` Transfer complete |
| `DELE filename` | Delete a remote file | `250` Requested action OK |
| `PASV` | Enter passive mode | `227` Entering passive mode |
| `QUIT` | Logout | `221` Service closing |
| `CWD dir` | Change working directory | `250` OK |
| `TYPE A` or `TYPE I` | ASCII or Binary mode | `200` Command OK |

### 2.9 FTP Reply Code Classes (Memorize!)

| Code Range | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `1yz` | Positive Preliminary (action started) | `125` Data connection open |
| `2yz` | Positive Completion (success) | `226` Transfer complete |
| `3yz` | Positive Intermediate (needs more input) | `331` Need password |
| `4yz` | Transient Negative (retry possible) | `452` Insufficient storage |
| `5yz` | Permanent Negative (do not retry) | `530` Not logged in |

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Congestion Control:** Forms the backbone of Internet stability. Without it, the modern web (streaming, cloud, video calls) would collapse. **BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and Round-trip propagation time)** — Google's modern TCP — replaces loss-based detection with delay-based bandwidth probing.
- **FTP:** Still used for bulk content distribution, software repositories (legacy), and SFTP/FTPS in enterprise. Modern alternatives (SFTP over SSH, HTTP-based transfers) dominate consumer use, but FTP's protocol design principles persist in every "request-then-transfer" protocol.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Slow Start Window Evolution

**Problem Statement:** A TCP sender with $ssthresh = 32$ KB and initial $cwnd = 1$ KB enters Slow Start. Find $cwnd$ at the end of RTTs 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5. When does it transition to Congestion Avoidance?

**Step 1 — Establish the recurrence.**

In Slow Start, every ACK increases $cwnd$ by 1 MSS. Effectively, $cwnd$ doubles per RTT:
$$cwnd_{n+1} = 2 \cdot cwnd_n$$

**Step 2 — Solve the recurrence (homogeneous linear).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
cwnd_n &= 2^n \cdot cwnd_0 \\
cwnd_n &= 2^n \cdot 1 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each RTT (in KB).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
cwnd_1 &= 2^1 \cdot 1 = 2 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_2 &= 2^2 \cdot 1 = 4 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_3 &= 2^3 \cdot 1 = 8 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_4 &= 2^4 \cdot 1 = 16 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_5 &= 2^5 \cdot 1 = 32 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine transition point.**

Since $ssthresh = 32$ KB, Slow Start applies while $cwnd < 32$ KB. At RTT 5, $cwnd = 32$ KB, so the sender transitions into **Congestion Avoidance** at the start of RTT 6.

**Step 5 — Continue in CA for RTT 6 (linear growth).**

In CA, $cwnd$ increases by 1 MSS per RTT:
$$cwnd_6 = cwnd_5 + 1 \cdot \text{MSS} = 32 + 1 = 33 \text{ KB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** Students often incorrectly say "transitions when $cwnd = ssthresh$." The correct rule is **Slow Start continues as long as $cwnd < ssthresh$**, transitioning the moment it **reaches or exceeds** $ssthresh$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Mathis Throughput Formula

**Problem Statement:** Derive the approximate steady-state throughput of a TCP Reno sender as a function of loss probability $p$ and RTT.

**Step 1 — Assume steady-state "saw-tooth" behavior.**

In TCP Reno, cwnd oscillates between $W$ and $\frac{W}{2}$ due to AIMD:
- After a loss event: $cwnd \leftarrow \frac{W}{2}$
- Linear growth resumes: $cwnd$ adds 1 MSS per RTT.

**Step 2 — Compute the window "swing" before next loss.**

The number of RTTs to climb from $\frac{W}{2}$ back to $W$:
$$N = \frac{W - \frac{W}{2}}{1} = \frac{W}{2} \text{ RTTs}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total data sent in one cycle.**

Approximating the average window over a cycle as $\approx \frac{3W}{4}$ (area under saw-tooth):
$$B = \frac{3W}{4} \cdot \frac{W}{2} \cdot \text{MSS} = \frac{3W^2}{8} \cdot \text{MSS}$$

**Step 4 — Relate $W$ to loss probability.**

A loss occurs roughly every $N$ RTTs. With one loss per cycle:
$$p \approx \frac{1}{N \cdot \text{(ACKs per RTT)}} \approx \frac{2}{W^2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $W$ and substitute into throughput.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{p}} \\
\text{Throughput} &\approx \frac{B}{N \cdot \text{RTT}} = \frac{\frac{3W^2}{8} \cdot \text{MSS}}{\frac{W}{2} \cdot \text{RTT}} = \frac{3W \cdot \text{MSS}}{4 \cdot \text{RTT}} \\
\text{Throughput} &\approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The constant **1.22** comes from more refined analysis (Padhye et al., 1998) accounting for timeout-based losses too.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula shows:** Throughput is **inversely proportional to $\sqrt{p}$**. Reducing loss from $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ gives a **10× throughput boost** — the entire motivation for optical fiber upgrades and clean wireless links.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: FTP Session Time Calculation

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the total time for an FTP transfer of a $10$ MB file over a link with $RTT = 100$ ms, $MSS = 1$ KB, assuming Slow Start only (no losses, no flow control limits). Use $cwnd_0 = 1$ MSS.

**Step 1 — Find RTTs needed to reach sufficient window.**

Since Slow Start doubles per RTT, we need:
$$2^n \cdot 1 \text{ KB} \geq 10 \text{ MB} = 10240 \text{ KB}$$
$$2^n \geq 10240$$
$$n \geq \log_2(10240) \approx 13.32$$
So $n = 14$ RTTs.

**Step 2 — Compute total data sent cumulatively.**

Total data sent after $n$ RTTs in Slow Start:
$$D_n = (2^n - 1) \cdot \text{MSS}$$

**Step 3 — Compute transferred data after 13 RTTs.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{13} &= (2^{13} - 1) \cdot 1 \text{ KB} \\
&= 8191 \text{ KB} \approx 8.0 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Remaining data after RTT 13.**

$$D_{\text{remaining}} = 10 \text{ MB} - 8.0 \text{ MB} = 2.0 \text{ MB} = 2048 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 5 — RTT 14 completes the transfer.**

After RTT 14: $D_{14} = 16383$ KB $\geq 10$ MB. ✓

**Step 6 — Total time.**

$$T = 14 \cdot RTT = 14 \cdot 100 \text{ ms} = 1400 \text{ ms} = 1.4 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This shows why **TCP initial window (IW) was raised to 10 MSS** — it saves approximately **3-4 RTTs** on small transfers, dramatically improving perceived page-load times for HTTP/3.0 over TCP.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TCP Variants Compared

| Feature | TCP Tahoe | TCP Reno | TCP NewReno | TCP CUBIC (Linux default) | TCP BBR (Google) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow Start** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (with Hystart) | ✓ (Probing) |
| **Fast Retransmit** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Fast Recovery** | ✗ (resets to 1 MSS) | ✓ (basic) | ✓ (improved for multiple losses) | ✓ | N/A (delay-based) |
| **Congestion signal** | Loss | Loss | Loss | Loss (cubic function) | Delay + bandwidth |
| **Growth function** | Linear (CA) | Linear (CA) | Linear | Cubic in time | Bandwidth-delay probing |
| **Loss recovery** | Timeout back to SS | Halves cwnd | Up to timeout | Cubic reset | Ignores small loss |
| **Used in** | Historical/educational | Legacy | Mid-era | Linux/Windows modern | YouTube, Google services |

### 3.5 Worked Example: Fast Retransmit Trigger Calculation

**Problem:** Suppose a TCP sender has transmitted segments 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and segment 3 is lost. Sequence the receiver's ACKs and determine when Fast Retransmit fires.

**Step 1 — Receiver sends ACK for highest in-order byte received.**

- Receives 1 → ACK 2
- Receives 2 → ACK 3
- (3 lost)
- Receives 4 → still ACK 3 (out-of-order)
- Receives 5 → still ACK 3

**Step 2 — Sender observes 3 duplicate ACKs (all ACK 3).**

**Step 3 — Trigger Fast Retransmit at the 3rd duplicate.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** "Duplicate ACK" means ACK with the **same acknowledgment number** as the previous ACK. **Not** the same segment number. Many students miscount.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TCP Congestion Control State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SlowStart: Connection begins / Timeout
    SlowStart --> SlowStart: Each ACK: cwnd doubles per RTT
    SlowStart --> CongestionAvoidance: cwnd reaches ssthresh
    SlowStart --> SlowStart: Timeout: cwnd reset to 1 MSS, ssthresh halved
    CongestionAvoidance --> CongestionAvoidance: Each ACK: cwnd grows linearly
    CongestionAvoidance --> FastRecovery: 3 duplicate ACKs
    CongestionAvoidance --> SlowStart: Timeout
    FastRecovery --> CongestionAvoidance: After cwnd = ssthresh + 3 MSS
    FastRecovery --> SlowStart: Timeout (rare)
    note right of SlowStart
        Phase: cwnd less than ssthresh
        Growth: Exponential (2x per RTT)
        Trigger: Connection start or timeout
    end note
    note right of CongestionAvoidance
        Phase: cwnd greater or equal to ssthresh
        Growth: Linear (1 MSS per RTT)
        AIMD Additive Increase
    end note
    note right of FastRecovery
        Trigger: 3 duplicate ACKs
        cwnd set to ssthresh + 3 MSS
        Skips Slow Start
    end note
```

### 4.2 Congestion Window "Saw-Tooth" Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    startA((Connection start IW=10 MSS)) --> ss[Slow Start: cwnd doubles per RTT]
    ss -->|cwnd = ssthresh| ca[Congestion Avoidance: cwnd +1 MSS per RTT]
    ca -->|Packet loss detected| event{Type of loss event?}
    event -->|3 dupACKs| fr[Fast Retransmit and Fast Recovery]
    event -->|Timeout| to[Back to Slow Start with halved ssthresh]
    fr -->|ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd = ssthresh + 3 MSS| ca
    to --> ss
    style ss fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style ca fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style fr fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style to fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

### 4.3 FTP Connection Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    client[FTP Client User Interface] -->|Control channel TCP port 21| server[FTP Server Process]
    server -->|Control replies 3 digit codes| client
    client -->|Data channel TCP port 20 active mode| serverdata[FTP Server Data Process]
    serverdata -->|File payload or directory listing| clientdata[FTP Client Data Process]
    server -->|Spawns per transfer| serverdata
    note1[Control connection persists entire session]
    note2[Data connection opened and closed per file]
    style client fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style server fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff9800
    style clientdata fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style serverdata fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff9800
```

### 4.4 Active vs Passive FTP Mode Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server
    Note over C,S: Active Mode (Server initiates data)
    C->>S: Connect to port 21 (control)
    S-->>C: 220 Welcome
    C->>S: USER anonymous
    S-->>C: 331 Need password
    C->>S: PASS guest
    S-->>C: 230 Logged in
    C->>S: PORT 192,168,1,5,150,200
    S-->>C: 200 PORT command OK
    C->>S: RETR file.txt
    S->>C: Connect from port 20 to client port X
    S-->>C: 150 Opening data
    S-->>C: 226 Transfer complete
    Note over C,S: Passive Mode (Client initiates data)
    C->>S: Connect to port 21 (control)
    S-->>C: 220 Welcome
    C->>S: PASV
    S-->>C: 227 Entering Passive Mode h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2
    C->>S: Connect to advertised port
    C->>S: RETR file.txt
    S-->>C: 150 Opening data
    S-->>C: 226 Transfer complete
```

### 4.5 FTP Session Timeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Client opens TCP port 21] --> B[Server replies 220]
    B --> C[Client sends USER]
    C --> D{Reply}
    D -->|331| E[Client sends PASS]
    E --> F{Reply}
    F -->|230| G[Authenticated]
    G --> H[Choose mode PASV or PORT]
    H --> I[Establish data connection]
    I --> J[Send file commands RETR STOR LIST]
    J --> K[Data connection transfers files]
    K --> L{More files?}
    L -->|Yes| J
    L -->|No| M[Client sends QUIT]
    M --> N[Server replies 221]
    N --> O[Control connection closes]
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style G fill:#d4edda
    style K fill:#fff3cd
    style O fill:#f8d7da
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between flow control and congestion control in TCP. Mention the variable used in each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Flow Control | Congestion Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Prevent receiver overrun | Prevent network overload |
| **Scope** | End-to-end (sender ↔ receiver) | Network-wide (involves routers) |
| **Variable** | `rwnd` (Receiver Window) | `cwnd` (Congestion Window) |
| **Feedback** | Receiver advertises window in ACK | Implicit (loss, dupACK, RTT) |
| **Limiting window** | $rwnd$ | $cwnd$ |

Effective TCP send window: $\min(rwnd, cwnd)$. **(3 marks: 1 for definition of each, 1 for variable, 1 for effective window formula)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the significance of using two separate TCP connections in FTP? Name them with their default port numbers.

**Model Answer:**

FTP uses **two parallel TCP connections**:
1. **Control Connection** — port **21**. Carries commands (`USER`, `PASS`, `RETR`) and replies (`220`, `331`, `226`). Persistent throughout session.
2. **Data Connection** — port **20** (active mode) or ephemeral server port (passive mode). Used for actual file payload. Opened/closed per file.

**Significance:**
- Decouples command dialogue from bulk transfer.
- Allows user to browse (`LIST`) while a download is in progress.
- Control channel is small (text) while data channel is large (binary). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, CO4]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain TCP congestion control with the help of a neat diagram. Discuss the Slow Start and Congestion Avoidance phases in detail.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define congestion control:**

TCP Congestion Control is the set of mechanisms by which a TCP sender regulates its transmission rate to avoid overwhelming the network, treating the network as a black box and inferring congestion from observed packet loss, duplicate ACKs, and RTT variations.

**Step 2 — Slow Start Phase:**

When a TCP connection begins or after a timeout:
- Initial window: $cwnd = 1$ MSS (older) or **10 MSS** (RFC 6928).
- For each ACK received: $cwnd \leftarrow cwnd + \text{MSS}$.
- Effective behavior: $cwnd$ **doubles every RTT** (exponential growth).
- Slow Start continues while $cwnd < ssthresh$.
- Once $cwnd \geq ssthresh$, transitions to Congestion Avoidance.

Mathematically:
$$cwnd_n = 2^n \cdot cwnd_0$$

**Step 3 — Congestion Avoidance Phase:**

Once $cwnd \geq ssthresh$:
- For each ACK: $cwnd \leftarrow cwnd + \frac{\text{MSS} \cdot \text{MSS}}{cwnd}$
- Effective behavior: $cwnd$ increases by **1 MSS per RTT** (linear / additive increase).
- Maintains equilibrium near network capacity without overshooting drastically.

Mathematically:
$$cwnd_n = cwnd_0 + n \cdot \text{MSS}$$

**Step 4 — Loss Response:**

- **Triple Duplicate ACK:** $ssthresh = \frac{cwnd}{2}$; $cwnd = ssthresh + 3\cdot\text{MSS}$ → enter Fast Recovery → resume CA.
- **Timeout:** $ssthresh = \frac{cwnd}{2}$; $cwnd = 1$ MSS → restart Slow Start.

**Step 5 — Diagram (refer to Section 4.1).**

**(7 marks distribution: 1 definition, 2 Slow Start with formula, 2 Congestion Avoidance with formula, 2 loss response + diagram)**

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A TCP connection has initial cwnd = 1 MSS and ssthresh = 16 KB. Assume MSS = 1 KB. The connection operates without losses for 10 RTTs, then experiences a single packet loss detected via 3 duplicate ACKs. Plot the cwnd evolution RTT-by-RTT and identify when Slow Start ends.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Slow Start phase (RTT 1 to 5):**

Since $cwnd$ doubles each RTT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
cwnd_0 &= 1 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_1 &= 2 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_2 &= 4 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_3 &= 8 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_4 &= 16 \text{ KB} \quad \text{(reaches ssthresh)}
\end{aligned}
$$

At RTT 5, $cwnd$ reaches $ssthresh = 16$ KB → **Slow Start ends**.

**Step 2 — Congestion Avoidance phase (RTT 5 to 10):**

Linear growth (+1 MSS per RTT):

$$
\begin{aligned}
cwnd_5 &= 16 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_6 &= 17 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_7 &= 18 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_8 &= 19 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_9 &= 20 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_{10} &= 21 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fast Recovery triggered at RTT 11 (after 3 dupACKs observed post-RTT 10):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ssthresh_{\text{new}} &= \frac{cwnd_{10}}{2} = \frac{21}{2} = 10.5 \text{ KB} \\
cwnd_{\text{new}} &= ssthresh_{\text{new}} + 3 \cdot \text{MSS} = 10.5 + 3 = 13.5 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Resume CA from RTT 11:**

$cwnd_{11} = 13.5$ KB, then $cwnd_{12} = 14.5$ KB, ...

**Step 5 — Tabular summary:**

| RTT | Phase | cwnd (KB) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | SS | 1 |
| 1 | SS | 2 |
| 2 | SS | 4 |
| 3 | SS | 8 |
| 4 | SS | 16 → transition |
| 5 | CA | 16 |
| 6 | CA | 17 |
| 7 | CA | 18 |
| 8 | CA | 19 |
| 9 | CA | 20 |
| 10 | CA | 21 |
| 11 | FR | 13.5 |
| 12 | CA | 14.5 |

**(7 marks: 2 for SS table, 2 for CA table, 2 for Fast Recovery calc, 1 for transition point)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, CO4]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the File Transfer Protocol (FTP) in detail. With the help of a diagram, describe how FTP establishes control and data connections in both active and passive modes.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition & Architecture:**

FTP is an application-layer protocol (RFC 959) providing reliable file transfer over TCP using **two parallel connections**: control (port 21) and data (port 20 in active mode).

**Step 2 — Control Connection:**

- Persistent throughout session.
- Client connects to server's port 21.
- Uses telnet-like NVT ASCII commands (`USER`, `PASS`, `RETR`, `STOR`, `LIST`, `CWD`, `PASV`, `QUIT`).
- Server replies with 3-digit codes (`1xx` preliminary, `2xx` success, `3xx` intermediate, `4xx` transient error, `5xx` permanent error).

**Step 3 — Active Mode:**

1. Client establishes control connection to server port 21.
2. After authentication, client sends `PORT` command with its IP and ephemeral port (e.g., `PORT 192,168,1,5,150,200` → port 150·256 + 200 = 38600).
3. Client issues `RETR filename`.
4. **Server initiates** TCP connection **from its port 20 to client's advertised port** for data transfer.
5. After transfer, data connection closes; control remains open.

**Problem:** Fails when client is behind a NAT/firewall (cannot accept inbound connections).

**Step 4 — Passive Mode:**

1. Client connects to server port 21 (control).
2. Client sends `PASV`.
3. Server replies `227 Entering Passive Mode (h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2)` — its IP and ephemeral port.
4. **Client initiates** TCP connection to that advertised port.
5. Data flows.

**Advantage:** Works through NATs/firewalls (outbound connections only).

**Step 5 — Diagram (refer to Section 4.4 for sequence diagram).**

**(7 marks: 1 definition, 2 control connection, 2 active mode, 2 passive mode)**

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compare FTP and HTTP. Explain the FTP command-response mechanism with examples of at least 4 commands and their corresponding reply codes.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — FTP vs HTTP Comparison:**

| Feature | FTP | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| **Default port** | 21 (control), 20 (data) | 80 |
| **Connections** | 2 (control + data) | 1 |
| **State** | Stateful (server tracks user, dir) | Stateless |
| **Auth** | Required (USER/PASS) | Optional (often none) |
| **Use case** | Bulk file transfer | Web page retrieval |
| **Data type** | Binary or ASCII (mode set) | Mostly HTML/text |
| **Security** | Cleartext (use FTPS/SFTP) | HTTPS for security |
| **Persistent?** | Control: yes; Data: per file | Single persistent (HTTP/1.1) |

**Step 2 — FTP Command-Response Examples:**

**Command 1: `USER anonymous`**
- Reply: `331 User name okay, need password.`

**Command 2: `PASS guest@example.com`**
- Reply: `230 User logged in, proceed.`

**Command 3: `LIST`**
- Reply 1: `150 Opening ASCII mode data connection for /bin/ls.`
- Reply 2: `226 Transfer complete.`

**Command 4: `RETR report.pdf`**
- Reply 1: `150 Opening BINARY mode data connection for report.pdf (2048576 bytes).`
- Reply 2: `226 Transfer complete.`

**Command 5: `QUIT`**
- Reply: `221 Service closing control connection.`

**Step 3 — Reply Code Classification:**

- `1yz` → Positive Preliminary (action started)
- `2yz` → Positive Completion (success)
- `3yz` → Positive Intermediate (more input needed)
- `4yz` → Transient Negative (retry possible)
- `5yz` → Permanent Negative (do not retry)

**(7 marks: 3 for comparison table, 4 for commands/replies with code classification)**

---

### 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in TCP Congestion Control Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing `rwnd` and `cwnd`:** Many students interchange these. Remember: `rwnd` = receiver window (flow control); `cwnd` = congestion window (network protection). **−1 mark** if mixed up.
> 
> 2. **Slow Start trigger confusion:** Slow Start does **NOT** continue "until cwnd > ssthresh". It continues **while cwnd < ssthresh**, transitioning exactly when $cwnd = ssthresh$.
> 
> 3. **Mistaking Fast Recovery for Slow Start:** After 3 duplicate ACKs, cwnd is set to $ssthresh + 3 \cdot MSS$, **NOT** reset to 1 MSS. Only **timeouts** trigger a full Slow Start reset.
> 
> 4. **FTP PORT command syntax:** Students often confuse the format `PORT h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2`. The port is computed as $p1 \times 256 + p2$, not $p1 \cdot 2^{16} + p2 \cdot 2^8$ (which would be network-byte-order). **−2 marks** for wrong port derivation.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting FTP reply code classification:** Examiners love asking "What does reply code 331 mean?" Just the number is not enough — students must classify it as a **Positive Intermediate (3yz)** and explain it requires further input (e.g., password).
> 
> 6. **AIMD direction confusion:** Additive Increase = grow cwnd; Multiplicative Decrease = halve cwnd. Some students reverse these. **−2 marks.**
> 
> 7. **Active vs Passive Mode:** In **active**, **server initiates** data connection. In **passive**, **client initiates**. Examiners check this explicitly.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

#### TCP Congestion Control Essentials:
- ⭐ **Effective window** = $\min(rwnd, cwnd)$ — always remember this.
- ⭐ **Slow Start**: $cwnd$ doubles per RTT; runs while $cwnd < ssthresh$.
- ⭐ **Congestion Avoidance**: $cwnd$ grows by +1 MSS per RTT (linear); runs while $cwnd \geq ssthresh$.
- ⭐ **Initial cwnd** = **10 MSS** (RFC 6928, raised from 1 MSS).
- ⭐ **3 Duplicate ACKs** → Fast Retransmit + Fast Recovery (skip Slow Start).
- ⭐ **Timeout** → Slow Start reset ($cwnd = 1$ MSS), $ssthresh = cwnd/2$.
- ⭐ **AIMD**: Additive Increase (CA), Multiplicative Decrease (halve on loss).
- ⭐ **Mathis formula**: $\text{Throughput} \approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}$.
- ⭐ **TCP Reno vs Tahoe**: Tahoe always resets to 1 MSS on loss; Reno uses Fast Recovery.
- ⭐ **TCP CUBIC** is Linux default; uses cubic (not linear) growth post-loss.
- ⭐ **TCP BBR** (Google) uses delay-based, not loss-based, congestion detection.

#### FTP Essentials:
- ⭐ **Two connections**: Control (port **21**, persistent) + Data (port **20** active, ephemeral passive).
- ⭐ **Active Mode**: Server connects **to client** for data — fails behind NAT.
- ⭐ **Passive Mode**: Client connects **to server** for data — NAT-friendly.
- ⭐ **Commands**: `USER`, `PASS`, `LIST`, `RETR`, `STOR`, `DELE`, `CWD`, `PASV`, `QUIT`.
- ⭐ **Reply codes**: `1xx` preliminary, `2xx` success, `3xx` intermediate, `4xx` transient error, `5xx` permanent error.
- ⭐ **PORT command format**: `PORT h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2` → port = $p1 \cdot 256 + p2$.
- ⭐ **FTP is stateful** (unlike HTTP) — server maintains user session, current directory, transfer mode (ASCII/Binary).
- ⭐ **Modern alternatives**: SFTP (over SSH), FTPS (FTP over TLS) — both encrypt credentials/data, unlike plain FTP.

#### Quick-Recall Mnemonics:
- **"3 Stops the Slow"** → 3 duplicate ACKs trigger Fast Recovery, skipping Slow Start.
- **"21 controls, 20 carries"** → Port 21 = control, Port 20 = active-mode data.
- **"Asymmetric Initiation"** → Active mode: Server Initiates Data; Passive mode: Client Initiates Data.
- **"Mathis' √p"** → TCP throughput is inversely proportional to the square root of loss probability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Domain Name System (DNS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Domain Name System (DNS)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Domain Name System (DNS)** is a distributed, hierarchical, and globally scalable naming database defined in **RFC 1034** and **RFC 1035** that translates human-readable **domain names** (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into machine-readable **IP addresses** (e.g., `103.25.61.18`). It functions as the **"phonebook of the Internet,"** decoupling service-locator identifiers from human-memorable names. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PEMRT751, Module 4), DNS is classified as an **Application Layer** protocol of the TCP/IP suite that uses primarily **UDP Port 53** for standard queries and **TCP Port 53** for zone transfers and large responses exceeding **512 bytes**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Global Postal Address System**
> 
> Imagine the Internet as a huge global city where every building (computer/server) has a numeric address (IP address like `192.168.1.1`). Humans cannot remember hundreds of 10-digit numbers. So, the postal department introduced a **Directory Service**:
> - You want to send a letter to "Rahul's Coffee Shop, MG Road, Kochi."
> - You ask the **local post office** (Resolver).
> - The local post office does not know the exact numeric PIN code, so it contacts the **Kerala Directory** (TLD Server `.in`).
> - Kerala Directory forwards to the **Kochi City Directory** (Authoritative Server).
> - Kochi Directory returns the PIN code (IP address).
> - The local post office **memorizes (caches)** this for next time.
> 
> DNS works **exactly the same way** — replacing names with numbers using a hierarchy of name servers.

### 1.3 Why DNS is Necessary (Problem it Solves)

- **IP addresses are 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) numerical values** — impossible for humans to memorize at scale.
- When servers change IP addresses (due to ISP changes, cloud migration, load balancing), users should not need to remember new numbers.
- A **decoupled naming system** allows decoupling of host identity from network location.
- DNS also supports **redundancy**: a single domain name can map to multiple IPs (load distribution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DNS is a **distributed database** — there is **no single server** containing the entire Internet's mappings. This design guarantees fault tolerance and scalability.

### 1.4 Design Principles of DNS

| Principle | Description |
|---|---|
| Hierarchical | Tree-like structure from Root → TLD → Authoritative |
| Distributed | Data is spread across millions of name servers |
| Cached | Responses are cached at multiple levels (OS, ISP, browser) |
| Redundant | Multiple servers exist for every zone |
| Application Layer | Operates over UDP/TCP at the OSI Application Layer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DNS Namespace Architecture (The Domain Tree)

The DNS namespace is an **inverted tree** with the unnamed **root** (represented as `.`) at the top.

**Tree Hierarchy (Top-Down):**
1. **Root Zone (`.`)** — Managed by 13 logical root server clusters (A–M), operated globally by organizations like ICANN, VeriSign, Cogent, etc.
2. **Top-Level Domain (TLD)** — `.com`, `.org`, `.net`, `.edu`, `.gov`, `.mil`, country codes like `.in`, `.uk`, `.de`.
3. **Second-Level Domain (SLD)** — Registered name like `google`, `ktu`, `amazon`.
4. **Subdomains** — `www`, `mail`, `ftp`, `api`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN):** A complete domain name that traces unambiguously from host to root. Example: `www.engineering.ktu.edu.in.` — note the **trailing dot** indicating the root.

### 2.2 Structural Breakdown of a Domain Name

A domain name like `mail.engineering.ktu.edu.in` is parsed **right-to-left**:

- `.` — Root
- `.in` — Country Code TLD (ccTLD) — India
- `.edu` — Generic TLD subcategory — Educational
- `.ktu` — Second-level domain — APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University
- `.engineering` — Subdomain — Engineering Department
- `mail` — Host name — Mail Server

The **label** is each dot-separated chunk; maximum length per label is **63 characters**; total FQDN length is **255 characters**.

### 2.3 Types of DNS Servers

| Server Type | Function | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **Root Name Server** | Returns addresses of TLD servers | Global (13 logical clusters) |
| **TLD Server** | Returns addresses of authoritative servers for a TLD | Per TLD (`.com`, `.in`) |
| **Authoritative Name Server** | Holds actual DNS records for a zone | Per domain |
| **Local / Caching Resolver** | Recursive queries on behalf of clients; caches results | ISP / Enterprise / OS-level |
| **Secondary / Slave Server** | Backup authoritative server via zone transfer | Per domain |

### 2.4 DNS Resolution Modes

#### A. Iterative Query (Referral-based)

The contacted server returns the **best answer it has** — either the actual record or a referral to another server. The client (resolver) then queries the next server itself.

#### B. Recursive Query ("Do it for me")

The contacted server takes full responsibility and returns the final answer, querying other servers internally if needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Examination Note:** Recursive queries are typically between **client ↔ local resolver**; iterative queries are typically between **local resolver ↔ root/TLD/authoritative servers**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Formula | Value / Expression | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Default DNS Query Port (UDP) | **53** | Port Number |
| DNS over TCP Port | **53** | Port Number |
| Standard UDP Query Size Limit | **512** | Bytes |
| Maximum Label Length | **63** | Characters |
| Maximum FQDN Length | **255** | Characters |
| Number of Logical Root Servers | **13** (A–M) | Clusters |
| DNS Message Header Size (Fixed) | **12** | Bytes |
| Time-To-Live (TTL) Range | **0** to **2,147,483,647** (2^31 − 1) | Seconds |
| DNS Protocol Type | **UDP** (with TCP fallback) | Layer 4 |

### 2.6 DNS Resource Records (RR) — The Database Schema

DNS stores all mappings as **Resource Records (RRs)**. Each RR has the format:

$$RR = \{ \text{Name}, \text{Type}, \text{Class}, \text{TTL}, \text{RDATA} \}$$

| Record Type | Code | Purpose | Example RDATA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 1 | Maps hostname → IPv4 address | `103.25.61.18` |
| **AAAA** | 28 | Maps hostname → IPv6 address | `2001:db8::1` |
| **CNAME** | 5 | Canonical name alias | `www.ktu.edu.in → ktu.edu.in` |
| **MX** | 15 | Mail exchange server (with priority) | `10 mail.ktu.edu.in` |
| **NS** | 2 | Authoritative name server for zone | `ns1.ktu.edu.in` |
| **PTR** | 12 | Reverse lookup (IP → name) | Used in `in-addr.arpa` |
| **SOA** | 6 | Start of Authority — zone metadata | Primary NS, admin email, serial, timers |
| **TXT** | 16 | Arbitrary text (SPF, DKIM, verification) | `"v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all"` |
| **SRV** | 33 | Service locator | `_sip._tcp.example.com` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOA Record Highlight:** Every zone **must** have exactly **one** SOA record defining the zone's serial number, refresh interval, retry interval, expire time, and minimum TTL.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

DNS is critical in modern engineering ecosystems:

- **Cloud Load Balancing:** Round-robin DNS distributes traffic across multiple servers.
- **Content Delivery Networks (CDN):** Akamai, Cloudflare use DNS to route users to nearest edge server using **GeoDNS**.
- **Email Delivery:** MX records determine mail-routing path; failure to configure MX correctly means lost emails.
- **Service Discovery:** In microservices (Kubernetes), internal DNS (CoreDNS) replaces hard-coded IPs.
- **Cybersecurity:** DNS is heavily exploited in **DNS tunneling**, **DDoS amplification**, and **cache poisoning attacks**, making DNS security (DNSSEC) a vital engineering concern.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed DNS Resolution Walkthrough (Worked Analytical Example)

Let us trace the complete resolution of the FQDN `www.engineering.ktu.edu.in` initiated by a user in Kerala whose local DNS resolver is at `8.8.8.8` (Google Public DNS).

> [!NOTE]
> The following step-by-step logic forms a **commonly tested 14-mark question** in KTU ESE Module 4.

**Initial State:**
- User enters `http://www.engineering.ktu.edu.in` in browser.
- The browser asks the operating system's **stub resolver** to resolve the name.

---

**Step 1 — Stub Resolver → Local Recursive Resolver**

- The OS stub resolver sends a recursive DNS query to the local resolver (`8.8.8.8`).
- Query Packet: `QNAME = www.engineering.ktu.edu.in`, `QTYPE = A`, `QCLASS = IN`.

**Step 2 — Local Resolver → Root Name Server (Iterative Query)**

- The local resolver queries one of the 13 root servers, say `a.root-servers.net` (`198.41.0.4`).
- Since the local resolver has no cached entry, this is a fresh query.

**Step 3 — Root Server Response (Referral)**

- The root server **does not know** the IP for `www.engineering.ktu.edu.in`.
- It replies with a **referral** to the `.in` TLD servers:

$$RR_{\text{referral}} = \{ \text{NS Record for ".in"} \rightarrow \{a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h,i\}\text{.registry.in} \}$$

**Step 4 — Local Resolver → `.in` TLD Server (Iterative)**

- The resolver contacts, say, `a.inregistry.in` and queries again.
- The TLD server replies with a **referral** to the authoritative server for `edu.in`:

$$RR_{\text{referral}} = \{ \text{NS Record for "edu.in"} \rightarrow \{\text{ns1.ernet.in}, \dots\} \}$$

**Step 5 — Local Resolver → Authoritative Server for `ktu.edu.in`**

- The resolver queries the authoritative name server of `ktu.edu.in`.
- This server **has the actual record** and responds:

$$RR_{\text{Answer}} = \{ \text{Name: } www.engineering.ktu.edu.in, \text{Type: A}, \text{RDATA: } 103.25.61.18, \text{TTL: } 3600 \}$$

**Step 6 — Caching at the Local Resolver**

- The local resolver stores the mapping with TTL = **3600 seconds (1 hour)**.
- Any subsequent query for the same name within 1 hour skips the entire resolution process.

**Step 7 — Response to Client**

- The local resolver returns the IP `103.25.61.18` to the stub resolver.
- The browser opens a TCP connection to `103.25.61.18:80` (or `:443` for HTTPS).

---

**Total Round-Trip Time (RTT) Analysis:**

Let:
- $T_1$ = RTT from client to local resolver
- $T_2$ = RTT from local resolver to root server
- $T_3$ = RTT from local resolver to `.in` TLD server
- $T_4$ = RTT from local resolver to authoritative server

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= T_1 + (T_2 + T_3 + T_4) + T_1 \\
T_{\text{total}} &= 2T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example numerical evaluation (typical Internet RTTs):**
- $T_1 = 5$ ms (LAN)
- $T_2 = 80$ ms (root server, transcontinental)
- $T_3 = 100$ ms (.in TLD)
- $T_4 = 50$ ms (ktu.edu.in authoritative)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= 2(5) + 80 + 100 + 50 \\
&= 10 + 230 \\
&= \mathbf{240 \text{ ms}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why **DNS caching at multiple levels** (browser, OS, ISP) is critical — it converts this 240 ms lookup into a near-zero lookup for repeat visits.

### 3.2 Reverse DNS Lookup (PTR Query) — Analytical Walkthrough

Reverse DNS converts an IP back to a name using the special domain `in-addr.arpa` (for IPv4) or `ip6.arpa` (for IPv6).

To resolve `103.25.61.18`:

1. Reverse the octets: `18.61.25.103`
2. Append `.in-addr.arpa`: `18.61.25.103.in-addr.arpa`
3. Query the **PTR record** of this name.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Query} &\rightarrow \text{PTR(18.61.25.103.in-addr.arpa)} \\
\text{Response} &\rightarrow \text{PTR: www.engineering.ktu.edu.in}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 DNS Message Format (Header Section)

Every DNS message begins with a **12-byte fixed header**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Header (12 bytes)} &= \underbrace{\text{ID}}_{2B} + \underbrace{\text{Flags}}_{2B} + \underbrace{\text{QDCOUNT}}_{2B} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\text{ANCOUNT}}_{2B} + \underbrace{\text{NSCOUNT}}_{2B} + \underbrace{\text{ARCOUNT}}_{2B}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Flags** field (2 bytes) contains:
- **QR** (1 bit) — 0 = Query, 1 = Response
- **Opcode** (4 bits) — Standard query (0), Inverse query (1), Status (2)
- **AA** (1 bit) — Authoritative Answer flag
- **TC** (1 bit) — Truncation flag (use TCP if set)
- **RD** (1 bit) — Recursion Desired
- **RA** (1 bit) — Recursion Available
- **Z** (3 bits) — Reserved (must be 0)
- **RCODE** (4 bits) — Response code (0 = No error, 3 = NXDOMAIN, etc.)

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Recursive vs Iterative Queries

| Parameter | Recursive Query | Iterative Query |
|---|---|---|
| Who does the work? | The contacted server | The client/resolver |
| Returns final answer? | **Yes** | **No** — returns referral or answer |
| Load on contacted server | High (must resolve fully) | Low (one referral only) |
| Typically used between | Client ↔ Local Resolver | Resolver ↔ Root/TLD/Authoritative |
| Failure handling | Server returns error directly | Resolver tries next referral |
| Caching | Often cached at the resolver | Each level may cache independently |

### 3.5 DNS Caching Time Calculation

If a resolver caches a record with TTL = $T$ seconds, the cached entry is valid for:

$$
T_{\text{valid}} = T - t_{\text{elapsed}}
$$

Where $t_{\text{elapsed}}$ is the time since the entry was cached. Once $T_{\text{valid}} \leq 0$, the entry is **expired** and a fresh resolution is triggered.

**Example:**
- An `A` record cached at 10:00 AM with TTL = 3600 s.
- Query arrives at 11:30 AM (90 minutes = 5400 s elapsed).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{valid}} &= 3600 - 5400 \\
&= -1800 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $T_{\text{valid}} < 0$, the cache has **expired**, and the resolver must perform a fresh iterative resolution.

### 3.6 DNSSEC Trust Chain (Brief)

DNSSEC (DNS Security Extensions) uses **public-key cryptography** to authenticate DNS responses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parent Zone Signs Child Zone's DS Record} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Resolver Validates Signature Chain to Root} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Trust Anchor (Root KSK) Validates Entire Chain}
\end{aligned}
$$

This prevents **cache poisoning** and **man-in-the-middle attacks** on DNS.
