# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — SNMP Message Format (SNMPv1)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often tests whether students can decode the byte layout of an SNMP packet. Here is the **complete structure with every field** traced:

```
SNMP Message (BER-encoded)
├── SEQUENCE (Tag 0x30)
│   ├── VERSION            (INTEGER 0=SNMPv1, 1=SNMPv2c, 2=SNMPv3)
│   ├── COMMUNITY STRING   (OCTET STRING — e.g., "public")
│   └── PDU                (Context-specific, constructed)
│       ├── request-id     (INTEGER — matches request to response)
│       ├── error-status   (INTEGER — 0=noError, 1=tooBig, 2=noSuchName, ...)
│       ├── error-index    (INTEGER — which varbind caused the error)
│       └── Variable Bindings (SEQUENCE OF)
│           ├── name  (OBJECT IDENTIFIER — the OID requested)
│           └── value (variable type — NULL for GET/SET requests,
│                                       actual value for RESPONSE)
```

### 3.1.1 Byte-Level Walkthrough of a GET-REQUEST

Suppose the manager sends: `GetRequest for sysDescr.0 (OID 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0)`

$$
\text{SNMP Message} = \underbrace{0x30}_{\text{SEQUENCE tag}} \, \vert \, \underbrace{\text{Len}}_{\text{Total length}} \, \vert \, \underbrace{0x02\,0x01\,0x00}_{\text{version = 0 (v1)}} \, \vert \, \underbrace{0x04\,0x06\,\text{"public"}}_{\text{community}} \, \vert \, \underbrace{0xA0}_{\text{GET-REQUEST tag}} \, \vert \, \dots
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the outermost ASN.1 wrapper.**

$$
\text{Tag byte} = 0x30 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Universal (00), Constructed (1), Tag number 16 (10)} = \text{SEQUENCE}
$$

This tells the receiver: "What follows is an ordered sequence of fields."

**Step 2 — Read the Length byte.**

If the length is < 128, it is encoded directly. Otherwise, the long-form bit (bit 7) is set and the remaining bits give the number of subsequent length bytes. For our small example, length is something like `0x26` (38 bytes).

**Step 3 — Decode the `version` integer.**

$$
\text{Bytes: } 02\,01\,00 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Tag } 02 = \text{INTEGER}, \; \text{Length} = 1, \; \text{Value} = 0 \Rightarrow \text{SNMPv1}
$$

**Step 4 — Decode the community string.**

$$
\text{Bytes: } 04\,06\,70\,75\,62\,6C\,69\,63 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Tag } 04 = \text{OCTET STRING}, \; \text{Length} = 6, \; \text{Value} = \text{"public"} = [0x70, 0x75, 0x62, 0x6C, 0x69, 0x63]
$$

**Step 5 — Decode the PDU.**

$$
\text{Tag } 0xA0 = \underbrace{10}_{\text{context-specific}} \;\underbrace{1}_{\text{constructed}} \;\underbrace{00000}_{\text{tag \# = 0}} = \text{GET-REQUEST}
$$

**Step 6 — Decode `request-id`, `error-status`, `error-index`.**

Each is a 3-byte BER integer (`02 01 xx`):

| Field | Encoding | Value |
|---|---|---|
| request-id | `02 01 01` | 1 |
| error-status | `02 01 00` | 0 (noError) |
| error-index | `02 01 00` | 0 |

**Step 7 — Decode the VarBind list (SEQUENCE OF).**

Outer wrapper: `30 [len]` (SEQUENCE OF). Inside, each VarBind is a SEQUENCE:

$$
\text{VarBind} = 30\,[len]\,06\,[OID-len]\,\underbrace{2B\,06\,01\,02\,01\,01\,01\,00}_{1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0}\,05\,00
$$

The trailing `05 00` is **NULL** — the value placeholder for a GET request (since we are asking, not providing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NULL?** Because in a GET request, the manager is *asking* for the value. The agent fills it in the RESPONSE. In a SET request, the manager would put the new value here (instead of NULL).

### 3.2 Worked Example — Counter32 Wrap-Around Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions in performance-management sections use the wrap-around formula. Let's solve a typical numerical.

**Problem:** A router's `ifInOctets` is a 32-bit counter. The interface runs at **1 Gbps**. How long does it take for the counter to wrap around to zero?

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Max counter value} = 2^{32} - 1 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}295 \;\text{octets}
$$

Converting to bits (1 octet = 8 bits):

$$
\text{Max bits} = (2^{32} - 1) \times 8 \approx 3.436 \times 10^{10} \;\text{bits}
$$

Interface rate: $R = 1 \,\text{Gbps} = 10^9 \,\text{bits/sec}$.

$$
T_{wrap} = \frac{(2^{32}-1) \times 8}{10^9} \approx \frac{3.436 \times 10^{10}}{10^9} \approx 34.36 \;\text{seconds}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is **only ~34 seconds** — far too short for a busy 1 Gbps link. This is exactly why **Counter64** was introduced in SNMPv2c: it would wrap in $\frac{(2^{64}-1) \times 8}{10^9} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ seconds ≈ **1170 years**. Hence the modern `ifXTable` uses `ifHCInOctets` (High-Capacity, 64-bit).

### 3.3 Worked Example — Throughput from Two Polls

**Problem:** Two SNMP GETs on `ifInOctets` of a 100 Mbps interface returned:
* $C_{t_1} = 5{,}200{,}000$ octets at $t_1 = 14:00:00$
* $C_{t_2} = 5{,}824{,}000$ octets at $t_2 = 14:00:30$

Find the average input throughput in **Mbps** and the utilization %.

**Solution:**

$$
\Delta C = C_{t_2} - C_{t_1} = 5{,}824{,}000 - 5{,}200{,}000 = 624{,}000 \;\text{octets}
$$

$$
\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = 30 \;\text{seconds}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{624{,}000 \times 8}{30} = 166{,}400 \;\text{bits/sec} = 0.1664 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

$$
U\% = \frac{0.1664}{100} \times 100 = 0.1664\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the standard method by which SNMP-based tools (Cacti, MRTG, PRTG) draw traffic graphs — by polling the same counter twice and dividing the difference by the time interval.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which PDU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is a common KTU 14-mark question topic ("Explain the different SNMP operations with appropriate scenarios").

| Goal | PDU to use | Direction | Port |
|---|---|---|---|
| Read a single known variable | **GetRequest** | Manager → Agent | 161 |
| Walk an entire MIB subtree (e.g., `ifTable`) | **GetNextRequest** (or `snmpwalk`) | Manager → Agent | 161 |
| Read many variables in one round-trip (large tables) | **GetBulkRequest** | Manager → Agent | 161 |
| Modify a device's configuration remotely | **SetRequest** | Manager → Agent | 161 |
| Receive agent's reply to any request | **Response** | Agent → Manager | (ephemeral src, dst 161) |
| Notify manager of an event (best-effort) | **Trap (v1)** or **SNMPv2-Trap** | Agent → Manager | 162 |
| Notify manager reliably (must be acked) | **InformRequest** | Agent → Manager | 162 |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define SNMP. List any four SNMP operations.**

**Model Answer:**

> **SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol)** is an application-layer protocol (RFC 3411–3418) used for monitoring and managing network devices. It operates over UDP ports **161** (manager-to-agent) and **162** (agent-to-manager).

Four SNMP operations:

| # | Operation | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GetRequest** | Manager → Agent | Read value of a specific MIB variable |
| 2 | **GetNextRequest** | Manager → Agent | Read next sequential variable (used in MIB walking) |
| 3 | **SetRequest** | Manager → Agent | Modify/write a variable on the agent |
| 4 | **Trap** | Agent → Manager | Asynchronous notification of an event (e.g., link failure) |

*(Additional valid operations: GetBulkRequest, GetResponse, InformRequest.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between MIB and OID. State the role of the community string in SNMPv1.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **MIB (Management Information Base)** | **OID (Object Identifier)** |
|---|---|---|
| What it is | A standardized collection / schema of managed objects | A unique dotted-decimal address that identifies a single managed object |
| Nature | A **module/file** defining many variables | A **single node's address** in the global ISO tree |
| Example | MIB-II (RFC 1213), ENTITY-MIB, IF-MIB | `1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10` (= `ifInOctets`) |
| Format | Human-readable ASN.1 module | Sequence of integers separated by dots |

**Role of Community String (SNMPv1):**
The community string acts as a **plain-text password** between manager and agent. The default well-known strings are:
* **public** — read-only access (GET, GET-NEXT)
* **private** — read-write access (GET, SET)

It is sent in every SNMPv1 message in clear text — a major security weakness that SNMPv3 later fixed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **SNMP architecture** in detail. Identify the roles of **Manager, Agent, and MIB**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **SNMP message format (SNMPv1)** with the structure of a **GET-REQUEST PDU**. Explain the function of each field. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a)

The **SNMP architecture** is a distributed **manager–agent model** consisting of four logical parts:

1. **Network Management Station (Manager / NMS):** A workstation running management software (e.g., SolarWinds, Nagios). It is the **control center** that initiates all polling and displays the collected data.
2. **Managed Devices (Agents):** Routers, switches, servers, printers, etc. Each contains an **SNMP agent** — a software process that maintains local management data and responds to manager requests.
3. **Management Information Base (MIB):** A **structured collection of managed objects**, defined using ASN.1, that the agent exposes to the manager. MIB-II (RFC 1213) is the standard version.
4. **SNMP Protocol:** The application-layer protocol (UDP-based) carrying messages between manager and agent.

**Communication flow:**

* **Polling (synchronous):** Manager → UDP/161 → Agent; Agent → UDP/161 → Manager.
* **Trap (asynchronous):** Agent → UDP/162 → Manager.

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b)

An SNMPv1 message is a single **SEQUENCE** containing three fields:

```
SNMP Message ::= SEQUENCE {
    version     INTEGER,
    community   OCTET STRING,
    data        ANY   -- contains the PDU
}
```

The PDU for **GET-REQUEST** has the following structure:

```
GetRequest-PDU ::= [0] IMPLICIT SEQUENCE {
    request-id    INTEGER,
    error-status  INTEGER (0 = noError in request),
    error-index   INTEGER (0 = no error in request),
    variable-bindings SEQUENCE OF VarBind
}

VarBind ::= SEQUENCE {
    name  OBJECT IDENTIFIER,
    value ObjectSyntax  -- NULL in a GET request
}
```

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `version` | Protocol version: 0 = SNMPv1 |
| `community` | Plain-text password (e.g., `public`) |
| `request-id` | A sequence number used by the manager to match a response to its request |
| `error-status` | In GET-REQUEST this is 0; in RESPONSE it indicates success/failure (1=tooBig, 2=noSuchName, 3=badValue, 4=readOnly, 5=genErr) |
| `error-index` | In RESPONSE, points to which variable in the varbind list caused the error |
| `variable-bindings` | A list of `{name, value}` pairs — the OIDs being queried; the value is NULL in a GET request |

The whole message is encoded using **BER (Basic Encoding Rules)** of ASN.1 — each element becomes a **TLV (Tag-Length-Value)** triplet.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] — Alternate Choice**

**(a)** Compare **SNMPv1, SNMPv2c, and SNMPv3** with respect to security, features, and PDUs. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **MIB-II** with its major object groups. Show the **OID tree** for `system` and `interfaces` groups. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a)

| Feature | SNMPv1 | SNMPv2c | SNMPv3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| RFC | 1155, 1157, 1212, 1213 | 1901–1908 | 3411–3418 |
| Security | Plain-text community string | Plain-text community string | USM with MD5/SHA auth + DES/AES privacy |
| GET-BULK | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| INFORM | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Trap PDU format | Special format (`0xA4`) | Unified with other PDUs (`0xA7`) | Same as v2c |
| 64-bit counters | ❌ | ✅ (`Counter64`) | ✅ |
| Authentication | None | None | ✅ HMAC-MD5 / HMAC-SHA |
| Encryption | None | None | ✅ CBC-DES / CFB-AES |
| Use today | Legacy / rare | Widely deployed | **Standard for new deployments** |

**Conclusion:** SNMPv3 is the most secure and feature-complete; SNMPv1/v2c are unsuitable for untrusted networks because community strings traverse the network in plaintext.

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b)

**MIB-II (RFC 1213)** organizes the standard set of managed objects into **ten groups**, each representing a protocol layer or system attribute:

| Group | OID | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `system` | `1.3.6.1.2.1.1` | Host/router info: `sysDescr`, `sysObjectID`, `sysUpTime`, `sysContact`, `sysName`, `sysLocation`, `sysServices` |
| `interfaces` | `1.3.6.1.2.1.2` | Per-interface counters: `ifNumber`, `ifTable` (with `ifIndex`, `ifDescr`, `ifType`, `ifSpeed`, `ifInOctets`, `ifOutOctets`, `ifAdminStatus`, `ifOperStatus`) |
| `at` (address translation) | `1.3.6.1.2.1.3` | Deprecated — maps Network → Physical addresses |
| `ip` | `1.3.6.1.2.1.4` | IP statistics and routing table |
| `icmp` | `1.3.6.1.2.1.5` | ICMP counters |
| `tcp` | `1.3.6.1.2.1.6` | TCP connection table, retransmissions |
| `udp` | `1.3.6.1.2.1.7` | UDP datagram statistics |
| `egp` | `1.3.6.1.2.1.8` | Exterior Gateway Protocol (obsolete) |
| `cmot` | `1.3.6.1.2.1.9` | CMIP over TCP (rarely used) |
| `snmp` | `1.3.6.1.2.1.11` | SNMP traffic statistics |

**OID tree for `system` and `interfaces`:**

```
1.3.6.1.2.1     mib-2
├── 1.3.6.1.2.1.1       system
│   ├── 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1   sysDescr           (OCTET STRING)
│   ├── 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.2   sysObjectID        (OID)
│   ├── 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3   sysUpTime          (TimeTicks)
│   ├── 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.4   sysContact
│   ├── 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5   sysName
│   ├── 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.6   sysLocation
│   └── 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.7   sysServices
│
└── 1.3.6.1.2.1.2       interfaces
    ├── 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.1   ifNumber           (INTEGER — count of interfaces)
    └── 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2   ifTable
        └── 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1   ifEntry
            ├── ...ifEntry.1    ifIndex
            ├── ...ifEntry.2    ifDescr
            ├── ...ifEntry.3    ifType
            ├── ...ifEntry.5    ifSpeed
            ├── ...ifEntry.10   ifInOctets
            ├── ...ifEntry.16   ifOutOctets
            ├── ...ifEntry.7    ifAdminStatus
            └── ...ifEntry.8    ifOperStatus
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing port numbers:** Students frequently write "manager listens on 162 and agent on 161." Correct: BOTH manager-to-agent **requests** and agent-to-manager **responses** use **port 161** (the agent listens on 161; the manager picks an ephemeral source port and replies to it). Only **traps/informs** go to port **162**.
> 2. **Forgetting that SNMP uses UDP, not TCP.** Writing "TCP port 161" loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing MIB with a real database.** The MIB is a **schema**; the actual values live in agent memory. Writing "MIB stores all the polled data" is incorrect.
> 4. **Treating SNMPv2 and SNMPv2c as the same.** v2 (RFC 1441) failed in the IETF and was replaced by **v2c** which reverted to community strings. If asked, mention both.
> 5. **Omitting the BER encoding step.** Whenever describing SNMP messages, mention **BER-encoded TLV structure** — it is a frequently tested sub-point.
> 6. **Writing community string as "encrypted" or "hashed."** It is **plain text** — that is precisely why SNMPv3 was designed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SNMP — Final Rapid-Fire Revision List (print this before the exam):**

- ✅ SNMP is an **application-layer** protocol running over **UDP** (not TCP).
- ✅ **Port 161** = manager ↔ agent request/response traffic.
- ✅ **Port 162** = manager receives asynchronous **TRAP / INFORM** messages.
- ✅ SNMP has **four architectural parts**: Manager, Agent, MIB, SMI — remember SMI as the *type-system* that defines MIB.
- ✅ **SNMPv1** PDUs: GetRequest, GetNextRequest, SetRequest, GetResponse, Trap.
- ✅ **SNMPv2c** adds **GetBulkRequest**, **InformRequest**, and unified Trap format.
- ✅ **SNMPv3** adds **USM** with **HMAC-MD5/SHA** authentication and **DES/AES** privacy; recommended for all new deployments.
- ✅ **Community string** = plain-text password. Defaults: `public` (RO), `private` (RW). Case-sensitive.
- ✅ **MIB-II** is defined by **RFC 1213**; groups are `system`, `interfaces`, `ip`, `icmp`, `tcp`, `udp`, `snmp`, etc.
- ✅ **OID** is a globally unique dotted-decimal address in the ISO tree. The standard MIB-II subtree is `1.3.6.1.2.1`.
- ✅ **Common data types:** INTEGER, OCTET STRING, Counter32/64, Gauge32, TimeTicks (10 ms), IpAddress, OBJECT IDENTIFIER.
- ✅ **Counter32 wraps** in $\approx 34$ seconds at 1 Gbps — reason **Counter64** was introduced.
- ✅ **Throughput formula:** $\dfrac{(C_{t_2} - C_{t_1}) \times 8}{t_2 - t_1}$ bits/sec.
- ✅ **BER encoding** = **TLV** (Tag-Length-Value), recursive, ASN.1-based.
- ✅ **Trap** = best-effort, no acknowledgement; **Inform** = reliable, manager must ACK.
- ✅ Polling is **synchronous and periodic**; traps are **asynchronous and event-driven**.
- ✅ SNMP manager software examples: **Nagios, Zabbix, PRTG, SolarWinds, Cacti, MRTG**.
- ✅ Standard SNMP tools: `snmpget`, `snmpwalk`, `snmpset`, `snmptrap`, `snmpbulkget`.
- ✅ Final mantra: **"SNMP = GET/SET + MIB + BER + UDP"** — if your answer covers all four, you have answered well.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Wide Web (WWW) - Architectural Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT751 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** World Wide Web (WWW) - Architectural Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

The **World Wide Web (WWW)** is a distributed, hypermedia-based information system that operates over the Internet and enables the retrieval, linking, and presentation of resources (text, images, audio, video) through a unified addressing and transport framework. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, the WWW is fundamentally an *application-layer* system whose architecture is composed of four tightly coupled pillars:

1. **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** — the request-response application protocol.
2. **Uniform Resource Locator / Identifier (URL / URI)** — the global naming and addressing scheme.
3. **HyperText Markup Language (HTML)** — the document representation and rendering language.
4. **Web Client (Browser)** and **Web Server** — the end-systems exchanging the resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Anchor:** The WWW is **not** the Internet itself. The Internet is the *network infrastructure*; the WWW is an *application service* that runs on top of it (alongside e-mail, FTP, DNS, etc.).

## 1.2 The Four Architectural Pillars at a Glance

| Pillar | Role | Default Port | Standardized By |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP / HTTPS** | Communication protocol (request–response) | **80 / 443** | IETF (RFC 9110, 9112) |
| **URL / URI** | Resource addressing & identification | — | IETF (RFC 3986) |
| **HTML** | Document formatting & hyperlinking | — | W3C / WHATWG |
| **Browser ↔ Web Server** | Client-server end-systems | — | De-facto market standards |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Library on a Global Telephone Network"

Imagine a **gigantic, planet-wide library** where:

- **The Library Catalog Number** of every book is its **URL** (e.g., `https://libraryktu.ac.in/books/computer_networks/page42.html`).
- **You, the Reader**, send a written request slip through a **telephone courier** — that is **HTTP**.
- **The Librarian (Web Server)** walks into the stacks, finds the book (HTML file, image, video), and sends it back through the same telephone courier.
- **HTML** is the formatting language the librarian uses so the book arrives in a readable, page-numbered, cross-referenced form.
- The **telephone exchange** through which your request travels is the **Internet** (with TCP providing reliable delivery and IP providing addressing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy works:** The WWW architecture is essentially **addressable (URL)** + **retrievable (HTTP)** + **presentable (HTML)**. Remove any one of the three and the system collapses.

## 1.4 The "Web" vs. "Internet" vs. "Intranet" Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Confusing Triad (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva):**
> - **Internet** = Global network of networks (infrastructure).
> - **WWW** = A *service* that runs *on* the Internet.
> - **Intranet** = A *private* WWW-like service, restricted to an organization, using the same protocols but not publicly routable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components — Layered Functional View

The WWW architecture can be broken into **six functional blocks**, each with a sharply defined role.

### 2.1.1 Resource (the "What")
Anything that can be named by a URI — a static HTML file, a dynamically generated PHP page, an image, a PDF, a JSON API response, or a streaming video.

### 2.1.2 Client / Browser (the "Who Asks")
Software on the user end. Responsibilities: resolve the URL, open a TCP connection, send HTTP requests, parse HTML, render, manage cookies, execute JavaScript.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples:** Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Safari, Microsoft Edge, Brave. Internally, every modern browser contains an HTTP engine, an HTML parser, a CSS engine, a JavaScript engine (e.g., V8), and a rendering engine (e.g., Blink, WebKit, Gecko).

### 2.1.3 Proxy / Cache / Gateway (the "Middleman")
Sits between client and origin server. Functions: caching, filtering, anonymization, load balancing, security scanning, content adaptation.

### 2.1.4 Origin Web Server (the "Who Answers")
Hosts the resources. Typical stack: **Apache HTTP Server**, **Nginx**, **Microsoft IIS**, or Node.js-based servers. Serves HTTP responses with appropriate headers.

### 2.1.5 Backend / Database (the "Where Data Lives")
For dynamic content, the web server forwards a request to an application server (e.g., Tomcat, Django, Express.js) which queries a database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB).

### 2.1.6 Hypertext & Markup (the "How It Looks")
HTML + CSS + JavaScript define the visual and interactive presentation. Multimedia objects are referenced via embedded URLs and fetched in additional HTTP requests.

## 2.2 The URL — Formal Grammar

A **URL (Uniform Resource Locator)** follows the generic syntax:

$$\text{URL} = \underbrace{\text{scheme}}_{\text{protocol}} \text{://} \underbrace{\text{authority}}_{\text{host[:port][userinfo]}} \underbrace{\text{/path}}_{\text{hier-part}} \underbrace{\text{?query}_{\text{optional}}_{\text{optional}} \underbrace{\text{#fragment}}_{\text{optional}}$$

**Field-by-field meaning:**

| Component | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **scheme** | Protocol used to fetch the resource | `http`, `https`, `ftp`, `mailto`, `file` |
| **authority** | Server identity (and optional credentials) | `www.ktu.edu.in:8080` |
| **host** | DNS name or IP of the server | `www.ktu.edu.in` |
| **port** | TCP port (default if omitted: 80 for http, 443 for https) | `8080` |
| **userinfo** | Optional `user:password@` (deprecated in browsers) | `admin:secret123@` |
| **path** | Hierarchical resource location on the server | `/courses/pemrt751/notes.html` |
| **query** | Parameters sent to the server | `?sem=7&module=4` |
| **fragment** | In-page anchor on the client side | `#section-2` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fragment** is **never sent to the server** — it is interpreted entirely by the client browser to scroll to a specific in-page location.

## 2.3 HTTP — The Backbone Protocol

HTTP is a **stateless, request-response, text-based (HTTP/1.1) or binary (HTTP/2, HTTP/3)** application-layer protocol defined initially in **RFC 2616** and currently in **RFC 9110 / 9112**.

### 2.3.1 HTTP Request Methods (Verbs)

| Method | Idempotent? | Safe? | Body? | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GET** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Retrieve a resource |
| **HEAD** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Same as GET but response body omitted |
| **POST** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | Submit data, create new resource |
| **PUT** | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Replace a resource entirely |
| **PATCH** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | Apply a partial modification |
| **DELETE** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Remove a resource |
| **OPTIONS** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Discover allowed methods (CORS preflight) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Idempotent = repeating the request has the same effect as doing it once.** **Safe = it does not modify server state.** These two properties are heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 2.3.2 HTTP Response Status Codes — Five Classes

| Class | Range | Meaning | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Provisional response | `100 Continue` |
| **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request accepted & processed | `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content` |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action needed | `301 Moved Permanently`, `302 Found`, `304 Not Modified` |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Faulty request from client | `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `403 Forbidden`, `404 Not Found` |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed to fulfill valid request | `500 Internal Server Error`, `502 Bad Gateway`, `503 Service Unavailable` |

## 2.4 Connection Styles — Persistent vs Non-Persistent

### 2.4.1 Non-Persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.0 default)
A **separate TCP connection** is opened for *each* object. After the response, the connection is closed by TCP FIN.

### 2.4.2 Persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.1 default)
A **single TCP connection** carries *multiple* requests and responses in sequence, or even in parallel (pipelining / multiplexing in HTTP/2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HTTP/1.1 made persistent connections the **default** because the TCP handshake cost (one RTT) and slow-start penalty make opening new connections per object prohibitively expensive.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Performance Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Round Trip Time (RTT) | $\text{RTT} = 2 \cdot t_p$ where $t_p$ = one-way propagation | seconds | Assumes symmetric path |
| 2 | Non-persistent response time (1 object) | $T_{np} = 2 \cdot \text{RTT} + \text{RTT} + \dfrac{F}{R}$ | seconds | $2\text{RTT}$ for TCP handshake, $1\text{RTT}$ for HTTP request/response, $F/R$ for file transfer |
| 3 | Non-persistent response time (n objects, sequential) | $T_{np}(n) = n \cdot \left(3\text{RTT} + \dfrac{F}{R}\right)$ | seconds | Worst case |
| 4 | Non-persistent with k parallel connections (HTTP/1.0 browsers) | $T_{np}^{(k)}(n) = \left\lceil \dfrac{n}{k}\right\rceil \cdot \left(3\text{RTT} + \dfrac{F}{R}\right) + \text{overhead}$ | seconds | Most browsers used $k = 6$ historically |
| 5 | Persistent response time (n objects) | $T_{p}(n) = 2\text{RTT} + n \cdot \left(\text{RTT} + \dfrac{F}{R}\right)$ | seconds | Single TCP setup amortized |
| 6 | Pipelined persistent (HTTP/1.1) | $T_{pipe}(n) \approx 2\text{RTT} + \text{RTT} + n \cdot \dfrac{F}{R}$ | seconds | Burst of requests, burst of responses |
| 7 | HTTP/2 multiplexed | $T_{mux}(n) \approx 2\text{RTT} + \text{RTT} + \dfrac{F}{R}$ | seconds | Single TCP, frame-interleaved streams |
| 8 | Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{F}{T_{total}}$ | bits/second | Used for link-utilization analysis |
| 9 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = R \cdot \text{RTT}$ | bits | Critical memory = pipe capacity |
| 10 | Utilization (stop-and-wait) | $U = \dfrac{\dfrac{F}{R}}{\dfrac{F}{R} + \text{RTT}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ where $a = \dfrac{\text{RTT} \cdot R}{F}$ | ratio (0–1) | Classic Tanenbaum formula |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CDN design** — The persistent vs non-persistent tradeoff directly motivates Content Delivery Networks (Akamai, Cloudflare), which terminate TCP close to the user.
- **Web Performance Optimization (WPO)** — Google's Core Web Vitals use response time formulas like $T_p$ to score pages.
- **TLS handshake cost** — Adding HTTPS roughly doubles the initial RTT, making persistent connections even more important.
- **API design** — Modern REST/GraphQL APIs follow the same HTTP verb + status-code paradigm, so understanding HTTP/1.1 is essential for backend engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — URL Parsing

**Problem.** Parse the following URL into its grammatical components:

$$\text{URL} = \text{https://admin:pwd@www.ktu.edu.in:8080/courses/pemrt751/notes.html?sem=7\&module=4\#sec3}$$

**Step-by-step decomposition.**

- **Step 1 — Identify the scheme.** The substring before `://` is the scheme.
$$\text{scheme} = \text{https}$$
  *Logic:* The grammar is `scheme ":" "//" authority path? "?" query? "#" fragment?`.

- **Step 2 — Identify the authority.** Everything between `://` and the next `/`.
$$\text{authority} = \text{admin:pwd@www.ktu.edu.in:8080}$$
  *Logic:* Authority begins at the first character after `//` and ends at the first `/`, `?`, or `#`.

- **Step 3 — Split the authority into userinfo, host, port.**
$$\text{userinfo} = \text{admin:pwd}, \quad \text{host} = \text{www.ktu.edu.in}, \quad \text{port} = 8080$$
  *Logic:* Userinfo is terminated by `@`. Host is followed by `:`. Port is a decimal integer (1–65535).

- **Step 4 — Identify the path.** From the first `/` after authority up to `?` or `#`.
$$\text{path} = \text{/courses/pemrt751/notes.html}$$
  *Logic:* Hierarchical path, components separated by `/`.

- **Step 5 — Identify the query.** From `?` up to `#`.
$$\text{query} = \text{sem=7\&module=4}$$
  *Logic:* Query is `key=value` pairs joined by `&`.

- **Step 6 — Identify the fragment.** From `#` to the end.
$$\text{fragment} = \text{sec3}$$
  *Logic:* Fragment is *not* transmitted to the server.

- **Step 7 — Final canonical form (table):**

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| scheme | `https` |
| userinfo | `admin:pwd` |
| host | `www.ktu.edu.in` |
| port | `8080` |
| path | `/courses/pemrt751/notes.html` |
| query | `sem=7&module=4` |
| fragment | `sec3` |

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Non-Persistent vs Persistent HTTP Timing

**Problem (classic KTU numerical).**
A web page consists of **1 base HTML file** and **9 referenced image objects**, each of size $F = 100$ KB. The end-to-end RTT is $\text{RTT} = 50$ ms and the bottleneck link bandwidth is $R = 1$ Gbps. Compute the total response time using (a) non-persistent HTTP with 6 parallel connections and (b) persistent HTTP with pipelining.

**Constants:**

$$F = 100 \text{ KB} = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ bits}, \quad R = 10^9 \text{ bits/s} \implies \frac{F}{R} = 8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.8 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 1 — Per-object transfer time.**
$$\begin{aligned}
t_{file} &= \frac{F}{R} \\
&= \frac{100 \times 1024 \times 8}{10^9} \text{ s} \\
&\approx 8.192 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\
&\approx 0.82 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* File size in bits divided by link bandwidth in bits per second.

**Step 2 — Per-connection overhead (non-persistent).**
For one object over a fresh TCP connection:
$$T_1 = \underbrace{2\text{RTT}}_{\text{3-way handshake}} + \underbrace{\text{RTT}}_{\text{request+response}} + \frac{F}{R}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 2(0.05) + 0.05 + 0.00082 \\
&= 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.00082 \\
&\approx 0.15082 \text{ s} \approx 150.8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* TCP SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK costs one RTT; HTTP request and response together cost another RTT; then file transfer.

**Step 3 (a) — Non-persistent, sequential.**
$$T_{np,\text{seq}} = n \cdot T_1 = 10 \cdot 150.82 = 1508.2 \text{ ms} \approx 1.51 \text{ s}$$

*Logic:* Worst case — each object waits for the previous TCP connection to fully close.

**Step 4 (a) — Non-persistent, k = 6 parallel.**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{np,6} &= \left\lceil \frac{n}{k}\right\rceil \cdot T_1 \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{10}{6}\right\rceil \cdot 150.82 \\
&= 2 \cdot 150.82 \\
&= 301.64 \text{ ms} \approx 0.30 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* With 6 simultaneous TCP connections, two "rounds" (6 + 4 objects) are needed.

**Step 5 (b) — Persistent with pipelining.**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{pipe} &= \underbrace{2\text{RTT}}_{\text{TCP setup}} + \underbrace{\text{RTT}}_{\text{first HTTP req/resp}} + n \cdot \frac{F}{R} \\
&= 2(0.05) + 0.05 + 10(0.00082) \\
&= 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.0082 \\
&= 0.1582 \text{ s} \approx 158.2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* All requests pipelined in one RTT, responses stream back. File transfers may overlap in the bottleneck pipe.

**Step 6 — Comparison matrix.**

| Strategy | Total Time (ms) | Speedup vs Sequential |
|---|---|---|
| Non-persistent, sequential | **1508.2** | $1\times$ |
| Non-persistent, k=6 parallel | **301.6** | $\approx 5\times$ |
| Persistent, pipelined | **158.2** | $\approx 9.5\times$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Even on a fat 1 Gbps link, the **RTT dominates** ($F/R$ is negligible). This is precisely why persistent connections and HTTP/2 multiplexing are essential for modern web performance.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Stop-and-Wait Utilization

**Problem.** Compute the link utilization for a single HTTP request where the response file is $F = 100$ KB, $R = 1$ Mbps, and $\text{RTT} = 100$ ms.

**Step 1 — Compute transmission time.**
$$t_{trans} = \frac{F}{R} = \frac{100 \times 1024 \times 8}{10^6} \approx 0.8192 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the parameter $a$.**
$$a = \frac{\text{RTT}}{t_{trans}} = \frac{0.1}{0.8192} \approx 0.122$$

*Logic:* $a$ is the ratio of latency to transmission time. $a \ll 1$ means the link is fat; $a \gg 1$ means the link is long-and-thin.

**Step 3 — Compute utilization (stop-and-wait).**
$$U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.122)} = \frac{1}{1.244} \approx 0.804$$

*Logic:* $U = 80.4\%$ — meaning 19.6% of the link's capacity is wasted waiting for the ACK.

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
With only **one outstanding request**, $U < 1$ always. To approach $U = 1$, we need pipelining or HTTP/2 multiplexing, which keeps the pipe full.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WWW End-to-End Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    USER[End User] --> BROWSER[Client Browser\nChrome / Firefox]
    BROWSER -->|DNS Lookup| DNS[DNS Resolver]
    DNS -->|IP Address| BROWSER
    BROWSER -->|HTTP Request over TCP| PROXY[Proxy / Cache Server]
    PROXY -->|Cache Hit Return| BROWSER
    PROXY -->|Cache Miss Forward| WEBSRV[Origin Web Server\nApache / Nginx]
    WEBSRV --> APPSRV[Application Server\nDjango / Express]
    APPSRV --> DB[(Database\nMySQL / MongoDB)]
    DB -->|Query Result| APPSRV
    APPSRV -->|Dynamic HTML| WEBSRV
    WEBSRV -->|HTTP Response| PROXY
    PROXY -->|Cached Response| BROWSER
    BROWSER -->|Render HTML CSS JS| RENDER[Rendered Web Page]
    RENDER --> USER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Exam):** When asked to "draw the WWW architecture", always show **at least these five blocks** — Browser, Proxy, Web Server, Application Server, Database — and label each arrow with the protocol (HTTP, TCP, DNS, SQL).

## 4.2 HTTP Transaction Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Browser
    participant S as Origin Web Server
    participant F as File System / DB
    Note over C,S: TCP Connection Establishment
    C->>S: SYN (seq=x)
    S->>C: SYN+ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
    C->>S: ACK (ack=y+1)
    Note over C,S: HTTP Request-Response (1st object)
    C->>S: GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\nHost: www.ktu.edu.in
    S->>F: Read index.html / Query DB
    F-->>S: HTML bytes
    S->>C: HTTP/1.1 200 OK\nContent-Type: text/html\nContent-Length: 1234
    Note over C,S: Persistent Connection - Reused
    C->>S: GET /logo.png HTTP/1.1
    S->>C: HTTP/1.1 200 OK\nContent-Type: image/png
    Note over C,S: Connection Teardown
    C->>S: FIN
    S->>C: FIN+ACK
    C->>S: ACK
```

## 4.3 HTTP Request and Response Message Format

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HTTP Request Message] --> A1[Request Line\nGET /path HTTP/1.1]
    A1 --> A2[Header Lines\nHost, User-Agent, Accept, Cookie]
    A2 --> A3[Empty Line CRLF]
    A3 --> A4[Message Body Optional\nPOST data, file upload]

    B[HTTP Response Message] --> B1[Status Line\nHTTP/1.1 200 OK]
    B1 --> B2[Header Lines\nContent-Type, Date, Server, Set-Cookie]
    B2 --> B3[Empty Line CRLF]
    B3 --> B4[Message Body\nHTML JSON image bytes]
```

## 4.4 WWW Protocol Stack Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    L5[Application Layer\nHTTP HTTPS FTP SMTP DNS]
    L4[Transport Layer\nTCP UDP]
    L3[Network Layer\nIP ICMP]
    L2[Link Layer\nEthernet WiFi]
    L1[Physical Layer\nCables Radio Fiber]

    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The WWW is purely an **Application-layer** construct. It rides on top of TCP (port 80/443), which sits on top of IP. Some KTU exam questions test whether students mistakenly claim WWW is a network-layer protocol — it is not.

## 4.5 Connection Style Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[New HTTP Request] --> Q1{Is connection\nalready open?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| PERSIST[Persistent HTTP\nSend request on existing socket]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{HTTP version?}
    Q2 -->|HTTP/1.0| NPERSIST[Open new TCP connection\nper object]
    Q2 -->|HTTP/1.1 or later| PERSIST
    PERSIST --> Q3{Pipelining enabled?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| PIPE[Pipeline multiple requests\nin one RTT]
    Q3 -->|No| SEQ[Sequential request-response\non same socket]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.** Define the **World Wide Web (WWW)**. List its **four main architectural components** with one-line descriptions.

**Model Answer.**

The WWW is a distributed, hypermedia-based information system that operates over the Internet, enabling users to access and link resources (documents, images, videos) via a unified addressing and transport mechanism.

| # | Component | One-line Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **HTTP** | Request-response application protocol that transports resources |
| 2 | **URL / URI** | Global naming scheme that uniquely identifies every resource |
| 3 | **HTML** | Markup language that structures and formats the resource for display |
| 4 | **Web Browser / Web Server** | Client and server end-systems that consume and produce resources |

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.** Differentiate between **persistent** and **non-persistent HTTP** connections. State which one is the default in HTTP/1.1.

**Model Answer.**

| Parameter | Non-Persistent | Persistent |
|---|---|---|
| TCP connections | One per object | One for multiple objects |
| Setup overhead | Repeated per object | Once, amortized |
| Default in HTTP/1.0 | **Yes** | No |
| Default in HTTP/1.1 | No | **Yes** |
| Latency for n objects | $\approx 3n \cdot \text{RTT}$ | $\approx 2\text{RTT} + n \cdot \text{RTT}$ |
| Server load | High | Lower |
| Example use | Legacy HTTP/1.0 browsers | Modern HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only *"persistent is faster"* and lose marks. You **must** state the default versions for both and explain the **RTT amortization** reasoning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architectural components of the WWW** and the role of each component in serving a web page. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **HTTP request and response message formats** in detail, including general and entity headers, with examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a).**

The WWW architecture follows a **multi-tier client-server model** with optional intermediaries:

1. **Client (Browser).** Resolves the URL via DNS, opens a TCP connection to port 80/443, sends the HTTP request, and renders the response. Modern browsers also parse HTML, execute JavaScript, and manage cookies/local storage.

2. **Proxy / Cache Server.** A forward proxy sits between the client and origin server. It caches responses, reducing latency for repeat visits, and may enforce security policies. A reverse proxy (e.g., NGINX in front of Apache) shields the origin.

3. **Origin Web Server.** Listens on TCP port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS). Hosts static files and forwards dynamic requests to the application tier. Software examples: Apache, NGINX, IIS.

4. **Application Server.** Executes server-side code (PHP, Python, Node.js, Java) to generate dynamic content. Often communicates with a database.

5. **Database Server.** Stores persistent application data; queried via SQL or NoSQL APIs.

**Information flow:**
$$\text{Browser} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP}} \text{Proxy} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP}} \text{Web Server} \xrightarrow{\text{API}} \text{App Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SQL}} \text{DB}$$

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block).**

---

**Model Solution (b).**

**HTTP Request Message Format:**

$$\text{Request} = \underbrace{\text{Request-Line}}_{\text{method, URI, version}} \;|\; \underbrace{\text{Headers}}_{\text{key: value pairs}} \;|\; \underbrace{\text{CRLF}}_{\text{blank line}} \;|\; \underbrace{\text{Body}}_{\text{optional}}$$

**Example Request:**

```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.ktu.edu.in
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html, application/json
Accept-Language: en-US
Cookie: sessionid=abc123
Connection: keep-alive

```

**HTTP Response Message Format:**

$$\text{Response} = \underbrace{\text{Status-Line}}_{\text{version, code, phrase}} \;|\; \underbrace{\text{Headers}}_{\text{key: value pairs}} \;|\; \underbrace{\text{CRLF}}_{\text{blank line}} \;|\; \underbrace{\text{Body}}_{\text{entity}}$$

**Example Response:**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Mon, 15 Jul 2024 10:30:00 GMT
Server: Apache/2.4.57
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
Content-Length: 1024
Set-Cookie: sessionid=xyz789; HttpOnly
Connection: keep-alive

<html><body>Hello KTU</body></html>
```

**Header categories:**

- **General Headers** — apply to both request & response (`Date`, `Connection`, `Cache-Control`).
- **Request Headers** — `Host`, `User-Agent`, `Accept`, `Accept-Encoding`, `Cookie`, `Authorization`.
- **Response Headers** — `Server`, `Set-Cookie`, `WWW-Authenticate`.
- **Entity Headers** — `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Last-Modified`, `ETag`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **structure of a URL** with a suitable example. Discuss **absolute vs relative URLs** and **URL encoding**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A web page consists of a base HTML file of size $F_h = 50$ KB and $n = 9$ embedded image objects each of size $F_i = 200$ KB. The RTT between client and server is 100 ms and the bottleneck link rate is 100 Mbps. Compute the total response time using (i) non-persistent HTTP with one connection per object, and (ii) persistent HTTP with pipelining. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a).**

**Generic URL Syntax:**

$$\text{URL} = \text{scheme://authority/path?query\#fragment}$$

**Example with all parts:**

```
https://admin:pwd@www.ktu.edu.in:8080/courses/s7/cn/notes.html?module=4&type=pdf#section3
```

| Field | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| scheme | `https` | Protocol |
| userinfo | `admin:pwd` | Credentials (deprecated) |
| host | `www.ktu.edu.in` | DNS name of server |
| port | `8080` | TCP port (default 443 for https) |
| path | `/courses/s7/cn/notes.html` | Resource location |
| query | `module=4&type=pdf` | Parameters |
| fragment | `section3` | In-page anchor (client-side) |

**Absolute vs Relative URLs:**

| Aspect | Absolute URL | Relative URL |
|---|---|---|
| Contains scheme & host? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Resolves against | DNS directly | A *base URL* |
| Example | `https://www.ktu.edu.in/cn/notes.html` | `/cn/notes.html` or `../notes.html` |
| Use case | Cross-server links, bookmarks, emails | Same-site navigation |

**URL Encoding (Percent-Encoding).** Characters outside the ASCII unreserved set are encoded as `%HH` where `HH` is the hex code. Space $\rightarrow$ `%20`, `?` in data $\rightarrow$ `%3F`. This guarantees that the URL grammar is unambiguous.

---

**Model Solution (b).**

**Constants:**

$$F_h = 50 \text{ KB} = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ bits}, \quad F_i = 200 \text{ KB} = 1.6 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}, \quad R = 10^8 \text{ bits/s}, \quad \text{RTT} = 0.1 \text{ s}$$

**Transmission times:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_h &= \frac{4 \times 10^5}{10^8} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms} \\
t_i &= \frac{1.6 \times 10^6}{10^8} = 1.6 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Per-object non-persistent cost (for each object — HTML or image):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{one} &= 2\text{RTT} + \text{RTT} + \frac{F}{R} \\
&= 3\text{RTT} + \frac{F}{R}
\end{aligned}$$

For HTML: $T_h = 0.3 + 0.004 = 0.304$ s.
For each image: $T_i = 0.3 + 0.016 = 0.316$ s.

**(i) Non-persistent, sequential (worst case):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{np} &= T_h + n \cdot T_i \\
&= 0.304 + 9 \cdot 0.316 \\
&= 0.304 + 2.844 \\
&= 3.148 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Persistent with pipelining (HTTP/1.1):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{pipe} &= 2\text{RTT} + \text{RTT} + \frac{nF_i + F_h}{R} \\
&= 0.2 + 0.1 + \frac{9 \cdot 200 + 50 \text{ KB} \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{10^8} \\
&= 0.3 + \frac{14.4 \times 10^6}{10^8} \\
&= 0.3 + 0.144 \\
&= 0.444 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result Comparison:**

| Strategy | Total Time (s) |
|---|---|
| Non-persistent, sequential | **3.148** |
| Persistent, pipelined | **0.444** |
| **Speedup** | $\approx \mathbf{7.1\times}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion** — $1$ KB $= 8 \times 1024$ bits (use binary, not decimal) is a frequent loss point. Marks are cut for sloppy units.
> 2. **Forgetting the TCP handshake** — Students often omit the $2\text{RTT}$ for SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK.
> 3. **Mixing persistent and non-persistent formulas** — clearly state which model applies *before* substituting.
> 4. **Final answer without units** — always write the result in **seconds** or **ms** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **WWW ≠ Internet.** Internet is infrastructure; WWW is an application service.
- [ ] **Four pillars of WWW:** HTTP, URL, HTML, Browser/Server.
- [ ] **Default ports:** HTTP = **80**, HTTPS = **443**.
- [ ] **URL formula:** `scheme://host[:port]/path?query#fragment`.
- [ ] **Fragment is client-side only** — never transmitted to the server.
- [ ] **HTTP is stateless** — every request is independent unless cookies/sessions are used.
- [ ] **HTTP methods recap:** GET (safe + idempotent), POST (neither), PUT (idempotent), DELETE (idempotent), HEAD (safe).
- [ ] **Status code classes:** 1xx info, 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
- [ ] **Common codes to memorize:** 200, 201, 204, 301, 302, 304, 400, 401, 403, 404, 500, 502, 503.
- [ ] **Persistent vs non-persistent:** HTTP/1.0 default is non-persistent; HTTP/1.1 default is persistent.
- [ ] **Performance formula (non-persistent, sequential):** $T_{np}(n) = n \cdot (3\text{RTT} + F/R)$.
- [ ] **Performance formula (persistent, pipelined):** $T_{pipe}(n) = 2\text{RTT} + \text{RTT} + n \cdot F/R$.
- [ ] **HTTP/2 multiplexing** keeps a single TCP connection and interleaves frames — eliminates head-of-line blocking.
- [ ] **WWW stack position:** Application layer only — runs on TCP/IP.
- [ ] **Proxy benefits:** caching, anonymity, security filtering, load balancing.
- [ ] **Stop-and-wait utilization:** $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = \dfrac{\text{RTT} \cdot R}{F}$.
- [ ] **Idempotent vs Safe:** Idempotent = same effect on repeat; Safe = no side-effect at all (GET is both; POST is neither).
- [ ] **Statelessness mitigation:** Cookies, sessions, JWT tokens.
- [ ] **Always convert KB → bits** as $\times 1024 \times 8$ for numericals (or $\times 1000 \times 8$ if SI — be consistent and state assumption).
